author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: One easy way to separate out the fat is to pour the cooled liquid into a ziplock bag and wait about a minute for the fat to rise to the top of the bag.  Then snip a little bit off a corner and let the liquid fall into a bowl, stopping when the fat gets to the bottom of the bag.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: What are English-cut as opposed to the short ribs in the supermarket?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
English cut short ribs are those that you will typically see -- cut parallel to (along) the bone so that you have a single bone about 4 inches long. The other cut is flanken cut which is cut across several bones so you have meat surrounding 3 or 4 short bones surrounded by the meat.

Hope this is helpful.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Image result for "English cut" short ribs?
English style: The ribs are cut parallel to the bone, with one bone per piece. They tend to hold their shape even after a long braise and make an impressive presentation. Flanken style: The ribs are cut across the bone. Each piece has three to four short sections of bone with a generous portion of meat around them.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: English cut are just short ribs cut along the bone in one piece with one bone. Flanken are cut across the bone with several bones in each strip.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Here's an article about different cuts of short ribs: http://www.bonappetit.com/test-kitchen/cooking-tips/article/beef-short-ribs

English style: The ribs are cut parallel to the bone, with one bone per piece. They tend to hold their shape even after a long braise and make an impressive presentation.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Christmas Eve dinner and it was divine! I didn't tell my dad, a Trump voter, that it's called "Obama's Ribs", so it felt extra nice when he complimented me on them!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
These turned out like an excellent meal in a fantastic restaurant. Set them on a bed of egg noodles. Would serve just this way for a dinner party.
I would like to try it a little more rustic for just us. We used grass fed short ribs from a small local farm that has such a buttery flavor.
Thanks Obama.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Made for a dinner party so doubled the recipe, used boneless short ribs. I made it over two days - browned the meat and made sauce in a sauté pan, put 20 pieces in a roasting pan and cooked in the sauce for two hours, then chilled overnight, pulled the cold fat from surface, put ribs on a platter and puréed the veggies and broth, returned all to roasting pan, put in oven for 2 hours at 325 to rewarm, lingered over drinks so turned heat down to 200 for another hour. Tender & perfect.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Made this over two days. Followed directions exactly for quantities. Used boneless ribs and browned thoroughly. After braising 2  hours refrigerated overnight. Removed fat cap. Set aside ribs and puréed all veggies in sauce. Returned ribs to sauce. Heated thickened sauce drenched ribs at 325 for an hour. Served over spaetzle. With roasted thyme seasoned carrots Unbelievably delicious and rich. 3.5 lbs boneless ribs made 10 servings. If I keep feeding him this well I will never get rid of Husband

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Made it tonight and it was delicious. I didn't have lemongrass. Nor did I puree the sauce, rather used small diced veggies and it was tender, moist, and rich. Used "short ribs" from my butcher, they didn't know what English cut was either.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: If the bones come out, then remove them and trim gristle from the bottom.  Most restaurants present short ribs like this.  Reduce the sauce somewhat after the ribs are done.  After defatting, you can puree half to thicken the sauce a bit, and leave half with the chopped veges, combine the two.  Also, one star anise adds depth and complexity.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Made this and it was delicious--props from the finicky teenage son. Skimming off the fat and adding in the puréed vegetables makes the dish--recommend not skipping the steps. There is plenty of sauce for leftovers, which we'll serve over egg noodles. This recipe would likely also work well with other cuts of meat that have enough fat and braise well.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I kept all the ingredients and ratios the same except for the liquids.  After reading others' comments about having a lot of extra sauce, I cut the liquids in half.  The result was excellent flavor & perfect viscosity for the sauce/ gravy.   This is one of my favorite NYT recipes!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Made this for the first time and doubled the recipe. Used dried lemongrass. Followed recipe and was delicious! Served it with roasted brussell sprouts, fetuccini nest noodles tossed with olive oil and parmesan, and a chopped kale salad. It was a hit and guests loved it.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Very flavorful for a winter Sunday dinner. Made them in a slow cooker on low for 8 hours. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly. Wouldn't change anything. Had home made egg noodles on the side.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: By far the best NYT recipe I've made so far.  Absolutely delicious- perfect for dinner on a cold day.  This being said the second time I cooked the recipe I used 6 short ribs (totaling 3 lbs- same as 8 6oz short ribs).  This fit very snug in my dutch oven and I definitely wouldn't be able to fit more.  For anyone cooking the recipe I would recommend asking your butcher for 3lbs of short ribs seeing that the actual pieces themselves often vary in size.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This is an incredible recipe! I made it with white wine instead of red and it was better in my opinion. I have made it at least ten times over the years and it's always at hit. The white wine makes it a bit lighter but still delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I made it over two days - browned the meat and made sauce in a sauté pan, put 20 pieces in a roasting pan and cooked in the sauce for two hours, then chilled overnight, pulled the cold fat from surface, put ribs on a platter and puréed the veggies and broth, returned all to roasting pan, put in oven for 2 hours at 325 to rewarm, lingered over drinks so turned heat down to 200 for another hour. Tender &perfect

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Incredible dish. Made this for my father on his visit to my very first “grown up” home. My father is not a big eater and never appeases anyone with false polite commentary. He went back for 2nd and was disappointed when there wasn’t enough short rib for 3rds! He settled for more sauce on the horseradish mashed potato’s. I made my own plum sauce out of prunes which added….an immense amount of time, yes…but the depth to the dish it served was undeniably worth it. Made me proud to serve this dish<3

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Outstanding recipe for its flavor and ease of cooking. I made it with boneless short ribs because that's what my butcher had, and after 2 hours, they were superbly tender but not falling apart (I had worried given the ribs were boneless).

I served it with roasted baby carrots and fingerling potatoes (& horseradish), but next time (and there will be next times) I will serve with either mashed potatoes or wide noodles because the sauce is so splendid you don't want to miss a drop.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I made it with horseradish smashed potatoes - yummy!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: First time I made this with no improvisation - wonderful. Yesterday I increased the ginger, lemon grass, & garlic, and added a splash of worcestershire. Used fist-sized chunks of chuck. Did all the browning & saute in cast iron, then "braised" in the Instant pot - 50 minutes high pressure. Keep warm OFF, de-pressurized naturally. Should have been 45 minutes - we were on the tipping point of going dry; but fortunately, did not cross it. Next time, shiitake!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I always makes a short rib dish over 2 days.  I brown ribs, add veggies, red wine, stock, tomato paste…just a glob, braise 2-3 hours at 300, cool, strain liquid ( veggies discarded), put ribs on plate covered overnight in fridge.  Next day , pop off fat cap and finish recipe as one wishes.  But this for me is only way remove remove fat.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: My butcher didn't have any short rib so I used a chuck roast instead and it worked beautifully!  Delicious sauce.  It's a keeper!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
So deelish! Lip-smacking good. So worth the step of processing the vegetables. Added just a 1/2 cup of braising liquid to the vegetables and then added the puree back into the remaining liquid which I reduced a bit after removing the veggies. Made for a rich, flavorful sauce.
Served with wide noodles and broccoli.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This was a great dish.  I followed recipe until oven, then moved all of it into slow cooker for 6 hours.  At that point, I cooled everything so I could blend solids and reduced liquid sauce over high heat by half.  My husband moved it to the top of our hall of fame.  I served with white rice and a salad with anchovy/lemon/egg (Caesar-ish dressing).  Will make again soon.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Awesome, as most Obama stuff is. I think I’ll make sure the ribs are submerged next time, and will make sure they are falling apart when done. Mine were in the oven for two hours but were not completely tender.  But super tasty and delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: What the heck are 'English cut" short ribs? I'm a sophisticated cook but don't have access to a fancy NYC butcher & am mystified. If they are simply 'off the bone' please say so, & if it's different please describe. Recipe, btw, sounds delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: "English cut" The ribs are cut parallel to the bone, with one bone per piece. They tend to hold their shape even after a long braise and make an impressive presentation.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: https://www.google.ca/search?client=safari&rls=en&q="Englis...

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
English Cut ribs are cut parallel to the bone, so you have 1 bone per piece. Another cut is called Flanken, cut across the bone.

The english cut will hold their shape better after long cooking, the Flanken cut tends to separate into pieces.

For this recipe the English Cut makes a nicer presentation.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Here's a good description of English cut short ribs as opposed to the thinner flanken short ribs.

http://www.thekitchn.com/whats-the-difference-between-flanken-and-englis...

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
These were tasty, but I felt that the sauce lacked body. I think what could help with this is to double down on the garlic and add red chili flakes at the very start also. (I also like a previous reader's suggestion to try Hoisin sauce in place of the plum).

Does anyone else have any other suggestions on increasing body, I'd love to make this more savory.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: What about removing the meat and reducing the sauce on the stovetop before serving?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious - I'd serve it to  company.  Next time I will try cutting the plum sauce in half or perhaps eliminating it - or reducing the amount of carrot - in order to better balance sweet/savory.  Also, I added an extra 1/2 cup of liquid to the puree and next time will probably add 1 cup, making it a total of 2 1/2 c.  I served with rice on the side for me, and cooked some carrot chubs and fingerlings in broth to serve with the ribs for my dining partner.  We were both satisfied.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I cut the plum sauce to 1/4 cup the second time I made it and thought the flavors were better balanced - not so sweet.  I think adding the shaved the horseradish is a plus, as it gives the dish some pop.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and delicious!  Didn't have fresh lemon grass, but will be looking for some for a repeat performance.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Read others' comments about having lots of extra sauce so I kept the veggie ratios the same but only used half the liquids. Result:  excellent flavor & perfect viscosity for the sauce/gravy

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Follow other commenters and reduce plum sauce to 1/4 cup next time for better balance between sweet and savoury. Also included shaved horseradish for serving for a better zip.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious!  Although next time I will reduce the amount of plum sauce as I thought it was too overpowering.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have plum sauce or lemon grass, so I punted.  I didn't try to substitute the lemon grass, but I threw together something for the plum sauce:  some whole grain mustard, a little chipotle sauce and a spoonful hot and sweet pepper jelly from TJ's, all amounting to about 1/3 cup.  It was delectable.  Served it with mashed potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Substituted Hoisin for plum and was excellent

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight for a holiday dinner. It was fantastic. Not difficult to make and was a huge hit with the family. The recipe easily fed 6 (instead of 4) and we even had a little left over. It went paired beautifully with Roasted Root Vegetables with Polenta (also from the Times Cooking site.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I used a slow-cooker for the braising, 5 hours on high. Only needed 2cups of stock to provide enough liquid to cover the ribs in the cooker. After removing the ribs I let the braising liquid cool in the fridge so the fat was easy to remove, then used an immersion blender. Great recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Can this be done in a non-enameled cast iron dutch oven? The long braise and red wine scares me but am hoping the 3 cups of broth is enough to dilute the acidity. Anyone have issues with a metallic taste after cooking this in seasoned cast iron?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I made this for the first time tonight in a non-enameled cast iron dutch oven and I did not detect any metallic taste in the finished dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Followed the recipe exactly but used low sodium soy.  Used my slow cooker on high instead of the oven.  Went out to run an errand and when we came back the house smelled heavenly.  Wonderful flavors!!

This was a dry run.  Thinking I will make this for a holiday meal!!  Love everyone's side suggestions.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Add one star anise

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Used tawny port instead of wine, omitted the lemongrass, had the ribs cut across the bone, two cups turkey stock and one cup water (polar vortex, no way was I going outside for ingredients) and served with spaetzle crisped up in brown butter and duck fat.  Perfect dish

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Whoops, forgot I added a quarter cup of dried mushrooms, cepes, straight into the sauce before putting in the oven

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Prepared through step 4,tasted for seasoning and texture, refrigerated and finished the next evening. Very good the first day but fabulous the next.  I might make this with a chuck roast next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: After cooking and removing ribs, I put juices in a plastic take-out container and strained the veggies. Used mini cuisinart for the veggies and stored it all separately overnight in the fridge. Skimmed fat, added back vegetables and shook the container vigorously. Reheated ribs in 2/3 of the sauce and served over Martha Shulman’s polenta with pops of color from sautéed Brussels sprouts and carrots. Excellent as is. Don’t decrease cooking liquids - flavors would be too rich/concentrated.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Could these be done in a slow cooker?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I made half of this recipe. I didn't have plum sauce, but I had plum jam that someone gave us for Christmas- so I doctored it up with vinegar, red pepper flakes, garlic and onion. I also didn't have lemon grass (not available in our area), so I used the peel of a small lemon. This turned out delicious. I have lots of sauce frozen in cubes for future use! Thanks for the idea to add a star anise to the pot while it was braising. It added a lovely flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: We've made this twice and its AMAZING.  We usually have to cook it a bit longer than 2 hours depending on the size of our short ribs- we wait until the bones are falling out and its really tender.  I've made it the day before we planned to eat it - its even better the next day!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! The prep isn't too bad, but I wouldn't call this an entry-level recipe. We used our instant pot for the cooking and it was absolutely scrumptious!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
These were delicious! Made them exactly as instructed except for the end where I discarded the spent vegetables after straining the gravy. Popped the gravy into the freezer for an hour so the fat could congeal and removed it. Thickened the gravy with a beurre manie and re-added the ribs. It was amazing. 

Served with black-eyed peas and basmati rice cooked in coconut milk, roasted carrots and spinach salad. Made a great New Year's Day  meal.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: @glammie. Sounds like a great dinner!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Cooked this as written and it was amazing over egg noodles.  Crazy layers of flavor. Even the picky teenagers liked it. Couldn’t find fresh horseradish so we used a dollop of prepared horseradish and it did the trick.  

Serve with something fresh and textured like a salad or blanched/sautéed veggies as it’s quite heavy. 

Grocery store butcher didn’t know what English style ribs are so I told him “not flanken” and he knew what I was talking about.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Marvelous. I returned the separated cooking liquid and veggies to the pan and just hit it with my immersion blender. Left a few chunks of veggies hiding in the sauce. Amazingly flavorful! Perfect for a snowy Sunday.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Where do you get plum sauce?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe, purée definitely makes a difference. No need for the lemon grass, it doesn’t add any flavor. Serve with something green for color and cauliflower mash or mashed potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious but a lot of extra work compared to other recipes with a similar outcome. The lemongrass gets totally lost and could be left out. Blending the veggies into a sauce is totally genius though and should be done for every braise recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
I’m sorry, but is this pork ribs, lamb ribs or beef ribs?
I’m Australian and we don’t seem to have the same terminology ..

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
The recipe calls for bone-in beef short ribs. English-cut refers to being cut parallel to the ribs, so that there is more melt off the bone goodness.

But when all the butcher has is boneless short ribs, I buy them because they are still very good. The trick of adding some gelatin helps.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Are these meant to be served with the bone - or is it best to remove the bone before serving?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: English-cut short ribs are cut along the rib bone, so that each piece contains one long bone in it. Flanked-cut short ribs -- which you may have seen in Korean bbq places (indeed, they're great on the grill) are cut across the bone, so each piece is like a ribbon, with several little nubbins of bone in it. In NYC at least, the English cut is much more common in supermarkets and butcher shops.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: No, you don't cut the meat from the bone. Serve with bone in, and your diners cut the meat from the bone themselves. Sounds delicious -- looking forward to colder weather. I like the idea of horseradish mashed potatoes, too.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
This was delicious! I couldn't find the exact ingredients but goodness if I had!

My husband, being the major cook of the family, requested I make this at least 10 more times until Summer grilling season starts again.

Very economical as well!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I had all the ingredients except for the plum sauce so I tossed in some Hoisin instead.  Other than that I followed the recipe exactly. It tasted like more traditionally prepared short ribs but with added zip.  Excellent flavors!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Anyone tried this in a slow cooker?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Is Dutch oven covered or uncovered in oven?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I put it in covered and it worked nicely!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Just finished making this. Delicious, rich. Serving with rice and carrots. For the final step, after skimming off fat, I put veggies and sauce in food processor together, which was fine. Wish more sauce was produced.  Followed recipe to the letter.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I have made this and it is wonderful. I want to make it for a larger group, so will do in two batches. Question:   Will it suffer if I make it a day ahead?  My sense is NO, but has anyone done that?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This is pork, right?  Can one make it with beef short ribs?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Short ribs, by definition, are beef.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. The scent while cooking was sublime. I could smell every element particularly the lemongrass, plum sauce, and soy which I think deepens the otherwise classic flavors. I agree with others who said don't miss out on the benefits of pureeing the sauce.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Does the liquid have to cover the short ribs while they are in the oven?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Used boneless short ribs (I have no idea if they were English cut) and didn't have any plum sauce. They were still excellent!  Opted for 8 hours on slow in the crockpot.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This is a great dish.  Cooked it by the book.  Place the rib on mashed potatoes and ya got yourself a really nice, pretty dinner party meal.  Now I just need to get whole foods to have leaner ribs

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I was worried the plum sauce would add too much sweetness, but followed the recipe as is and it was wonderful. Served with plain boiled potatoes to make use of all the extra sauce and charred broccoli.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Add star anise to braising liquid

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I was afraid plum sauce may be too sweet, so I substituted it for hoisin sauce. The favor was wonderful!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but for me at least, not worth the time and effort I put into making the dish. However, mine is probably the only negative comment.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed. Worth it.  Delicious!  Before serving, I removed any bones and the elastic, "tough" connective membranes.  Also freezes well and makes a great lunch weeks later.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic! I reduced the broth to 2 cups. Used beef broth instead of wine as I am a teatotaller. And it was still the best short ribs I've had. The gravy is welcome on the plate for just about any side from yams to cornbread to brussel sprouts.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: What kind of starch or side do you all prefer to serve with these? Potatoes, noodles, polenta? I would love to make something in advance!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I think it would be great with Melissa Clark's mashed potato casserole, which you can also make ahead.  (Maybe add roasted garlic or fresh horseradish to the potatoes. Yum!)

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Are these boneless or bone-in?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: you can do it with either, or both.  I ordered both from my butcher (I think bones add flavor).  A BIG hit at our dinner for 12

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: One of the best recipes I’ve made in a long time. This one is a keeper! Ribs came out restaurant quality. No need to dine out when you have this recipe! I saved the extra liquid in ice cube trays as suggested and I’m looking forward to using them.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: bought "boneless short ribs" by mistake and did not have thyme, but followed the recipe otherwise. Delicious! I also prepared through step 4 and did the rest the next day. Did not have time to process the vegetables. Will do it the next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the recipe and used bone-in beef shanks instead of short ribs. I couldn't find plum sauce. I substituted half a cup of mirin (quarter cup for a single recipe) but otherwise followed the recipe as-is. Delicious over egg noodles. Great depth of flavor. Made it in the morning and everyone loved it at dinner. Excited to try it tomorrow after it melds some more. Thanks, Obama!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Much too sweet. Cut back on plum sauce.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Simply delicious prepared as written. I brought some horseradish in from the garden to grate over the top. This completed the dish perfectly.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Great flavor and the lemongrass really makes it. I doubled the recipe and needed about 3 hours to get to fork tender.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: 2 hours braising is nowhere near enough time to dissolve the fat and connective tissue. Needs 5-6 hours or the fat globs dominate.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Doubled carrots, onions and celery. 
Otherwise kept proportions ( eg spices,liquids) the same.
Did not purée at end.
Lots of sauce left over.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Used about 5.5#ribs.

No need to make extra sauce.
Used immersion blender to blend sauce. 

Didn't have plum sauce.... subbed....1/3c apricot jam, extra garlic clove,  a little extra ginger,  1T. Rice vinegar,  red pepper flakes, and 4 allspice berries.

AMAZING!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: You can stop looking: this is an amazing recipe. I increase the carrots and add parsnips and like to slice my onions for braises and stews. Before adding the wine, I add 1/3 cup of flour to make a velvety sauce. I skip the purée of the sauce at the end because I like hunks of veggies. As mentioned, the leftover sauce is a great flavor booster to other beef dishes.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: We made this using brisket for passover. Didn’t have lemongrass so substituted Gourmet Garden Thai seasoning paste, It was spectacular.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
served over creamy polenta was amazing
 boost the flavor seasoning for the stock in beginning 
 honestly we didn’t strain the fat beyond the strainer because didn’t have the correct equipment and we really liked how it turned out.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I've never been able to find plum sauce in the grocery store, so I use  blueberry preserves plus 1–2 tbsp hoisin sauce. It's fabulous. 10/10.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I suppose it would be rude to lick the pan?  These were definitely a BIG YUM!  I too substituted for the plum sauce with some Major Grey's Chutney.  Worked out just fine.  I fully cooked them on day one - and reheated on day two - I would recommend that if planning on serving the second day (to make degreasing easier) - cook about 1/2 less and reheat the following day, wrapped in tin foil, for 1/2 hour.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Used San-J Hoisin because I couldn't find plum sauce and thin sliced boneless short ribs instead of bone in and/or English cut. Only needed to braise for 90 min. I also only added 1/2 cup of the braising liquid to the pureed veggies to make a chutney. It was 5 stars. I'll absolutely make this again. I served with Sam Sifton's sauteed kale as well as simple roasted carrots and mashed potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Made this for new year’s eve and came out great, buttery meat and full of flavors. Couldn’t find a can of plums when shopping at Wholefoods, but found a Japanese-style spicy plum purée, of which I added just a teaspoon. I roasted it in a Le Creuset lower Dutch oven, worked great, but it surprised me that I had a strong reduction in liquid after the 2h in the oven with the lid on. This recipe is a keeper for sure!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good recipe. The short ribs could have been more tender though. Perhaps more time braising?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with chicken? I’m going to try. Change red wine to white? Any other thoughts?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I think that's a bad idea.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
If you do, I'd recommend making a high-intensity chicken stock as the foundation, then only using chicken thighs. Much of the dish's magic is all that dissolved connective tissue. 

If you have a lot of leftover sauce (like I've had both times), you might want to try stewing chicken in it?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Wow...  Really great.  I had 2.  7/8 LB ribs.  Thick, and even.  That was helpful.  But Really, it's too big a portion for most.  It's rich...  great flavor though...

Definitely do it again..

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Incredible recipe. Braised for about 30 minutes longer than the recipe and they came out perfect. Served with potato purée which absorbs the sauce nicely.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is fantastic! The lemongrass flavor is more subtle than I anticipated. It tastes more similar to a beef stew than lemongrass beef IMO. I couldn’t find plum sauce so I subbed hoisin sauce instead. I served with fresh rice noodles & a bright salad to balance the sweetness / saltiness of the stew. Will definitely make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Made this last night and as suggested by several other cooks, reduced the liquids by half.  Didn't bother separating out the fat or pureeing the veggies. 
 It was perfect.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Substitute mushrooms for onion.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Why do this?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this dish twice exactly as written and it gets rave reviews.  My husband and I rarely eat meat but have served this when we’ve had visiting family who are all carnivores, and even my husband likes this.  It takes some time and effort but don’t skip a step!  I serve with mashed potatoes but noodles would be good too.  The sauce is quite yummy.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Cook longer than 2 hrs depending on stove. Mine needed 2.5 hrs and could have gone 3 hrs even for tenderness and absorbing the flavors

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!  my local supermarket lemongrass was garbage, and I only ended up I with maybe half a tablespoon, but it was still really, really good. No changes except hit it with chopped cilantro at end, served with carrots roasted with good evoo and sumac. No leftovers.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Simply amazing.  Gusts couldn't get enough of the sauce.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Wish I'd read the comments before cooking. Next time, I'll reduce the amount of broth. Otherwise, made per recipe and it was wonderful! Served with Jasmine rice.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Easy to prepare and tasty

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: You don't need grapeseed oil, puh-lease. Canola is fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: How would this seasoning pair with a brisket rather than short ribs?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: @PoppyNYC.  I don’t know, but it would probably be very good.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: To separate the fat you can also pull out the shortribs, put them a separate bowl.  Put the braising liquid in the fridge and the fat will congeal on the surface and it very easy to scoop it out.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Excellent Meal. On my first try I followed the recipe as printed except for the lemon grass. As for the fat separation, I took out the short ribs and poured liquid into a glass bowl submergd in to a pot of cold water and it was easy to see to spoon out the fat. The next few weeks on my 2nd attempt I used the lemon grass, I can't say there was an improvement in flavor but it was just a different delicious flavor.  My new go to for entertaining. Served with creamy mashed potatoes/ brocollini. Yum!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: This was to die for food. Rich, tender.  Get a really good red wine to have with this. We had a French grench, it was wonderful.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: Don’t really need the lemongrass, and pureeing the veggies was a helpful step.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody:
Used 5 short ribs because that equalled 24 oz. Next time will use 8 short ribs.

Skipped lemongrass because I couldn’t find it at the store. Used dried thyme because I couldn’t find it at the store and I don’t have herb garden.

Made the dish the night before. Stored the ribs, veggies, and broth separately. Puréed veggies and broth the next day. Warmed purée and ribs in pot.

Served with crispy pan-fried gnocchi and roasted Brussels sprouts. 

Soooo good! Will make again and again and again.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Red Wine-Braised Short Ribs With Lemongrass and Soy Recipe
commentBody: I absolutely love these.   Have made them twice, doubling recipe to give extras to single friend.  i did not have Plum Sauce so i put together  my own version: Hosin, Soy, Jam once used  berry another time used apricot a bit of special vinegar (Olivers blood orange) . This tastes even better the next day.  I don't use "English Cut" because they are too big to put on a plate.  Don't know the point of them.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I'd like to know how the Times calculates the conversion of weights from cups to grams.  In this recipe we're instructed to use 195 grams all-purpose flour for the equivalent of 1 1/2 cups all-purpose flour.  By most conversion charts 1 1/2 cups should be anywhere from 180 to 187 grams flour to equal 1 1/2 cups.  King Arthur Flour's conversion chart says that 1 cup all-purpose flour  is equal to 120 grams.  This diversion has been in many Times recipes. What gives?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Maybe 195 grams included the amount used to flour the pan? It's not written that way, but that could account for the overage beyond the 1 1/2 cups.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I can't speak to how the Times calculates conversions, but I baked this based on the metric measurements and it worked well.  I would NOT reduce the amount of flour.  In any event, I don't believe that when recipes give both metric and imperial measures that they are necessarily intended to be exactly equal.  It is best to choose to use either metric or imperial for a particular recipe you are cooking and then use those directions throughout.  Don't mix and match.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: In my experience, weight has always been known to be more accurate, as it doesn't vary as you say. The Times is just rounding, as are your other sources most likely since measuring dry ingredients in cups will always produce inconsistencies. I always go with the weights listed, since I may pack something a little more or less, but the proper amount will always be the same weight.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Salted or unsalted pistachios??

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I would assume unsalted.  Easy enough to find at TJ's

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Unsalted! You're already adding salt into the cake, and salted pistachios are often far too salty.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: unsalted

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe, though my cake needed 75 minutes at 325 degrees to fully bake. I substituted the season's first cherries for strawberries. The cherries complemented the flavors very well. Overall, scrumptious!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Vik, did you use lavender with the cherries?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Mine also took a long time in the oven, closer to 80 minutes at 325.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! The only issue: it remained underdone in the center where the cake cracked. So good I will cook it again but maybe boost the heat to 330 for an hour in convection oven. Or maybe just a little less milk? Worth experimenting!

We macerated the berries with sugar and a splash of port and added mint at the end. Perfect.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight and it was delicious and easy; my 2 favorite things about any recipe. I used unsalted pistachios. Will definitely make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Baking in times of Covid.... shelled old salted pistachios, with a pinch of almonds, omitted salt in recipe. For sugar I used 1/2 cup light brown and 1/4 cup maple syrup (I always reduce sugar). Made baking powder 3/4 tsp. Divine!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Thanks Brigid!  Thinking of making this for my daughter's 16th birthday tomorrow.  Covid challenges - ugh.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I too had an undone cake. Toothpick came out clean, but bottom center was runny. Broke apart when I took out of the pan. Definitely bake longer than stated. Also, too sweet for me. Doubt I’ll make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: John Golden-Gold Medal/Pillsbury All Purpose White Flour 130 g = 1 Cup. I wrote to the company & they were kind enough to send me a list of their flour weights. King Arthur Unbleached Flour 120 g = 1 Cup. KAF states they have a higher protein content therefore weight is lower per cup. If I have a recipe with APF, I assume it’s bleached unless it’s stated. So you were right to question the weight in this recipe. Adjust your cup weight based on the flour you have on hand. Hope this info helps :)

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Made this yesterday.  I baked it for about 70 minutes, testing with a toothpick starting at 50 minutes.  I think the cake is too dry.  The whipped cream and berries certainly compensated and overall it was a big hit at my party.  Next time I might cook it for 60 minutes and cross my fingers.  I used basil and it was delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Cake has great flavor and is not too heavy for a summer dessert. My strawberries were perfect plain, so i left them alone and added the lavender vanilla sugar to the cream as I whipped it.  Turned out great.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: roasted or unroasted pistachios?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Amazing cake, I loved the pistachio addition. I made it as is and found the cake too sweet, next time will lower the sugar amount. Otherwise it’s the absolutely perfect summer dessert.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody:
I risked salmonella poisoning for the first time in 27 years (that's when I got salmonella from fresh eggnog).  It was worth tasting this batter.  I really loved making and eating this cake, but thought that the strawberries (I just macerated some in sugar) made it soggy, so next time, will put the strawberries to the side. 

Honestly, I prefer eating this cake by itself.  Delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Ooh, I want to add this. Weighed all ingredients and baked till Internal temp was 210.  It's dense.  Followed recipe exactly.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Made this today on a whim and it was delicious! Not as sweet as I expected it to be, which was a good thing. Used the lavender for the strawberries, and it added such a lovely subtle floral note, and I don’t typically like lavender. Served with vanilla ice cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: OMG good. 1. Reduced sugar in cake to one cup and butter to 11 tablespoons. It was plenty sweet and rich. 2. Lavender and vanilla in the strawberries aren't prominent. You can skip those hard-to-get ingredients. 3. Check the center of the cake often toward the end of baking. (I use a thin wooden grilling skewer.) There's an ideal moment when the dampness disappears and you need to yank it out before it gets overdone. 4. I used "lightly salted" roasted pistachios and the full amount of salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: This is my go to recipe for my love of all things pistachio!  I reduce the sugar to 3/4 cup, add a bit more almond extract (1/2 tsp) and sometimes I add chocolate chips.  I usually make muffins instead of cake.  Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody:
This is one of my favorite desserts for summer strawberries and I make it several times a year.  This time I followed the timing recommendations in the recipe: 5 minutes for creaming the butter and 30 seconds per egg.  I ended up with a way too dense brick of a cake.  For the first time it was a bust.  I think I over mixed the batter.  I'm going back to my much shorter beating times that give me a nice crumb.  

Live and learn, now I know!  The recipe still gets five stars from me.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: This pound cake is superb! It did take longer to bake than the stated time. Served it with strawberries, and looking forward to trying it with cherries later in the summer!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I used 1/2 tsp of pistachio extract and 1/4 vanilla extract. I also put one T of pistachio paste into the wet ingredients

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I put 1/4 tsp of vanilla extract and 1/2 tsp of pistachio extract. I also put 1 T of pistachio paste into the wet ingredients

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Perfect combination of cake, strawberry topping and cream alas the cake itself was too buttery. Might try again with less butter.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Made the cake for a friend's birthday celebration. It was made as directed using grams came together wonderfully. Was a bit at party!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe! I used lightly salted pistachios and omitted the salt instead. Moist, flavorful cake that’s perfect w/ a dollop of cream!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I only added 8T butter and subbed in one quarter banana for the rest so a little less heavy. Next time I would also cut the sugar in the strawberries by half

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Only made the poundcake and served w/ fresh berries. Reduced sugar to just 1 cup of turbinado sugar for a light level of sweetness, which works for our family. We loved this recipe so much that my daughter requested it a second time just a few weeks later. Delicious and super easy.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: This is a new favorite.  My family swooned!  I used a combination strawberries, raspberries, and a peach, and used the suggested mint.  The cake is amazing, and as others note, this is not hard to put together.  I was deliberating among a few strawberry desserts and I'm so glad I chose to make this one.  Go for it!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: This is my go to recipe!  Instead of making a loaf, I make muffins. I always reduce the sugar a bit and sometimes add mini chocolate chips.  It's a winner.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: I share the confusion in some of these notes about the weight of a cup of flour. I suspect, however, that the recipe developers and testers measured by weight, so when weight is given I go by that.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Is this recipe for a loaf pan or Bundt cake pan?? Can’t figure it out 🤪

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: @Rookie Baker step 1 says 9x5x3 loaf pan

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: Popular with everyone!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pistachio Pound Cake With Strawberries Recipe
commentBody: We rarely have dessert, but it was Father’s Day so why not. It was fantastic!  I baked at 325° with convection, so started checking at 45 minutes and it was still batter. Another 7 minutes and it still stuck to the knife. Another six min and it was done. Probably would have been perfect a minute sooner. Served with local strawberries and blueberries, freshly whipped cream sprinkled with minced mint leaves from the garden. Loved every bite.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Rye flour - is this a must?  Are there any less esoteric substitutes?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can just use regular all-purpose instead of the rye flour.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used almond flour. It was delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you don't want to buy even a small bag of rye flour, markets with good bulk sections often have it. If you don't mind buying a bag, Bob's Red Mill or Kind Arthur sell less than 5 lbs. Rye is a great addition to pie crusts for cherry and other fruit pies, too.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Have made this at least 4 times this summer already. Did not use rye flour but have used almond flour - delicious and simple.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The rye flour with the subtle acid from the apple cider.  Special.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just checked the cookbook and thank you for including weights in the cookbook and in the recipe! Although I'm American, I strongly prefer to bake by weight vs. volume (and in metric units -- but I'll take what I can get) and it's so refreshing to see a gradual move in baking toward this!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Amen. D'accord.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Consider skipping the strawberry jam if you have sweet enough strawberries or lots of them.

The rye does make it more interesting and a nice change from the classic pastry crust. I didn't tell out guests until they had eaten it and all were pleasantly surprised.

Also, consider cooking less than 20 minutes -- we found the tart was more tender and cooking it 20 minutes, it was tougher and hard for people to cut. Then again, it all depends on your oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Excellent and easy.

While a few have asked about the necessity of the rye flour, it really does add a nice subtle favor that's worth picking some up. Necessary? Maybe not. Worth it? Yes.

A did stripes of strawberries and blueberries, which would be attractive for a patriotic look.

I added the jam to the mascarpone to make it easier to spread and introduce the flavor throughout.

As others have noted, my crust was finished baking before the recommended 20-25 minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
The last thing we need right now is a "patriotic look" given all the jingoistic raving that we are contending with at present.

That said, blueberries are delicious and taste great with strawberries.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Ah, yes! Oui! Le drapeau tricolore! An homage to the French roots of this tart. Perfect. I'll make this tart again in July to honor Bastille Day.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thanks for your notes about the baking time. We've updated the range to 15 to 20 minutes (from the original 20 to 25). I hope that helps!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious. I skipped the jam and drizzled the berries with a balsamic glaze (good quality balsamic vinegar boiled with brown sugar), and also added a little vanilla to the marscapone. Would also be very good with mint.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Balsamic glaze! Inspired! I will definitely try that.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This has been my favorite strawberry dessert this summer. The crust is delicious. I melted the jam in a little saucepan and tossed the berries in it instead of dotting.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How many people does this serve? I'm preparing dessert for 11 and I'm wondering if I need to make two of these...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I would definitely double for 11 - I had 8 people polish this off easily.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It says serves 8 at very top of recipe

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It depends on how big the pieces are.  1 cup mascarpone divided by 11.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: made it as directed. Rye flour added great flavor. The family raved. Super easy because the crust could be made in the a.m. and the rest assembled just before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used sugar cookie dough as a crust was in a hurry, had picky eaters. Followed the rest of instructions, everyone raved.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used the almond flour/flour mix. Added strawberry ( bonne maman), to the mascarpone,no sugar at all,  and cooked 13/14 minutes at 400. Yum! By all at the table.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I struggled with getting the pastry crust to come together into an oval, and instead pressed it into a pie tin.  I wanted something more pillowy than the mascarpone, so I mixed in some vanilla extract and added the mascarpone to a bowl with whipping cream I had whipped into soft peaks.  I spread the strawberry jam straight unto the cool pie crust, filled it with the mascarpone/cream mixture, and topped with lots and lots of stawberries. Absolutely delicious! The rye crust really adds something.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Asking a dumb question...do you cook the crust with the parchment paper under it or not?  I didn't see any instructions on that step. Very anxious to try this recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes, baking parchment paper is made to go in the oven and acts as a non-stick surface, no greasing required.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Scrumptious and very easy. I’ve made this 4 times this summer, experimenting with a variety of flours. Using a scale it’s easy to mix flours to total 170 grams. A mix of AP, rye and almond works well, as does almost any fine milled flour. After rolling the dough on the parchment don’t trim, just fold crust edges over to form an oval. I don’t have a baking sheet so I placed the crust on upsidedown rimmed pan. When done it slides right off onto a serving board. Cuts easy with pizza cutter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: CIDER (not side:(  vinegar

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This recipe is from a 1970's cookbook, 'Cooking with Pomiane' - rub 1 part butter to 2 parts plain flour together, with just enough water to make a dough.

Refrigerate for 30 minutes, then roll out, place in a greased tart tin. Bake in a 350 oven, pastry weights, or dry rice added, until golden brown. Remove tart shell, take out weights, and let cool. 

Spread base with pure cream, put whole strawberries on top, and brush with warmed raspberry jam to glaze, just before serving

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I made a mini version of this tonight using enough dough for two people and storing the rest.  The crust was perfect after 17 minutes and cooked on parchment paper on top of an upside down cake pan as one reviewer suggested.  This way it slides right off and doesn’t crack.
I left out the sugar and jam.  It was delicious in its simplicity but in the end, because the crust is not sweet, I felt it needed more sweetness on top for balance—or a sweet crust.  Would definitely make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For all those asking, I'd really try to find rye flour. I made as recipe asked and the crust was wonderful!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Question: Should the tart be chilled before serving?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: There's no need to chill it, and it's best to not let it sit around after you've added the mascarpone and berries.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Based on comments about a soggy crust, I brushed a thin layer of white chocolate onto the warm crust. It did the trick and kept my crust crisp for about two days.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: just made this to rave reviews.  I used the rye flour and it added a wonderful complexity to the crust. I added a bit of vanilla to the mascarpone. My strawberries  were more like piled on rather than arranged

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Always looking for something to do with fresh strawberries when our local ones are in season. This really does them justice. Made it for company and everyone loved it. I loved the rye in the crust and think it might work for any type of pastry for a savory pie.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: About the flour.  I sometimes crumble wheat digestive biscuits and top those with plain goat yogurt and sliced sweet strawberries. From that taste experience, I would try a whole wheat flour in this recipe, maybe a pastry grind.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made it twice, both to rave reviews. Didn't use the jam because my berries were juicy and sweet (& couldn't see the point). I did splash some Cassis liqueur in the marscopone (which turned it a pretty lilac color). Second time, I sprinkled some fresh mint-a great addition!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delicious. I used almond flour instead of rye becuase I had that already. Everyone loved it. I will definitely make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can whole wheat flour be used instead of the rye flour.  Also, can this be made in the morning and served that evening of does it need to be served immediately?  Thank you

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My guess would be immediately.  If you put it all together and leave it for any length of time, the crust will absorb the moisture of the mascarpone and become soggy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a wonderful dessert for Summer. As suggested in the comments I used almond flour instead of rye and even omitted the egg wash as I didn’t see that it added anything to the result. The filling was hard to spread so I used a mix of marscapone, cream and icing sugar. I melted raspberry jam and added it to the sliced juicy strawberries before piling them onto the base and it looks and tastes spectacular every time and so easy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Surprisingly easy and satisfying! Used all-purpose and almond flour instead of rye, because that's what I had on hand. Also used a tart tin. Worked great! Skipped the jam, but drizzled a small amount of tupelo honey over the strawberries and let them marinate for a little bit. Also added a few grinds of black pepper to the strawberries and some lemon zest to the mascarpone. Really delightful.

author: Emily Weinstein

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So delicious and not at all hard to make. Love the flavor and texture the rye flour gives this.  I made it in a large round tart pan, avoided any problems with the crust. I may have to follow the advice of another commenter and make extra dough to keep in the freezer for when I want a quick and elegant dessert.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is exactly what I have been looking for in a rustic summer dessert that celebrates the fruit of the season. One great thing about a simple dessert like this is that it's so easy to personalize. I omitted the jam and tbs of sugar on top, and the sweetness was perfect (I think it would have been too sweet for me otherwise). I also added about a half teaspoon of cardamom to the mascarpone, which gave it a wonderful floral note. Highly recommend!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
A perfect dessert for Mother’s day , such a delicious and easy to make  tart! The mascarpone makes it easy, so much easier than the classic tarte aux fraises with crème pâtissière as we use to make in France!
The crust is thin and tasty (maybe the rye flour adds something special ). It did not break or crumb through the transfer (I did not wait it was totally cold to move it in a plate). I used sugar free strawberry  spread instead of jam.

I made it FAST: all crust ingredients (plus 2 tbs sugar, I like tart dough lightly sweet) in food processor, a few pulse later placed dough between two layers of parchment paper, rolled it thin (up to here, around 10 min), put it in the fridge while oven was heating, trasfered cold dough from fridge to oven on a thick aluminium baking sheet, no egg wash, cooked for 20 min. Intentionally cracked in pieces to make bites. Amazingly good and simple.

commentBody: I have used a similar recipe for decades for all pies and tarts and passed it on to other generations. However, no rye flour, just regular unbleached flour.  The big difference is that I use the blender to mix it all. This is an easy no-fail method. I use ice water. Chilling/freezer is a must. author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Question: should the butter be completely combined with the flour mixture when ready to roll out in a disc? Or should there still be small lumps?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: There should ALWAYS be small lumps of butter when making pie crust. You either add the butter cubes to the flour and rub them between your fingers, or mash them with a fork, to make pea-sized lumps. Then add the liquid but ONLY mix until there is no obvious flour and the dough holds together. Then STOP. You should see streaks or lumps of butter all over the dough when you roll it out. The other must-do: you must keep the dough cold so that the butter DOESN’T melt into the flour until baked.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Second question - I am curious as to just how much difference a particular type of vinegar makes in a recipe. Yesterday I searched far and wide for sherry vinegar for a fish recipe and ended up using the recommended substitute - sherry wine plus red wine vinegar. Now I'm wondering - do I really need to buy apple cider vinegar for this recipe or can I just use the red wine vinegar? I ask these questions as a snowbird with limited space in my winter kitchen.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can use any mild vinegar: apple cider, white vinegar, red wine, champagne. (Not balsamic, of course). My 50-year old pie dough recipe calls for apple cider vin just like this one. One minor reason is that substituting vinegar for some of the water makes less gluten. Water + flour = gluten. Vin + flour = no gluten. Gluten formation makes a tougher crust. This is a minimal effect though (if at all).  Mostly the vinegar helps prevent over-working of the dough, and the dough turning gray.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The rye flour is really what sets this tart apart from others, and I was not a fan of that aspect; it gave it too savory of a flavor for me and a kind of grainy texture. Feel like the rye could work if this went in a more “transitional” course direction—maybe sub crème fraiche for mascarpone, omit the jam, and do a balsamic reduction drizzle over the strawberries. Otherwise, I think this would be better with all AP flour or maybe something like almond flour instead of the rye.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
A couple of things...
1. Since Hasselback is called out here, acknowledgement to our friends in Sweden @ the Hasselback Hotel are in order. Legend has it this dish was invented there.
2. A lot of time can be saved arranging potatoes on their ends by taking a nice Yukon Gold, placing it in a wooden spoon (the spoony-end) and slicing till your knife hits the spoon. The tater stays together nicely at the bottom and it's very easy to handle. You can google this.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Nope this is not Hasselback potatoes.  It is only the thin slicing that reminds one of Hasselback potatoes, which incidently are not sliced all the way through but are kept whole and then baked with a bit of salt and butter, maybe bed crumbs or cheese but no sauce

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I take a thin slice off what will be the bottom edge of the potato to keep it from rolling around.  Then place the potato between a pair of chopsticks and slice down until the blade contacts the chopstick.  Works like a charm.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yeah, but these aren't Hasselback potatoes, but rather a sort of au gratin, so the potatoes need to be cut through.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent! Added a grated onion to the liquid/cheese mix for flavor. Made it through first 2 thirty minute bakings in advance. Then covered and chilled. Brought to the dinner, sprinkled cheese on top and did final baking of about 40 minutes at the meal (removed foil half way through.) Texture was great! I was worried because initially liquid barely covered bottom of pan, but juices came out with baking and it was perfect! Good crusty bits on bottom too. A real keeper of a recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I made this dish for Thanksgiving with sweet potatoes (not yams) to wild success. My family practically hoisted me upon their shoulders. When I called my mother a week later, she answered the phone without a hello: "Those sweet potatoes were the best thing I've ever eaten."
I said, "Hi, Mom. How's everything?"
She said, "God, those sweet potatoes were good."
I give my full throated endorsement for this recipe. It can make you feel like a conquering hero.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @J Hoppe did you add the cheese and everything? Never thought about doing sweet potatoes with milk, Gruyère and Parmesan.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've read reference here to soaking the potatoes in water before making the dish- as a shortcut or "timesaver" I suppose- even from a food editor at the Times.  Big mistake. Any of these gratin Dauphinois kind of potato dishes (sliced potatoes baked in cream) need the natural starch of the potato mixed with the cream to thicken it, not to mention the flavor of the potato. Soaking the sliced potatoes washes starch & flavor away and reduces the quality of the finished dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Even a short soak in acidified water to halt discoloration?  Or is that not a problem if they are well coated with the milk mixture?  I plan to make through step 2 early day of serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Do you also think that a short soak in an acidifer to stop browning would wash away too much of the starches?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My experience is exactly the opposite - but I prefer crispy potatoes to softer ones - that is the difference. Even with dauphinoise, I soak and rinse potato slices at least three times to get as much starch out as possible so the potatoes get crispier. If you prefer a softer potato, then I wouldn't soak. But a dish with this much cream and cheese doesn't "need" the starch to thicken, in my opinion.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I've now par-cooked this slightly in advance a few times.  I do the first two rounds of cooking in the morning and the last immediately before serving:

1. cook for *35* minutes, covered, at 400
2. cook for 30 minutes, uncovered, at 400
3. cool until lukewarm and then cover so it doesn't dry out
4. immediately before serving, cook for 30 minutes, with topping cheeses added, 15 minutes at 400 and 15 minutes at 350 (I do this so I can cook the biscuits with it without burning them)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Soaked cut potatoes for 1 hour in cold water. Doubled cheese (which I grated fresh),  Added 1t dried mustard, 1/2 cup diced onions sautéed brown in butter, 1T black pepper and 1T salt to mix before tossing potatoes and putting in dish. Baking takes 50 minutes (done at 400 covered/420 uncovered). Excellent!

For those who don't have a mandoline due to the cost, look for a Japanese mandoline. Professional chefs use them because of their durability, ease of use, and low cost. One brand name is Benriner. I used mine to slice the potatoes in about 3 minutes.

not to mention that it would destroy this beautiful recipe

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Indeed?  Pink potatoes?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm thinking thin sliced onion would make a nice addition, interspersed with the potato slices.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Probably couldn't hurt, but would be another layer of complexity when putting the dish together.  And would probably change the taste rather significantly....

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Whatever you like on your loaded baked potato would be worth considering.  I’m thinking sour cream (acidifier), maybe bacon (if everything else doesn’t have it!) and lots of chives!  It’s Thanksgiving!  You can fast tomorrow.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I followed advice from several commenters -- I used lots of salt, extra garlic, added a bit of smoked paprika (yum), and increased the cooking time by 30 minutes (45 minutes covered, 45 minutes uncovered, 30 minutes with additional cheese). It was marvelous -- perfectly cooked and not bland. The leftovers, which I admit I was surprised we had, were terrific the next day reheated in the oven covered with foil. Making them again for NYE.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thanks for the tips on more seasoning and longer cooking time.  They turned out perfect this time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Increase Gruyere
Add sherry
Use Yukon Gold potatoes, not russet
Can substitute sweet potatoes

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm gonna be the recipe heretic and admit to covering the top of the dish with crumbled bacon along with the remaining cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Crunchy, cheesy potatoey heaven.  Wonderful recipe, worked perfectly, just stuff all those taters in there to stand up like little soldiers and you're on your way.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added some sherry in substitution for a corresponding amount of cream. Adds a slightly sweet balance and complexity. Will add some sage next time. With 3 pounds of potatoes it was undercooked by about 15 min. But really good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sherry is FANTASTIC addition to heavy cream sauces. Would have overlooked this in this particular recipe. THANKS!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For those who don't want to use all that heavy cream, I substituted cashew cream (2 cups raw unsalted cashews, cover with water, blend until creamy). It's MUCH easier on the stomach, and healthier too!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was good, but not as wonderful as a traditional rich potato gratin or traditional Hasselback potatoes. Nutmeg is needed here...but I love nutmeg. I might try this again. This is definitely worth experimenting with.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Favorite dish of Thanksgiving. I used an apple peeler which peeled and gave a perfect spiral cut, instead of using the mandolin. Great time saver.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: An apple peeler is a great tool for practically any tuber.  If by apple peeler you mean the one that you jam the potato onto and then spin the handle which both peels and spiral cuts the potato just like you do with apples.  Turnips, rutabagas....whatever you cook in cream is delish!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
We've been playing around with this recipe for a while now, and we've come up with some additions we really like:

1. We increased the Gruyere from 3 oz. to 4 oz., divided between the sauce and the topping.

2. We added 1/2 cup of grated cheddar to the top of the casserole during the final baking period. Nice caramelization!

3. We added about a teaspoon of smoked paprika to the sauce (this dish LOVES smoked paprika!)

4. We've added a little ground rosemary to the topping.

author: Emily Weinstein
