commentBody: There's a reason this calls for russet potatoes. I did a side-by-side comparison with russet (starchy) vs yukon gold (waxy). The russet potatoes break down a bit more, but they soak up all the flavors and creamy goodness. The yukon golds may have had a "better" texture, but they did not absorb the flavors and sauce the same - and the sauce had broken (as many others have noted). The yukon gold version was still delicious of course, but the russets produced a superior dish in my opinion.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made half recipe in 8x8  Pyrex glass casserole.  Mistakenly added all cheeses to cream and it was still excellent.  Roasted for 1 1/2 hours at 400 and had delicious crispy crust.  Served 8.  My potatoes were hand sliced and used Yukon gold and even though they were not uniform in size they still were delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: To prevent potatoes from browning, add a teeny bit of ascorbic acid powder to the water holding peeled potatoes. Or citric acid.....a half of a vitamin C tablet.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Or lemon juice.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Per other commenters ....rinse, don’t soak.  Make sure they are coated well with the cream mixture.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: i made this for thanksgiving with a variation of using sweet potatoes.  once i had everything ready to go in the oven the casserole dish looked a little low on liquid (not up to halfway mark, so i added some buttermilk instead of more cream; the results were marvelous, high marks from around the table of very picky foodies.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I LOVE THIS DISH. We've made it three times and each time it comes out wonderfully. It's the only reason I will drag out my clunky mandoline, which is essential as I can't cut potatoes thinly and consistently enough by hand. We've only used Yukon Gold and have no complaint. It DOES INDEED take an hour and a half.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The end product of this recipe is wonderful. However, note that the recipe calls for already-sliced potatoes and for already-grated cheese.  These all take time that I don't think is adequately factored into the time estimate.  Maybe the recipe writer had a slicing/grating elf for the prep work.   I have made it twice so far and will probably make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made the mistake of using regular whipping cream vs. heavy cream, and the result was oily instead of creamy. And I'll add more cheese next time. Otherwise, it was a pretty and tasty dish, perfectly cooked with only 20 mins at the end. Leftovers were good for breakfast.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Buy whipping cream IS heavy cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Whipping cream is a bit adulterated, vs. heavy cream, which should have only cream as its only ingredient. Also, heavy cream whips up beautifully with either sweet or savory pinches added and with the help of a hand mixer.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I'd like to try this for just the two of us--and that would mean only two potatoes, for dietary reasons. Which seems like a lot of work.

So I'm wondering about doing the whole recipe, and freezing portions of it after the first meal?

Any feedback most welcome.

And Happy Thanksgiving to all you folks down there who celebrate in the wrong month! ;-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Potatoes (yukons) don't freeze well - in my experience. I would guess the same of the russets. A waxy potato might be ok but will not deliver the creamy mouthfeel.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this last night as my husband and guests watched me "massage the rich cheesy goodness all over those spuds." Only the tops tasted like they held the sauce.  Maybe I shouldn't have packed them in so tightly (per the recipe); I was disappointed that more of the sauce didn't cover more of the potato slices, after all my massaging.  Maybe my onlookers were jealous they weren't getting special treatment!  Also used the Cuisinart on #4 to slice potatoes.  Success!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This took a LOT longer than the stated baking time....probably at least 45 minutes longer.  The exposed tops cooked much faster than the lower, packed-together-in-the-pan (per the recipe's instructions) halves..... By the time the tops were cooked, the bottoms were still pretty much raw....

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Once you've slice the potatoes I advise going ahead, damn the torpedos, full to the finish per cooking instructions. I refrigerated the assembled gratin overnight and the exposed potatoes turned an unsightly black color.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Some have commented that the sauce is bland. Obviously, it's important to use a strongly flavored, mature Gruyere.  I'm wondering if subbing in some buttermilk our sour cream would perk it up?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
On the note about the gruyere being bland: I made batches on successive weekends for two different holiday dinners. I have to say, the first batch I could only get my hands on a “cave aged” gruyere, which had a texture closer to the parmesan.  Those potatoes were amazing and didn’t need much salt. 

Second batch was a softer, less aged gruyere and the results were noticeably less flavorful. Still tasty, but the mature cheese was remarkable. Worth the investment.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this once as written with russets and it was delicious - they soaked up the cream at the bottom and were soft and fabulous, and crispy at the top. Another time with Yukons and they were not done after the directed cooking time, and just swam in the cream. Probably with some modifications it can work well, but if doing as written, go with russets.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I, too, have found that Yukons take longer to cook through.  I know that the combination of purple, golden, and white is beautiful, but find that I must cook them separately and then combine at the last few moments.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I made this with Asiago and oregano (what I had on hand). Divine. There wasn’t a scrap left. 

Advice of note: don’t rinse or soak your potatoes, coat the dish heavily with butter ( you could tell there was potential for a real mess), and don’t use all the cream (I failed to get a good amount of crisp because the fluid was too high).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
So, if I understand from comments:
Yukon Gold potatoes 
Sherry replacing a little of the cream
More garlic
Maybe add some sage
Don’t under salt
Add pepper
More cheese
Make ahead until 3rd bake and add reheat time to that last 30 min

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Rebecca  And…simmer some herbs and garlic in the cream first to infuse the flavors.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I replaced the gruyère with "cantal"  cheese, readily available here in France and a great deal more savory than the initial recipe. Other than that, an excellent recipe that got rave reviews.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Agree with Linda, this is a time consuming version of this recipe.  There are a number of hasselback recipes on the web that are quicker.  That said, this recipe coats all the slices nicely with the cheese mixture which other recipes don't do as well.  This recipe would be good for special occasions and/or holidays.  I am adding to the Thankgiving menu this year.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Don't be scared to add more cheese. I skimped and regret it.

And ensure you don't overfill!

Great dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These were delicious. My husband said, "Oh my god. I love you. These are so good," while he was eating them. I think that comment is up there in the top comments I've gotten for my food. I used Yukon Gold potatoes but followed the recipe otherwise. Someone said 1 tsp of salt was not nearly enough so I used close to 2 teaspoons and it was on the brink of too salty, but everyone still loved them. I also sliced my finger pretty deeply on the mandoline, so be careful yall.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Whoever recommended using sweet potato instead of russet potato was genius!  We made both and much preferred the sweet potato version.  We did not have enough cream mixture to pour over to make it come up halfway, but it worked out OK.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Outlier result. Could be the high sided casserole dish I chose; could be the convection oven I had available. In any case, here's what i got when I served it: the cheese had curdled out of the liquid; the bottoms of the potatoes were undercooked and the tops were almost like crisps; and the flavor was very mild, almost too milky. Put it back in the oven at slightly lower temp and it cooked through.  Very supportive diners that night.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tried it with gruyere and thyme but didn't go over as well as when I subbed asiago and oregano, with the parm. That was a hit with my brunch crowd!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A warning to those who are considering the great shortcut of cutting the potatoes partially using the wooden spoon barrier method.  I did this. Dish looked fabulous - until I tried to ensure each slice was coated with the cheese mixture.  Impossible.  I would have been there for a month of Sundays trying to pry apart each slice. I gave up, had to pull apart each potato and then I FOLLOWED THE RECIPE 😊and coated each slice easily and messily and popped them back in to complete the masterpiece.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can any steps be made ahead of time?  Not sure I can dedicate 1 1/2 hrs oven time on Christmas - but everyone is clamoring for this.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can slice the potatoes several hours in advance; keep them submerged in cold water until you're ready to assemble the dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Maria In the video, Kenji says you can prepare ahead with the first hour of baking in the oven and then finish with the last 30 minutes of baking just before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is delicious. Good to note that it takes 90 minutes baking in the oven. Next time I will bake for the first 60 minutes ahead of time, chill it, and when ready to serve it for a meal, put in the oven for the last 30 to 40 minutes. Cover with foil for the first 20 minutes. Will also try half and half instead of the cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did you try it??? I am thinking about making this instead of the controversial (in the US) Jansson's Temptation - a Swedish gratin which has Swedish anchovies in it. I thought the Hasselback + gratin method might be a fun alternative for our Swedish Christmas party. I'd love to make this dish ahead of time to cut back on work the day of the party. It sounds wonderful

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thanks Mary - 1/2 and 1/2 was all I had and it worked wonderfully.  Next time I will remember to stock up on butter/creams, etc. PRIOR to Christmas Eve at 4:00 and see how the cream turns out!  Also while 90 was clearly in the recipe, your reminder was key to dinner timing.  Someone above also said they went 120 minutes, which I left room for and did as well with great results.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Caution: If you make these, your guests will demand these over and over again. DELICIOUS!

For those wanting to make ahead, prepare all the way through the second 30 minutes in the oven. I went ahead and added the cheese right after taking them out of the oven. Then, hold off on the final 30 minutes until you’re nearly ready for dinner. Pop them in 30-40 minutes before dinner time and you’ll have fresh from the oven hasselback potatoes!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Amanda. Thanks!  Was looking to make this for Thanksgiving with a roast and only have one oven. Appreciated

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love this recipe. I make it all the time, especially when I need something easy but seems fancy/high effort. Tips: to figure out how many potatoes you need, peel potatoes, placing them inside the casserole until you fill the whole dish tightly with potatoes. Then peel one extra one. This has gotten me the perfect amount every time. Also, you don't have to do the gruyere cheese. It's delicious, but I've made this with supermarket preshredded cheddar-jack and it's amazing too

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this once with the heavy cream and once with a béchamel (or mornay) sauce. The mornay substitute was more popular with my diners. Either way, I think this is a very good recipe and I immediately went out and bought López-Alt's book.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made them again, and this time did a better job following the instructions. Since I was making it for 2 people, I used a mini loaf pan and 3-4 smallish russet potatoes. After removing it, I let it sit and it wasn't greasy; maybe it was absorbed into the potatoes. I covered it for 30 min, then uncovered for 30 min, then added cheese and cooked ~20 min. Success. I hand sliced the potatoes since it was for two it worked OK. If it were for a crowd, I would use my food processor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a once a year crowd pleaser!  I made the dish a day ahead and baked for the first two 30 minute baking periods, saving the last 30 minute baking period for the day of serving and the texture was excellent.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Make sure to add in enough salt - maybe 2 tsp in order to get enough flavor and also to serve with enough of the cheesy sauce.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! Didn't take too much time, especially with a mandolin to help slice the potatoes - even with arranging them vertically. Of note, I used yukon gold potatoes, and the gruyere-cheddar mix from Trader Joe's - still delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
believe the original recipe is called "hasselbackspotatis". swedish. Hasselbacken is an ancient and well-known restaurant at Stockholm's plein air zoo and cultural museum, Skansen. learned tocook it in senior high in the 1950s. potatoes oval, peeled and "slit" superthinly (like a breadloaf) ALMOST to the bottom(potato still hangs together). in the original no cream, but LOADS of melted butter/lots of grated cheeze.
yumyum.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @ann-c berglund.  This is not hasselback potatoes. Similar only in slicing, but not really,

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added a bit more salt but mostly followed this recipe. I made one batch with Yukon gold then a second with russets. Russets were far better. The Yukon got somewhat mushy while russet stayed firm. I cooked per the instructions the night before event and reheated for 30 minutes in oven the day of - was a huge hit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The recipe calls for russets, so it makes sense that they would give the best results.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this recipe a few times and it always takes MUCH longer than the specified time - 1 hour covered, at least 45 minutes to an hour uncovered, so plan accordingly or parcook the potatoes before. Have made it as specified and a "lighter" version with milk (it doesn't separate like the cream) and a touch of Parmesan, not for caloric reasons because I used what I had on hand. Other than cooking time, it's a delicious recipe and a nice change from standard dauphinois

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this while on Quarantine during the COVID-19 pandemic which means I had to utilize the ingredients I had in my house. I heated up my Oat Milk with sharp cheddar, white cheddar, & fresh parmesan with nutmeg, salt & fresh cracked pepper. I recommend melting the cheese in the milk before tossing the potatoes in it- this is basic distribution of ingredients that we learn at culinary school/ industry . I used a round 9" round pie pan,formed the potatoes into a flower starting from the outside.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My lady friend and I are over 60.  The two cups of heavy cream sound fulsomely rich at any age.   I seek a substitute.   I was thinking of whole milk thickened with a modicum of cornstarch or arrowroot.  Bechamel I would gainsay also.   Any suggestions for the gall bladder impaired crowd?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Try a cup of cream and a half cup or so of chicken stock. It will thin the cream but not so much that the potatoes won't be well coated. Use more stock to bring the liquid level to halfway up the casserole walls.

You need cream for the rich flavor. Whole milk won't taste the same. Still, it would probably be absolutely delicious even with milk.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Milk worked fine for me. I never use cream in quiches, gratins, etc. If I want some richness that milk normally doesn't provide, I simmer it for awhile to reduce it and caramelize the sugars a bit. Yum.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: One poster suggested cashew milk which I think would work.  Strain out the bits using a fine sieve after blending though so as not to spoil the creaminess.  Also, one suggested the addition of buttermilk which would add a bit of tang if that tempts.  Not sure a bechamel would give you the same effect but would be delicious on its own merits.  Add some herbs for more brightness.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
The potato is one of the most delicious products one can have. The problem is the North American potato. I have never tasted a good potato on this continent until I started to grow my own in healthy soil and with natural soil enrichments every year. I use European seed potatoes.
I keep wondering how North American farmers manage to grow such a poor product engeneerd to death from dead soil cicked up with chemicals.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You do realize that potatoes came from the Americas?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you really pack the potatoes into the dish, just be sure to add additional bake time.  1.5 hours was not enough for a “crowded” dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great dish! I have made it numerous times both with potatoes and also with sweet potatoes.....both dishes are incredible. I have also made this in ramekins single size (I use two nice-size potatoes) just for me when I feel like treating myself to an indulgent comfort food....I will be making it again this Thanksgiving for sure.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made this last year for Thanksgiving and followed the suggestion from another reader to use half sweet potatoes/half white potatoes. The family raved about the dish and it’s now on the list of favorites! We also have discovered Japanese sweet potatoes, which have a white flesh and more subtle flavor than the traditional sweet potatoes and work very well in this recipe while adding more nutritional value. In case that matters to anyone at Thanksgiving! Ha!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sounds good for a potluck but would need to make it ahead of time.  What's the shelf life?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: NYT says best day of serving.  I plan to make it early in the day through Step 2 and keep my fingers crossed.  (I’ve read ALL the comments and incorporated them in one post awaiting review.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very nice.   I used red potatoes and cheddar only, and substituted a cup of milk for half of the cream, because that's what I had on hand, and it came out fine.   Also, because I'd used a hand grater on the cheddar and it was a little stringy, I used an immersion blender on the sauce, which I think was a good move.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is a keeper--unlike lots of scalloped potatoe dishes, this one has crunch, flavor, presents well and I expect will re-heat well for breakfast. We had some Boar's Head ham on hand and added that for extra flavor plus some random bits of smoked gouda. Laying the slices on their sides really worked. Fresh herbs helped with flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this with only aged cheddar since it was all I had on hand and it was delicious. The cream mixture really needs a lot of salt - it tasted very salty to me before I poured it over the potatoes but the finished dish still needed an extra sprinkling. I think this is probably why some commenters find the dish unappetizing.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I agree about the salt. Potatoes tend to need more salt. This dish came out wonderfully. I also added aged cheddar since this is what I had on hand (as well as some Parmigiano-Reggiano). I brought it to a pre-Thanksgiving potluck and it was a hit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone made this in advance? Any recommendations?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
To those caught between soaking the potato slices in water (avoiding discoloration) and not soaking (keeping the starches), just make the cream mixture first and toss the potato slices into the mixture as soon as they’re sliced. Problem solved!

The recipe is amazingly delicious, btw :-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I badly wanted this recipe to work. But it has failed three times. I try to follow recipe as given, but the result has been poor at best.
My problems,
1. The sauce breaks (becomes grainy and greasy) after it comes out of the oven.
2. The potatoes are undercooked at the times given.
3. It is straight-up bland. The crispy bits on top are ok, but everything else, nope.
This recipe has 5 star average over 6000 ratings. I’m happy people have success, but the recipe simply hasn’t worked for me.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just had the same exact experience, except I didn't even get the cripsy bits on top - it somehow managed to start burning on top without getting crisp in the slightest. This was a very large amount of effort for an absolute failure.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Maybe some sliced beets in stacks of their own mixed in with the potatoes. . .

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yuck! Beets would ruin  this perfect creation.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Doing this with just beets is a great riff on the dish. I'm wondering if you would want to start with cooked beets and possibly use a slower oven after the first half hour or so?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Outstanding! At the last minute on Christmas Eve I found that I was to cook Christmas Dinner as the original hostess was ill. I ran to the store - and Christmas Day tossed in the turkey and sliced up the potatoes - Easy-peasy and the best dinner ever with coconut carrots and impromptu dessert. The potatoes were a dream - couldn't find gruyere so substituted Baby Swiss -

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this twice over the holidays. Used the peeled russets the first time; tried unpeeled butter potatoes the second time. Both versions were delicious! The first time I also interrupted the final bake and finished it just before the meal. The second time, I followed the recipe to the letter and took it hot to my sister's for Christmas Eve. Once again, both versions were very, very good. This recipe is definitely one I will make again. It also makes a great presentation at the table.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a showstopper! After reading many of the reviews regarding partially or uncooked potatoes in the final dish, decided to “par cook” in the microwave on high, uncovered for 20 minutes after assembling. Following that the dish went into the fridge after it cooled until about an hour before my guests arrived. Then after coming back to room temp. went into a 400 degree oven, foil covered, for 30 minutes, then 10 minutes foil removed and topped with remaining cheese—Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I tried the suggestion given by others to prep the dish and cook for the two 30 min sessions the night before Thanksgiving and finish if off on the day of. I found that it was not as good this way. It took much longer than the final 30 minutes, the potatoes were not as smooth and creamy as when done in one long session and the cream did not cook down as much. I would never do it again this way as I felt you lost the superior flavor and texture of the recipe as is published.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We followed the recipe exactly, except we used Yukon Gold potatoes instead of russets. We tacked an extra 15 minutes onto each phase of the baking time to ensure the potatoes would get done. The result was outstanding, with lots of crispy edges that were irresistible to everyone. Definitely bake the casserole on a foil-lined sheet pan. Some of our cream mixture bubbled over, creating a charred, burnt-dairy mess. Our  sheet pan saved the day. What a relief that we could just toss that foil liner.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Three notes on this fabulous recipe:

1. If you have a convection oven, this is a good time to use it and don’t adjust the temperatures down. If found the timing tends to work better if I have convection on.

2. For those newish or wary of using a mandolin, my best advice is don’t try to be a hero and slice the ingredients down to the nub. Just make peace with the fact that protecting your fingers is worth wasting a little bit of raw ingredient and definitely buy an extra potato or two just in case here.

3. Really listen to the advice not to fill the dish more than halfway up with the remaining cream mixture. The potatoes will release their own liquid as they cook.

Otherwise, my only changes were to add some mustard and fresh nutmeg as I always do with most gratins or Mac and cheese because I like those flavors.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this yesterday and since my potatoes were large, I didn'the need as many.  Easy to make and bake.  This also had a thin toasty layer on the bottom.  Everyone loved it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I had a disaster when I tried to prepare this ahead of time.  I partially baked the dish, let it cool enough to be able to cover with plastic film and put in fridge.  The exposed portion of the potatoes discolored.  When I saw the potatoes were starting to discolor I tried to save it by brushing on lemon juice to the tops.  No luck.  What a waste.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: No heavy cream on hand, so I whisked 1% milk into mascarpone cheese, added cheddar and parm and garlic, massaged sliced potatoes with cheese blend, and layered into casserole. Seasoned with salt and pepper and baked as written. Holy cow, delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you don't have a big enough bowl to put the potatoes and cream mixture together, then do it by tossing handfuls of potatoes at a time. Super tasty recipe. Leftovers reheat beautifully.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cut the recipe in half and used a bread pan instead of a casserole dish. Came out great!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made almost exactly as directed.  Pre-baked for the first two half hours, then sat for 2 hours as guests arrived, then finished off the last 1/2 hour with cheese and a few herbs sprinkled on top - excellent!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For make-ahead: I have been making this for years and have done it in stages several times. This cooks in three stages, so you can prepare and cook through the first or second stage before cooling and back in the fridge. Then finish as normal before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
1.) used Yukon gold potatoes (food processor makes the prep and slicing WAY easier), doubled the cheese, used 6 cloves of garlic, and added 2 tablespoons of Dijon, 1 1/2 teaspoons of smoked paprika, and two minced shallots to the sauce (combining a lot of other commenters’ recommendations) 
2.) cooked covered at 425 for first 30 minutes, cooked uncovered at 425 for next 50 minutes, and added cheese on top and cooked at 400 for final 30 minutes — tried original heat and cook time recommendations the first time I made this and it was not cooked through (as other commenters have mentioned— my second batch was much better with these adjustments given the density of the dish) 
3.) The flavor of the potatoes is definitely much better after they sit a bit. I enjoyed these more reheated the second day. I made a lot of adjustments to original recipe and can’t imagine them having any less flavor — can definitely see how original recipe could be bland without the doubled cheese, extra garlic, Dijon, smoked paprika and shallots.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made it through first 2 thirty minute bakings in advance. Then covered and chilled. Brought to the dinner, sprinkled cheese on top and did final baking of about 40 minutes at the meal (removed foil half way through.) Texture was great! I was worried because initially liquid barely covered bottom of pan, but juices came out with baking and it was perfect! Good crusty bits on bottom too. A real keeper of a recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Wanted to add a quick note about russet potatoes. They are not all created equal! The best tasting russet potato is the Russet Burbank. Most potatoes in the grocery store labeled as Russet are Norkotahs. 

Norkotahs are prettier (more oval, more shallow eyes, few knobs), but less tasty than the classic Russet Burbank. I highly recommend the Russet Burbanks variety if you can find them. I come from a farming family in Idaho and take these things very seriously!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was simply awesome.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am planning to make this for Thanksgiving. I think I will do the first two 30-minute bakings the day before, and then finish it while the turkey is resting. I hope this works.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm hoping to do this same thing! Did it work for you?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just took the casserole out of the oven (it's Thanksgiving eve) after the second 30-minute bake. It looks and smells pretty darn good. Now to let it cool a bit and then refrigerate overnight.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Baking ahead worked and did not affect the texture at all. The potatoes stayed quite firm in the casserole after the final 30 minute baking. I took the casserole out of fridge ahead of cooking.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved it.  Everyone dived into it.  We had 11 and made a little extra.  With the other sides, there was very little left.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was rushing. I'm a little stressed over the election & not at my best. I forgot to add the cheeses after the potatoes were neatly arranged.  Dumped them back in the bowl, mixed them with the cheese, and rearranged them a little less neatly.  Then I started to wonder when I was supposed to put in the salt and pepper and looked more closely - oops.  Out came the potatoes again, salt & pepper added, arranged semi-randomly, and all was well.  They were good just not so beautiful.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It looked beautiful! Just like the picture. And the crispy, cheesy tops blew me away. Unfortunately the bottom half of the dish was pretty bland. Salt helped, but it’s a lot of work for bland potatoes. Some bacon crumbles might be great addition. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: 12/25/2019. Made for Xmas. Used 2 mms Cuisinart blade to cut potatoes, 10 small-medium. Used cheddar cheese and Parmesan, and half and half. Used dried thyme-1 tsp. Almost all eaten. Very good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this last night for a large group, and everyone agreed they were the best scalloped potatoes ever! As mentioned, they can take a bit longer. Mine were in the oven for 45, 45, and then about 30 minutes. Make sure to taste the cream mixture to make sure you use enough salt and pepper. I did add a small amount of cayenne pepper. Next time I will up it to 1/2 tsp.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: EPIC FAIL!! I’ve never had a dish turn so wrong in such a spectacularly public fashion. I cooked the first two steps the day before Thanksgiving. Pulled out the dish the day of, let it come to room temp only to discover that it was not cooked all the way through, so exposed potatoes had turned black. It then took TWO HOURS in the oven and most potatoes were still the consistency of apple slices. My guests were charitable and kind, but it was awful. Next year? Good old fashioned mashed potatoes!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was gravely disappointing.  

The liquid thickened while I was dipping the slices, and I ran out.  I added more cream to finish up the dipping.

Then the "liquid" loosened up when it went into the oven, and mostly submerged the vertical slices so I had almost no crunchy layer at all.

Also, oils from the cheese separated during baking, leaving a greasy oil slick at the bottom of the pan.  

This was marginally better as leftovers.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this yesterday to go with southern Maryland stuffed ham. It was a huge hit! I knew it would be lots of work so put on Pink’s new album and went for it! 

I was feeding 24 and had some celeriac and turnip in the fridge (the end of my winter farm share) so added those to the doubled recipe. 

I baked it in my mother’s 72 year old le creuset casserole dish, just as specified and it came out perfect! Not one potato leftover and everyone raved about it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! On our go-to side list for holidays. Modifications: doubled both cheeses. Notes: ended up over salting, so limit to 1 tsp of coarse kosher in the cream mixture and none on top (cheeses are salty on top). If you are using the mandolin and potatoes are actually 1/8” thin then cooking time is perfect. Did soak the potatoes in cold water as we peeled them so they didn’t brown but you shouldn’t do so any longer since they need the starch to make the gooey sauce as it bakes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This dish looked great, but I didn't think it was as great as many here did.  Granted I did use half-n-half and chicken stock instead of heavy cream, but added more cheese than the recipe called for.  It was easy enough to make though, so I would maybe do it again and add some leeks and/or ham or bacon as some have suggested.  It was better reheated the next day.  It did take longer to cook than the 1.5 hours in the recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent and much simpler than it sounds.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone made this ahead of time (like night before)? Wondering how well it does reheated. Thanks

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I read this in one of the comments:  "Don't make ahead of time without baking--it will turn black in the refrigerator. I made it through the second baking round the day before a party. Took it out of the fridge in the morning and finished baking before we left for the hosts' home, then re-warmed it in their oven before dining. Worked great. Just don't cover it with plastic after it's finished: use a towel."

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @joey I’ll tell you tomorrow!  I’m going to copy another commenter and extend the first two 30 minute stretches by 5 or so minutes to make sure the potatoes are done. Then I’ll keep an eye out when I reheat and do the final bake time

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: the time of this is WAY WAY off. Unless you are pre-cooking the potatoes, you will need to cook them like at least 30 minutes, more like an extra hour longer than Kenji says. I checked my oven temp with two different thermometers, I checked the temp of the liquid during cooking. His time is way too short. I love Kenji, and follow his recipes and posts regularly, but this one is so far off it feels negligent.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow, I made this for Thanksgiving. Honestly, probably the best potato dish I've had. I probably am going to make this dish a holiday tradition. I mistakenly used golden Yukon potato and it still turned out to be perfect. Although the baking time is a little time of since golden Yukon has more water content, just need to watch over while it cooks! Really love love the flavor. One more thing just as Chef Kenji mentioned, this dish needs to be heavily seasoned so that it is flavorful.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This dish is so great for a holiday or dinner party. The potatoes were flavorful, came out looking exactly like the photo, and were perfect for our Christmas dinner.

The prep is a wee bit labor intensive (active prep was at least 30 min, as I don't have a mandoline and grated the cheese by hand), but none of the steps were difficult. And it was so worth it. The results were, in my dad's words, "out of this world!"

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wonderful recipe!  A hit at Christmas dinner.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Finally made this dish the other night. I sauteed some onions in butter for added flavor, increased the Gruyere to 6 oz, and along with the Parmasean I added about 3 oz of shredded Pepper Jack cheese for a little kick.  It was delicious! I sent the leftovers home with my guests so I wouldn't ruin my diet! Very hard not to have seconds of this, and I finally got my mandolin out of moth balls and used it for a change. I highly recommend this dish! Nice presentation as well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious with alternating sweet & Russet potatoes. Used garlic salt b/c out of fresh garlic. Will def make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I grew up with the flat-layered, canned mushroom soup version of scalloped potatoes that is everywhere here in the South. I no longer welcome canned soups into my life, so this recipe is a welcome find for me. I made it with goat milk instead of cream, and the result is super savory and tasty. I’ll definitely be making it again, with different cheeses and maybe a combination of cream and goat milk for a little more of a saucy result. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: One of my very favorite recipes. Use plenty of cheese AND plenty of onion. Requires a mandolin to get even thin slices. Excellent leftover, reheated and served with fried eggs for breakfast.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We used 1.5 Tbsp of kosher salt in the liquid mixture and that seemed to be the perfect amount. Could maybe do a little less depending on your preference.  Just for reference!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: meh. this was kind of a lot of effort to be not as delicious as i was hoping for. the recipe needs to specify how much salt. i used about 3/4 TBS and this was nowhere near enough. could have doubled the amount of cheese. and definitely do not need 2 TBS of butter just to grease a casserole dish. the thin potato layers with a bit of crispness were nice but i dont think ill be making this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I found that tightly packing the potato slices, as illustrated, achieved a beautiful look. However, after cooking as per the recipe, the top edges were nicely browned but everything below that was virtually raw. I had to separate the potatoes and extend the cooking time considerably. The description "very tightly packed" might need some adjustment.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This is Hasselbackspotatis, no cream, no parmigiano, no garlic, God forbid. Just butter and breadcrumbs on top. Use firm potatoes, Yukon, never russets.
Put each potatoe in a wooden spoon, slice but so that it keeps together in the base. Plenty of butter on top, breadcrumbs and bake in the oven for an hour, basing with more butter. Hasselbackenis a pretty good hotel/restaurant in Stockholm owned partly by Bjorn Ulvaeus from ABBA.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
@From Stockholm
Enjoy as you like in Stockholm. I used an excellent French cheese and no breadcrumbs

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was lured by the photo and followed the directions as written, packing the potato slices tightly in an oval baking dish.  After 3x30' @400, the potato edges looked beautiful, but the inner section was virtually raw. I had to pull it apart and cook for another hour. In notable contrast, another NYT Hassleback recipe calls for cooking single sliced potatoes @425 for 60-70', which open up naturally during cooking. (Lidey Heuck, #1024487). It seems odd that both recipe temps/times are correct.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Prep alone took 1.5 hours but it was well worth the effort. Doubled the garlic and cheese, and added a shredded shallot, 1 tsp dijon and 1.5 tsp smoked paprika to the cream sauce. If I make this again I'll invest in a mandolin 😅

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love this recipe exactly as presented. It’s our thanksgiving potato dish, and as there are just two of us I recycle the leftovers in to a lovely potato soup. Added more cream and another handful of cheese

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Go saltier than you think you need. My end result was slightly undercooked and somewhat bland, despite the fact I added an extra garlic clove and was generous with the gruyere. I think mixing in pecorino and maybe even a stronger gooey cheese, like white cheddar might make the final dish richer and more indulgent. But again, more salt!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How much salt?  Thanks.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Making for a party, is it possible to bake the day before and then reheat in the oven the night of? Or will it lose freshness?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mandolins and fingers are a deadly combo.  After too many sliced fingers I found gloves that are specifically made to protect fingers from sharp objects.  They’re lighter and more flexible than I expected, and I’m sure they’ve saved my fingertips. I try to remember them whenever I’m using really sharp objects. Leave them on to do the dishes and they’re washed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Was disappointed by the lack of flavor, really needed more garlic, more salt, and something to give it some kick.  Also made the mistake of using heavy whipping cream vs. heavy cream, and the sauce separated into a sea of oil.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love gratin potatoes, but dang, this recipe is the best. Thanks so much for the creation, Chef Lopez-Alt. Changed nothing; okay added a bit more cheese, but I'm from Wisconsin!   Made a test batch prior to an upcoming family gathering, and now I know it'll be a major hit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious recipe.  We tested 4 ways: Yukon gold peeled and unpeeled and Russet peeled and unpeeled. (Kenji recommends Russets in the video due to starch content and he peels them.)  We normally prefer Yukons over Russets for all but fries, but in this case the unpeeled Russets were the favorite.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This needed so much more cooking time than stated in the recipe, and it was still unevenly cooked in spots.  Not sure where I went awry.  Tasty, and looked great, though.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These potatoes are beautiful to look at but are not worth the trouble. They definitely fell flat at our house.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used a 13x9 baking dish and left the skins on the potatoes - used all but 1 potato from a 5lb bag. Did not really have any leftover liquid, as the potato starches bound to the cream. Super easy and fast using a mandoline. Very good showstopper side dish. Served along side prime rib and a fresh green salad for a big family dinner. Would definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can anyone give advice on what steps can be done ahead of time including some of the baking time? I am concerned with the oven time on thanksgiving where I will be a guest and our host's have one oven that will be in demand. Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving a day ahead of time and it was great! I cooked it up until you sprinkle with remaining cheese and return to the oven. Thanksgiving day I reheated it, covered with foil, on 375° for about 30 ish minutes until it was fully warm. I then added the cheese to the top and cooked it uncovered for an additional 30 minutes. During the initial baking I checked and discovered that the potatoes were a little too hard and needed an additional 10 more minutes of baking.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I think I may have been mistaken when I assumed that the entire two tablespoons of butter in the ingredients list were meant to be smeared into the baking dish to butter it, as directed. The finished result tasted good but there was a 1/8” lake of melted butter remaining in the bottom of the pan when the potatoes were served which looked very accidental.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a miss. Followed recipe exactly but never browned like this. Rather bland and soupy

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this Not-Hasselback-Potatoes recipe 3 X as written with our local starchy potato (I grow my own here in Norway and have alot to play with), and always had a problem with the middle undercooked.  I kept trying because the layers are so pretty.   My solution with the fourth try:  reserve 1/2 of potato slices without the cream mixture, assemble the outer ring of potatoes in the baking dish, blanch the reserved slices in boiling water 2 minutes, then toss with cream mixture and finish assembly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The only change I made was to add a clove of smashed garlic to the cream and steep it, like you would for Dauphinois, before adding it to the cheese mix. Came out great. This is definitely a keeper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This really wasn't too hard to make and was a stunner with the arrangement of the potatoes.  Had many oblong russets from the garden that were about the same diameter and they worked perfectly.  Using a mandoline is essential but I just know I'm gonna slice a finger tip off on it someday.  I ended up using a 2.2qt/11x7 baking dish that was just the right size but didn't pour all the cream in otherwise it would have overflowed. Fill it no more than halfway up to get the best browning effect.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Fran You can purchase special knit gloves with metal in them, I forget exactly what they are called. They will protect your hands when using the mandoline

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've cooked this according to the instructions a couple times- real winner. But I just tried it in a bigger pan ...and tried doing the first 2 30m segments then the next day 40m as someone in the comments suggested. The potatoes in the middle are still too crunchy and the edges are getting really dark...maybe for a bigger pan you have to bring it back to RT before baking or bake longer at a lower temp.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone made this in advance and reheated in the day of? Any tips?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This looks delicious. Could it be prepped a day ahead of baking?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We 1) used a mix of similar-sized russets and golds, 2) reduced the amount of potatoes/size of the baking pan but kept the cream mixture the same and 3) simmered the cream with fresh sage, parsley, and thyme to infuse a bit of herb flavor in there. Everything else was the same per the recipe. It was SPLENDID.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this twice. Both times it was excellent.  I added smoked 1.5 tsp. of smoked paprika and a Tablespoon of dijon mustard the second time which took it up a whole new level.  People asked to take the leftovers!  This recipe is a keeper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make this twice every fall/winter. I add a couple of teaspoons of Dijon, a tiny bit of smoked paprika or cayenne, and a shallot or leek. BUT if you are worried about undercooked potatoes, do a quick parboil and then dry thoroughly before proceeding. I toss on sheet pans with the cream and cheese mixture as it’s easier than in a bowl.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this last night and it turned out great. Needed several T of salt added to cream and cheese before mixing in with the potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a great recipe exactly as written.  I did not line up the potatoes as they did because I was in a time crunch.  I used a bigger pan than a 2 quart.  More like a 13 x 9 which was perfect.  They turned out exceptionally good and I don't think I will ever used a different recipe again.  I did use a bit of evaporated milk because I had just a bit left over in the fridge and it was fine.  Thank you for such a great potato recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Unnecessarily complicated. How I wish I could get this time back. It's cheese and potatoes y'all. Touch grass. Two stars only because I got to prove to my husband that it's possible to use a mandoline without bleeding so I WON.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a hit every Thanksgiving. LTo make slicing easier, lay the potato between two parallel wooden chop sticks to make cutting them almost all the way through quick and easy.  Cut off the short ends and just fan them open like a book and proceed to arrange them in your casserole dish.  This is also great with sweet potatoes!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make a potato gratin that's similar, but I start by sautéing about 1 cup of chopped onions with salt in butter until soft. Then add cream and thyme, bring to gentle simmer to reduce the moisture. Remove from heat and add grated Parmesan / Romano and stir until melted. Let cool before adding to potatoes. The extra steps take 10 minutes (plus cooling) and yields a more favorable gratin.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this yesterday. Wow! So delicious! I couldn’t stop eating it LOL. I did a half recipe just to try it out prior to making it for thanksgiving. 2.5 lbs of russet potatoes (3 large ones). My husband does NOT like cheese… I love cheese! so I used Comte and parmigiana, my thinking on that was the Comte isn’t as strong of a flavor as Gruyère can be! I baked it in a 1.5qt round Pyrex casserole dish. Used all of the sliced up potatoes as well as all of the cheese/cream mixture, the cream mixture only came up halfway when I poured it over the potatoes. Nothing overflowed! Lots of great browned crispy edges. The cooking time was spot on for me as well. AND BEST OF ALL my cheese hating husband liked it!!!! Woohoo! Major freaking win! Will definitely be making this again and for thanksgiving!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these for the first time every last year for Christmas.  I used a mandolin and thought it was super smart to slice them up the night before.  Well a lesson I learned is that if you do that -your potatoes will turn a grayish color.  We had no choice so we cooked them but I coudln't get past the gray color.  Don't worrry we screwed up almost every other dish we made as well!!  This year we are going to redeem ourselves- wish us luck!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am not all that skilled in the kitchen. However, I have made this several times exactly as described in the recipe and it is always delicious and a big hit. It is pretty easy and not all that time consuming.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Normally I make sure I have read the comments before trying a recipe, and normally they are helpful.  This time, not so much.  Substituting Yukon Gold Potatoes instead of Russet potatoes was a big mistake, they took forever to cook and never really achieved that desired creaminess.  Adding smoked paprika did nothing to make the dish better.  If I make this again, will stick with Kenji’s recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I thought I knew every potato recipe out there.  This is amazing!  Made a little casserole just for the 2 of us;  plenty, anticipating leftovers for my lunch.  Nope.  I used Yukon Gold potatoes, my favorite. Making huge casseroles for the Christmas crowd next week.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: did you make the full recipe for 2 people?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you don't have a slicer, or a food processor with a slicer,  I think a better option could be to use two chop sticks the square round ones and slice almost to the bottoms. Like a 'fan' shape this could help with the room issue.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Stay with recipe as written.  I doubled the recipe, prepared it a day early, baked covered 45 min - 1hr  (as others here had recommended), let it cool on the counter and refrigerated overnight with foil cover.  The next day the tops of the potatoes where black (as also happened to others here).  I cooked it anyway for another 1.5 hrs or so, but the black portion of the potatoes was hard and inedible and the rest of the potatoes were rubbery and not what you would want to eat.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I wonder if you could prep this ahead of time, but instead of soaking the sliced potatoes in water (which, as someone else stated, gets rid of necessary starch that helps thicken the sauce), you could just toss them in the cream mixture and keep them in the fridge until ready to assemble/bake. That way the starch still ends up in the dish, but you could still get all of that slicing done ahead of time. Thoughts?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: did anyone try to do the make ahead suggested?   I want to make this for a clients dinner party and don't have a great deal of time between when i get off work and when i have to deliver the finished meal.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did this one year, not on purpose - I ran out of potatoes and had to get more to fill the casserole. It works, but the potatoes start turning brown. Doesn't matter though, you can't tell after it's baked. What people usually do is complete the first two bakes, then reheat in the oven and add the grated cheese on top for the last step on the day of.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I want to pass along a caution to those who may consider making this ahead of time (i.e., the initial pair of 30 minute bake times the day before, then doing the final bake the next day when serving). I followed the recipe to the letter, except for the above. It turned out to be a disaster for me.  The potatoes turned black when removing from the fridge the next day (in spite of being immersed in the cream sauce).  Also, the potatoes were undercooked after browning.  Had to toss the whole dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used russet, sweet and Yukon potato’s for a yummy mix of flavor and colorful display. Needed more creamy mixture.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was pressed for time so I put it in the microwave for 5 min and it saved me 20 in the oven. Taters cooked through and it browned nicely.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mine was super oily last night and I’ve made it 100 times!! Never had that problem. Anyone have any ideas??

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I know the first and only time I made it came out oily as well. I added more cheese and attributed it to that. I’m making alit again right now, wish me luck!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Don't substitute for heavy cream. I couldn't get heavy cream anywhere the day before Thanksgiving so I used half and half. Even though I added two TBS butter along with the half and half, the liquid curdled.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This turned out beautifully, but I made the mistake of under-salting. I feel like I added about 2 teaspoons to the cream mixture but that wasn’t enough. There are some instances when I wish the recipe would tell me an amount as opposed to “generously”. Next time I’ll taste it halfway. I’ll absolutely make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
If I could give this zero I would, the potatoes were
Totally crunchy when I served at thanksgiving, despite cooking them longer than recommended. Cannot be trusted. I will try again with sweet potatoes bc that seemed to work better. Terrible

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this exactly as written, my family is still mad at me for how horrible these turned out!! I am a great cook and cooked all of Thanksgiving dinner myself, I was the most excited about this recipe because I LOVE potatoes but no matter what I did these would NOT cook!!! I cooked them over an hour extra and they are CRUNCHY! not to mention, could really use some more flavor. I'm not mad I'm disappointed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Exact same thing happened to me---I was mortified when we served it, and unlike other recipes, you can't taste and adjust along the way. I cooked it for way longer than described and it was still unpleasantly crunchy. I am unsure whether rinsing to remove some of the starch would have helped. I want to try this with sweet potatoes, which have much less starch, as others who made this using sweet potatoes seemed to have fantastic results... so much work, and such a disaster!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was so excited about making this but sadly it ended up being bland, I don't think I added enough salt even though I thought I was being generous. Probably would have added more cheese than the recipe suggests too.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've made this several times and each time.it takes at least another 20-25 minutes for the potatoes to fully cook. At the recommended time and temp the potatoes are still firm to the tooth.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Same issue for me. I had to deconstruct the whole thing and cook for an additional hr to cook everything through.
will not repeat

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a very rare miss. Followed recipe exactly and they looked just like the picture. Top half of potatoes was actually to dried out buy the time it was golden brown. Taste was ok. I realized when more than half was still in my fridge 5 days after Christmas that no one was impressed with these potatoes in my family.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What are best steps to make ahead of time and reheat day of?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a good make-ahead dish for Thanksgiving. It's one of those that is better the second time it is heated through.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just made this and it was great.  It says 1:30 minutes of bake time and it takes every bit of that if not another 15 minutes.  So if you are timing this with your other dishes allot for an extra 15 minutes of bake time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not sure what went wrong - doubled recipe and cooked in casserole dish per another's recc - this made for 4 lines of potatoes. cooked for 90 mins per recipe and it looked beautiful.  served only to find out potatoes were still very raw.  put back in oven for another 30 minutes and they were better but still not 100%.  clearly missed the thanksgiving serving window however.  next time i will try making only 3 lines or extend the first (covered) cooking window by 30 min.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these twice over Thanksgiving (thinking the first failure was an aberration) and both times the recipe yielded nearly raw potatoes.  The flavors were good but the potatoes were not cooked - I even left them in longer than the 90 min. I kills me to waste food but I threw both batches out.  Deleting this from my recipe box.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Second year I made this for thanksgiving a guests were begging for the little bit of leftovers. Make sure once arranged to only fill liquid halfway otherwise the beautiful pattern gets lost in lots of glorious cheese and cream

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This turned out perfect and I’m not much of a cook. Followed the recipe exactly but incorporated two notes:

Cook for an additional 30 minutes (15 when covered and 15 uncovered). I also waited to add the last 1/3 of cheese in the last 15 minutes of cooking. 

Since the recipe called for individually layering each slice, I dredged individual potato slices in the cheese sauce as I put them in the pan. 

Next time I will increase my garlic and add scallions.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I regret that I made this recipe. It cannot begin to compare to classical French gratins, which are easier to make and to riff on. Mostly it seemed a waste of good ingredients. The top was tough more than "crunchy;" the lower half more gluey than soft and winning. Even with more salt & thyme, a waste of more gruyere & parmesan, it doesn't add up to more than a look for presentation's sake. Not worth it. Back to Julia Child again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This needs 2 hours to bake!  I used 8 russets in 9x13 shiny casserole.  They fit perfect.  Doubled the cheese and added some additional 1/2&1/2 to the heavy cream.  Filled up about 1/4 to 1/3 height of pan and worked perfectly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make this all the time and have tried it with Yukon Gold and Russet and feel like the Russet makes it better.  Has some texture.  YG were mushy compared to the R.  Trying it today with the skins on.  It's a fantastic dish, no matter how you make it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a delicious, fancy way to make potatoes. I made it as written but used a rectangular baking dish instead of oval because thats what I had. Next time I’d go a bit longer on the first cook with foil to get the potatoes a little more tender. I’d also use a little more salt than I normally would. Served with beef tenderloin and salad. Made for such a special meal.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone adjusted this recipe into a vegan version?  If so, what cheeses and butter did you use and how was the outcome?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was so excited about this recipe, but now, having made it twice, I am giving up. It just didn’t turn out well. It was oily and not cheesy enough. I tried several different cheeses and also doubled the cheese, as suggested by a few commenters. Oh well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Using only heavy cream as the liquid will create puddles of oil. Don't get me wrong, I'm a heavy cream addict, but that puddling is a bit too much, so I use half and half, with corn starch or rice flour dissolved in it to keep that final work of art thick enough to go live.  And hey, you can always throw some fresh butter on top if you like those puddles.  The freshly melted stuff tastes much better than the cooked stuff.  And don't forget the chopped parsley on top of the butter!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Well, I see. Most of these comments/suggestions are from seven years ago, but certain problems persist such as the length of cooking time seems to be longer than listed in the recipe.  After the first 30 minutes, I removed the foil and baked for 45 minutes, and the potatoes were still not golden.  I’ll put this in the refrigerator and reward it and do the final baking at thanksgiving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this but had to make it lactose free for a family member. Whisking lactose free sour cream with lactose free whole milk (about 1 cup sour cream to the two cups of milk) made for a good heavy cream substitute. Gruyère aged over 60 days and Parmesan covered the cheeses. Used a mix of skin on Yukon gold and red potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Luci what a good idea. I never keep cream on hand.  mixing milk with SC or even yogurt is a very useful hack. Thanks.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a first time delight and huge favorite as the potato dish for Thanksgiving. I used half and half instead of cream and it was still a major hit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was so easy to make and it was incredible. Followed the recipe to a t. I had to run errands in the middle of prep, so I had it sit in the fridge for a few hours before baking - did not seem to affect it at all. Will make it again for sure!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I watched the video and it’s not true that there’s no published kind of shingled potato gratin like this. I made a recipe that’s almost exactly the same from Carla Lalli Music last Christmas on the Epicurious app.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make one very like it that I got from a Martha Stewart recipe. Shingled in rows, but the garlic is cut and rubbed on the sides and bottom of the glass dish. The mixture of dairy is mostly heavy cream with a small amount of whole milk. Other than that, you heat the dairy mixture with salt and a teaspoon on grated nutmeg, pore it over the potatoes in a mixing bowl and being sure all pieces are covered you begin stacking. Once done you sprinkle 6 oz of Gruyere on top. Amazing!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a decade-old recipe from his first cookbook - it didn't come about the day before the video came out.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be frozen ahead?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I wouldn't, but refrigerating for several days might even enhance the final product as it alters the starches in the potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these a week ago and cut the recipe in half since there are only 2 of us. Worked perfectly! Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Ngrimmick came here for this! What size pan did you use?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this recipe the other day exactly as its written and it was wonderful.  I used a square 8×8 2-quart glass baking dish.  I think if I had a longer, slightly shallower cooking vessel, it would have allowed for better browning and even more of a crunchy consistency on the tops of the potatoes.   I didn't need all of the cream mixture.  I poured the cream more than half way up the side of the pan (oops!) and I had some cheese bubbling over onto the handles of the dish.   Thank goodness I put a sheet pan directly below the dish because I did have oil drip down and collect on it.   In hindsight, I recommend covering the sheet pan with foil for easier cleanup.  The smell of the potatoes cooking filled my house with the most delightful aroma of cheese, garlic,  and thyme.  Definitely worth making!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Regarding everyone freaking out about which potato type to use, I can end the debate right now. I checked Mastering the Art and Julia says to use “boiling potatoes” for dauphinois gratin, by which she means Eastern whites, Yukon Golds, fingerlings, or any waxy potato that holds its shape—not russets, which are exactly the “baking” potato type she’s warning against for this dish. She wanted potatoes that behave like the French varieties—Charlotte, Ratte, etc.—that maintain their structure and create distinct, tender slices rather than a starchy, broken-down mass. Specifically she says “We shall not classify potato varieties by name. We shall merely specify baking potatoes when we mean the type of white potato such as Idaho which turns floury after cooking, and boiling potatoes when we are talking about the white potato which retains its shape. It is particularly important that you use the right kind of potato for such dishes as scalloped potatoes, for the potato must not disintegrate during cooking; in such cases we have used quotation marks in order to draw your attention to the necessity for using “boiling” potatoes.” ” CASE CLOSED Excerpt From Mastering the Art of French Cooking, Volume 1 Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle & Simone Beck https://books.apple.com/us/book/mastering-the-art-of-french-cooking-volume-1/id436691174 This material may be protected by copyright.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: buddy, i hate to tell you this, but if you paid any attention the recipe you might have noticed this isn't a traditional dauphinois gratin

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @I asked Julia I agree that Julia is the expert. But in this case the recipe was written to use a starchy potato that does break down more, so the starch can thicken the cream mixture.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Definitely a special occasion recipe - very decadent and a real crowd pleaser! I reviewed the comments (thank you) and added 3.5 teaspoons of salt and 1 teaspoon of paprika. It was perfectly seasoned! Used a mandolin to slice the potatoes and cooked for the full 90 minutes

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Almost a whole 5 lb. bag of russet potatoes fits into an 8x8 casserole dish! Who knew? I arranged them very tightly, maybe too tightly, and only had two potatoes leftover. It's frustrating the salt quantity isn't even loosely suggested in the recipe, since that is probably the biggest factor in success versus failure. After a bunch of reading, I tried 3 teaspoons of Diamond Crystal and it was good, but 2.5 teaspoons would have been better.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is so divine that we floated to the ceiling like Snuffles the dog. I halved the recipe because it's just the two of us. Worked out just fine cutting all the ingredients in half. Except the garlic. We are fans of the garlic bulb. The final bake was more like 20 minutes than thirty. Exceptional.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Use a mandoline slicer. So much faster and regular thickness to the potato slices.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just be careful when using your fingers near the end of the potato.  Make sure you use the plastic pusher to protect them.   I'm now sadder, but wiser.   :-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can buy cut-proof metal laced kitchen gloves for $10; well worth it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made these for Thanksgiving, doubling the recipe, with great success. The day before, cooked them up to the final 30 minute bake then let come to room temperature on Thanksgiving, topped with remaining cheese and baked for the final 30 minutes. Using a mandoline made relatively quick work of the slicing. Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is an incredible recipe! I ended up using red potatoes because that's what I had on hand, and it was still so delicious. Will absolutely make this again - plus it looks beautiful coming out of the oven!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I'll remove the butter, cheeses, and cream and use olive oil and few other herbs (rosemary and the likes).
Would work better with my Mediterranean taste.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Do you think the cream could be substituted with good chicken stock?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just wondering how much you can do in advance......I have a big crowd for Christmas and it would be great to have some stages out of the way. ? has anyone done the first two stages the day before.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Look for Kate C's response from a year ago in the "Most Helpful" tab of the comment section.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes, someone made the first 2 stages a day ahead and finished up the browning part the next day for 40mins...mentioned in an earlier note.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn't make this exactly with the sliced potatoes and heavy cream. However, I followed the recipe in spirit. The flavor was fantastic. Everyone loved it. It's all about the cheeses. The Gruyère makes all the difference.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this with an assortment of russet & sweet potatoes (not yams, they’re too soggy). It was “ok” - not fantastic. Found it to be a bit on the bland side, and I added a bunch of garlic & minced shallots, etc. But it does make a massive dish, so it is easy for a big crowd. I would definitely boost the flavours next time & will add some Dijon to the cream mixture to see if that helps.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
1/2: Puritanical recipe zealots will be apoplectic with my modifications, but this was a DELICIOUS use of odd bits of fridge shrapnel:

9.5 x 7 x 2.5 baking dish

962 g skin ON, runt-sized russets at the bottom of the bag

Cheeses: 45 g St Agur, 48g Pecorino Romano, 87g aged white cheddar – yes, half the potatoes and more cheese, because that's what I had that needed using
 
The rest in 2/2...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
2/2 (cont) - for Cream Goop:

190 mL whipping cream
70 mL mix of feta and caper BRINE
2 tsp LeeKumKee chicken powder
¼ tsp kelp powder
½ tsp garlic powder
½ tsp onion powder
½ tsp ground mustard
½ tsp sea salt
pepper
1 tsp EACH minced fresh thyme and savory
2 tsp minced fresh sage
3 tbsp minced fresh parsley
1 med finely minced shallot
all the St Agur, half of the shredded pecorino and cheddar

Mix into thick goop, follow recipe but add 15 min to 1st bake. WOWZA.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you make this ahead?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My daughter and I did two separate baking dishes of 4.5 pounds of potatoes, convection a little less time at 375.  Perfect!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these two years ago for Thanksgiving.  It was a complete disaster in every way imaginable.  I followed the recipe exactly as stated.  I consider my skills as intermediate to advanced depending on the dish.  Everything about this dish didn't work well from the cream sauce (which broke during baking) to the assembly to the actual cooking and final texture of potatoes.  The risks here is the cream sauce breaking, undercooked potatoes, and a gooey mess of a dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I make this the day before Thanksgiving and reheat it the next day in the oven?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Per the other comments here I did a few things:
1. I added a tsp each of ground mustard and smoked paprika and upped the salt to 1.5 tsp 
2. I was very nervous about my cream splitting so I made a mornay with a couple tbs of butter and a tbs each of flour and cornstarch, then added my cheese. I ended up having to thin it with a couple splashes of boiling water so the cornstarch was probably overkill, but I liked how the thicker sauce coated the potato slices well! 
A huge hit, would make again

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used a Cuisinart food processor and had to choose between a 4mm and 2mm blade--1/8 inch is 3.175mm.  Went with 4mm and it came out great!    Leftovers reheated well in the oven next day.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Incredible, restaurant-quality result achieved by my 17-year-old son, who improvised with a 10” springform pan since we didn’t have the right size casserole dish. He used a mandolin and needed extra potatoes to fill the pan tightly. The last 30 minutes of baking was in convection mode to really crisp up the tops.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tasty. I added more garlic and smoked paprika. If using a food processor on russets, cut the potatoes in half across the short side before processing. This keeps them from falling over in the feed tube thereby avoiding elongated slices.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these in advance and thought I'd save the final 30 mins for just before serving. The result was that the potatoes were undercooked, and in retrospect this strategy was flawed as the potatoes didn't have time to get up to heat  and finish cooking--next time I'll cook it all the way through and just reheat before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a GREAT recipe!  I made it exactly as is BUT  I was in such a rush I forgot to coat the potatoes in the cream & cheese mixture before I stacked them!  I poured the mixture over the top-Hence, after cooking for the full hour and a half some of the taters were less done in the middle and the top was extra cheesy.  but STILL it was delicious my guests raved and I would absolutely make this again. Oh I and I used dried thyme and a very generous amount of kosher salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I tried cooking the first two 30 min. steps and then covering/refrigerating the dish before the third. The potatoes oxidized and I had a brown casserole! Live and learn. Probably should have cooked the total dish first and warmed it up.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made for Thanksgiving and as many other cooks have said it was the star of the show. Doubled the recipe and used sweet potatoes, added a little garlic and herb seasoning but otherwise as RXd. Was concerned about cook times and my over runs a little cool, so I boosted the heat to 425 for the first two 30 min cooks and down to 400 so as not to burn the cheese topping on the final 30 mins. Foiled the dish, put it in the car, and it sat for another 45 minutes until the full meal was ready. Killer.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I get it now with second batch.  Bowl of milky cheese before you start cutting potatoes.  I then used food processor, throwing every full bowl directly into mix.  First batch went in water and wrong texture.  Lucious creamy goodness and as good looking as the cook.  Nice holiday dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
1. I think this a fundamentally good recipe, time and temp ok 
2. "Season [the sauce] generously with salt and pepper" means that it should be almost too salty to taste. Covering unseasoned potatoes in the sauce will substantially lessen how salty everything tastes, and if you don't put enough salt in the sauce it's hard to fix later.
3. 1/8in slices are key, try slicing 8 slices and stack them up to check your sizing
4. Test with a skewer or fork once it looks done, if not cook more

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My guests loved these but I thought they were just ok, and they didn’t seem to taste any better or have any more crisp than a traditional potatoes gratin. I made a double batch with a bit of extra cheese. 1 TBSP of salt was not enough for a double batch, I’d probably double it. I used a 14x11 pan and squeezed in 5 rows. The outer potatoes were creamy but the inside potatoes were not as well cooked so it could go for more time if you do a double.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Don’t underestimate the cooking time. They are beautiful potatoes but if you don’t cook long enough can be a bit raw in the center.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Following another note, I made this the day before, up to the last bake (with cheese), then took out of the fridge to come to room temp and baked for 45 minutes. I wish I had baked a bit longer as the potatoes still needed it (15 minutes?), but overall a delicious and very well-received side for lamb on Christmas Eve!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe twice, in my opinion it’s overrated. I would say the texture doesn't come out creamy or gooey enough on the inside. I tried adding more cheese the second time but both cheeses are pretty sharp so it was a bit overpowering. I love a sharp cheese but this recipe would be way better with a mild cheese in the mix, more cheese overall, and Yukon gold rather than russet.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this to serve with an Easter ham, solid innovation on a classic, worth your time. It's a simple enough dish that I didn't measure ingredients and mostly just followed the technique. I used onion powder and nutmeg instead of thyme and garlic, and ended up basting the top with butter towards the end to get more golden crispiness instead of covering it with more cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Make with sweet potatoes

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My family loved this! My only comment is the cooking time. I followed the recipe exactly but after last 30 min baking I( total 90 min) slid out a slice and the potatoes were not cooked!
