It looked so beautiful but had to put in oven for another hour!! I put in frig for party the next day, brought to room temp and put back in oven at 350. Also added more Gruyère’ and parm .. why not?? So good!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: can you freeze this at the 1 hour mark?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Have been making these potatoes since the fifties in Stockholm, copying restaurant Hasselbacken’s way of making them. Here I used this recipe and must say I really liked it, especially the addition of thyme. I did use less of cheese and more of cream and it came out wonderfully creamy with a very mild taste of the cheeses.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and followed the recipe as written.  It was terrible.  Potatoes were hard and dry.  Bland.  No one ate them and it was a lot of effort for a flop of a dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Sarah same. The sweet potatoes came out great, the white russets were hard even after an extra 100 mins of cooking:(

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This lives up to the hype. Made a massive amount for Christmas Eve, so many i didn’t have enough oven space so i put a fully made but not cooked dish in the fridge. Two days later I cooked as described and I thought it was even better than the 3 I made day of. Texture and everything was perfect, I felt the flavors were more enhanced having sat for two days. For all of them, though, I would be generous with salt. Will definitely make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for our Christmas dinner because I wanted to change things up a bit from our usual mashed potatoes. It was a big hit, but took about 20 min longer to bake than the recipe suggested. I think I didn't slice the potatoes thin enough and will try that next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was actually little effort for an impressive dish!  I used the food processor for slicing so that saved time.  Be sure to buy small enough potatoes to fit vertically in the food processor chute so you get nice round slices which will look best when stacked. The one tip I would add would be to keep an eye on the cooking process.  We started it as instructed at 400 and even after removing the foil the potatoes just didn't seem to be browning so for the last half hour we increased to 425.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’ve made these twice and both times the potatoes were not cooked through. I used a mandolin and sliced them very thin, took time to separate the slices and cooked them even longer than the recipe instructs. All the other rave reviews are so confusing to me! I’m a very experienced cook so am really upset these were such a time consuming flop! The potato slices in the video look a bit thicker than mine and the potatoes look like they’re packed a little less tightly, but the recipe specifically says to pack them tightly. Any insight into what went wrong would be much appreciated.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Jane I had the same issue the first time I made them and found success cooking covered at 425 for 30 mins, uncovered at 425 for 50 mins, then adding cheese and cooking at 400 for final 30 mins. Hope this helps!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Ashley Thanks! Maybe I’ll give it one last try…

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Jane Turns out my oven was broken! Will definitely give this one more chance with a working oven!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Worked very well for me. Followed the recipe pretty precicely.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just loved this recipe. I was experimenting before taking later to holiday gathering... it turned out really beautiful and tasty to boot! I could have added more salt than I did , but worked fine to add at the table. Thanks for this one!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used grated Emmental cheese (funky Swiss cheese) and added a big dash of coarse Dijon mustard to the cream with extra chopped thyme.. going for like a Croque Monsieur kinda vibe. It’s still in the oven but it smells promising.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Any idea of how much Kosher salt they're using in this recipe? He says to oversalt it, but there is no indication of how much to use. Any advice from those that have made it before is useful, thanks.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Jenn D. Taste as you go! When the cream tastes salty to you, you’re good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: At listed temp, times and colors- the potatoes came out a little al dente. I will probably slice thinner than the recommended 1/4” next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Joshua the recipe calls for 1/8” potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How many days ahead can this be prepared & refrigerated?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Linda Friedman When I reheat these, I like to pour a little bit more cream in the dish I’m reheating and they always turn out nicely. I imagine making it all the way through and storing in the oven for a couple of days would be fine. When you reheat, I would pour another ~1/2 cup of cream and an extra sprinkle of cheese and bake until warmed through.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Store in Fridge if making ahead.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Have made this many times and it is delicious. I also add some thinly sliced onions; that makes it even better.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make this with a Greek lemon and oil base and it's amazing.  Not cheese or cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Given the packing density encouraged in this recipe, I found that 1.5hrs at 400 deg F is simply insufficient to cook the potatoes completely.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My family devours this! I made twice, the first time with store brand cream which was I think 36% fat, it did break at the end. Potatoes were good, but there was a bit of oil on the botttom. Next time I made with Darigold 40% fat heavy cream and it was perfect.  I can only find 40% cream at Costco where I live.  I also had a little space at the end to fill the gratin pan, I just sliced some regular potatoes without the sauce to make it a tight fit.  Worked great! I will continue to make!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love this recipe. I follow some others and double the cheese and add 1T salt, 1T Dijon, 1t pepper to the mix. Sometimes it doesn’t have any of the sauce left to pour over, but that’s never been an issue. Always a crowd pleaser.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We found this dish lacking pizzazz. Granted it was our miss, but it was under salted. I'd appreciate a suggestion for a base quantity of salt. We found it inadequately cheesy. We used a high quality gruyere. Swap in pecorino for the Parmesan? It was a very welcome addition to our feast and a lovely option to our very good mashies. But it didn't really rock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Delicious.  Served for thanksgiving and was a hit! I honestly didn’t think it was that hard to make. But those crunchy edges are so so yummy.

Helpful hint - don’t make ahead too soon.  I ran ahead of schedule and just put it in a cool spot till I needed it.  Unfortunately the liquid of the potato increased in the dish, I ended up needing to drain some of it because it was 3/4 or more towards the top and didn’t want to overflow in oven.

I will make this again 100%

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Hasslebavk, gratin who cares about technical definition this was delicious 

The mandolin made it easy to get thinly sliced potatoes within minutes. 

I used whatever cheese was on hand at home which was Champagne cheddar from Trader Joe’s. I think it paired well. 

Will be adding to my holiday menu permanently!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My teen-aged son loves this recipe so I will make it again. I like it but may try experimenting with different cheeses as I'm not 100% sold on the taste of the Gruyere in this dish. Also, make sure to salt the dish sufficiently before cooking--it needed more salt than I expected.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I know everyone complains about the cream’s oil breaking, but if you go into the comments section of the original Serious Eats recipe, you can see that Kenji left a comment on top saying that it’s bound to happen. 

Anyway, I’m too scared to ever use a mandolin but I do have a food processor with a slicing disc attachment. I did have to cut a few larger potatoes to make it fit into the food processor’s “tube” but it was still super easy. 

I was a bit behind schedule so I did increase the oven temperature to 425 at the end but the potatoes still turned out great!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made these last night. Fantastic, but here are my tips: I used a complete 4.5 bag of russet potatoes. I discarded the tiny slices you get from the ends of the potatoes and what remained fit absolutely perfectly snug in a 9X9” baking pan. I used an extra ounce of both Gruyère and Parmesan cheeses, and I used exactly 1tsp of salt. Everything else exactly as written. Everyone loved them but next time, I’ll add yet another ounce of each cheese and 1.5tsp rather than 1tsp of salt. I’ll probably also extend the second bake (uncovered) from 30 to 45 minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made first time as per directions. It was good, but not *great*. Second time I added Dijon mustard, smoke paprika and shiitake mushroom powder into the cream sauce and it boosted the flavor into the stratosphere. I also used a mix of comte and slightly funky cave-aged gruyere. Turned out phenomenal. Make a double batch for 8 people and you won't have any leftovers

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I have a story to tell.

Two Christmases ago, I attempted to make this dish. I did not have a mandolin at the time, and attempted to slice the potatoes by hand with a chef’s knife. I convinced myself that my slices were even and thin enough that the recipe would work.
At dinner, I watched as my girlfriend’s sister attempted to cut a potato in half with her fork and was forced to use her knife. The potatoes were not evenly cut and some had not cooked through. It was pretty embarrassing as she is a wonderful host who cooks incredible food. 
I promised myself I would try again, using a mandolin. Last year we did not host, so this year I revisited the recipe and went over all the details to make sure I was prepared. My girlfriend let me know that she was getting me a mandolin for Christmas.
On Christmas Day I joyfully unwrapped my new mandolin, and that afternoon got it set up and started peeling potatoes. I decided to do one tester potato, just a few slices to see how the machine worked.
It worked much faster and better than I expected. Within seven seconds I had sliced off an eighth of an inch of my right index finger. Off to the ER we went.
The rest of the afternoon is a bit of a blur. I remember shouting instructions on how to finish the potatoes as a nurse anesthetized my finger and got the bleeding stopped. 
My amazing girlfriend did manage to complete the dish, and I have to say, it came out absolutely perfect and was a total showstopper. Definitely worth the wait/blood!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Jamie Reynolds welp! This was our New Year's Day. I was unscathed but my partner swooped in to help with the last potato and sliced the side of his pinky off. A gratin rite of passage indeed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My 1.8L casserole would not hold 4 pounds of potatoes.  I had to use another 1L casserole to hold them all.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Question: Can this dish be made the day before?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We recommend making this dish the day you plan to serve it; potato dishes tend not to do well in the refrigerator, where they get heavy and gluey, and may discolor. If you want to make potatoes ahead, try this recipe for mashed potato casserole, which is an exception to the do-not-refrigerate-potatoes rule. The potatoes are mashed with sour cream, and so while they are dense (rather than fluffy), they are also creamy and smooth: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1013923-mashed-potato-casserole

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone tried alternating sweet potatoes and russet potatoes for this dish?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It looks like other commenters have tried that with great results. Or you could make this two-potato gratin: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/7072-two-potato-gratin

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It makes a beautiful presentation but is bland.  It's easy to make if you use a mandoline.  I might make it again but only if there is a variation that will make a more exciting flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: made this for thanksgiving. It was really beautiful, but I wholeheartedly agree with the comments about the sauce being too bland. Will add some Tabasco next time!  It's really very easy to make with a mandoline but looks complex and impressive.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We followed the recipe exactly and although the dish looked perfect as we served it, the lower half of the potatoes were completely undercooked, almost raw even. It needed another hour of cooking and the results were still not great. I think russets are the wrong choice - if we ever make this again we'll be using yukons. A disappointment.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made a non Kosher version of this with bacon and onions and it was delicious! I also substituted Gruyere for Mozzarella and added some smoked paprika for the color.  It, probably, did not worked out to its intended shape but was very tasty in its own way. The full report is on my blog Golden Apron. Thanks a lot for the simple and tasteful recipe inspiration!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made for a large crowd.  Rave reviews from all.  Top is crispy and bottom is dredged in sauce.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made this and found that for the amount of work involved, it wasn't worth it.  I think a classic gratin is a better use of time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Totally and completely agree!! I was so looking forward to this--and forget it. There is a reason the classic is the classic. It's easier and tastier.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: i agree too. i made this awhile back after seeing it on serious eats, but was disappointed as it was billed as both creamy and cheesy and what i got was neither. the potatoes were both pretty and tasty, but it wasn't what i was expecting.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This made me smile!. It looks like a delicious porcupine!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can't wait to make this dish. Would love to figure out how to introduce a bit of bacon and green onion to it at some point.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is it sacrilegious to ask if cheddar would work in place of the Gruyère?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I took note of the comments regarding inadequate cooking time and cooked this 30 mins longer than called for. Even with a 2 hour total cooking time in a convection oven, the potatoes were underdone. The appearance was fine but the finished product a disappointment.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was absolutely fantastic. I used my food processor with the 4mm blade, worked perfect, and added extra cheese because we are cheese nuts.  Used oregano and thyme - it was rich delicious and everyone loved it. I worried because Idid not have a lot of extra cream after coating the potatoes but it was fine.  Will make again and maybe try some fun variations.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I found that /18 of an inch for the potatoes was too thin. I sliced them at 1/4 inch.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Used Idaho Russet, sliced lengthwise.
Potatoes were raw after the 1 1/2 hour baking process.
Mandolin a plus.
Would try again with a different potato.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The picture looks amazing, right?  I followed the recipe exactly and my dish looked different -- it had big pools of grease (from all the butterfat) floating all over the top.  Make this version only if you love your food dripping with fat.  It might have worked for me to use half and half instead of heavy cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’ve read others said to let it cool a bit and that the “grease” gets reabsorbed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is wonderful! It needed a bit more cooking time (maybe 15 min) to get the potatoes tender enough.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cut this in half and used a smaller dish, came out well, although I wound up cooking about 1:45 vs 1:30... Just depends on your potato slices, dish size, etc. Will make this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The second time I made this, I switched cheeses and used a goat gouda and an aged cheddar - fantastic racy zing that really elevated the dish, imo.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This took longer to cook than expected, I know potatoes are dense (especially packed like this) but was glad we weren't rushed for time. Also it was extremely greasy -- when reheating leftovers (which were delicious) I did so in a soup plate rather than a dinner plate because the liquefied fat the reheated potatoes gave off overflowed the dinner plate. Word of advice: have the trash can ready to receive the excess fat. Do NOT pour it down the sink.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I wonder how this might be with sweet potatoes, and maybe a piquant cheese?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Needs salt, but so good!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Now, I understand why one doesn't want to use all of the liquid...it hides the design of the potato pattern. And I put all of the little pieces under the big pieces and used them to fill in spaces....
We love it❣️

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this as a side dish for Christmas dinner. Will make this again. Made it just as the recipe states.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a huge hit every Thanksgiving.  It takes a bit of work but a lot of prep can be done ahead of time.  Careful not to over cook it.  You want a creamy yet crispy texture. It's great as written but riff away on this one!  The possibilities are endless.  My favorite so far was to add smoked salmon, sauteed red onion, capers and sour cream to the dish as a topping.  The full Lox and bagel treatment.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Potatoes, cheese, heavy cream...what's not to like? I served this with a large holiday meal. It was devoured.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great recipe! I chose to substitute 1 part of cream with chicken stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be made ahead and then reheated?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Alan.  Yes, someone made this a day ahead and finished up the browning part the next day for 40mins.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Nice, sorry but Hasselback potatoes is something else !

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We used about 9 potatoes, so that we could use the center sections.  We used about 1-1/2 tsp salt, 1/2 tsp black pepper, 1/8 tsp nutmeg, more than 1 tsp of thyme, added also about 1 tsp of chopped fresh rosemary and 3 tbsp finely chopped shallot.  On this particular occasion, the smallest section of Gruyere in our store was .75 lb.  We used all of it, plus a proportionate amount of Parm-Reg.  The cooking time was a bit longer than called for in order for the topping to start to brown.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sadly, this was a dish that turned out significantly less than the sum of its parts for me. I followed the recipe, but somehow, even with this much cheese, it turned out pretty flavorless. Perhaps I under-salted the potatoes....?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this quite close to recipe but with Yukon Golds and, having forgotten to pick up Gruyere, used a basic low quality Swiss cheese with the fresh Parmesan. Added a pinch of cayenne and Herbes de Provence to kick up the flavor and the potatoes were a hit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used a 3 quart square casserole, and did 1.5 times the recipe, and it worked great.  Was worried about slightly different sized rounds, but it wasn't a problem.  They stick together coming out of the cream and the height variation contributes to browning.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Please tell those of us who don't own a scale what 3 oz. of gruyere and 2 oz. parmesan translate into cup sizes.  This does look like a nice dramatic dish to bring to our host and hostess this Thanksgiving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for a Thanksgiving potluck yesterday. It was a lot of work and frankly not really worth it. Just not a lot of flavor on the non-crispy parts of the potatoes. It looked pretty but no one came back for seconds.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These were good. They simply weren’t my favorite. They were just kinda bland. And this cheese isn’t super cheap so I wanted more flavor without having to add a bunch of salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I love this recipe.  I have always used Yukon Gold.  I was concerned about the potatoes discoloring, and so I poured the whole batch into the liquid/cheese bowl and mixed them with my hands to assure they were all coated.  Then I arranged them in the pan as the recipe suggested, finally pouring the remaining liquid over them.  They look perfect, taste perfect, and the cooking time seemed just right in my experience.

I have made them 4 times now and they are a complete hit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you make a day or so in advance?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is now a regular feature for Thanksgiving and Christmas at our house! The trick is matching the pounds of potatoes to the perfect dish. It's hard to go back to the beginning to slice potatoes when you're up to your wrists in cream! I recommend making extra potatoes from the start (we start with six pounds) and either tossing the extras (that will never happen here) or putting them in a small dish to bake alongside the beautiful larger one. So pretty in an oval casserole dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a very yummy side that's impressive when it hits the table. We made it 3 times, making tweaks along the way. Parboiling the sliced potatoes worked well for us in getting the dish completely baked in a timely manner.  The recipe as written left us with really underdone potatoes, especially in the middle.  We also multiplied the gratin sauce ingredients by 1.5.  Without doing that the potatoes were too dry.  With those tweaks we found this to be a really tasty and attractive dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We liked them, but they were a tad bland.  Next time, I would slightly season the slices with salt and pepper and double the sauce.  Still a good recipe with good ingredients.  Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Flavor was fantastic! But any advice for a broken sauce? Anyone else have this issue?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The reason for the broken sauce might be the high fat content of the heavy cream and cheese combined with the high oven temp.  When I made this, instead of doing it as written, I made a roux with butter and flour, whisked in whole milk, then stirred in the cheese.  The roux helps emulsify everything into a smooth creamy sauce.  It's a little bit more work, but the result was beautiful!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Double the gruyere, bake at least 45, until brown and bubbling. Consider covering with foil first. And uncover for last 20-30 min.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Go heavy on the salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving with 4 large Yukon gold potatoes and used half and half. We were not in a hurry so baked the potatoes for 2 hours, At 350 for 90 minutes with 45 minutes covered, and at 400 for the last 30 minutes after adding the remaining cheese.  The tops were golden brown and the bottoms cooked perfectly.  Creamy, cheesy, carby goodness.  The next day reheated the leftovers covered in a 300 degree oven for 30 minutes.  Nice special occasion dish but it takes time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving for the first time this year. I should have read through the comments here first as my husband and I were both disappointed. The dish, as made per the instructions produces a bland half mushy half crispy mass of potato slices. I had high hopes because, potatoes and cheese, right? I will try again with the pinion, paprika, mustard powder, more cheese and herbs suggestions and see if that makes a difference.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We chose to try these as part of our little family Thanksgiving.  They were outstanding and are now on our list of “must haves” for special occasion potatoes and other foods.  This is indeed a very rich dish due to the cream and cheese.   What made it truly outstanding for me was was not only the richness but the subtle way the garlic and thyme impacted the flavor.  I actually enjoyed them the next morning as well as a great little breakfast leftover treat.  All 3 of us recommend this dish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn't have thyme and used dried tarragon instead. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: OMG this was amazing, even haphazardly made.  I didn’t follow the measurements (less cream, more cheese, enough unweighed potatoes to fill the dish), added lots of minced onion, used the chopstick method of Hasselbacking Yukon Gold potatoes, submerged them in the cream & cheese mixture before arranging them in a gratin dish, then baked them for nearly two hours (90 minutes at 400, then 20 minutes at 350).  We all swooned at the results.  Make this.  Often,

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The flavor was amazing, but tried to do first 2 rounds of cooking then put in fridge overnight. Next day had to cook for 1.5 hours and potatoes still never cooked through.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mine split and ended up with pools of fat separated out from potato and dairy solids. Anyone else? Still tasted good, but visually not ideal.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mine split as well... interested in feedback on how to prevent for next time!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes, same.  Such a bummer

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made as the recipe is written but used a cup of cream and a cup of half and half because I ran out of cream. But holy heck it was fantastic! I also used a generous amount of butter in the baking dish and the crust on the bottom was a fabulous cherry on top, so to speak.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I needed much more than an hour at 400, more like 2 hours and I had brussel sprouts and a tenderloin in an oven. I used all my gruyere and grated a small onion as another reviewer suggested. It was good but I didn't love it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: used about 2x cheese and 1/2 & 1/2. 5 thin people ate a ridiculous amount. So much for serving leftover tomorrow...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Disaster.  This had to cook for at least 30 minutes more than called for in the recipe and by the time the potatoes were cooked, the sauce totally separated and it was basically thinly sliced potatoes in oil with a bit of crispy cheese on top :(

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It was a little undercooked, and pobably needed 1.5 hours to cook. I should have tried some before pulling it out, but we were ready to eat, and ate it anyways. :)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made this for Xmas dinner and it was AMAZING. First time I've ever made a gratin that didn't separate! Made a few slight changes... Sliced on the mandolin pretty thing and then cooked for an hour covered with foil then for 30 minutes with the foil off and another 30 covered in cheese. I also used more cheese (how much more, I don't know!) and cooked some leeks in butter and just sort of dotted them into the potatoes once cooked. I also cooked in a pie plate. Leftovers were fantastic! Loved!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for holiday dinner with Prime Rib to rave reviews from the family.  After reading the notes, I doubled the amount of cheese.  Definitely have a tray on the rack below the potatoes to catch sauce that bubbles over.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Disappointingly, this recipe didn't work for me at all. I used 5lbs of russet potatoes but still had a ton of leftover cream mixture. The sauce curdled in the oven and was generally much too greasy, heavy, and unappetizing. I ended up draining out ~1/2C liquid from the dish after cooking, a sad waste of the quality parm and gruyere I splurged on. Next time will try replacing some of the cream with stock, or a different recipe altogether.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe doesn't give the amount of salt which made my first attempt blandly under seasoned.  Second try, I added two teaspoons, channeling my inner Ina.  Huge improvement.  I mean, it is A LOT of potato that needs a lotta oomph.  I also added some garlic powder to double down.  Now, I love this recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Good Christ this was so absolutely delicious.  Creamy, Cheesy, nice browned bits on top and at edges.  I made exactly as written and I wouldn’t change a thing.  I would like to try doubling and baking in a 9 x 13 pan because one recipe, while generously serving 8 as a side, well this was just so delicious we wanted more.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a hit! Made half the amount for fewer servings but kept the original level of garlic and thyme. Added extra salt and cheese thanks to other reviewers and my own preferences. The Russets worked well for me but curious to try Yukon. The crispness is perfect. Looking forward to making this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yukons are starchier than Russets.  I like reds, myself, as they seem to be a nice in-between.  I use Russets for baking--they are meaty and fluffy.  I use reds for mash and Yukons when I want a bit of golden color.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Homerun dish. Did I say homerun? I meant grand slam! I followed the directions, no substitutions and it was great.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We really liked this recipe- flavor and texture were spot on. The creamy potatoes from the bottom half mixed with the crispy potatoes on the top creates a delicious bite. You definitely need to add salt and we felt it could use a bit more cheese in the potato mixture- just our personal opinion. Happy to have a new potato side dish for upcoming holiday meals.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made this with sweet potatoes instead of russets, and it was absolutely amazing. Very delicious (both savory and sweet), and exactly as you promised: creamy potato and crusty cheesy edge in every bite. It was transcendent.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Awesome! I don't like surgery sweet potatoes and normally just mash with butter but wanted something different for Tday so you're not serving 2 kinds of mash. Definitely going to do this one and cook up to last bake then hold until we heat up the sides. Thanks!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was wonderful! I did sweet potatoes on one side and russets on the other and it was fabulous for the adults as well as toddlers

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
These were delicious. My experience — 
Definitely buy extra potatoes. My two-quart dish required 5 pounds.
By the time I got all the potatoes in the dish, the liquid was already halfway up the side and there was very little left.
The potatoes were browned by the end of the second 30 mins. I added the cheese and a bit of the leftover sauce to keep them from burning.
The potatoes were done after one hour and 15 mins.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Outstanding. Be sure to salt generously; potatoes take so much salt. I tasted the cream/cheese mixture for salt and salted the top before it went in. Those who said it came out bland surely underseasoned it; remember how delicious dished with nothing but potatoes, salt and oil are, as long as they are seasoned properly. I halved the recipe, used a 1 quart dish, and it was done at about 1:10.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Does anyone know if this dish can be assembled a day ahead of baking? Is there anything about the potato sitting in the milk/cheese overnight in the fridge that will alter its composition? I'd like to get this labor out ahead of thanksgiving. Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It's fine assembled the day before.  Its going to be best cooked the day you want to eat it though.  The solids and grease can separate a bit upon reheating.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This has been my go-to Thanksgiving potato dish for a few years and its always a star at the table.  Feel free to up the garlic and herbs and cheese since potatoes can act like bland sponges.  Usually I mix the cream, herbs, cheese and garlic and let it sit at room temp to allow the flavors to infuse the liquid before tossing the potatoes in it and stacking them in the casserole.   Additional cheeses add even more depth of flavor.  Smoked or aged Gouda are particularly nice.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I read in previous notes that others have made this ahead of time and finished just before serving.
Preparing and bake for the first 2 30 minute baking periods, then chill in the fridge until ready to bake for the last 30 minutes covered with foil (first commenter said she went with 40 minutes at the end instead of 30 and worked out great.) Remove the foil covering with 20 minutes to go.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It looks impressive out of the oven, but I found it bland and disappointing, even though I added paprika and dijon mustard, as suggested by other readers. You can’t really taste the cheese. And the cheese gets oily and clumpy and just sits at the bottom. What’s the point of splurging on gruyere and parmigiano reggiano if you can’t taste them? Not worth the effort or expense. Sticking with mashed potatoes next Thanksgiving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a beautiful contribution to a friend's meal. A Japanese mandoline made quick, beautiful slices. I baked for the first 60 minutes, then cooled and popped in the fridge overnight.  Baked the next day covered for 20 minutes, then added the cheese for 20 more. Don't skimp on the salt!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved the concept, but alas, the sauce broke. I thought I bought heavy cream, but got heavy whipping cream. While the dairies say there is no difference in fat content, we had crispy potatoes swimming in an oily sauce. Any thoughts on what went wrong?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tasty but if I made it again I would add onion and garlic for extra flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: They were a hit! Equally divine and easy to make. Definitely place casserole on a rimmed baking sheet lined with foil. A 9x13 dish required 8 large potatoes and was at least double what 4 people could eat in a sitting. The garlic smelled very intense while cooking, but was not overpowering. I intend to try again adding grated onion, a pinch of mustard powder, and double the cheese (unless I halve the potatoes with a smaller dish). If you hand cut the potatoes thicker, bake 40-45 min covered.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yukon golds, cut hasselback style so potatoes still intact. Challenging to get the cream/cheese mixture between the slices, but it all worked out. Followed directions for the first 2 30-minutes bakes, let cool and refrigerated overnight, then cooked for about an hour next day to make sure potatoes were done. Delicious! Two notes: the hasselback cuts made them look slightly insectile/crustacean once cooked; don't make a sharp turn when driving to a Friendsgiving- melted gruyere cream all over!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These potatoes were very yummy and used all of the liquid I had.  I highly recommend putting your container on a rimmed baking sheet in the oven.  All of the oozy/cheesy goodness bubbled over the edge of the pan into the baking sheet.  A lot easier to clean the baking sheet than the oven!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I doubled this recipe and failed to double the time - it took two hours of cooking in a four quart pan for this dish to be completed. It was excellent but we ate it the next day due to the extended cooking time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Increase cooking time to 45’, 45’, and keep original 30’

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I cooked this first time tonight. It’s fabulous, absolutely fab!!

I used tow one-quart casserole dishes. Baking the first 30 minutes at 400F (accurately monitored with a digital thermometer) was fine, but I would do the 2nd and 3rd 30 minute stages at 375.

Also, I used 3 cups of cream and cheese mixture. Turned out great! Watch the last 30 minutes of the bake. Might want to take it out 5 or 10 minutes early to prevent burning.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We have made the recipe from NYT on to occasions and it just misses. The potatoes are always slightly underdone, but still very crispy on top. I have discovered that the original recipe from Kenji calls for a whole pound LESS than the NYT adapted recipe. I will give this recipe one more try, but use Kenji's recipe. https://www.seriouseats.com/hasselback-potato-gratin-casserole-holiday-food-lab

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: After the first 30 minutes, I had a very creamy consistency. Once I removed foil and cooked for the next 30 minutes, I noticed it was starting to look “oily”. From my understand the temp became too hot in my oven and cause the cream to separate. At this point my potatoes were cooked all the way through, so I added more cream to try and save the sauce. The taste was there just not the texture. Next time I would probably lower the heat after the first 30 mins and monitor more

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was so excited to make this and it looked beautiful! However, it was very bland. There was plenty of liquid when cooking and I carefully coated every piece. I did add some time to cooking so the top would be browned. Any suggestions from you seasoned chefs ? Need to redeem myself with my Dad who is an excellent cook lol.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Garlic, thyme, salt/pepper and 2 kinds of cheese... and you found it bland? What did you leave out?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This takes an awful lot of time in the oven, but the results were fantastic. The crispy/cooked contrast was very interesting, and although the crispness was lost when I froze a few rations in the freezer, the flavour was almost better the second time around. There was literally no conversation around the table as my guests licked the plates clean.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've made this twice with a doubled recipe.  I've had it come out undercooked both times.  For shot #3, I'm going to make the first cooking increment an hour instead of 30 minutes (1 hr covered, 30 min uncovered, 30 mi with cheese on top).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sweet Potatoes terrific and consider a layer of leek slices as a the bottom layer

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Thanks for a saver recipe. 
I agree about using your “food processor” slice blade for this repetitive prep and firming the potatoes in the fridge prior for a cleaner cut. 
I BlueBinned my mandoline 4 months ago with my dear wife’s blessing after my third blade nick in 2 years. Even with the toothy presser embedded well in the goods there were those moments of “trip” with near disastrous results. I need all those fingertips to play guitar…..

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I need to make this a week ahead. Where would you break to freeze it? Before baking?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Sub half & half
More cheese
Grated onion in milk mixture

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be made in advance? Ideally, the day before? Would love to know as it looks so delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can save time doing the first 2 steps in a microwave oven, and forget about aluminium foil, 40 minutes will do, just use the oven for the last grill part.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be done ahead and reheated?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes I’m sure it would reheat fine. You could also do the first two 30 minutes in the oven, let it cool and refrigerate over night and do the final 30 minutes the next day (maybe add an extra 15-20 minutes)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Nice combo of a gratin and the original Hasselback potato. The latter was created at restaurant Hasselbacken in 1953. A restaurant with that name has been on the location on the outskirts of Stockholm since the 1760-ties. Still has good food.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you screw this up and mash the potatoes, it is still delicious. Trust me. Guess how we know.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this in 2021 and the sauce curdled. It still tasted great but wondering what went wrong. Followed the recipe as written. Mom thought temp might be too high (and mom is a great cook; me, daughter…decent cook but not passionate about it)…….so I follow recipes to a T. Any thoughts?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a big hit and didn't require much effort at all. Five stars. I will say the potatoes do need quite a bit of salt. When the recipes says "season generously with salt and pepper" I did about 3-4 large pinches, and separated the potatoes a bit to let the sale penetrate.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Used russets, 5 cloves garlic, rounded tablespoon of fine salt with cream, 6 oz gruyere, and wooden spoon method for slicing.  So I mostly followed the recipe, using all other ingredients. Baked in a 9x13 pan.  Everyone was very happy.
I make a very similar recipe with Yukon which is delish as well.  Honestly, when you put heavy cream and cheese on potatoes I don't think there's a right or wrong.  Next time, I think I'll add some rosemary.  I didn't taste the thyme very much.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this thanksgiving 2022 but put in 8x8
Square and it wasn’t enough room and it burned. Also it’s important to split up the cheese. The cheese needs to get in between the slices before stacking.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Something was definitely missing. Potatoes were unevenly cooked and BLAND. Grease (from the sauce or the buttered dish?) was floating on top. A couple of things I may have messed up: used a small convection oven (adjusting -25 degrees per convection roasting). Also, I peeled and sliced the potatoes ahead of time and - as per Emily's albeit begrudging make-ahead suggestion - kept in water to prevent discoloration. This may have robbed the potatoes of their starch. I will try again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: There are lots of notes here so someone probably already said this, but we used the thinnest spiralizer head on the KitchenAid attachment to slice the potatoes (and save fingers). It took a bit to get the hang of how to re-wind them into potato shapes again, but once we got the pattern, it was super easy to get them stacked correctly looking like whole potatoes. I used the potato "cores" in the veggies under the spatchcocked turkey (per Kenji Alt-Lopez) and they were a great addition there!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was totally attracted to this recipe, but had I read more of the reviews, I would have cooked longer as well. My potatoes were definitely not cooked, needed at least another half an hour, but two hours of cooking ??? Will try again but with more heat and more time!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thanks to the reviewers who suggested longer cooking times. I use sweets and russets and I do 45, 45, and 30. This year I added purple sweets. Of course they turned the cream sauce purple when coating prior to assembling in the dish. I recommend coating your purples last to minimize this. I was worried I’d still have purple cream in spots, a la Bridget Jones’ blue soup, but it worked out - the potatoes absorb the cream in the last 30 mins. Final dish looked stunning; rave reviews, now a staple.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this with extra sharp cheddar cheese that I needed to use up and it came out delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used half and half instead of cream. Plenty rich.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Much like many NYT cooking recipes,  I found the cooking time way under what was needed. Every oven is different but I found first cook covered more like an hour, second part good at 45 minutes and final cheese addition more like 15 minutes. I would make these again with adjusted cooking time to get creamy below and crispy up top.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You might want to get an oven thermometer.  Once I got a thermometer, I found that my oven preheats extremely slowly and is often actually 100 degrees below what it reads on the external control panel.  It's a terrible oven but, unfortunately, I can't replace it right now.  But oven thermometers are an essential kitchen tool, I've found.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe made me realize what a bad cook I am. I thought I made it according to the recipe but the result was underwhelming. Top was crispy, dry and almost burnt. The inside was inconsistent - some parts were undercooked and generally pretty flavorless. No one ate it and I ended up throwing most of it away. Wish I knew what I did wrong.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Such a good dish. It was such a hit and it's great as leftovers the next day. I nearly doubled the recipe and doubled the cheese and everyone was happy about that.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I carefully followed this recipe and the potatoes remained VERY firm.  According to some sources there may have been too much fat and not enough fluid in the recipe.  Has anyone else had this problem?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
4 lbs is a lot of potatoes for me. So for those who are looking to portion control, I hope this is helpful.

I appreciate how versatile this dish is. I cut the recipe to 1/4 and aligned the potatoes along my muffin pan. Created 6 servings. Did a little bit of substituting with cheese, seasoning, herbs that I had on hand.

Cooked at 400 degree covered for 30 min. Uncover and sprinkled a bit of cheese and crispy jalapeño pieces. And cooked at 425 for 45 min.

I’m excited for the results.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious, and super simple, crunchy, buttery rich - I used cheeses I had on hand, Blue, Gruyère and Parm. Instead of buttering dish I used bacon fat, and added half a diced onion to cheese mix. Sprinkled with smoked paparica. SO good! Had it with the BEST Kielbasa, will eat with bacon and eggs for breakfast.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you have a spiralizer use it for the potatoes!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed the recipe as written to bummer results: bland flavor, undercooked potato slices, and a singed surface. All modifiable, but I'd probably be disinclined to try this dish again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made with about 3.5lbs Yukon gold instead of russet and put in a deep 9” pie dish…I don’t know if it was my oven messing up, but after the full 1.5hr cook time the potatoes still had a raw crunch…not sure where it went wrong, but the flavor was still great

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Russets would not have been crunch after 1.5 hr. It's always a good idea to follow the recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love this but it is truly hands on to monitor. Also, I mix the potatoes and sauce in a large sheet pan with my hands.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I want to make this for Christmas dinner...I have no mandoline.  I have fairly decent knives and a good eye.  Will take forever to cut 1/8" thick.  Can I use the slice attachment on cuisinart...I know it will ruin the appearance.  Also can I just 'eye' the thicknesses and adjust the time somehow in the oven?  Please help if you can and thank you!  I love our recipes and the responses of fellow NYT cooks.  CH

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wish I could post a pic. So beautiful and the fragrance OMG. Only change I made based on the recommendations was sub 1/2 cup of cream for sherry and added 1 shallot sliced with the uconn golds. ( oh spell check did not catch my Alma matar!!!) This is amazing

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can anyone explain why the cheese/cream "broke" and the dish got oily? I followed the recipe exactly...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made these last night exactly as the recipe is written but halved it as it's just the two of us. I wouldn't change a thing. This is a keeper!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Help!  If using sweet potatoes instead, do I need to adjust cook time??

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The middle 30 minute section was more like 60 minutes for me.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was incredibly labor-intensive and payoff was not even close to worth it. Some of us need our hands held a little when it comes to seasoning so at least giving a place to start on amount of salt and pepper would have been great. This looked impressive but there wasn't even close to the right amount of cheese (too little) and it was almost flavorless which was sad because we splurged on a block of fancy cheese for it. Also, I definitely recommend (carefully) using a mandoline.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: with gratins its really important to taste the cream mixture before, it should be borderline salty. Close to tablespoon would be my guess.  I eyed it and tasted it but thats really key for flavor..

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was super tasty!  Used Yukon golds (skin on) and doubled the cheese per reviewers comments.   Those were great adjustments.  I had a combination of dairies on hand so used equal amounts of  heavy cream,  half and half and  buttermilk.  Tasted great but next time will err on the side of the creamier liquids and skip half and half.  Good in a pinch but the consistency wasn't as rich.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I really wanted to like these. Following the suggestion of another reader, I made these through the first two 30 minutes bakes as oven space was tight on Thanksgiving day. I brought them out well in advance, let them come back to room temp and returned them to the oven, with the expectation that it would take longer since they were no longer piping hot. They never got piping hot. They never cooked through, even after a return trip the oven. For over 2hrs. Seriously disappointed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: delicious... for me it's more about the learning; vertical stacked potatoes add texture to every bite, I would have never known. Certainly appreciate the measures ( fill the casserole half way ... 400 F, cover for 30, back uncovered for 30+). I rarely have all ingredients on hand in the right proportions yet certainly appreciate the  guidance on how to achieve the  intended flavor profile

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Golden? Yes. Glorious? Not really. Although this was tasty and looked nice--it came out exactly as advertised--from a flavor standpoint I found it more show than go: It tasted of potatoes and cream. The Maillard reaction browning the potatoes didn't transcend what is essentially a bland dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Fun to make- Used Yukon gold potatoes, 6 oz Gruyère and sautéed leeks, 3 tsp kosher salt, 1 tsp paprika, half & half instead of heavy cream. Good flavor but despite an extra 10 minutes, still needed more time. Next time I will plan on 2 hours cooking time but begin checking for doneness at 1.5 hr.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this last night its SO delicious.  Besides the peeling and slicing of the potatoes its actually a pretty easy dish to make.  Ran out of russets in the middle section and added a sweet potato to cream and those were great too.Paired with a salad and we literally said every 3 bites this is insane, and we cook a lot.  Bravo, great recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a terrific dish but a few tips on my 4th year: add shallot/leek, add a spoon of Dijon, different peppers, use a safety glove for the mandolin and allow 2 hours to cook properly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cooked this as a trial before Christmas, what a disgusting oily mess. So glad I tried it ahead of time. Never again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For those that have a Cuisinart, we got beautifully sliced potatoes using the slicer insert and feeding the whole potato in.  We used Yukon gold and they fit pretty well through the tube.  We don't have a mandolin and had never used this Cuisinart attachment till now and it was clutch.  We loved this recipe and will make again.  Will also make a 2nd one with sweet potatoes based on other reviews.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
We have made this twice now. We find that 3 and 1/2 pounds of potatoes work for us in a 8x8” square dish. 

We also subbed fontina and Romano when we didn’t have gruyere the second time and it’s delicious. We also used basil and that was a great substitute as well. 

We also had to add about 10 minutes to the cooking time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cooked this following the directions and added more potatoes to make sure dish was tightly packed. Cooking 1 1/2 hours resulted in a very tasty but dry dish. Next time I’ll make sure to add more liquid with the extra spuds.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made as written, so successful. If you are considering making this - fyi it is so much less fussy, and quicker, than it seems! The recipe is just very detailed (which is great) but this is not a time suck at all.  Make it!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: More salt than you think

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Horrible recipe.  Tried it twice as directed, watery soup each time.  Potato leek gratin is a much better recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The photo looks nothing like the finished product.  Use an immersion blender & maybe you’d get a passable soup.  You’d need to add a lot of flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I tried to lighten this dish using whole milk and 1/2 and 1/2. Don't do it! It simply did not cook properly, did not crisp up as nicely, and definitely did not turn out creamy and good. I made it a second time with all the heavy cream, and it was fantastic. YMMV, but do the cream on this one! :)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very good, but a lot of slow labor in addition to the long cook time. The slicing takes practice. It's hard to get the melted butter to go between all those slices.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The only flourish I add to this deliciously naughty dish, is a light sprinkle of fresh lemon thyme.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have a similar gratin recipe, no cheese with horseradish cream and it called for parboiling the potatoes(1/4 sliced turnip) in the cream. I’m going for a mixture of Yukon, sweet and turnip. Par cook in cream. And cheese and pour over the potatoes Haselbeck style. The beauty is the potatoes are almost completed cooked.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Came out great. Was a huge hit. Added some thinly sliced leeks beteween some of the potatoes for a little extra flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made it a couple times. Time consuming but with the effort. It is showy and delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made two batches for Easter, one using Idaho russets, and the other using Yukon gold. Both were successful, but I preferred the Yukon golds which had creamier texture and a little more flavor. Both batches were baked a good 30 minutes longer than suggested to make sure the potatoes were completely tender. It’s a keeper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this recipe for a family dinner and it was a big hit. The cream sauce is delicious and brings a lot of flavor to the dish. It was not as time consuming as i expected, especially using a mandolin to slice the potatoes. Might try Yukon gold next time to see how the flavor differs, but russets worked very well. My crust did not look as perfect as the recipe photo but there was a nice crisp to the top of the potatoes which is the goal of stacking the potatoes in this direction.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love this as does everyone at table.  Several times.  Another cook said to do first two heats, then you can chill, bake and remove foil half way thru for 40 min, genius and opens up my oven

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a big favorite in my house. I have used storebought grated cheddar and colby jack instead of gruyere and it is divine. I also ran out of heavy cream this time and subbed in half a cup of whole milk. Worked out great! Probably couldn't sub out all the heavy cream, but it's nice to have the flexibility when I'm feeling spontaneous.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and ended up with waaaay more cheesy heavy cream than I needed. But that shouldn’t matter I suppose should it… I’m all for  100 butter and 10x fat but that just feels a smidge wasteful. Otherwise 10/10 stars on the 5-star scale. Nop totch recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving. Doubled the cheese, added 4 tsp of Dijon mustard and a lot of paprika and pepper. No garlic. Didn’t have any thyme but, had a lot of fresh oregano from my garden so, used oregano instead. Stuck with the Russet potatoes. Other than that followed the directions. It was a big hit!! Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delicious! Used gold potatoes, 1/2 cream 1/2 half and half (what I had on hand) did separate a bit - but no one cared! Was great side dish for Thanksgiving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not sure what went wrong here. Followed the recipe as written and it took almost 4 hours to cook. When they were finally done, very little to no flavor. Skipping this recipe next time and doing old school scalloped!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Use Yukon Gold and don't peel

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make this recipe on Thanksgiving Dinner every year. It always come out nicely. I’ve been adding a pinch of mustard powder and dash of anchovy juice/essence that I purchased from an Italian Grocery Store and it gives a depth of flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We make these potatoes every year for Christmas. It's messy and requires 4 hands to stack the potatoes but the result is impressive and tasty. I always have extra potatoes on hand to slice last minute if needed because we never seem to have enough and the whole thing won't hold together if you don't have your dish crammed completely full.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not sure why this recipe was such a fail for me. This gratin was so greasy. Potatoes just swimming in cream and cheese. I followed the recipe step by step. I packed the potatoes into my dish and poured the cheese/cream mixture half way up the dish. Throwing this recipe out.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The results were underwhelming and not worth the effort.  We made the mistake of using a pan larger than 11x13 since we doubled the recipe and the potatoes failed to cook.  The seasoning was very mild and the cheese sauce was not cheesy enough.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: When you multiply a recipe like this and use a larger pan you usually have to increase the cooking time also. I often multiply a standard-sized recipe to serve 50 and find this holds true.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made it as written using my new mandoline and it was wonderful. Not an everyday dish but a fancy looking and tasty. I might add some leeks next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this twice exactly as written and both times I ended up with a casserole dish full of oil. The cream had split. It tasted delicious both times, but it is definitely no showstopper and I was embarrassed to bring it to a potluck.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this almost exactly as written but using only half the amount of red pepper flakes based on the comments below & it was still pretty "warm" for me. Next time I might add more cheese to the top for the final bake. It really is absolutely delicious--and it would be substantial enough to have as a main course with a nice green salad. I ended up using only about 3.75 lbs of potatoes in my 2qt dish and it fit nicely. It finished cooking 20 minutes earlier than the recipe states.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was cooking for 16 people, so I made one with sweet potatoes and one with Yukon Gold. There were only leftovers of the sweet potato, but I thought it was the better one. 3 teaspoons of salt was just right.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Was cooking time for sweet potatoes the same?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Really popular with the guests on two occasions now. For me, it’s needed a solid hour or more extra of baking to soften the potatoes sufficiently

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Any tips for taking this to a dinner? By the time I arrive, the potatoes will be cold. Rather than reheating them in the oven, I’m wondering if I should save the final 30 min bake for when I’m at my friends house?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: that would seem to be a no-brainer "yes".....

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Beth Kenji’s video for this recipe says you can bake 1st two stages ahead and then do final bake just before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I found that the amount of liquid in the recipe produced too much liquid in the casserole dish. I used a basting syringe to remove much of it after an hour.

I also think the cooking times are far too long.

Otherwise it was very good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Read other comments and learn that some readers found that the cooking times didn't properly cook the potatoes. Remember that the potatoes and equipment affect cooking time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have been making a version of this recipe for a number of years for Christmas dinner. I use white cheddar and parm. This recipe is so easy, and it tastes good. My family really enjoys it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: As long as you scrub the potatoes clean, you don't need to peel them. Potato skins are delicious as they bake and crisp up

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Does anyone know the casserole dish being used? I love it!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I make this and freeze it for dinner next week?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I use a food processor instead of a mandoline to slice the potatoes?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a total hit at the office Thanksgiving potluck — I won the prize for Best Side Dish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a huge hit at Friendsgiving! I didn't read the recipe in advance and only had a little over an hour to cook it, so I followed the first 2 steps in the stove (30 mins covered, 30 mins uncovered), and then finished under the broiler until some of the tops of the potato slices were burnt. It saved a few minutes time and the burnt pieces were beautifully dramatic!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not as creamy, but I think you could get away with using whole milk instead of cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This sounds and looks amazing. And as someone that makes both of these, mixing them is brilliant….
And I will make sure to have a child cut them for me! lol…. That comment made my wife and my day!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did not work for me at all here in Germany. I'm guessing the type of potato was too different / didn't get the right ones. It turned out undercooked (and not lovely-gooey) at the bottom, almost burnt (but not in a good way) at the top. The cheese didn't come through at all. Also my experiment of leaving half of the potatoes unpeeled was not a success, to put it mildly ;)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Methinks most of these positive reviews are from Gérard Depardieu. Foie gras for breakfast and milkshakes to swing back meds. It's too much. Too much cream and cheese. Also, the slicing and arranging gave me an arm ache. I tasted it and it wasn't mind blowing - not worth calories or effort. Same flavor profile but much easier - pan fry the potatoes with some butter, then toss some gruyere and thyme at the end. Done.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A general comment on the NYTimes cooking recipes, to which I've subscribed for many years. I work in metric and find it really inconvenient to constantly have to convert all the measurements. Could NYTimes please provide metric measurements as well for all the subscribers who have not grown up with imperial measurements?? Please???

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What a complete disappointment after reading all the rave reviews. I followed the recipe exactly and after taking it out of the oven, instead of a creamy mixture in the potatoes it was just a pool of grease. I made these potatoes because of the thousands of five star reviews instead of regular mashed potatoes for Thanksgiving. Won’t make that mistake again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This looked fabulous. My guests all said, "What is THAT?" upon seeing it. It is quite impressive visually. I wish I had left it in the oven a bit longer though because the potatoes were undercooked. Still edible but slightly underdone, even though the potatoes were sliced to the specified thickness. I will make again for Thanksgiving next year but keep it in the oven for at least another 15 minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made this twice, and after the first time, we cut down on the cream b/c the first version was too rich. The second time, we used a random assortment of shredded cheese and about half of the recommended cream and it turned out great.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made these for Thanksgiving; sadly they didn’t work. I used Yukon Gold as others did, but the potato slices were giving off a lot of water- not sure why. I spread them out and dried them in tea towels as best I could. I doubled recipe and added extra cheese as shared. However, there was far too much cream mixture. I baked as directed, a total of 90 minutes, but they were still on the hard side. Plus the cream and cheese didn’t meld. I think the potatoes were still too wet.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I thought these would be cute in individual gratin ramekins for thanksgiving, but it was NOT worth the extra effort. My main complaint is that the cream used a lot of cheese that just melted in without giving us enough of the cheesey scalloped potato flavor we wanted. The crispy top was just okay… Will make something more standard next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The juice wasn't worth the squeeze for me. These were too bland, although my family really liked the fresh thyme. I probably wouldn't make these again. I ended up adding a lot more cheese during the bake.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just not enough of anything.  Not flavorful enough, not cheesy enough, and not crispy enough.  It was an unremarkable mush.  I followed the recipe to the letter, too.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A bit of guidance on the amount of salt and pepper would have been nice. I didn’t use nearly enough and it’s pretty bland

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How can you store the leftovers and how to reheat?  Thank you!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These potatoes are unbelievable. A feast for the eyes and the palate!  I’m not an accomplished cook by any means, so my mistake was actually not adding enough salt. I think I imagined the cheese would be salty enough so I held back, but it was not the case. I had to ask my husband (who actually is a chef) to slice the potatoes on the mandolin to avoid leaving a finger in the potato mix, so if I was alone I’m not sure how this would have gone!  Regardless, it’s absolutely fantastic and I will definitely make again, but next time with more salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this and was very disappointed. I followed the directions and used a mandolin to slice the potatoes to the directed 1/8". That was too thick, and looking at the photo, I wish I had gone with the thinner slicing on my mandolin. There is no way the potatoes in the photo were 1/8" thick. Also I wish I had used a dish with higher sides, since it bubbled over a lot. Will try again with these adjustments.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for thanksgiving and it was a huge success!! It’s very easy to make. I had leftover heavy cream, so made it again a week later in small batch….again amazing results!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I make this ahead of time amd freeze? If yes, how do I reheat (at what temperature and how long)? Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @jenn Potatoes change texture when frozen, and not in a good way.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not knowing what is a good amount of salt meant that this little project baked up a little bland.   I think more information about over salting the milk/cheese mixture would jangda made this perfect

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’m already serving creamed spinach and am looking for a potato casserole that’s less cheesy. I saw a similar epicurious recipe that just uses butter and chicken stock. Thoughts?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Warning...if you double this recipe, as some have suggested for a crowd, prepare for a substantially longer cook time. At least in my oven at a verified 400F temperature this needs in the ballpark of 2 hours, sometimes more.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Halved the recipe and followed instructions as written. Wow! Delish! While eating these potatoes we decided this will now be the dish we bring to friend gatherings and make for birthdays. Thank you Kenji!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I guess that I’m the odd man out. I made a double recipe exactly as described, adding only a little milk to thin the sauce a bit.

The only problem was that the guests attacked it as if it were a pile of gold (not Yukon), and might have consumed dome of the enamel from the casserole dish.

I used a mandolin to slice the potatoes, and it went quickly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mandolin was the way to go! Perfect, albeit a little dangerous, tool for even slicing of potatoes. This worked out great for my Christmas Eve dinner - thumbs up!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love this recipe! I was tired of the same old garlic mashed potatoes and, while au gratin was on my mind, I saw this recipe and boy was it great. I did have one little unintended switch though. I grow and dry herbs for cooking and, what I thought was my thyme, used dried basil. It was an acceptable swap and worked out well. I love stacking the potatoes instead of layering. It makes for a much easier serving and looks great on a plate! Didn't hurt that it was delicious. Recommend!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This looks beautiful but it was not good. This is the only NYT recipe I’ve made that just didn’t work. I recommend not cooking it. Even after baking for much longer then recommended it was never fully cooked

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: my experience exactly, didn't cook through despite cooking way way longer than written

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Oh man I make this all the time. Especially for special occasions because it's easy to scale up or down. And I'll tell you a dirty secret... I don't always use the fancy gruyere cheese. Sometimes (especially if I decide to make this on the spur of the moment) I just use the preshredded stuff I keep around for making quesadillas at 3am. And it's honestly still really REALLY good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: To J Hoppe - In the U.S., all what is called yams are actually sweet potatoes. You need to specify what variety you used (Hannah, Garnet, Jewel, Beauregard, Satsuma-Imo, etc..). Yams are starchy tuber grown in Africa or Asia and are from a totally different family. Sweet potatoes are grown in the Americas and some are called yams (often if they are orange), but truly, they are sweet potatoes. I assume you used Hannahs (white kind, and quite dense, so needing almost as much cooking as potatoes).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What a beautiful dish - quality potatoes are the key. This is very easy and quick. Ends up crispy, flavorful, and packs a punch with the cheese choice that’s not offensive. We’re going to try sweet potatoes next and maybe pair them with another type of cheese or just make as written - am sure it will turn out fantastic. A keeper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just made this for Easter, one pan russet with skin on and one pan of sweet potato.  I highly recommend the sweet tator too.  Very good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
So delicious! I added one grated sweet yellow onion and 3oz of grated white cheddar cheese. 1 heaping tbsp of Italian dried herb. I also just dumped it into a deep 8x8 casserole dish. And baked for an additional 20 minutes. 

This can be made ahead of time! I sliced the potatoes and make the creamy cheese sauce last night, dumped it into the baking dish and baked it the following night for dinner.. it was not gummy or anything like that. It was perfect.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is my FAVORITE way to prepare potatoes. What a great dream!  I just served this to my family with chicken paprikash (instead of noodles) and they raved. Bravo, sir!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this as written to bring to a potluck dinner. It was a huge hit. I have since bought a rotary cheese grinder so I don’t have to manually shred the cheese next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’ve always sprinkled in some small cubes of ham during the layering process before baking. I highly recommend!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This dish was a hit and I’ll be making it again and again!  Thank you to those who provided salt guidance - I used 4.5-5 lbs of Yukon gold potatoes and 2.5 tsp diamond crystal kosher salt + .5 tsp black pepper and it was perfectly seasoned.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Excellent. Made as written except, I used salted butter. 
I made and cooked this 4 hours in advance and reheated before serving. Nothing whatsoever leftover.  The last crispy fragments on the edge were all eaten up!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Does anyone have make-ahead tips? I would love to have one prepared in advance in the fridge.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Because raw potatoes discolor rapidly, I think the only thing you could do ahead of time is assemble the cream sauce, which doesn't save much time.

However (this is just a slightly-educated guess), I bet you could follow the recipe through the second 30-minute bout in the oven, then stop. At this point you could take it out, cover, and store.

To finish, the called for final 30 minutes won't cut it, I don't think, since it will have to be brought back up to temperature, so add some extra time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Slice potatoes and place in a bowl of water with 1/4 cup white vinegar added. Make sure the potatoes are completely covered. Cover with plastic wrap and refrigerate.  Remove bowl from fridge an hour before assembling. Drain, rinse and pat dry potatoes before assembling.  I've used this method and potatoes were fine overnight. Just remember they are really cold and need that warmup time! Can't live without my mandolins slicer, either. Worth every penny and drawer space!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
In one reply NYT says best made day of.  I’d do all prep first thing, a very short soak in acidified water to discourage oxidation but not wash off needed potato startches and proceed through step 2, make sure to bring to room temp before baking and maybe add 15 more minutes.  

Add any unused cream before final baking.  That will be my strategy this Thanksgiving.  I added a compilation of all the tips I plan to use so I hope this post works.  (I had written a long reply and it disappeared!).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great potato texture and browning, but we wanted more cheese and general punch. I will try this technique for the assembly and baking, but probably use a Julia Child sauce recipe next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just put this in the oven. So many emotions. I bought a mandoline for this!?!? Well, we'll see how it goes. Which Julia Child sauce would you use? How about for punch?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cayenne! in moderation

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm planning to harvest the last of my Jerusalem artichokes Thanksgiving morning and thought I'd use them in this recipe instead of potatoes. I am also thinking about substituting plain almond milk for the cream. Any thoughts?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Jerusalem artichokes don't have enough starch to use them alone. Possibly you could put some slices in between the potato slices, which have enough starch to make up for it. Almond milk doesn't sound appetizing. Try cutting the cream with some broth instead, as another commenter suggested.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: LB, off topic but I'd love to know what your favorite recipe for Jerusalem artichokes is?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone tried making this ahead? I know it's not ideal, but I'd love to hear anything people have done ahead of time!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Lot’s have....most through step 2 though.  NYT says better day of.  I plan to make early day of, through step 2 after brief rinse in acidified water.  Add extra liquid if necessary and don’t crowd pan too much.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I made one the night last as a sample for my family, am adding a bit more cheese tomorrow, but as someone who does not cook a lot, it turned out well.
Fingers crossed I can do it twice. It did take a bit of time with cutting all the potatoes, but seemed worth it!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Really, this only serves 6? sounds like it would be more like 8 - 10. Any answers or experience?

Thanks,

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I would say it serves 6, but it depends on what else you are serving. I made a double batch in an 11x13 pan and it was about 12 one-cup servings. My guests fell on it like starving wolves and ate every scrap, so you should probably err on the side of too much rather than too little. It is THAT delicious.

Since my dish was rectangular I put the slices in rows. There was a gap along the edge of the pan, so I pushed the rows together more tightly to make room for another full row.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made a single batch for four people, and we ate about half as a side dish. Eight servings is completely reasonable.

author: Emily Weinstein
