commentBody: Wonderful! I used Yukon Gold potatoes as others have suggested. I also only used 3.5 lbs of potatoes. A major hit at our dinner party.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made it with Eastern potatoes sliced in the food processor and rosemary instead of thyme and it worked great. Keeping the potatoes on water with a little lemon juice or plain white vinegar until ready to assemble will keep them from browning. Will try a vegan version with a good plain creamer and some brewer's yeast mixed in. No idea how it will come out, but if it's a success, I'll let you all know.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Can this be baked the day before, and then re-heated?
It looks delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made it through the second baking round the day before a party.  Took it out of the fridge in the morning and finished baking before we left for the hosts' home, then re-warmed it in their oven before dining.  Worked great.  Just don't cover it with plastic after it's finished: use a towel.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We were disappointed.  It was nice and crusty, but even with extra cheese it was somewhat bland.  Needs lots of added salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
In a traditional Hasselback potatoe dish the potatoe is cut to but not
through leaving it very capable to stand on its own. Slices of cheese
are inserted between the thin cuts. This would work well for this dish
also.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: After 2 helpings I'm in a potato and cream coma... What a fabulous dish. I didn't pack the dish as tight as instructed and maybe used a too much cream. Took a little over an hour to cook. Diffently  a Yukon potato dish! It's a keeper!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This came out wonderfully - very popular with the guests. We didn't have quite the crisp/crunch that is evident in the photo here but still a hit. I made the recipe exactly as described - but doubled the volume for a huge Thanksgiving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cooked it as described in the recipe, using Comte as that's what I had. It looked exactly like the picture above. Sadly, it was virtually tasteless. Such a disappointment as it looked beautiful! Luckily I also made mashed potatoes. If I make it again, I would use Gruyere and I would use Yukon Gold potatoes to see if that would amp up the taste. I didn't cook the turkey but wondered how I would have timed everything with only one oven. And yes, a mandolin is essential for this recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This makes a serviceable potato gratin, but honestly, it's not worth the effort, as little separates it from a regular potato gratin than the angle of the potato slices. The top browns, but it doesn't offer the crispy-cheesy potato nirvana that the picture promises. Grease from the fat in the cream and cheese pools everywhere. And the flavor is nothing special.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These did not come out especially tasty or impressive. A friend who made them said the same thing. They were better reheated as a leftover, adding a little milk to moisten them.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Stacking the potatoes together tightly was a big mistake. They were in the oven for over 2 hours And never cooked through!
Brought them to an Easter dinner, and had to throw the whole mess out...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Wow, is a great starter. Used only 3 pounds of russets for a smaller dish.
Followed rest of the recipe to a T. Came out fantastic! Can't wait to make for the rest of the family.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made classic Hasselback spuds as my husband grew up with them in Denmark.  This recipe took them one step beyond and it worked out great.  Used red skin spuds and left skin on.  Added a little extra garlic too.  Made use to use a good heaping tablespoon of fresh thyme.  I cut down on cream a little using milk with it, and used less cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a tasteless dud for me. It looked great but tasted boring. I must have done something wrong.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you slice pots in a cuisinart?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I slice mine in an older Kitchenaid processor using the 4 mm blade.  I know that's a little thicker than called for (1/8 inch is 3 1/8 mm), but it both works and, well, is a massive time saver.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes, I sliced mine in a Cuisinart.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This looked great, but was a lead balloon on Thanksgiving; no one liked it, especially the chef.  The aroma from the hot gruyere cheese is sour and offensive, both on the plate and on the palate, turning me against that product for the rest of this lifetime.  A complete waste of potatoes, time and money; I threw out the entire, mostly untouched dish.   Bubbled over and wreaked my oven, as well; set off the smoke detector.  Zero out of 5 stars.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Huh...I find this strange since I've made this dish the last 3 Thanksgivings and actually doubled it up this year since it disappears so quickly I almost didn't get any the last two years.  Not shooting you down, but maybe there was something you "missed" on that made it so unpleasant?  One thing I find is that you need A LOT of salt.  The taters need to be seasoned during cooking, so a sauce that is OFFENSIVELY salty before cooking is just right for the finished dish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did you use gouda by mistake?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is it possible your cheese was bad or you purchased a flavored grayer?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be prepared a day ahead? Thinking about making this for christmas but don't want to be in the kitchen for too long

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made it through the 1st 30 min of baking, and cooked it the next day. Covered the little bit of oxidartion with cheese and baked the next hour. Turned out great. I also tripled the recipe for 12.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this as written, but was underwhelmed- it looks beautiful (just like the picture), but the taste just wasn't there. Since it doesn't beat the barefoot contessa au gratin recipe, I'm going to stick with that.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for our pre-Christmas Eve, Christmas Eve dinner. It was absolutely delicious but mine did not look like the picture at all. I think, had I not added extra sauce to the baking dish, it would have been fine. As it was, the sauce bubbled up to the top of the casserole and the edges of the potatoes were barely visible. So good, though, that I'll give it another try.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Served this Christmas Day to raves. Had to hand out the recipe to everyone.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great recipe but I would highly recommend using ALL of the cream mix and then some. I ended up cooking it for another 45 minutes after adding another half-cup of cream/butter mix. Very good the next day as well, would make them again for a future holiday meal.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone tried to make this without cheese? I wonder if it would turn out ok with just the cream. I know there will be gasps of horror at this idea, but I just don't like the stuff. But we all love potatoes at our house and I think this would be popular.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: That should be doable. Add some grated onion. Maybe boil the cream to thicken. My favorite sub for parmesan cheese is to fine grate some flavorful green olives over the top and give it a few minutes in the oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. With a food processor was a breeze.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is the recipe from the Food Lab! I made it a couple weeks ago and it was utterly amazing

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Amazing dish!  My college-aged son and I made it together, so he'd learn a nice "fancy dish" for when he's on his own.  It was easy, delicious and amazing to look at when it came out of the oven bubbling away.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Your son will never forget this parental episode.
Good for you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for Easter as written. It was delicious and enjoyed by all. I used a low sided glass bundt pan and it helped with the stacking. Made a lovely presentation out of the oven and was easy to serve from with tongs.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: if I were to make it again I would try something to spice it up. the presentation was lovely.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I loved the recipe. I ignored proportions, used whole milk because I had it, didn't stack the potatoes. added various dried herbs not fresh because I had them, and it's in the oven bubbling now. Yukon golds. We'll see.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This just became a new favorite for us and for guests. If it’s just the two of us I make a small dish, with just a few potatoes, altering the measure of the other ingredients to accommodate.  Well worth using the mandolin to get even, thin slices.  I love the rather jagged presentation of them standing upright with different heights and shapes due to the irregularity of the potatoes.  I bought “gold” potatoes (not Yukon)  cause I thought that’s what the recipe called for. They worked very well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Would this work using Sweet Potatoes?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tallie. Yes, someone made the dish using sweet potatoes - mentioned in an earlier note.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have a Cuisinart food processor; what size of slicing disk should be used?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A lot of work,  but well worth the presentation. I did the recipe buy the book. Based on my guests feedback, too much cream not enough cheese and needed at least a half an hour more of cooking time. This is not portioned to six people it’s at least 48 to 10 people. And I would suggest more salt and flavor to punch it up a bit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a most delicious, special potato dish that I cooked on Thanksgiving. My husband declared it our new tradition, and guests begged for the recipe. I used yellow sweet potatoes and it was fabulous! Other than that, I followed the proportions exactly, using Alpine cheese and Parmesan. It served eight perfectly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used half and half instead of cream, and the dish was still quite rich. I used all the liquid even though it came up a little more than half way up. (I certainly could not throw it out, and the fridge was already overflowing.) I might cook it a bit longer next time, as the potatoes were not as soft as I would like. That may be just my preference, as everyone enjoyed it and one couple took home the little bit of leftovers we had.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What about a little Dijon mustard as a substitute for the garlic (guest who is alergic)?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: As someone who is also allergic to garlic, that's a great idea! I'm about to make this recipe for the second time and plan on adding 2 tbsp Grey Poupon to it, since simply omitting garlic the first time left something to be desired. Make sure your Dijon doesn't have garlic in it, though—I'm okay with trace amounts, so check with your guest.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: try shallots instead of garlic

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We’ve cooked this several times, really good!  It does take time we added 30 minutes to the recipes 90.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
In advance: the first 39 min backings then cover and chill
40 min at the end

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wait, what? Need bake ahead instructions—39 minutes in the oven and 40 minutes later?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: According to previous comments, it seems the best thing to do is do #1 and #2, then do the first two 30-minute baking sections (total 60 minutes); then cover and refrigerate until you're ready to finish it; take the cover off, add cheese and put it back in the oven for 40 minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did just about everything wrong with this dish: used a 3qt dish instead of 2qt, did not peel the yellow russets, 2 large garlic cloves instead of medium, and the dish still came out so yummy and inviting! I did use a mandolin to slice the potatoes 1/8”—uniformity is a must. Intricate but easy dish for a special occasion.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The cream and cheese separated while baking. Curious as to why. Followed recipe to the T. Tasted great, looked just like the photo :) ...just not sure why cheese cream mixture curdled.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did you use cheese that was pre-shredded?  Cheese pre-shredded is coated in a wax to keep it from sticking.  Problem is, it also inhibits its ability to melt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Everyone loved it. Cooked it 45 mins covered, 30 uncovered as it had started to turn golden, and I was transporting it to the party. The. About an hour later baked it again uncovered for 45 mins and finally briled it to get the right crisp.
Excellent recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Finally got around to making this after it was bumped from our Christmas menu.  Followed the recipe exactly with about 1/2 t salt and using the Comte. I forgot the garlic but was able to sprinkle some fine garlic powder over everything about 20 minutes into the first bake session.  It came out wonderful and delicious.   I did not have the issues some had with blandness or extra cooking time.  90 minutes was perfect.  Use good cheese and this will not disappoint.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe can be a little tricky to get salted properly—with all the cheese, you don't want to oversalt. I used about 1T kosher, which seemed to be about right. I misread the instructions and put the reserved cheese in before beginning cooking—but it was fine. I've just made it once, and there was a fair amount of liquid in the dish. If I'd cooked it longer without worrying about the cheese burning, I might have succeeded in drying it out just a bit. Used 3T fresh thyme. More'd be fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tried cutting the recipe in half for "just the two of us." Ended up with too much room in the casserole dish and no more russets on hand. So, I ended up running some red new potatoes through the mandolin to fill in the center and pack the dish tightly. There was barely enough of the cream sauce to coat the additional potatoes so I poured a bit more cream over the assembled dish. In the original recipe, two tbs of butter to grease the casserole seems like overkill. Will definitely make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've done this one twice, and even massaging each and every potato slice to be sure it was coated, it still doesn't have the moist-ness and flavor of pommes au gratin cooked in the traditional horizontal form.  And the "look" of it is not that impressive, certainly not enough so to make it worth the time.  Won't be doing this one again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe needs more sauce and milk. I would choose Pepin or Julia child’s recipe over this version which is a bit too dry

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Due to time constraints, made two changes:
— replaced the first 30 minutes of baking time with five minutes in boiling water
— baked horizontally, alternating layers of potatoes and sauce. 

Baked 30 minutes, topped with remaining cheese and baked 30 minutes more. Soft and cheesy inside, crispy on top. Will plan better next time so I can make them vertically.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed this recipe to the letter. Looked beautiful, loved the crispy bits, but overall was a little bland for my taste. Will add more pepper, maybe shallots and other herbs next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be halved?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: In Australia, use "Lady Christl" potatoes - smooth and creamy, delicious flavour

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I’ve made this for Thanksgiving several years in a row and tweaked it slightly each time. Read through many of the notes but I’d still appreciate a few opinions. 
1.  Russet vs. Yukon. How do either potato affect the final dish?
2.  Making ahead. This dish does take effort to prepare and time to assemble, so getting the dish prepped as close to the oven as possible would be awesome. Emily notes that this could get heavy and gummy. Any experiences? And would the choice of potato affect it?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Am I able to cook the first two parts the day before and do the last 30 minutes the day of meal???

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this to a few years ago to bring to a friend's Christmas Eve potluck -- that I only get invited to every few years.  The pressure was on to make something worthy of a repeat invitation, and the Cheesy Hasselback Potato Gratin was just the ticket:  easy, delicious, impressive presentation, and scalable to make for a crowd.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this true to the recipe for Friendsgiving this year. Everyone loved it - the potatoes were perfectly golden. I didn't have a mandolin so I sliced by hand and it was fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Taste is Good

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Have never made a potato gratin dish but always wanted to try. Instructions were easy to follow and ingredients easy to find. Used 6.5 lbs russet potatoes to fit 9x13 dish. Mandoline slicer a must IMHO. Increased cooking time to 45/45/30 because I live at higher elevation. Result was a great looking and tasting dish. Had to make mine the night before T’giving due to time constraints. Would have been better cooked/eaten on the same day but still got a lot of compliments. Thank you NYT Cooks!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was the favorite recipe on the Thanksgiving dinner table.  I might add more cheese, but it was amazing as it was.  It's pretty easy too.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I will make more of the "liquid" mixture the next time.  This was delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've made this for the past 3 Thanksgivings and I end up doubling it and adding extra cheese for more flavor, but I still think it needs more oomph - maybe I should add onion as some have suggested? Maybe more butter? It's always a bit bland, even if I taste the cheese/cream mixture and think I've heavily seasoned it. @Kenji, could you provide a baseline for how much salt you add?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: double the cheese, add more garlic? maybe try onion?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Use a wooden spoon to not slice all the way through

Consider doing with sweet potato

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Looking forward to trying this recipe

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe fit well in my 9x13 pyrex cookware. Had to slice 1 extra potato to fill it completely. Be careful not to overfill the dish with the cream mixture, as it will bubble over and won't brown as well. I had almost a cup of extra cream at the end, might reduce that next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
ok. Not a lot better than regular scapploped pototos though more crispy. 
1/2 whipping cream, 1/4 veg stock, 1/4 half and half

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We modified this recipe for a Burns Night supper by mixing up russet potatoes, purple potatoes, and turnips. The only other modification I made was to parboil them before cooking so everything was ready at the same time. Delicious and beautiful on the table!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made it exactly as the recipe specified and it was perfect. I needed an entire five-pound bag of potatoes to fill the dish. Used Yukon Gold potatoes and they were so pretty. Every last bite was eaten at Christmas dinner.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made this as part of our Christmas dinner and it was DELICIOUS. The cheese on the top is so good. I’d say a bit less thyme and a bit more salt & more cheese on top. I will def make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not worth all the trouble au gratin would have been fine

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this exactly as directed and it came out perfectly! It’s visually impressive as well as really delicious (the different cheeses add a nice, complex flavor) and, as mentioned, pairs perfectly with a holiday roast.  Would also be a great dish to bring to a potluck.  Five stars!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I agree with the others who said this was bland. I used 1Tb kosher salt, based on another note, and added an extra clove of garlic, but meh. Not worth it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made these for Christmas dinner and they were delicious. We used Asiago instead of the Parmigiano-Reggiano. We cooked it at the recommended time but the middle potatoes were still too hard. We put them back in for 20 minutes and it was cooked through.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I appreciated Kris' suggestion of using a wooden spoon for slicing the potatoes.  It worked like a charm!

As written this appeared to be a labor intensive recipe, but it was not, much to my pleasure.  I followed the recipe as written and the result is a delicious side dish which I served at a dinner party much to the delight of me and my guests.  The presentation is of the finished dish is impressive, too.  I had to take a photo to share with those that were not present for dinner.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Our favorite potatoes - ever. Every time we make these it feels like a luxury. Absolutely divine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Amazingly good with chopped onion added to the creamy cheese mixture, and used all but two Yukon Gold potatoes from a 5lb bag. Added gouda to the smoked gruyere, and it was yummy. I added a little milk to the cheese cream mixture during assembly because I was running out of liquid to coat the potatoes, but next time I'll try not to...it was a little runny when it came out of the oven, but delicious. Will make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this tonight, and it was a big family success. Next time we might add a bit more cream, but otherwise a huge win!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Argh.  Not cheesey at all. Next time 2 less potatoes, and more cheese.
Though some in my family liked it I felt it was flavorless.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I dont know what the recipe called for 2 Tablespoons of butter? I barely used 1 teaspoon to grease my 2QT dutch oven.  Either way - followed the recipe & was delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These are so amazing and addictive!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this tonight. Will do again with more cheese and cooking it much longer than called for.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: 7 average potatoes, 8 oz gruyere, Bennington Pottery casserole dish

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very fun and very pretty dish. I used a 13x9 Pyrex casserole dish, and had my potatoes wrap around it like an oval, with a center line of potatoes whose petals lean out like a flower.  The dish was filled nicely with 7lbs of round Yukon golds. I used buttermilk, 1/2 and 1/2, gruyere, nutmeg, and sage in my cheese sauce. I also increased the cream 50 percent. Still, all the sauce evaporated by the end of cooking, and tops were pretty desicated. Don’t let it overcook.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was disappointed. The potatoes were tasty but not crisp. I followed the recipe and the cooking times. I think the problem might have been that the potato slices were covered with too much cream and packed too tightly in the dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Submitted too soon! To continue: the cream and cheese sauce was so thick it couldn’t be poured around the potatoes. I spooned some on top, but the potatoes were tightly packed so it couldn't seep through. The taste was fine but the consistency was just like baked potatoes, nothing special. I was disappointed as my son had specially requested this dish, having seen it in the NYT Thanksgiving article.
Any suggestions (in case I try it again?)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was great, my Thanksgiving pod absolutely loved it.  Next time we will place the 2 quart dish on a lined baking sheet ... I was a little generous with the cream and it overflowed in the oven, filling the whole house with smoke for the last half hour of cooking! Even after that, no one regretted replacing our mashed potatoes with this lovely dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Maybe it was my oven, but I had to leave it in the oven quite a bit longer for the potatoes to fully cook; but the flavor of those cheeses and garlic!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have a question about this recipe. I've made it twice now and always follow the directions precisely as written. But, while I do get the nice crispy edges on top, the centers of the potato slices are always crunchy, not creamy. This year I even baked them for another 30 minutes at 350ºF and then yet another 30 minutes at 400ºF, but to no avail. The centers remain crunchy. So, has anyone else had this problem and, if so, did you find a solution? Helpful advice is much appreciated. Thanks!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: could it be that your cuts into the potato aren't deep enough?  I've made hasslebacks before using russet baking potatoes and found this same issue if I left too much "solid" or uncut potato at the bottom.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this with 2.75 lb Yukon Golds sliced to 4mm in food processor. It sat in the fridge a couple hours before baking and seems that some moisture came out of the potatoes because the liquid cream sauce rose to the brim and there was extra space in the dish. I poured off the excess cream sauce and added another potato to keep it packed in. It was excellent. Didn't change anything. 4 people almost polished off the whole thing. Yum.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone made this ahead? Refrigerated and then baked later?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this, and I must say it was overwhelming not cheesy enough for me. I was sort of shocked that it wasn't cheesy enough, considering I doubled the amount the recipe original calls for. I guess I was expecting the dish to have more pungent flavors (and I mean that the best way possible). To me, the dish was just quite bland.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be made a day ahead?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It bakes in 3 times 30 minutes, first under foil, then uncovered, then with cheese on top. You could do the first hour of baking a day ahead, then finish it with the cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a great holiday treat! Mine filled a 4 quart casserole, though, which surprised me since the recipe calls for a 2 quart casserole. Perhaps my potatoes were a smaller diameter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have officially had all the cholesterol I should have for the entire year...... and I would do it again!! Amazing!! Made this exactly as recipe called for and it came out perfect. Best potato dish ever and my arteries do not care :)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made for Christmas Eve and while it was a hit, I thought it was a bit bland. Definitely will season more generously next time. I also like the suggestions of adding grated onion. Note: Peeling and slicing potatoes was a great job for my older teens.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone tried this in the Instant Pot to cook the potatoes faster before baking?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Really delicious with yukon Gold potatoes but the small ones are too short to poke above the cream and crisp well.
Definitely would be good with some mustard powder or smoked paprika. Salt well!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Dang, these are good. One of my fav recipes for holidays. Looks beautiful and tastes amazing! You definitely need more salt than you’d expect, despite the amount of cheese in the recipe. Putting the potatoes in the pan really isn’t as much of as hassle as it sounds. I simply grabbed a pile of potatoes and shoved them up against the previous pile. No neat stacks necessary! Only pain is slicing all those potatoes, even with a mandolin it requires a little time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this for Christmas and followed recipe
To the T. I served it as a side with a rib eye and a spinach salad. 
It was excellent! Definitely will make it again. I used all the cream sauce.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Yum! I pretty much followed this recipe to a tee and it came out delicious. I scaled the amount of thyme back to about half of a tablespoon and ended up using one heaping teaspoon of kosher salt in the cream mixture; the flavors were spot on.
It did end up a little soupy so next time I might adjust the cream mixture. But no complaints :)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: absolutely delicious -- no wonder it received five stars.....

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I set my food processor slicing disc too narrow, & didn’t catch it in time (note to self - next time process 1/4 potato, then assess before processing the rest), so instead of arranging potatoes on end, I put them into the baking dish horizontally, poured the cheese-cream mixture over and baked 30 min covered, then 45 min uncovered. Delish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe was absolutely amazing, everyone loved. I did have to double the heavy cream in order for the liquid to be about half way up in the pan. Other than that, I followed the recipe exactly as is.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I read the reviews and was scared this would turn out flavorless and undercooked but based on all the high ratings, I went for it and it’s seriously awesome. I did increase (doubled) the cheese and garlic because others recommended and what else am I going to do with the rest of that awesome cheese? I used my food processor on 4mm slice of blade and put slices in a Dutch oven with water until ready to assemble. I thought this would be a lot of work but it really wasn’t and it was so delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I LOVED this recipe and will never make scalloped or au gratin potatoes again. The crispy edges are perfection. Be sure to have a few extra potatoes as they mentioned. I did not and needed them.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These were a real hit on Christmas. Our daughter couldn't join us given Covid restrictions, so we agreed on a menu together and cooked together . We both used Yukon gold potatoes, which she cut through completely, while I kept mine more hasselback-like. Plenty of salt, as some suggested, but otherwise, we followed the recipe to delightful results. This will be a welcome new addition to family's favorite recipes. Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this as is and it was absolutely delicious! Easier than I thought it would be - definitely needs the cooking time and a little extra cheese never hurts. This will be a new tradition at Christmas!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe came out excellently!  Because I had a 6 oz piece of Gruyere, I used all of it.  Also placed thinly sliced shallot on the bottom of the casserole before adding potatoes.  I like the adaptability of ingredients, but stick to the method, which is perfect!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I must have packed my potatoes too tightly because after 2 hours in the oven they were still undercooked.  Edible, but only because the prime rib had already been waiting an hour.  Baked the leftovers 2 more hours the next day and that did the trick.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a great take on Potato Gratin. It's extra crispy and actually much easier to make than a traditional horizontally layered gratin. I'm definitely going to make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very disappointed. The cheese separated and was clumpy and oily. It was in the oven for a long time, but the potatoes were not cooked quite enough. They were thin, sliced with a mandoline. I made these in placed of mashed for Thanksgiving, and I have regrets. Luckily no guests were here to share my regrets, though.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Takes a long time, makes a lot and very rich

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cut the recipe in half to feed my husband and me. The potatoes fit well in a bread loaf pan. I will make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am making this for two, so probably three decent size russets at the most, in a small cast enamel casserole. I’m assuming the cook time will need to be reduced. Any experience or feedback on how long?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Amazing. Everyone asks for this recipe, usually with their mouths full!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Easier than it looks but.... I started an oven fire with cheesy liquid drippings !   Never again without a pan under it!  Also I felt it was a bit dull... next time I’ll add Dijon and maybe caramelized shallots.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Patty was right. Put a pan on under it! We had a small fire start in the oven from the cheesy cream dripping. It’s not me I swear I’m a good cook! The recipe should flag to put a pan under it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used the ‚wrong‘ type of cheese but the dish is still delicious. Slicing the potatoes was the only real effort required, but well worth it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious but needs some proportion fine tuning.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A lot of effort for a very bland taste. I made this for Thanksgiving last year and was extremely disappointed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Does anyone know if this reheats well? I'd like to make it for a vegetarian Thanksgiving but don't want to do all the work on Thanksgiving day. 

Thank you.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You could assemble the dish through step 2 the day before, cover it tightly with foil, and refrigerate it overnight. The next day, proceed with step 3. I don't think baking it the day before and then trying to reheat it would be successful -- just a guess based on experience; I haven't actually tried to do it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was fabulous! I did use Yukon Gold potatoes out of preference. Do salt and pepper generously as recipe suggests. 

This is a showstopper for any holiday or if you feel like splurging on a cold blustery night! 10/10

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious!! I made it with very fresh local potatoes. And halved the recipe because I didn't have a crowd. Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wondering how 4 lbs of potatoes become only 6 servings.  By my estimate this should serve 16-20 people at a meal.  Has anyone who made this dish found the serving sized suggested to be appropriate.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Halved this recipe. So good! The potatoes cooked for 1.5 hours (1 hour covered & 30 minutes uncovered) before adding cheese step which cooked for an additional 15 minutes and then put it under the broiler. The potatoes felt cooked but grainy to the mouth, but flavors melded beautifully as it cooled in the pan. This will be my new go to "scallop" recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Somewhat bland, but I’ll make again because the texture is lovely and it’s such a nice presentation.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It definitely isn't bland. Try tasting for salt before you put it in the oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am going to try this with half Yukon Gold and half sweet potatoes. I always want both at Thanksgiving but never have the energy to make two types of potatoes. Hoping a yin-yang design of potato varieties will do the trick.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Can sweet potatoes be substituted for russet potatoes? 
Thanks.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Chopsticks when cutting

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be assembled the night before then baked?  I've made Hasselbeck potatoes before and they were a great hit with my family, thought this would be a great addition to Thanksgiving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I worried about this one after reading all the comments- commenters said use a 4mm blade, dish curdled, bland taste, cooking time was off, not enough salt, … I went for it anyway and it was a hit. I did use good quality cheese, doubled it, added more thyme and rosemary. Still needed salt but not a big deal, guests added as they wished. I hand sliced and that was not perfect, and cooked for longer than suggested. Eyeballed. I will add less liquid next time for less spillage- still so good!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm planning on cooking this a day ahead and then warming up at our host's house. Does anyone have experience with reheating this dish?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The texture is best if you wait till the day of to bake it.  It can get greasy upon reheating so if you absolutely have to , do it gently and maybe moisten with a bit more cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’m making these for Thanksgiving tomorrow. I just finished the first hour of baking (will do the final half hour tomorrow), I teased out a slice to taste. WOW, it’s amazing, and I know it’ll be even better with more cheese and crisping. Now I have to resist temptation to sneak another slice. Used both russet and sweet potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
2 T of butter?  I kept looking for the step where we add it. If it's just to grease the pan, that's a LOT of butter for one pan!

No complaint here -- just wondering if I missed something.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Started to slice on the only mandolin-like thing we have, which comes on the cheese grater. Got pulled up short when I saw the recipe specified 1/8”. Mine were coming out about half that. Sliced the potatoes by hand. Now I’m looking at the photo and the only way those slices are 1/8” is if the potato was about a five inch diameter. They look paper-thin in the photo. What thickness is everyone else using?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I prepped this yesterday and left it overnight in the fridge and had leftover cream after it came up about 3/4 up the pan. I baked it perhaps a little longer than recommended but after 1 hour the potatoes were still a bit hard and I wanted to be sure they were soft. They were soft enough but bizarrely a little dry after all that cream. I think I need a deeper dish next time. Got good reviews at the table though.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Soooo Good!  This recipe is perfect just as it is.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made exactly according to recipe. Didn't love it. Greasy and unevenly cooked. Might benefit from a roux and additional spices.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving using sweet potatoes. Bad idea, despite the encouragement of several commenters. Sweet potatoes and Gruyere are not a good match! Otherwise the recipe works well. The crispy bits on top and velvety rich slices below are irresistible. I'd make it again as written or with Yukon golds.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Delicious!
We did take the advice found here of adding 1tsp of dry mustard and 1/2 cup of sautéed onion, 1T salt, 1T pepper.
Like many of you, the potatoes were seriously undercooked after the three 30 minute cooking phases. The top looked beautiful, brown, and bubbly. The interior was the problem area.
WHEN we make this again, we plan to increase the duration of the 1st, covered cooking phase to 60 minutes

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Potatoes in the middle of this casserole were barely cooked following the directions. I even turned up the temperature a little at the end. Everything tasted good but potatoes not completely tender like they should have been.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I used all the cheese & cream mix coating my potatoes and it didn’t look like it came close to halfway up on my casserole dish so I made a little bit more of it to pour in which was a big mistake because towards the end of the second half hour set of baking
it spilled all over the bottom of the oven & huge mess. If I’d just trusted that was enough cream-&-cheese mix it would’ve been perfect. Still super yummy!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The potatoes did not cook through. We even gave it an extra 10 minutes in the oven, got frustrated, and then separated it into two batches to go back into the oven. If I were to make this again, I would parboil the potatoes. I likely won’t make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone had success making this in advance and freezing?  Thanks

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Agree with those who noted a much longer cooking time - and I am tossing the results because after all that time trying to just cook it, the gruyere is separated into a disgusting mess. I also made the mistake of following the advice of another comment NOT to soak the sliced potatoes- which of course resulted in the potatoes oxidizing, which meant not only did I plunk down undercooked potatoes, but the tops have a horrible black color. I am sticking with Julia Child’s recipe from now on.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A hit from the first time I served it and it is on our Holiday table by demand every year.  I like to add a finely chopped Vidalia Onion to the mix and I'm heavy on the black pepper.   Always worth the time and effort.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Would make more sauce next time (1.5?). Added half a packet of onion soup.
Doubled the sauce when I made it the first time (2T salt   onion soup) = too salty.
Baked longer before adding cheese.
Buy more potatoes. Perhaps 10.
Delicious

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this as written but I must be missing something because it came out very bland. I made sure each slice of potato was coated before assembling- I bought nice cheese- not sure what I did wrong…?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: An easy recipe and a BIG hit! I modified with yellow potatoes, grated onion, smoked paprika, and mustard powder. I used the 3/16" setting w/ my mandoline (and increased baking time a bit), but I would try the recommended 1/8" next time to see how it turns out. I did the first 2 baking segments in advance, but I think doing the whole thing just before serving would yield the best results. I'll definitely be trying this recipe in the future with different seasonings and cheeses. It's a keeper!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added one finely minced shallot to the cream mixture and seasoned liberally with salt (2 tsp based on another note) and pepper. My potatoes were not falling apart soft at the end of the bake but I actually enjoyed that with the crispy tops. Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added some purple sweet potato about every 4 inches for looks :)  An hour and a half was too long for my batch. Potatoes were well done and the sauce became too oily. I think the half hour covered was OK, but next time will go with 20 minutes uncovered and 15 to 20 minutes after adding cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A great addition to our Thanksgiving table, which featured roasted King salmon this year. Very handsome dish, loved by all. Made with unpeeled Yukons and cheese mix included fontina. Easy to throw together and the mandolin made fast work of the slicing. Definitely needs the full 90 minutes in the oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for my contribution to our Supper Club.  Easily fed 8 with leftovers.  I did the suggestion of hasselbaching the potatoes which I thought would create a more uniform look rather than stacking cut pieces.  It worked out well except getting the cream/cheese mixture in between the slices was difficult.  When cooked I felt this affected the final taste.  I will try the stacking version next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: After reading through the notes I increased the cheese and cream, but next time I will follow the recipe exactly. While absolutely delicious, I suspect it doesn't need any tampering with. Since it was just my husband and myself we had leftovers for days. Would definitely make this for company, probably in advance as it does take time to prepare. Used Yukon golds and mandoline.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I make this up to the cooking part and leave it in the fridge overnight?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I think you'd want to cook is for at least the first 30 minutes, otherwise the potatoes may discolor. If you scroll through the reviews you'll find at least one that says it worked well to do the first two 30 minute cooking sessions in advance, then finish it before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Arghhh - so frustrating! Everyone raved about this recipe, but mine came out oily and not super flavorful or cheesy - what am I doing wrong?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: To anyone who has made this already - can I cook this the day before I serve it? And then rewarm the night of? Wondering how it will hold up, thanks!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did the first 2 bakes the day before thanksgiving and the last bake the day of. I used white sweet potato and it turned out fantastic

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is outrageously complicated to slice thinly and assemble, so be prepared. Delicious, though.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We loved these! Followed the recipe with the exception of adding half a grated onion, 3/4 tsp smoked paprika, and 1 tsp ground mustard. Used about 2 tsp salt and 1 tsp pepper. Did have to recover and bake another 20 minutes after the 'final' 30 recommended, but I really packed the potatoes in there. Texture and flavor were both excellent.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Didn’t have a mandoline so maybe that was the issue but was pretty careful to cut them to 1/8 in. Came out a little dry and slightly underdone after the 1.5. Went back in for another 20 min with a bit of milk and that helped. Overall I find I prefer scalloped.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
So so good! I followed the recipe almost exactly. In the last 30-minute bake, the top was perfectly browned and crispy after 20 minutes. I put the foil back on for the last 10 minutes to avoid burning. Also, I used a baking dish that is slightly larger than 2 quarts. Needed a few extra potato slices to fill it, but other than that it did not affect the recipe. 

The dish sat at room temp for a few hours and I popped it back in the oven for a bit to heat before serving. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: HELP! I have tried this recipe twice now, for Christmas Day dinners, and both times got the same disappointing results. I don't mind fiddly dishes as I love to cook so cutting the potatoes with the mandoline was not an issue.  The problem both times . . . after all the baking, the potatoes remained hard.  I should have saved them for another day and tried cooking for more time, but I was so frustrated, I tossed them  . . . has anyone else had this issue?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed this recipe exactly and it was absolutely perfect! The most time consuming part is peeling and slicing the potatoes (even with a mandoline) but it's totally worth it. Just a few simple ingredients but it tastes like a restaurant dish. Can't wait to make this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Would make this again. Followed recipe as written. Enjoyed the contrast of crispy tops & delicate bottom of pan potatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was thrown by the “7 to 8” setting on a mandolin. Both my mandolin and my food processor have numbered settings and 7 to 8 is too thick. Next time i will definitely aim for 1/8th inch. With slices being thicker they were sturdy enough to cram into every available space in the dish. Bad technique! I basically reconstituted whole potatoes and missed out on a lot of beneficial surface area

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I think that the 7-8 refers to the number of potatoes, not the setting on the mandolin.  From the picture included, it appears that the slice is very thin, maybe less that 1/8th inch.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Hi - help please - i made this yesterday and cooked it for 1.5 hours and the potatoes were not cooked through? I used a mandolin to slice the potatoes - maybe there were a bit thick but i really didn’t think so. Any advice would be much appreciated because i definitely want to make this again.  

Thanks!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delicious. Long cook time but worth it. Everyone enjoyed d it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Halved with 2 pounds potatoes. Used one cup milk mixed with 5 ounces of Brie and feta. Kept 3 cloves garlic and 1 T thyme and sprinkled cheddar at end. Used 1 1/2 teasp salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious even though I messed the order of the recipe up. Not as pretty as the picture with the recipe

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Whose fault is that?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A winner!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this a year or 2 ago and remember it was so delicious and all of my guests loved it. I can’t remember if I prepared it ahead of time- has anyone prepared it- stored in the fridge for a few hours and then baked it?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Definitely making again.  I did first 2 bakes the night before serving then added the rest of the cheese and baked another 40 minutes the next day.  The cream did separate some so I'll have to keep an eye on oven temp. Oh, I used fresh roasted garlic in place of raw. Nice, mellow flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tossing all that raw garlic in with the cream is, I guess, an American thing. Italians would recoil. I prefer a more subtle garlic aroma achieved by rubbing a cut clove all over the casserole dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Thanksgiving guests have practically begged for this every year since the first time I served it.  It's great the next day, too!  I set the potatoes between two chopsticks to cut them perfectly. 
 Well worth the effort.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: No need to peel the potatoes, but slice them as thin as a teenager's excuse. We lost patience with lining them up, so we layered them like scalloped potatoes and made an artful pattern with the top layer. No one thought ill of us.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was a great recipe - I'm sure it would have turned out even better if I hadn't missed the salt and pepper (whoops), when test driving ahead of Thanksgiving. 

Very delicious! Used some Beechers Smoked Cheddar along with the other cheeses, and will be doing that again. Will try another commenter's suggestion to grate some onion into the mixture, I think that will give it a really satisfying additional layer of flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How long can this sit in the refrigerator before cook it? I’m thinking of preparing it a day or two earlier? Any thoughts would be appreciated!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Don't have kitchen space for large equipment.  There are mini hand mandolines that work well,  Would half and half work?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes, there are mandolines very small. Search in amazon for mandoline slicer.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Any tips for partially baking the night before? I'm thinking of just reserving the last 30 minutes to crisp everything at the end right before serving? Planning for thanksgiving

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: the notes where people made it in advance says sometimes it does not work.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add fresh nutmeg, too - nutmeg, cheese, and cream is a heavenly combo.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The grocery store was entirely out of heavy cream so I substituted what they had available, light cream. It did NOT work, it ended up being more like cottage cheese and less creamy. I added 1 tsp kosher salt but I would likely double that next time as I found the potatoes to be a bit bland.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yeah, this…amazing.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I see garlic in the list but it is not mentioned in the instructions… ?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: In Step 1... "Add cream, garlic and thyme to cheese mixture."

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: my relatives raved about it when I made it at thanksgiving. next time I would add a dash of nutmeg and a tiny sprinkle of cayenne pepper along with the thyme into the creme sauce.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A lot of work for this woefully inept cook...but the result was outstanding! A guest added a sprinkle of paprika to her portion, which gave a welcome zip to this crisp and creamy dish--I'll remember it for next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: More cheese sauce

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These tasted pretty good the night of, but MUCH better the next day--the garlic and thyme really came through. If I make this again I think I'll pause after the second 30 minutes of baking to let the flavors develop, then add the cheese and bake for the final 30 minutes just before dinner.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious and easy, if messy, to assemble, but it needed twice as much gruyere.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed a few notes and did the first two bakes a day ahead, then finished the next day. The cheese was a bit gummy and hard - do not recommend. I think they would be excellent if made in one fail swoop!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Amazing dish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was out and out sensational. My husband added an egg for some reason, and also green onions.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Looked great. Didn’t love them. It was a lot of work for not a lot of joy. All the sauce seeps to the bottom and the flavor was disappointing. My husband dressed up the leftovers and it was better. I was hoping for more given all the reviews

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Really delicious. We were in a cabin in Lake Arrowhead and didn't have the correct casserole dish so we used a deep dish pie pan and it worked perfectly! But make sure to follow directions--don't use all of the cream if it goes higher than midway up, or you will have an overflow, like we did!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe was a huge hit as a side at Thanksgiving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great potatoes

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Potatoes were not cooked after 30 min. I added 20 and they were still not cooked. The reason is all the cream goes in cold and take a good amount of time to come up to temp in the oven. Better to make this in a skillet and bring to temp on the stove before transferring to the oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Everything except the creme was room temp. I sliced em thin and made sure to coat every square centimeter with the mixture and did arrange standing up but did not pack them too tight. Followed the directions making sure my oven was pre-warmed and checked temp with a thermometer. Followed ingredient list exactly. Perfection, people went nuts over em

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is it possible to make this dish one day in advance, and then reheat? What do you recommend?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This came to far more than 2 qts. but was delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made them to go with lobster tails for a special dinner with another couple. They stole the show!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Hasselbacks were a hassle. Not worth the work, though the aroma was irresistible. Followed the recipe exactly, and didn't get the cheese pay-off that many other cooks raved about. My 2 qt. casserole was too small for the 4.5 lbs. I sliced on my man-eating mandoline, so I packed the remaining spuds into a small saucepan. Added potato chip crumbs to the top of the experimental pot, which was great for the crunch factor. I'd add onion slices and more cheese if I ever make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this to go with prime rib for the big holiday meal and it was amazing. Perfect accompaniment to the beef. It’s a heavy dish so I won’t make it all the time, but will do again for a big meal. 
I used a slicing attachement on the kitchen aid mixer that spiral slices the potatoes. Worked great for stacking and made short, even work of slicing.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious. I worried about arranging the potatoes, but it turned out to be relatively simple. Great receipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a home run for Christmas dinner! My only note is that I used some cheddar and it made it a little oily on the top and definitely not as pretty as the photo! No one cared and we ate half the pan between 4 of us. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This gave me a bit of trouble this year -- turned out great, but the potatoes I used were huge, and got browned/burnt in some places. So it was really crispy on the outside and looked unappetizing. Maybe try the oven at 425 instead of 450? And/or cover it longer...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a tricky recipe if you are trying to cook other things in the oven at the same time. I recommend cooking way behind the suggested cooking time if you are cooking multiple things at once. We reheated it with more cream the next day and it was perfect.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This captured my heart as soon as I laid eyes on it.  Fantastic recipe. My advice would be to PUT a PAN under it!!!! I had some nasty oven spillage.  And use less liquid than you think. I read the instruction of not needing all the liquid too late! Very great combo of crunchy potato tips that stick up and creamy yumminess underneath.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this twice, the first time with russets following the recipe exactly (with the exception of halving it). The second time I made it with yukon gold potatoes and left the skins on, inspired by notes I read here (also halving the recipe). The first batch was much better as agreed by all. I do plan to make it again, but I'll go back to peeled russets.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This has become a mandatory side dish for our Christmas dinner. My girls love it. 

It’s really not a difficult recipe if you have a mandolin

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A very impressive crowd pleaser at Christmas dinner! Delicious! We used two round white Corning ware dishes and the result looked almost like a potato rosette. Topped with a little nutmeg and it was beautiful. I am not great with directions- so I forgot to coat each potato. Ended up  just dumping the cheese mixture over top and worked it in between the potatoes with my fingers. Still great! (I personally feel this was pretty easy, but would have been difficult without the mandoline.)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this with 7-8 potatoes.  The liquid wasn't nearly enough.  I added another half cup of cream.  The potatoes still came out very dry.  Next time I would add 3 T butter and more cheese for sure.  It looked pretty but didn't taste as well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I followed this recipe to a T except I substituted Pecorino Romano for the Parmagiano/Reggiano.  Cooked it exactly as called for on the middle shelf of my oven.  Came out brown and crispy but the oil separated and there were pockets of what looked like drawn butter.  I removed it and cooked it a few minutes longer.  Was delicious as advertised but can’t figure out why the oil separated.  Any ideas or insights???

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: One other note.  When I mixed the cheese and cream it was thick like a paste.  Covered all of the potatoes but there was no “liquid” in the bottom of the bowl to pour into the potatoes.  Added a bit of 1/2 and 1/2 to be able to pour it.  Maybe that was my error.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Adjustments for next time:

	Potatoes - Thinner slices on the mandolin
	Cheese - 1.5x quantity (5oz Gy, 4oz parm)
	
	Wait longer before adding final layer of cheese

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Found this way too fussy to bother with again. Flavor was rich but kinda bland, even after salting generously. Usually a fan of Kenji but this ain't it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made as directed and though delicious, it was a bit greasy at the end.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This dish ended up being more like 12 servings for our family. Tasty but heavy. Halve the recipe if you are a small group of two or three.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this dish using about 4 pounds of Yukon Gold potatoes. Turned out very good but I would definitely double the cheese because, it is 4 pounds of potatoes! I would also purée a small onion to add to the cream mixture and also add chopped chives for garnish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just made this for the first time. It was easy and a hit!  I took the advice from the cooking notes and added a grated shallot to the mix. Next time, I'll cut back on the thyme as I found it a little too overpowering.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mostly user error, but warning that technique is not that forgiving. This came out dry and bland. Need to aggressively season during assembly. I gave them extra time in the oven waiting for a ham, and they came out too dry, as all the tasty liquid was absorbed by the potatoes. I would follow the recipe more closely next time but up the flavors. More S&P and a sharper cheese. Perhaps some onion.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made as directed by recipe.  Turned out just as described.  Will make again

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Turned out great. Next time I won’t crisp it up so much at the end, too crunchy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added about 3 tsp of salt to the cream mix.  Should have added more--maybe sprinkle over once stacked in the dish. Left in warm oven hoping to hold for an hour or so--the top dried out. Otherwise delish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I make this the day ahead, stash in the frig and bake it when I need it?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made them last year for Thanksgiving at a friend's. They were a huge hit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How deep should the casserole dish be?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The recipe specifies a 2 quart casserole. If it's an 8" diameter dish, the depth will be greater than if it's a 12" x 12" square.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent, delicious !!!! and easy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
”Hasselbackspotatis” is a very popular dish in Sweden and is originally Swedish. The dish can be found in Jenny Åkerström's Princesses' cookbook, published in 1929. It was mentioned already 1911 in a recipe.
In both recipes the used cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am allergic to any aged cheese, any suggestions for substitutions for the Gruyère or comté cheese?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'd like to make this -- but not with heavy cream. Has anyone tried it with whole milk?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used fat-free half and half and it worked fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I see that the recipe says u can use a cuisinart.  My question for those that have made this:  Is it possible to crisp the bottom as well?  I dont love that gratin potatoes altho' delicious are crisp on top and soggy (but still yum potato) underneath.  I'm looking for some crispiness on the top, but also elsewhere in this dish - any ideas?  Thanks  CH

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used a Cuisinart thinner slicing blade. It worked perfectly!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Potatoes always taste better with garlic. Ad a couple cloves minced into the cream mixture. Divine!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I make this today up to the point of first baking just to get the potato slicing off the to do list?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Sherry
Paprika

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Simply fantastic!  This will be a new staple on Thanksgiving

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made for Thanksgiving and it was a big hit around the table.  Def recommend!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Seemed very dense and kind of dry. You couldn't add more liquid because it would bubble up over the potatoes. The cheesy topping was delicious. 
I had some of the cream and cheese mix left over and oh was it ever good mixed into scrambled eggs.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I halved the recipe and used Russet potatoes sliced with a Cuisinart 1/8" blade. Wasn't a ton of effort, but they were definitely a disappointment; crispy on top but undercooked on the inside. I have to assume they were packed too tightly? They were well coated and I didn't force them to fit or anything so not sure what went wrong here. I probably won't experiment further with this recipe as they were too bland and oily regardless of firmness.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made these for thanksgiving this year. I think Ina Garten’s fennel potato gratin still wins with flavor, but these ones are stunning and definitely wows a crowd. I might add some fennel or leek in here next time for extra flavor. But next time I may just make Ina’s instead.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made without any modifications. The tops of the potatoes were pretty good but I was overwhelmed by the softer parts. They were really bland and just tasted like plain potatoes. None of us were very impressed, unfortunately. 

It was an easy dish to put together (using a mandolins for slicing) so I might try it again and add more cheese and an online, like others suggested.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these with half and half instead of heavy cream & with tons of cheese as fellow home-chefs suggested. The potatoes were indeed amazing, but I think they might be even better if cut real thin with the mandoline slicer, instead of a serrated knife.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made two casseroles yesterday for Thanksgiving(over estimated crowd). It was a big hit, but I have one left. Has anyone tried freezing?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this for Thanksgiving.
The potatoes came out under-done. Now, I'm at a very high elevation and didn't add enough extra time. But I wonder if I could briefly boil whole potatoes before slicing without messing up starches, etc.?
Also, that was A LOT of cream and it was kind of swimming in it. I would reduce.
Will try again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made as described and it’s fine. Fun even. My mistake was making it for Thanksgiving dinner which is, to my plebian senses, a meal for nostalgic flavors. It did engender oohs and ahhs when served, but the flavor skewed the meal away from the traditional and not in a good way. The cheese just didn’t fit. Next year I’ll return to the tried-and-true. Sorry I made this but it’s not the recipe’s fault.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved this recipe - after i made some tweaks!! It was a little bland and lacking umph the first time around. When i made it again, i added approx 2 tablespoons of both Dijon mustard and yogurt to the cream mixture. So much better!!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A lot of trouble and expensive ingredients for a mediocre result. Packed the potatoes tightly and cooked as long as suggested. The middle wasn't cooked. For leftovers, I cut it all up and let it cook in a 300 oven for about an hour and a half. The repeat was much better than the original.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yummy. A great addition to our Thanksgiving table

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used part heavy cream and part buttermilk because that was what I had in the fridge.  Otherwise followed recipe exactly.  Delicious.  Also great with eggs the next morning.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add nutmeg, S&P

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Huge disappointment. The recipe calls for the potatoes to be packed tightly, but I packed them too tightly & the sauce floated on the top. I pushed the potatoes around to allow the sauce to seep to the bottom, but it was clearly not enough liquid. I added a bit more cream, but the potatoes still ended up dry. Yes, it was pretty and my guests liked it, but it was nowhere near as good as a creamy gratin. The sauce also separated on me, so there were oily puddles when it came out of the oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very underwhelming. No reason to ever change from the Julia Child scalloped potato recipe I've been making for years. That always has people clamoring for more!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Crazy delicious! Made with sweet potatoes. Everyone loved it!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Aesthetically, I liked seeing the ultra thin slices of potatoes stacked and turned golden. It was just so visually beautiful. 

I made a half-batch, but used the garlic recommended for the whole recipe and 3/4 of the thyme. Even though, I added extra, I would add more. Perhaps some rosemary, too. And a sprinkle of chives or parsley at the end. There was not enough variation of flavor or complimentary flavors. That’s typical of a gratin but I like complimentary herbs added in most of my food.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Comte works well. Yum.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These are amazing. Fourth year in a row making them for the holidays. I cut myself on the mandolin each year. This year I pretty much sliced the end of my thumb off. Anyone have a different way to slice them super thin? Also, I always get a ring around the top edge of the pan of burned cream and some black spots on top, it always tastes delicious, but I am not able to get the beautiful golden brown color as shown in the picture. Any tips would be greatly appreciated!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Do you have a Cuisinart by any chance?  We rarely use this wedding registry item but the slicing insert was a huge help.  I fed whole yukon gold potatoes in and it made short work of them.  You can adjust for different thicknesses too.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Try wearing a cut resistant glove.  They are available on line.  I also just accept that I am not going to get every last bit of potato sliced.  The ends can be used for mashed potatoes, soup, hash browns and so forth.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I found the assembly takes a very long time if you want it to look any where close to the picture.  A few things might help,  set the mandolin , or food processor to slice about 3mm, 4mm at most to make sure the slices are flexible enough to fit together smoothly.
Then sort the potato slices by size before coating them with the cream and cheese.  Then you can grab, coat, and place slices that you need to make a good fit, as you need them.
At least 1.5hrs to bake. Patience, and practice.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and the texture was horrible. Tops were crunchy and chewy and bottoms were too soggy. Would not make this recipe again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is it possible to do any of this the day prior?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was a great dish.  Surprisingly easy to prepare and assemble.

Beautiful presentation and the flavor!  

I made twice and found greater success sprinkling top with kosher salt and pepper.

They aren’t kidding about having extra potatoes on hand.   I’d say you’re safe with up to 6lbs if you slice as directed 1/8 inch.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a lovely addition to our Christmas dinner this year. I used 4 1/2 lbs of potatoes and they fit perfectly in a 9x13 pan. I used a bit more cheese than the recipe called for. Next time I think I would triple the garlic (6 cloves), add a couple of Tbsp. Dijon and maybe 1/4 tsp pepper flakes to give it a little oomph.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made for a large, complicated Christmas dinner. Did the first hour of cooking in the morning, then the last 30 minutes of crisping just before serving. Worked out great. There was quite a bit of oily liquid when cooked. It scooped it out with a spoon (couldn't find the turkey  baster) which made the potatoes just right-- crispy on top, soft on the bottom, rich but not greasy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These were delicious! I will say they def got a bit more oily than I expected, but no one minded. And I was hoping for a bit more crunch on the top half (bottom was a bit soggy…) but honestly the flavor was so good, I’ll keep trying til I find the right balance for me! Put a pan underneath for sure. (And for what it’s worth, I added a dash of Dijon and nutmeg.)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent - followed reciipe as written

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved this recipe! The flavors were next level. I did add some fresh Monterey cheese to the topping which I’d do again. Only complaint is the potatoes did not cook all the way through even after extra time and the next night having them cooked for another hour. Too crunchy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was the star of our Christmas Dinner this year! I had a large crowd coming so I doubled the recipe. I used a large, rectangular casserole dish and lined the potatoes in rows.  Don't skimp on the thyme.  After the first hour the slices were still not cooked through and I got a bit worried, but I trusted the recipe. Sprinkling on the extra cheese turned the top into a glorious, light brown crust and the dish was cooked to perfection! The crowd devoured this! A  true keeper of a recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’ve read the reviews…all the additional directions for additions don’t work.  Ascorbic acid?  I’d rather mash the potatoes than risk another failed attempt at this truly sad recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made it for Thanksgiving and wished I would have just made mashed potatoes.  I believe that I followed the recipe closely, but somehow it just didn't come together.  Too watery and too garlicky.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these for my family and got rave reviews, just terrific.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So good.  Everyone at dinner loved it and returned for seconds.  Would definitely serve it again.  Enjoyed the creaminess of the dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was good, and special feeling, but I did not think the flavor/texture/etc. was proportional to the amount of effort involved.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I don’t have many uses for my mandoline, but this is definitely now one of them.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Had 11 people - used 5 potatoes and had leftovers.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added twice as much cheese - aged gouda, parm, a bit of aged blue cheese. Added savory, onion and garlic powder - tasted more like sour cream and onion chips - perfect, crispy on top, soft and creamy on the bottom, got rave reviews!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Three thoughts after a shaky first attempt 

1. Give yourself an hour of buffer time, none of the steps are difficult but with overall logistics, I overshot dinner by an hour despite hustling. A few other folks also seemed to read this as 60min in the oven but when you read carefully, its 90min.
2. Being in a bit of a rush, I guessed at the size of the caserole dish. After slicing up eight potatoes, I still ran well short of required potatoes and liquid. Having run out of time and liquid to slic

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Does anyone know if you can assemble the dish a day in advance and just pop it in the oven to finish?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: After reading every single note from other cooks, I couldn't wait to try these. In the end, they were OK, but not worth the hassle!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm mystified because this was pleasantly forgettable for me.  So many others rave about it.  It was somewhat dry, and I had to put sour cream on it to make it taste good enough to eat, and then it was like an ordinary baked potato.  In the picture, the potatoes are leaned over, and mine were straight-up, so I suspect I may have overpacked them (too tight).  I will try this again less tightly packed and see.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Loved this dish and second time I cooked it. Good rating from the family too. 
 The only thing I would change is to reduce cheese by half and leave out the butter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very tasty but I thought it was a little dry. I definitely didn't have enough liquid to fill halfway up the dish after arranging the potatoes as the recipe stated, & could have used some 'excess liquid' to moisten it more. Will keep this in mind when I make it again. Still it was very tasty.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Question: I want to make this for the holiday, but I will be bringing it to someone else’s house. Do you suggest I do the first 30 minute bake at my house, refrigerate, and then do the last bake uncovered at their house? Or should I just make the entire thing at my house the day before, and then take it to their house the next day to reheat in the oven there? They will have limited oven space because of the turkey, so I really need to plan this one.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: ok, not sure what I did wrong, but the gruyere didn't fully melt and was a bit clumpy, tops were crispy and potatoes cooked through perfectly, Thoughts??

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We had extra cheese mixture after making two gratin batches, so I put the last of it in a carrot soup I made. I also added a couple rinds from the Gruyère And it was super satisfying!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made these with sweet potatoes in a medium size staub bakeware dish and here are my notes. 
Grab more potatoes than you think you need. Put a sheet pan underneath as the cream mixture does bubble up and drip. These really did cook for almost 2 hours and prob could have gone longer to get a bit more crispy. Everyone loved these 1000x more than the mashed sweet potatoes with marshmallows (yuck)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I recommend being careful with a mandoline, although it makes the slicing so much easier! I sliced through a bit of my hand this morning (Thanksgiving morning) so we will have a little extra protein in the form of human skin and blood mixed into our sweet potato hasselback.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Definitely choose harder, more mature versions of the Gruyère and Parmesan!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used one of those Apple peeler/slicers and it made preparing the potatoes a breeze. Easy clean up too

author: Emily Weinstein
