commentBody: Always a winner. A little labour intensive for me but my love of Gruyere makes it worth it! I used too much liquid this time but it was still delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Easy and very good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow. Just wow. So so so so good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am hoping to dovetail this recipe with a rib roast.  The roast will be in at 350 for most of the cooking time.  Has anyone ever tried this at a lower temp for a longer duration?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I found these to be mediocre. I admit that I did mistakenly purchase half and half instead of heavy cream, otherwise I followed the recipe as written. I will not be making them again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Really luxurious and delicious. Made 3 pans, one 1/2 qt gratin dish with Yukons, another with sweet potatoes, and then the rest in a 9 x 5 Pyrex loaf pan (both kinds of potatoes). A holiday treat

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These turned out amazing. I used a food processor to cut the potatoes, and they ended up being various thicknesses probably due to the angle of the blade. Some slices were very thin. As a result, the left over liquid was pretty starchy, so I just spread it over the top. Still turned out great. Next time I'll try yukons instead of russets.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My son made these for Christmas this year.  Incredibly delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this tonight. I would salt more generously. Otherwise, amazing, as expected. I did fry up some shallots and toss them in the cheese mixture. A special day dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made exactly as shown and they came out soooo good! Will definitely be making again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved this and even tasted great as leftovers in a family that usually doesn't eat them. On day 2 or 3, cut into pieces, cover with some grated parm and pop into the air fryer.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It was…okay? No idea why I did wrong. The tops of the potatoes were dry and the sides of the pan were burned. I doubled the amount of cheese topping at the end to save it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this about a dozen times.  It always turns out fantastic.  sometimes I leave out the garlic because some in the household don't like the flavour.  This recipe is 10 out of 10!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: scales up perfectly for 5 lbs in a qt baking dish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My potatoes leeched a lot of water in the first bake and even though I only half filled it , because my potatoes were packed tightly the casserole dish overflowed— may be good to not add any remaining cream sauce in the first bake and reserve it for the second or third bake

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this per recipe with substitution of yellow potatoes. Though it’s tasty with cheese etc, don’t feel it’s worth the effort to install potatoes vertically.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very happy with this dish.   I made a much smaller portion and it worked out well.   It was a test for the holidays.  It will be great!  Made it just as the recipe stated.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can any of this be made the night before? Or best to make all the day of?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can the prep and cook time for this be cut down considerably by using frozen grated or diced potatoes?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The key to Hasselbacks potatis is in the slicing the potatoe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Makes a MacDonald burger seem like diet food.  I figured this dish provides about 
750 calories per serving.  Sounds good though.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’m probably going to get drummed out of the comments section for this, but here goes nothing!  I really dislike gruyere cheese.  Thoughts on other cheeses to try here?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Susan Lydem you can use any melty cheese that suits your fancy! Immona try it with sharp cheddar.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes, Hasselbacks Potatoes were invented at the Hasselbacken Restaurant in the Royal Djurgården (Royal Animal Haven) island in central Stockholm, Sweden.  This happened just before the end of the 19th century.  In Swedish it's called Hasselbackspotatis.  And, it's still as loved today as back then.  It's an easy way of cooking something that has an extravagant touch to it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be made the day before and just cooked on Tanksgiving?  Or should it be fully cooked and reheated on the day of the dinner?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you make this in advance? If so, where should you stop the process?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: In the video Kenji says "no" to making it in advance because the potatoes would oxidize and turn an unappetizing color (but "it won't kill you").

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I wanted to love this recipe—but even after 100 minutes in the oven, the potatoes were still crunchy. Flavor is excellent. I just want softer potatoes!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Barrett same issue! I ran out of potatoes- so used a yam and simply shuffled it into the stacks to fill space. I took it out of the oven at time- then chose a random disc to test- so soft but…sweet? I had forgotten that I had interspersed those yam pieces. I chose another disc from another spot, same thing! Then I remembered, LOL what are the chances? Third try found a potato, and yes- al dente! Returned to oven for an additional 20 minutes at lower heat. Next time will simply slice thinner than the 1/4” recommended (I did think that was pretty thick while prepping…)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Danny This is such an easy, uncomplicated recipe. If it was too much for you, good luck with the rest of this app.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Hello, Can't wait to make this. Can you make the day before?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: can you make these ahead?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed instructions to a T, but the sauce broke. Any ideas what may have gone wrong?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mine did too, then the next time, my friend who is a chef told me to use a higher % fat. I found Darigold 40% heavy whipping cream at Costco and did another dry run today before Thanksgiving, it did not break and tasted great! I added a little extra cheese as well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you prep this dish the day before? Will it keep?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Jax I searched “in advance” and found some suggestions.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
BRAVO!!! Thank YOU, Swedes!!! Something truly WORTHY of our Thanksgiving and Christmas family feasts!!!

And, you CAN modify to exactly your preference, too...those are ALWAYS the best recipes!

We get soooo tired of most, as their CLICK magnets, with one adding say, salt, another adds, pepper, a third adds cinnamon .... NO REAL contribution, but GRERAT for the repetitive adverts...which we NEVER 'see'!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow... so much heavy cream, cheeses, and butter.  Not for those of us watching our heart health (and perhaps weight). I guess some people believe you can indulge in almost anything...as long as it's just on major holidays like Thanksgiving!  Cheers!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just the word I love to hear in reference to my food, engineered.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I strongly recommend watching the video of Kenji López-Alt (I think it was just added) -when you see the amounts of spices he uses, you will understand how some folks think this recipe is bland!  He gives reasoning for some things that we’ve questioned; why he prefers Russets, why he adds a lot more salt, etc.  And he recommends slicing the potatoes 1/8 of an inch thick, which should help the dish to fully cook.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you prep  ahead then back later

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So delicious. I used my food processor to slice the potatoes, worked like a charm and saved so much time. Also kept the skins on. Definitely add all of the seasoning and a bit more.  Next time I will add more garlic and salt. I will be making this for Christmas for sure.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving dinner. I had 3 lbs of russet potatoes so I scaled the recipe down. The only addition was some grated nutmeg which I usually add to any creamy dish. I used a mandoline to slice the potatoes. I could have added another potato to make the slices fit snuggly, but it worked out. I baked this in a convection oven at 400 degrees and it was finished in less than 90 mins. I usually bake recipes at about 25 degrees lower with the convection on, and probably should have done so for the final 30 mins. It came out great, and was relatively simple to assemble. It’s a very rich recipe and I can see myself making this for dinner parties.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How much salt?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Watch the YouTube video…more than you’d think!
@Jordan Coulston

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Jordan Coulston more than you think that you need. I added salt and the cheeses together to the cream and then tasted. The amount of salt depends on how salty your Parmesan is so adjust accordingly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These were fantastic! I combined sweet and russet potatoes, alternating around the casserole dish. They were freaking fantastic.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is the cheese measured by weight or volume?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for thanksgiving after seeing Kenji’s video. I added too much cheese and forgot to peel. It was still pretty good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I read the comments and tried this recipe using sweet potatoes.  It was awful.  I sliced the potatoes very thinly on a mandolin, used gruyere and grana pandamo, and followed the rest of the recipe exactly.  The potatoes and cheese scorched on the edges, the potatoes never cooked through and an oily mess surrounded the contents.  Yuck!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made it as is and it was amazing!  #asis

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is fire! 🔥 10 out of 10! Everyone loved it!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My family and I loved this recipe. It is creamy, rich, and flavorful. The instructions were spot on.  Had it for Thanksgiving and may do it again for Christmas. Yes, it's that good. (May try it with sweet potatoes as noted in a comment below. That sounds delicious too!)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this two years in a row (Thanksgiving )
As I was ever hopeful of these perfect ingredients doing each their best.  Nope. Cheese breaks. Potatoes do not cook through even with so much added time. Greasy mess. Not the luscious cheesy potato dish of your dreams. Do not fall for it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this dish 3 times, and each time has not cooked through. I would say that russets are the wrong choice and a softer potato is necessary. I am just going back to good, old fashioned gratins. :(

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these for Thanksgiving with sweet potatoes as others suggested. People swooned— not exaggerating. I used 4-1/2 sweet potatoes packed tightly in an 8” square Pyrex dish and didn’t change anything about the recipe. Same cook times too, except it did not need all the final 30 minutes. It serves more than you might think because it’s a very rich dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely incredible. The hit of Thanksgiving. I doubled the recipe to serve 14 people and used a larger oval dish with no issues. Made sure the creamy mixture was generously salted so the potatoes had lots of flavor. 10/10

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I followed directions perfectly and they were really dry and disappointing for all the work.  I was bummed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and followed the recipe exactly…it was AMAZING! Voted number one dish from some pretty incredible chefs. The only thing I did a little differently is make the cheese mixture a day ahead. I think that boosted the flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I followed this recipe to the TEE...I could hardly wait to try them!!!  However....the potatoes were still hard and crunchy after all of that time in the oven! Granted that I am far from a chef, but maybe someone will have an obvious suggestion that I should try next time.  I'm not giving up!!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: OK....after rereading the recipe and also the appearance of the articles photo I realized my BIG MISTAKE r/t my potatoes being under cooked....I rushed out to buy a mandolin at Walmart and there was only one choice FARBERWARE....which didn't allow for depth settings!!  So my slices were probably 1/4 inch, not 1/8 inch....darnit!!!  So I will attempt this recipe once again after I have an appropriate mandolin.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great recipe,  cooked as instructed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made exactly per the recipe. Mixed reviews from my family. The ratio of effort to flavor makes this a recipe I won’t prepare again. Buy a boxed mix or frozen au gratin and save yourself the effort.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you do this in an iron skillet? I don't have a round casserole dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this and halved the recipe with sweet potatoes for two. I used all the cream and it filled the pan. The finished dish tasted good but was more like a purée. After reading the reviews I know I did something wrong. Too much cream, I guess. Any other tips to make it less mushy?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these and they were a huge hit on Thanksgiving. Thank you Kenji!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this 2 days before my dinner party as a practice run.  Turned out beautifully.  Want to cover it and keep it refrigerated until the party.  Has anyone made it in advance?  Did it stay crispy?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I wanted these to work.  I have made them twice and everyone was intrigued by the concept and flavor,  but the duce broke birth times.  what i saw was that curd and whey mess.  I was expecting a creamy sauce,  like a Mac and cheese consistency.  
what did I do wrong?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is an amazing and foolproof recipe. I made the potato version and add-libbed the recipe and used butternut squash as a 2nd side dish (using a mandolin). In addition to the listed ingredients, I added 1/2 cup crumbled feta cheese, 1/3 cup brown sugar and 1/4 cup fresh mint to the squash version. The salty and the sweet with the squash was off the charts. I made both versions for Thanksgiving (large group) and everyone wanted the recipes for both. Highly recommend.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made these for Thanksgiving and again a week later. Now my sister wants me to make them for Christmas. Soooo goooood!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Could I use an iron skillet for this recipe?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving and it was not the greatest.  The flavors were great but my cheese melted into oil because of the time it took to cook. I followed everything exactly but it may be better of the potato was parboil because after an hour there were still crunchy slices and I used a mandoliner so the slices were paper thin almost.  I really had high expectations for this.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be put together the night before?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: how long did you bake them in muffin tins? @christine Andrews

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this last night. Followed all the baking directions and was a little disappointed. The potatoes came out a little firmer than I would have liked. Not sure if I packed them too tight or didn’t get enough cream between layers.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Jennifer how did the modifications work?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not sure what went wrong. It didn’t taste good. Very greasy and not creamy or cheesey. Threw it all into the trash. Complete waste. So disappointed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Pro tip: don’t use pre grated Parmesan cheese with extra cellulose in it because it soak up all the cream mixture. I had to compensate with adding extra milk to have enough liquid go halfway up the casserole.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just made this and it’s delicious! I put everything in a 9x9 pan with the potatoes arranged row by row. I had less cream than I thought so I did half cream and half chicken broth. I had a lot of liquid left over.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used a bit more minced garlic, 4 big cloves.  A heaping tablespoon of fresh thyme is about right — you can really taste the flavor of the THYME.  Next time I will salt and pepper a more, alternating when hand turning the liquid with the potatoes.  THANKS, it was a real people pleaser and looked lovely on the table.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these for Christmas dinner. They were so good and I’ll definitely make them again. As others suggested, I used an apple peeler/slicer. I wish the recipe specified how much salt to use. I was worried about over salting. Based on suggestions, I added about 1T, which was the perfect amount.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
No. I prefer my gratin potatoes horizontal rather than vertical.

I made them exactly as directed through step 2, then added the cheese and did step 3 about 5 hours later.

They were undercooked for the dinner party, and did not have enough sauce. 

Next day, I cooked the leftovers with some cream in a sauce pan for an additional 15 minutes and they were fine but a long way from creamy well done.

I prefer a more traditional gratin where you have a better notion of when the potatoes are done.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delish but depends on size of dish used cooking time varies.  Be sure to check potatoes.  Also added extra cheese and garlic.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wish there was more guidance on the salt, definitely did not do enough in mine (1tsp). Bake time was on point in terms of crispiness.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Semi-fail for Christmas dinner. Big problem: used Japanese sweet potatoes and they just did not work — forever in the oven and still hard; I ended up having to microwave. Also, the exposed surfaces quickly discolored. I will try once more (sometime) with russet or Yukon Gold. Other notes: (1) can’t imagine making without a mandoline; and (2) I made a 1.5x recipe which fit well in my 9x13 glass pan. (Glass pan may also have impaired cooking, now that I think about it).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These are perfect for a crowd. No notes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: These potatoes look amazing, but they lack a bit of flavor. Add a tablespoon of Dijon mustard to the saucy mix and a packet of Hidden Valley Ranch seasoning, and you’ll find yourself in pomme de terre bliss.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Fantastic - a new Christmas tradition is born! Crunchy at the top and soft at the bottom

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very tasty! I added about 3-4 tbsp horseradish, used caramelized onion cheddar cheese and another cheese with white truffle, and instead of thyme I used herbs de province. At higher altitude, it took about 1.5 hours to bake and came out perfectly!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Tip: I put the peeled potatoes in the casserole dish to see where I stand with quantity i.e. if I need to peel more or if I need to get a small second dish out for the excess. Nothing worse than needing to retrieve/peel/slice more potatoes while your hands are drenched in cream sauce! 

I used Yukon gold and the texture was lovely but the potatoes were very sweet naturally so that was a little off putting. 

I used whatever random cheeses I had in the fridge and it was awesome! Gruyère/swiss mix, grated smoked Gouda, and Parmesan. Will definitely cook again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did I miss a step where the oven needed to be turned down? Mine were very brown (not pale golden) at the end of the second 30 minutes so I added the cheese and only baked a couple of minutes more. Thankfully they were all cooked. Also, my sauce split. Kenji is a great chef, so I’m thinking I did something wrong. Great flavor though.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this for NYE dinner and it was a hit. My slices stuck together so tossing them with the cream mixture as in the video wasn’t working. So I picked up the slices one by one, swirled them around in the mixture, then stacked them in 2-inch units before arranging them vertically in the baking dish. It took about 15 minutes to fill my 8-inch square dish but it was worth the effort. Will absolutely make this again! Do place a tray underneath the baking dish in the oven to catch the drips.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent and surprisingly easy with a mandolin. Love the crusty top and bottom!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this yesterday and followed exactly. I heeded people’s comments to make sure the mixture is well seasoned and salted. It came out phenomenal!  The flavor was incredible, and the potatoes were done perfectly. Every mandolin is different based on quality/consistency. To get the thickness observed in the video, I had to dial mine to 3/8, as 1/8 on my mandolin was giving me almost potato chip type thickness (too thin). But this recipe is definitely a keeper and I’ll be making this again for sure!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these for Thanksgiving and Christmas… everyone LOVED them!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For a low carb version I replaced the potatoes with 4 lbs. of daikon radishes sliced 1/8" thick. I parboiled the slices until fork-tender, drained them well, and proceeded with the potato recipe. The cheesy sauce hides the very faint radish taste daikon has perfectly. You won't miss the potatoes! This recipe makes large servings (about 2/3 lb. per serving), a bit much for a weeknight meal. Next time I will cut ingredients in half and use a 1 quart casserole dish, adjusting cook time as needed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I needed more liquid after pouring the remaining over the potatoes so I added about a cup of cream.  Very delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made a mixture with cream and cheese - and then, if you fill the dish only halfway, you have to discard so much of it! And it's cream and cheese, both delicious and expensive. Why? Will just reduce the amount of the mixture in the future, since I'm sure the potatoes will still soak in what they need. Delicious otherwise!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Huge hit with my family!  Only changes I made was adding more garlic (and probably more cheese:). Used my mandolin and it made quick work of slicing potatoes .  Another commenter suggested baking the potatoes for about half the time earlier in the day and then finishing the baking just before the meal.  This worked beautifully for my time constraints and oven space.    Absolutely Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am surprised to be saying this, but the recipe was too rich.  I actually felt "full" just smelling it and had to air out my house afterwards.  I followed exactly except used new potatoes with skins and bought good quality cheese.  I think if I cut the sauce in half, that might have been better. It look pretty, but for someone who loves cheesy things, I was surprised I didn't love it more.  Guests liked it, but not a "go-to" recipe like I was expecting.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm not sure what I did wrong, but these were a disappointment.   They smelled fantastic, but seemed relatively low on flavor.   I've seen other comments about making a roux to ensure that the sauce doesn't separate.  Will have to do that if I make these again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Read the recipe, looked good and I've been meaning to try it for some time.  Don't waste your time.  Not worth the effort -- very disappointing.  Smelled fantastic in the oven, great presentation to the table ... but heavy, dry, a relatively tasteless on the plate.  Feeding the disposal with half of it.  Cooking time or temp seems off -- my oven runs coolish, and this was golden at 45 minutes (added cheese topping then) and deep brown an hour in.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Dry?? Tasteless? What did you leave out?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just made this for Easter and it was an enormous hit.  Following some suggestions, I used skin-on potatoes, added paprika and used about 20% more Gruyère.  I used a Breville food processor with the slicer set to 2 - it was perfect.  I doubled the recipe and cooked in an 11x14 Pyrex.  The cooking times were perfect and the potatoes came out beautifully browned with bubbling cheese.  Delicious.  Start to finish was about 2-3/4 hours, including the time to slice the potatoes and grate the cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this this weekend as a side for Easter dinner. Definitely dont sleep on the salt and the flavor will turn out fine. Now if you are like me, and will take a caliper directly to a sliced potato to confirm spec, you will find that a consistent 1/8" thickness is too thick to completely cook in the time identified. Again, still good, but the potatoes were all still toothepme. I will definitely be making this again but at an actual thickness of probably 1/16".

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: absolutely delicious! My casserole dish was a little too big so I was afraid the potatoes wouldn't cook correctly, but it was fine. Apparently this is now the only potato dish allowed at future family gatherings.  I used russets and they were perfect - but look forward to trying other types of potatoes as mentioned int he comments. The only thing I did differently was steeping the thyme and a clove of garlic in the cream before hand. I let the cream cool before proceeding with the recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made these for Easter brunch yesterday, made true to recipe.  One pan using russet and one sweet potato's.  They were both a huge hit.  My one comment/question is it normal for the fatt to seperate from the cream or cheese?  After they were done both dishes had a substancial amount of greese floating on the top.  I used my turkey baster to suck the oily stuff out as it was just too much.  I don't think that changed the flavor or texture, best dish I ever took to a potluck and I will make again

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I dunno…made this exactly per recipe for Easter. Didn’t feel like it had much flavor. Needs WAY more salt, didn’t taste the thyme at all, expense of Gruyère kind of wasted. Not really worth the effort.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is it possible to prepare this in advance, refrigerate it and bake it off later in the day? if so, are any adjustments needed?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Based on reading all the comments, these are the modifications I made and they came out perfectly. I recommended making additional sauce of you are using a bigger casserole dish because I ran out towards the end and had to make more. I also made it through the second bake the day before.

1. Increase heat to 425 for first 2 bakes; 400 for last
2. Add 1-2 tbsp Dijon mustard to cheese/cream mixture
3. Double the cheese and divide between sauce and topping
4. Increase kosher salt to 1.5-2 tsp (to taste)
5. Add lots of pepper
6. Increase garlic (up to 6 cloves)
7. Replace some cream with sherry
8. Don't pack potatoes too tightly
9. All bakes: 45 minutes
10. Last bake add cheese in last 15 minutes

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: From an old Bob Appetite recipe, puree about a third of the corn kernels in a blender with a little of the stock before adding it to the risotto. It makes a wonderful creamy risotto. No need to add cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thanks for sharing the 'creamed corn' tip. I was out of cream, and didn't want to leave to get any! It was a perfect substitute.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thanks for the tip, I made the recipe today and blended some corn and used it instead of the cream. It was delicious

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Larry G. Thx for the tip to blend 1/3 of kernels in place of the cream. I also used only 1/2 cup of Parmesan and 3/4 tsp of salt. It was very flavorful.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great summer dinner! Lobsters were on sale, so I bought two. After they were cooked and I removed the meat I included the shells when I made the corn stock. I chopped the lobster meat into chunks and added it at the end just before the whipped cream. I also added a bit of fresh tarragon. We have tomatoes in the garden, so I served the risotto with a simple tomato salad. Fresh tomatoes, corn and lobster: a perfect summer combination!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can we come over to your house for dinner?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: That sounds legit amazing man. You actually made me hungry

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I come to your house?!? Yum!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Still dreaming of this dish! Cooked it exactly to the recipe (no modifications) using a Chardonnay for the wine, home made chicken stock, and Parmigiano Reggiano. I served it in a bowl with a good chewy but crusty bread that I toasted in the oven and brushed with melted butter. My boyfriend couldn't stop raving about it, and almost ate the whole pot! He kept saying it tasted like something from a restaurant. Pretty sure I'm getting a ring some time soon.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So....update on the ring???

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent recipe. I made it even better by chopping a few slices of thick cut bacon and frying them prior to adding the onions. Fry bacon until crispy and remove until the end, then return to the risotto when adding the cheese. Enjoy!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved what was happening at the almost-done stage; the corn stock/fresh corn flavors were at the same time delicate and definite. Then, darn! I love cream, but its effect is to flatten flavor. I might use my go-to secret weapon next time (a bit of roasted poblano) but not sure. Don't want to bury the corn taste.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My grandmother taught me to make creamed fresh corn using corn cut off the cob.  Fresh corn needs only 3 or 4 minutes of cooking when making creamed corn, why does the recipe call for such a long time to cook the corn?  I think it could be added much later in the process.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love poblano and corn together. Each enhances the other. I’m going to try this recipe again and add a little! Great suggestion.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did not use the whipped cream and liked it better than the first time I made it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm going to try that next time too!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this in the Instant Pot! Sautéed leeks, then added rice until translucent. Added ½ cup wine, 1.5 cups rice, 3 cups corn stock, 2 cups corn. 5 minutes at high pressure, 15 minutes natural release than quick release. Added 1 cup parm, and a few spoonfuls of ricotta for creaminess. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Boiled 4 ears corn 4-5 minutes ( or just grill the corn ohhh yeah)  - added it to risotto on last cup of stock instead of midway. hybrid stock used chicken stock then simmered the corn cobs and other ingredients in it, green onions instead of leak.

Also sautéed green onion, 1/4 onion, thyme, sage, rosemary with rice during 6 min before wine goes in.   Added pinch salt pepper and sugar, used more parm, Creme fraiche instead of whipping cream.  Bomb af. Awesome recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious way to show off fresh corn. I did make the corn stock and I thought it was easy to throw together. I didn't need as much stock as the recipe called for, maybe 5 cups. I forgot to put the cream in and it was still delicious AND creamy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: On the recommendation of previous cooks, I added more corn. I also fried some diced bacon, drained it and cooked the leeks in the rendered fat. I had some frozen homemade chicken stock and threw in a couple of the cobs to boil with the stock as I made the risotto. Returned the bacon bits after adding the butter and Parmesan. Got rave reviews from the tall guy who eats my cooking : )

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can start using the corn cob broth after ten minutes of stewing, while sauteeing the leeks/onions/rice, and adding the wine. Keep the broth cooking as you add a ladle at a time to the risotto. I just made this in one hour, much shorter than the listed time. Excellent! Fresh corn is essential, more than listed in the recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love this recipe! I have made it a few times with vegetable Better than Bouillon and canned corn, and it still turns out delicious (though I'm sure the homemade corn stock and fresh corn are even better). I also skip the cream, and agree with the commenters who have said it isn't necessary. You'll get to the last step, realize you've already made a really good risotto, and think to yourself "wait, why am I about to go to the trouble of whipping a bunch of cream"? At least that's what I did.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Incredible flavor. Doesn't need cream at the end.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So excited about having my way with this recipe, although I do have a question.  Is there possibly a typo of using 6 cups of corn stock for 1 cup of Arborio?  Typically I would use about half of this amount of liquid.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: No, I used the full 6 cups and it was perfect.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used chicken stock to make the corn stock instead of water and I pureed some of the corn with the corn stock but still added the cream.  I topped each serving with 6 sea scallops sauteed in butter, inspired by a delicious dish I had at The Corner Kitchen in Asheville, NC.  This dish is the taste of summer at its finest.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delicious. I had a hankering for crab and corn risotto, so I made this risotto and added a 1lb of lump crab at the end and it was incredible. I made the corn stock as written, but then after 30 minutes, I removed everything, returned the cobs, and let them steep until I was ready to use it. I think I ended up using about 4 ears of corn, which resulted in a risotto that felt equal parts rice, corn, and crab. The balance was perfect. I would have paid good money for this at a restaurant.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used corn stock that was already in our freezer, which made prep. quicker.  Needed less than 4 cups of broth.  Whipping the cream was a bother, but worth it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great idea.  very sweet..   dropped the cream and stuck with butter and aged white cheddar to give a creamy sauce.  Also, it needs some kick.  Added a bit of horseradish, hungarian pepper with the corn and a the juice of a lemon at the end.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Alternatives:

1. Puree 1/3 of the cooked/grilled corn with some stock, then add when putting remaining corn in.

2. Bacon. 

3. Lobster (2) - add shells if making corn stock. 

4. Poblano Pepper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made this following the instructions exactly on night one and it was great. I decided to try it the next night in the instant pot and without the heavy cream. We enjoyed night two better because the corn retained its freshness since it wasn’t cooked so long while slowly adding corn stock. Either way it was great!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Summer on a plate!  Agree that the corn stock is a must for depth of flavor.  This goes beautifully with a simple tomato salad on the side.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made the corn stock on the stove, then used an Instant Pot to cook the leeks, adding salt, pepper, carnaroli rice (instead of arborio) and wine. I poured 3 cups of corn stock in the IP and cooked on high pressure for 6 mins, followed by manual release. Then switch to medium saute and stir in uncooked corn, before adding cheese, and a cup of stock. Mix well, and at desired consistency, add the chives. I skipped the cream and it was still dee-lish! For arborio, reduce pressure cook to 4 mins.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did this using Orzo rather than arborio. It was wonderful. And considerably easier.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe was delicious but it definitely does NOT need any heavy cream nor a full cup of parmesan. I highly recommend only about a half of a cup of parmesan and no cream for the perfect level of richness.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Whole family enjoyed this one. Went great with some salmon. Don't skip the corn stock if you have the time - it isn't hard and adds a ton of flavor!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this in the InstantPot, as I have made enough risottos on the stove to last a lifetime. It worked so well! I used 1 1/2 C arborio and 3C fabulous chicken stock that I added 4 corn cobs to. Cooked 5 min, so delicious, no cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you want to make an easy version with these same flavors, make Ina Garten’s “easy Parmesan ‘risotto’”, but sauté the leek in butter as described here, add it with the rice and hot stock and then 1/2 way through baking time add the corn, give it a stir and finish baking and finish the recipe as Ina describes.   Made it last night with ‘heavenly halibut’ from Allrecipes and a salad with a lemon shallot vinaigrette and it was amazing

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow! Fantastic recipe. After the water reduced in the stock, added chicken stock to yield 8 cups of liquid. Increased rice to 2 cups. Didn’t bother to whip the cream. just stirred in to the hot risotto with the parm and butter. Great with shrimp served on top, sautéed in butter and olive oil with sea salt and seaweed, pepper and garlic powder. Sprinkled chives (or slivered scallion greens) on top.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great recipe but … save your heart and don’t add the cream. The creamed corn or just adding a little Greek yoghurt or crème fraîche is tastier, less heavy and more righteous!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wasn't too crazy about it - I thought it was a bit bland. Since I like using fresh corn in different ways, I might make it again, but add a little red pepper flakes or garlic next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sautéed a pound of medium shrimp in butter…added the cooking liquid from the pan to the corn stock. Added cooked shrimp right before the five minute rest.  Shrimp and corn risotto rocked.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love Larry G.'s tip to puree some corn instead of using cream!!  Also, I find that risotto gets creamier (and is easier to stir) when cooked without any additional ingredients.  To that end, instead of putting the corn in half-way, I pre-cook it - simmer for 10 minutes in the bouillon  - and then add it at the end.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was excellent! The leeks melted right into the rice although their flavor was definitely there.  Followed the recipe exactly and served with some blackened shrimp for a contrast bite.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We enjoyed this. I made the corn broth which made it much more aromatic. Instead of an onion I used the leek trimmings and it was still good. The only substantive change I made though was to reduce the heavy cream and parmesan cheese. I only used a little bit of each because I wanted to taste the corn. No reason to bother making a corn broth and then add a cup of parmesan cheese and all that heavy cream. A sprinkle of cheese and a splash of cream was sufficient and you could still taste the corn

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Oh my! This is going to be Elotes Risotto with chopped Serrano chilis and subbing mayonnaise with cream. Oh yes!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Ok, so I haven't made this yet but I will. But here's the thing. I have been making risotto for years and with all the variations there are some constants. Risotto is supposed to have some chew or al dente if you wish. Coking it for 30-40 will result in mush. Sounds like a very tasty mush, but more like soup. The amount of stock is way high. Generally I use 6-8 cups for 2 cups of rice this calls for 6 for 1. Hence the soup. Check other NYT risotto recipes. 20-25 minutes cooking.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Try it..you’re also cooking the corn which needs the extra stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Ya know what…I had the same thought, cuz my risotto is ALWAYS perfect after 20 minutes, but I just made this and with all that juicy corn it took all of 40 minutes to cook down. I use Acquerello and it was perfectly creamy and still al dente. I can’t explain why it’s not porridge after all that time, but it just worked!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’m making this just because of her intro article today 😂 how could I ignore such corn adoration

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Also easy to “veganize” this lovely recipe - use the corn purée as suggested earlier instead of the cream, and toss a handful of walnuts and a cup or so of nutritional yeast in a blender (leftover keeps tightly covered in the fridge for at least a couple weeks) to substitute for the Parmesan - I skipped the butter, but you could use any vegan butter substitute if you want it to be richer.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this 4-2. Veganized by using olive oil, subbing coconut and cashew milk poured on top (also added tsp white miso, tsp umami seasoning; Nutritional yeast would work too).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Lined a bowl with spinach. Add corn risotto, pan fried garlic shrimp with lemon. Pair w garden salad and you have a meal.   Fresh peaches and vanilla ice cream dessert!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I have made this with bacon, using only a little of the bacon grease to cook the onion (or leeks). 

Also have made it with shrimp that I sauteed first and set aside.  

No cream ever!

And I highly recommend making the corn stock.  I may never throw away another cob again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious. Added shrimp shells to the stock (& ditched the carrot/ celery--just leek greens, onion, garlic, corn cobs, salt). I put in 8 cups of water & ended up with 4 cups of lovely yellow stock, which was all I needed as I adapted it for the instant pot. Used 3 ears of corn, and pureed a bit with some stock as suggested previously. Used 3 cups of stock in the instant pot; when it was done, stirred in corn kernels first and then the corn puree and then 1/2 cup of Parm. Ate with shrimp. Yum!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I chose to add lobster and a little bacon, I didn't add the cream and the dish was still creamy, next time I may try blending or roasting the corn first.  this was a winner - family loved it

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow.  Loved this.  Made a few tweaks.  Did three cobs of corn.  Tossed the cobs in my simmering chicken stock to add corn flavor without making the corn stock. I took about 1/2 cup of the kernels and sauteed in butter with shallot then pureed with a ladleful of stock to add creaminess to the risotto. I omitted the cream. Was fantastic with several layers of flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is excellent. Added some kielbasa atop it, just because we had it, but dang is this risotto good. Thank you, Jessica and Emily!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The addition of scallops makes this dish over the top delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So delicious! I followed the suggestion to blend water and corn (to eliminate heavy cream) and it was a smart move. I folded in fresh basil right before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The stock was a snap to make in 10 minutes in the instant pot.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this to pair with boiled lobsters for a dinner party and this risotto was a crowd pleaser! After reading the notes, I started by cooking pancetta in the pan before the leeks (still added a little butter), removed and the followed the recipe to a T. Added the pancetta in after the cream (it’s only 1/4 cup I don’t know why some comments act like this waters down the whole thing) and added the chives. So delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I've been making risotto for years. It's not necessary to stir constantly. Just stir often, vigorously. 

This is a stellar dish worthy of a fine restaurant and it's easy to make. Using a good European butter and going to the bother of whipping the cream is worth it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was fabulous. Added a fresh, chopped hatch chile along with the chopped leeks and used an extra ear of corn in both the stock and risotto. Adjusted the water to accommodate the extra volume. Didn’t add cream this time. Will add a lemon peel for brightness next time. 5/5!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @tiffany - an excellent idea to add chopped fresh Hatch chile, since it’s August in SONM - Chile time!!  I will try this tomorrow.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Cheated and made this in the instant pot.  (100% Italian - I don't do risotto on the stove anymore) with corn from our CSA and chicken bone broth.  After sautéing the corn, leek, garlic and rice in butter, dumped in 2 cups of chicken stock and cooked it on high pressure for 6 minutes with quick release of steam.  Stirred on keep warm for a couple of minutes while adding cheese.  Did not need the cream.  I could live on this in the summer.

Rice was tender, corn was crisp.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
If there were 10 stars, I would give it to this recipe.  I made the corn stock the previous day.  There were many steps, but so worth it.  I questioned the whipped cream at the end, but it was the crowning glory.

This is worthy of company food.  Served it with broiled, herbed walleye; my husband and I were in 7th heaven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very, very bland. Needs color, and flavor. If I make this again I'll (a) use cooked corn and (b) follow other cooks' suggestions to add something like crab meat. I did make the broth, my instant pot did all the work there.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Edith I agree.  To punch it up I added poblano peppers and a splash of Calabrian chile oil. I think my corn, which I had vacuum sealed and froze when corn was in season, was soggy once thawed and didn't help.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved it. There’s a lot of time standing at the stove stirring but that’s what audiobooks are for! You can leave it for a minute or two to tend to your other dishes. I whipped and added the cream but honestly I can’t imagine I could ever tell the difference with such a small amount of cream in an already very creamy dish. I did make the corn stock and it was very corn-y which was nice but also slightly on the sweet side for me, but my corn-loving husband ate every bit - no leftovers. Goes great with pork chops and artichokes for summer dinner!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Even though I had it ready to go, I bailed on finishing with the cream. As I was tasting I couldn’t imagine it was gonna add anything to what was already a wonderful dish. I think the cream would have muddled the pure corn flavors and made the final result too rich. I did squeeze in the juice of half a lemon, which brightened things with just a bit of acidity. Don’t pass on the chives…I actually used almost 3 tablespoons, plus a sprinkle on plating…they add a subtle, zesty oniony kick. 5 STARS!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delish! I made it up to the heavy cream part, and ran out of steam. But truthfully, I thought it tasted great as is so didn’t bother adding it! Creaming the corn would be a great addition, and a splash of lemon helps to brighten. I made the corn stock- super easy if you prep your kernels earlier. I’m sure the cream is a nice addition, but I was happy without.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this today with the last of the summer corn from the farmer’s market.  I took some of the suggestions from previous reviewers and we all loved it! Cooked some diced bacon and sautéed the fresh green onions (instead of leeks) in the fact.  Added bacon at end.  Pureed about 1/3 of the corn with the corn broth (which was delicious) and omitted the cream.  Had some cooked leftover lobster meat so I threw that in at the end with the cheese and bacon bits.  This is truly a special dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I accidentally bought orzo instead and it was one of the best things I’ve ever eaten. I served with chicken that was pan fried and finished cooking in a layer of crispy parm, sliced, on top. 10/10.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great with the addition of chopped fennel bulb with the leeks & chopped fennel frond at the end

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We absolutely loved this; I had a very large leek and omitted the chives and the cream, and I used three cobs instead of two. It accompanied leftover chili, and a bottle of Rioja rosé was perfect with this meal.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Make the corn stock with chicken broth as the liquid. Use 4 ears. Don't skip the cream just do it right. Have been making risotto for 30 years and this really is a good one.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Truly excellent recipe. I used onions instead of leeks because I'm a cheap-o grad student who picked up a bag of onions earlier in the week, and figured I'd get the same result. I added minced garlic to the risotto itself because you can't have too much garlic, and simply added the corn cobs to some basic chicken stock along with onion powder. Truly incredible.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: As a big fan of corn chowder, I loved this recipe! I used heavy cream but not whipped and it was fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I found the combination of corn, leeks, rice, whipped cream and Parmesan too bland. The next time, I punched it up with red onions (leaving out the leeks), a good spurt of lime juice (instead of the cream), 1/3 cup of shredded extra sharp white cheddar (instead of the Parmesan), a handful of chopped scallions (toward the end), and a good pinch of cayenne. Excellent!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Nice to see this again.  Made 2x; will again when corn is ready.  I use more corn and skip the cream.  If using chicken stock, add the corn cobs and leeks anyway.  I also use more garlic, but that's simply personal taste.  This can be a first course or side, but we make it a full meal.  As much as we love it this - and all risottos are LABOR INTENSIVE and killers of kitchen time.  Nothing quick about it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious without the heavy cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I, too, took the advice of others and added bacon (cooked the leeks in the bacon fat).  Doubled the corn, then puréed half of it to add instead of using cream. I caramelized the rest of the corn in brown butter (a la the best corn recipe from last summer...Julia Moskin's recipe for caramelized corn with mint, but without mint) and added that.  Always have corn cob stock in the freezer (it's great for polenta too).  This made great arancini the next night.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I used chicken stock then simmered the corn cobs and other ingredients in it, green onions instead of leak I also sautéed green onion, 1/4 onion, thyme, sage, rosemary with rice before wine goes in. 

Added a pinch salt pepper and sugar, more parm, Creme fraiche instead of whipping cream.

Bomb af.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made the corn stock as directed and was delicious. I wonder if using vegetable or chicken stock in place would overpower the delicate flavor of the corn? I think I'd make the stock every time.

I did not use a full cup of cheese; roughly 1/2 C Parmesan and 1/4 C Romano (only because I needed to use it up).

Used 1/4 C half-and-half instead of making whipped cream. I folded it in at the very end when directed to vigorously stir and season the risotto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sautéed chanterelles foraged by a friend topped this off beautifully, and contrasted nicely with the sweetness of the corn

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Simple, delicious and impressive. The corn cob stock really makes a difference, resulting in a nuanced risotto rich enough that we skipped the cheese and cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is a keeper. I have made it with corn stock and chicken stock and it was delicious both ways. Served it as a main with crusty bread and a salad. Leftovers were great as a side dish for grilled pork chops.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added a diced red onion and a minced jalapeño (minus seeds and spine) in the last ten minutes. I also added sautéed scallops to top. Total yumsville!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Unlike many other notes here, I followed this recipe to the letter. I thought the cream was a wonderful addition, the stock easy and definitely worth it, and the corn was a great contrast to the texture of the risotto. It was perfect and wonderful and I can’t wait to make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Extremely satisfying dish. Have made this twice with success and many compliments by following the recipe exactly (or as close as I could!) I suggest to add the cream little by little to your liking. 
Second time around I doubled the water for the corn stock and let simmer longer. Stored the extra in the fridge for a day or two and used for Elaine Louie’s Summer Corn Soup - terrific!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I left out the whipping cream altogether and I'm glad I did. It was very rich without it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
takes FOREVER do not make hungry
Make sure you have good parmesan 
green onions instead of leek worked

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cooked this for myself & partner. Delicious, BUT I had 2 comments we both agreed upon: (1) Adding the cream gives it an almost-corn chowder flavor. Still delicious, but a little unsuspected... (2) I would probably decrease the liquid by about 1/2 cup. Cooking at a fast simmer, it still tool ~50 minutes to cook down all the liquid (ladle-full by ladle-full), and at the end it came out a touch too liquid-y.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is one of our summer favorites.  I saute 1/3-1/2 of the corn kernels in a little bit of olive oil until they are browned - the combination of fresh and roasted corn adds a nice depth of flavor to the finished dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was very good. I didn’t have leeks so I used green onion instead. I don’t normally use cream in risotto and didn’t here. It was delicious. Some seared scallops would be a perfect accompaniment.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious. Perfect summer meal. Great for date night. The non-cook can do the constant stirring and feel a part of the fabulous meal!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Adding cream to any risotto seems unnecessarily unhealthy.  I'm guessing when I make this tonight without cream it will be lovely.  Sometimes I think you guys add ingredients to perfectly good recipes just to have something new to post. Like adding an S to a noun in Scrabble.   Its not really a new word, is it?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is delicious. Creamy on its own, I forgot to add the cream and it tasted just fine. I pan roasted the corn before adding to the risotto because i love the taste and crunch of charred corn. Also great with several tablespoons of fresh thyme just before serving. Will definitely make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is delicious. Even better with fresh basil and chopped cherry tomatoes right before serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Perfect recipe. Stiffening the peaks on the cream is a must! Added grilled lobster tail, pulled out and mixed in at the end as an extra little treat.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This may violate the purity of the corn flavor, but I added about 6 oz of roasted hatch Chiles in with the Onion. Stirred in some small tomato slices right at the end. Yum

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Didn't bother to whip the cream, though I suppose that would be pretty for guests... Otherwise, followed Larry G.'s advice and pureed some of the corn. Anyway, super yummy with a tomato salad from the garden.  A little muscadet and a good baguette, eatten outdoors on a tablecloth-clad table in August... who could ask for anything more?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Fantastic - corn stock came together quickly enough and gave a ton of flavor. Didn’t both with the cream but did purée 1/3 of corn with some stock, as other comments mentioned. Served with sliced scallions and roasted red pepper and mozzarella sausage - really outstanding,

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great summer dish. Served with trout. I followed Larry G’s advice and put a bit of the corn stock with a cobs worth of corn in a blender the added it to the risotto near the end. Made it very creamy and didn’t have to add additional cream. Forgot to add the extra butter at the end too and didn’t miss it. And I love butter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Leave out heavy cream

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious and decadent. May try less cream or crème fraiche to finish next time. Served on a bed of steamed spinach with seared scallops. Also had an arugula salad with a lemony dressing which was a nice contrast to the creamy and rich risotto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just made for the first time. Superb stock, happy I made more than suggested because I needed extra to finish the risotto. Did not do the cream, although I’m sure it’s divine. Next time will reduce the cheese a little to highlight the fresh corn flavor more. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this with 8 ears of corn from CSA haul. Used bacon fat instead of butter, otherwise followed the recipe (without doubling) except subbed cream cheese for cream (it's what I had). Fabulous - rich, creamy, corn-forward. Equally divine for breakfast with a poached egg and bacon strips on top.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'd love suggestions of other uses for the corn stock. It sounds easy and delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make a variety of risottos baked in the oven—no stirring needed! Has anyone tried this in the oven—perhaps adding the corn at then end?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love corn risotto -- my variation includes some toasted cumin seeds and tomatillos -- an idea from John Ash.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For the corn stock. I want to confirm is it 6 cups of water?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yep, but as always with risotto, it'd be a rare thing to use all the stock. You just don't want to run out toward the ends. I had about a cup left over.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The recipe sounds fabulous, but I’m trying to avoid too much cholesterol. I will try making it substituting olive oil for butter, and extra stock, maybe milk, or buttermilk for heavy cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this and watched it with "Full Metal Jacket", because CORN.  I mean, come on. And the recipe is BRILLIANT. My heathens requested andouille sausage on the side of the plate.  Whaddaya gonna do.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: OMG! the best thing I have cooked in years!!!! I did follow suggestions to incorporate lobster (which elevated it immensely). I used lobster stock  as I happened to have some (really, who doesn't?), but if you don't, make the corn stock as suggested and throw in the lobster shells. Then in step 5 add room temp cooked lobster meat. Devine, I tell you, devine!!!!! Some crusty bread drizzled with a good Olive oil and salt, heated in the oven and you have a perfect summer meal. *sigh*

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I added shallots in step one, oven roasted tomatoes and prawns with the parmesan. I used the corn slurry method instead of the heavy cream.  Also added a little thyme. Will definitely make.afain. This tastes like summer!

Used 4 corn cobs and kernels instead of 2.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: don't be put off by the listed time - this recipe only takes an hour, even if you make the stock; I agree that 6 cups of broth is too much (I used maybe 4 cups). Pureed the corn & a little stock per several excellent reader suggestions - perfect!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Whipping the cream at the end when you're trying to serve guests is just foolish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made as directed.  It was delicious but I don't think the cream is really needed.  It is already very rich and flavorful.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Dead easy and so good! Made as directed using chicken stock. Did sub in Creme Fraiche bc I was too lazy to whip the cream. This is a keeper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Agree about the cream being unnecessary.  Plus, why beat it when it just melts the instant it hits the risotto?  I'm going to try the lobster (or shrimp).  I felt like it was missing something, even with super good farmer's market corn.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made it with frozen corn kernels and chicken broth. It was excellent!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made the corn stock, not bcuz I'm an overachiever but the corncobs, leek top, onion, celery & carrot were handy; either simmer w/6 cups of water or discard -- why not? I made the risotto in my Instant Pot which only needed 3 cups of stock & no laborious adding & stirring. It was terrific! I have frozen the rest of the broth flat in a ziplok for another adventure.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Don’t waste your time making the corn stock. I make risotto all the time and took the time to follow this recipe and there was virtually no difference. Just substitute a good vegetable stock or chicken stock and save yourself the hassle. Also, the chives did not give this enough flavor, really needed a lemon or basil or something like that.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Labor intensive, but delicious and creamy.  Left-overs delicious cold as sandwich accompaniment. I gave it only 4 stars because my poor arm was exhausted by the time I was finished.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made exactly as written and it was excellent, although wondering if it’s really necessary to whip the cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Having made this a few times with the whipped cream, I've decided not to do it going forward. I don't think there's much air left after folding it into a heavy, hot risotto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Non-dairy household here, and our version was creamy and decadent. Word to the wise: don't skip the corn broth. I doubled to 4 tbsp of plant-based butter for sauteeing leeks. Highly recommend Miyokos. Opted for the corn puree: raw kernels of 2 cobs, 2/3 cup unsweetened soy milk, 1 tbsp plant-based butter, s&p into the food processor. Add puree for the last 5 minutes of risotto on the heat to cook through. Last, Violife Parmesan Cheese is best in the plant-based class. *chef's kiss*

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I left out the cream but made the corn stock, which I think is essential. I love stirring risotto when I'm not in a rush, and I enjoyed my time at the stove, glass of wine in hand while I caught up on my podcasts. I knocked this down one star because -- and I can't believe I'm saying this --  one cup of Parmesan was too much. It muted the corn flavor. I wish I had used a half-cup or less of Parm and then dressed the risotto with a little more on top. Next time!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you make this ahead of time and reheat it?  I would like to serve to my guests but I don't want to be stirring this up while they wait for dinner.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
It's been my experience that once cooled, risotto is best served as risotto cakes. But you could cook the risotto shy of done just before your guests arrive and then finish it just before serving. 

You could also just make oven risotto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This recipe didn’t make the cut to make again. We did the creamed corn variation instead using whipped cream. 

We found it bland. For the leftovers the second night we added some pepperoncini and crisped prosciutto, which helped but still bland. 

We will stick to mushroom or butternut squash risottos in the future.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I've made this several times. Frankly, I prefer it with either chicken stock or if I'm serving it with fish, shrimp or scallops, half clam juice / half water. Sometimes I add basil or Hatch chiles. 

In my opinion, the corn stock adds little to the finished dish other than additional prep time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great recipe, followed exactly and added some sautéed corn and diced poblanos as a topping. However I’d love to suggest a time saving tip: cook on medium heat instead of medium low— cut off about 10 minutes. Also you can whip the cream while the stock cooks and store in fridge until needed, use the 5 minutes resting time to set the table!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The corn adds a nice texture and flavor to the risotto. Doesn't feel as heavy as some other risottos, which is a good thing!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: One of my favorite summer 2014 discoveries, this risotto is a lot of work but well worth the effort. Sidestepping the stock preparation would shorten the time, but I think the corn stock (versus chicken) maximizes the summer flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is perfect for the summer, and is always a bit hit. The corn stock is a must. I use four ears of corn, but otherwise follow the recipe exactly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Gimmicky. Needed quite a bit of salt to boost flavor, to which, let me stress, I am by no means ideologically opposed! But the corn cob stock makes for insipid broth, even with outstanding local corn. The addition of whipped cream is the gimmick. It doesn't really produce the creaminess of a traditional risotto. Once I added tarragon, the dish became interesting. I wonder what factoring bacon would do. A rare disappointment among the NYT recipes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A lot of work but oh so good. Used 4 ears of corn so there was more corn in the risotto. Needs a bit more salt than called for but that may have been because I used Romano rather than Parmesan (grabbed the wrong package from the fridge and didn't read the label!).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I use highly seasoned chicken stock since I used frozen corn.
Omited the wine and stired in the cream at the end, rather than whipping it.
Cheese preference: Fontana instead of Parmesan... and even half a cup seems to work well.
Why do the Prep directions list using an electric mixer (which takes up a lot of counter space in small kitchens) for whipping just this small amount of cream. Do professional chefs forget alternatives: the old-fashioned rotary beater? Or the portable electric one?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this tonight with fresh corn from the Farmers' Market. Made the corn stock and used 4 ears of corn as other reviewers suggested. Yum! Will definitely make this again while I can get fresh corn. Did not feel this needed any extra salt as one reviewer stated and may cut back on the salt just a bit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: used chicken stock. Really creamy and tasty.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Summer sensation! I used yellow corn making a nice color contrast with the chives. I got rushed and skipped the corn stock, using chicken stock. Also carnaroli rice, since that's what I had. Salt is to individual taste, but I only added a little at the beginning per the instructions and didn't think it needed any more. The whipped cream was an unexpected delight! I enjoy making risottos for the "tending." I only ever briefly step away from the stove. Patience is always the key to creaminess!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Thought this needed some pep -- something
seemed to be missing, or maybe my corn
was insufficiently sweet?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a nice if unremarkable summer recipe--the fresh corn is yummy (though you will want four cobs' worth), and no one will run from the table crying. Definitely use the chives and serve with something crunchy, colorful, and piquant.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was great, but I did jazz it up a notch in the flavour department. I used organic chicken stock as the liquid base in my  corn stock. I added a finely chopped fresh cayenne pepper and chopped garlic when the leeks were almost done. I also grilled my corn and added it at the very end of the process, just to heat it through. I skipped the whipped cream stage. It was an above average risotto and I will make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added some leftover creamed corn from Martha Shulman's creamed corn without cream recipe.  Made it even creamier

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A yummy, make-again risotto. Loved the combination of textures. Used flat-leafed parsley instead of the chives, which worked really well also.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We used the "swimming" technique with good success. Perhaps because we had cut down the recipe some the cooking time was less. Will use this as a company dish along with the Southern Shrimp Scampi recope.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved this recipe as a starting point and loved the final result, but not sure if I can say I made this recipe since I made a number of changes to it.  Replaced the corn broth with my own homemade chicken broth.  Replaced the leeks with shallots and did not use the heavy cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Easy and delicious. Added some shrimp toward the end which turned out great.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I so want to make this. Thanks for all the comments. I'm wondering if using some chicken stock when making the corn broth would add more oomph. Anyone try that? Also, I might add some jalapeños. Love them with corn.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Like others, I added bacon and added just a touch of cream (unwhipped) to add richness without diluting the flavor. I would probably not add the cream next time. But I loved this recipe. Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Don't use freshly grated Parmigiana Regiano; it's too strong, and you want the corn to shine. I had to freeze fresh/picked corn, so I added it right before the last ladle, because it had already been cooked.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Love it! Another great way to use corn this season. Just like other risottos, I doubled ( well maybe quadrupled) the wine and took away some of the stock. It was great! Served it with Jerk Ribs.
Thanks!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: First try I took too long to get t all added and cooked. Use higher heat and add more liquid faster.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I misread the instructions in Step 2 and added the leak and the corn together. Rather than fry the corn to mush, I went ahead with the recipe. Perhaps because this was a hearty variety of corn, the kernels remained crisp. All in all, a wonderful, delicious recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is very good. So summery and fresh. Risotto is work though. I will make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you make the stock in and instant pot? Ms. Clark would know.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was wondering the same thing. Use a risotto recipe on the Instant Pot and use these ingredients. I think you could also hasten the corn broth and heighten it’s flavor in the Instant Pot as well!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: hmm - for dessert maybe - too sweet for me - a problem of the super sweet hybridized corn varieties - I might try again if I can find old-fashioned corn

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was great. Take Larry G.'s advice below - especially if you want to leave out the heavy cream which I did leave out. I also more than doubled the total amount of corn kernels.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Something to look at for menu

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Use a squirt of tomato puree instead of the cream - delish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Read my "Cooking This Weekend" email in search of something to cook and the corn risotto was perfect timing Saturday morning, with 5 ears of uncooked corn. I grilled the corn and chicken breast, which I added to the risotto.  I also headed Larry's tip to cream corn to substitute for the lack of cream, which was great.  Instead of making a corn stock, I cooked the risotto in homemade chicken stock I had on hand.  Great dish! It's a definite repeater.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a very savory and satisfying meal. Paired it with a California Sauvignon Blanc and used fresh basil as garnish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The addition of the heavy cream is really not needed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Do you think this would freeze well? I know risotto is a sensitive dish....I am thinking like fried calamari. A one shot deal??

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! Made exactly as described with corn stock.  This is super rich. I'm new to making risotto,  not quite sure I have the technique mastered. Mine may have been a little more liquid than pictured in recipe

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm italian and I can garantire that cooking ridotto for 30/40 minutes Will result in an overcooked, horribile blob. Risotto cooks in 18/20 minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this last night, AND IT IS DELISH! Enough to last through the week and share with friends. I did not use the cobs for the broth, but used Chicken bone broth which is now my favorite. Totally recommend.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For a quicker weeknight meal, I cut the corn kernels off the cob, and then simmered the naked cobs and leek trimmings in the chicken stock while I prepped everything else. The result was excellent, I will happily make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yum!!! No cream needed for my family, and only 1/4 of the parmesan so people could add more to their taste. I loved how easy and delish the corn stock was to make.  Def will keep in my recipe box.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just made this dish yesterday with chunks of cooked Lobster meat and Linguiça sausage mixed in.  Perfect end of summer recipe.  Loved the vegetable stock - besides the carrots and celery, I added in a quartered red bell pepper that I had on hand.  Eliminated the cream but did use the parmigiano cheese.  Excellent!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I made this last night and thought it was delicious; my husband said this morning that he was still thinking about it...

After cutting off the corn, I put the cobs into the vegetable broth to add a bit more corny flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was so amazing, used an off-dry Vouvray (chenin blanc) and it really made all the difference. I found the cream addition to be very important in the end, but it would be fun to experiment with some alternate thickeners: like coconut cream or creme fresh.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made as written with chicken stock.  I followed a suggestion below and put one of the corn cobs in the pot with the stock, which gave it a subtle corn flavor.  Two observations:  1.  6 cups of stock is way too much; you probably won't need more than 4 (which is all I ever need for risotto) and 2.  I thought the amount of parmesan called for overwhelmed the subtle sweetness of the corn.  Next time I think I'll go to the trouble of making the corn stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I topped ours with crispy pancetta and a small amount of chopped basil. Some pecorino, too. Stellar.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! I used grana padano cheese because it was what I had and added the cheese rind while it cooked. I had no heavy cream, but as others have said, it was really lovely without it. Next time, I'd add some more corn - maybe 2 or 2.5 cups.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is there a way to do this in the oven for most of the time?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: J. Kenji Lopez-Alt (of Serious Eats) has a method for pressure cooker risotto, but I don't know about oven risotto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Risotto needs frequent stirring, so oven cooking is not recommended,

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
While on vacation feeding a family, I did make this.  It was wonderful
served along side of two salads.
The men thought that they would need more, so I did buy a small skirt steak, (I would have preferred serving this with cod, or scallops- but we had a fish aversion amongst our crew)  And I did not use heavy cream:
I took an additional ear of corn, and put the kernels in a blender, and used this as a cream substitute.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Emily, this sounds fabulous. Do you cook the corn on the cob before taking the kernels off, or just scrape the kernels off raw?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Came out delicious - my kids ate it and oldest one willing to have as leftovers for lunch!  Mmm Mmm.  Didn't quite get how to add the cream without it melting, but probably didn't whip it hard enough, and as the intro says, it was still a wonderfully cream risotto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Have been making risotto for 30  years and this was one of the most tasteless versions of a risotto I have ever made.  Please note I used fresh locally grown corn in season and all top notch ingredients, even homemade chicken broth.  Did not bother with the cream as that is not necessary in a risotto.  Risotto is just naturally creamy and it’s the nature of the dish. Sincerely wanted to love this recipe. It went down the disposal sad to say.  Save time and $ and make a risotto Milanese

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I felt the same way before adding the whipped cream.  It transforms the dish

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Has anyone noticed:
1. The cooking liquid this recipe calls for is twice what you would normally use (1 cup arborio rice to 3 cups broth)
2. That the recipe actually calls for the cob, minus the corn, to make the corn broth.  People said they added the cob for extra cornyness, but that is what is called for.

author: Emily Weinstein
