commentBody: Fabulous and noteworthy for end-of-summer corn. Loved that all of the veg was used - some for the stock, remainder in the risotto. Subtle and layered flavors. The only question I had was about "whipping 1/4 cup cream". I suppose one could do this with a hand mixer but I chose to simply pour it in and whirl around. Delightful!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delicious.  Only addition was seared scallops to the top.  I may not add the whipped cream next time...just didn't seem necessary to us.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was superb made with the corn stock.  Other vegetables could be considered, like sugar snap peas, asparagus-steamed and added at the end.
The flavor and texture was outstanding.  Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was tasty, but 6 cups of stock was ridiculous.  There's no way 6 cups (only by LADLEFULS no less) could have been incorporated into the rice in 30-40 minutes at a medium-low heat.  This took me close to an hour even at a medium-high ish heat, and the rice was definitely not al dente at the end.  Could have very well stopped at 4 cups of stock.  I researched risotto tips after-the-fact, and it turns out that most recipes prescribe a ratio of one cup of rice to about 3-4 cups of stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Nice dish. I used chicken stock and, as many others reported, only needed about 3 1/2-4 cups of it. I used a shallot instead of the leek and added a diced green pepper with the corn. And I omitted the cream but I did use the chives. Mild in flavor, filling, and attractive.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a decent side dish but was intensely rich, which resulted in a large amount of leftovers even for a larger group. The only way I can think of repurposing it all is to freeze it, then next time I barbecue I’ll convert it into corn risotto fritters. I’ll let you all know how that turns out.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Waiting to hear about the risotto fritters.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have been making this recipe slightly differently, with great satisfaction.  I use more corn, onion, corn stock and finely chopped and pan fried red bell pepper.  Near the end, I add chopped fresh shrimp which are just cooked through at the finale.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Fantastic!  Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We did not care for this recipe as is. Tasting it prior to the cream and cheese was nice, the cheese made it less nice and the cream overwhelms it. The next day I added stock, more herbs and turned it into soup but even then it was lackluster.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent - I prepared just as described. I realized when I shucked the corn that it wasn't that great quality...but the dish was still delicious!  Following advice of other readers I also used more corn.  Next time I'll make a double batch of the stock and freeze some.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious.  Made according to recipe including the stock.  Only exception is that I didn't whip the cream.  Thanks for the lobster suggestion; great combo.  (The Italian in me had a little trouble with the parmesan and the lobster together, but got over it very quickly upon tasting it!)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We all loved this dish. I didn’t add any cream, the flavor from the stock was rich enough.  I made it in a wide skillet which I learned about from the ‘Food Lab’ book.  Quicker than in a deep saucepan.  Was even good reheated the next day (well, good enough, would not recommend making it ahead). We liked it so much we are making it again 3 days later...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made the corn stock and thought it was a bit bland.  The finished dish is fine on its own without the cream, IMHO.  Also, in my experience a cup of arborio rice will take about 4 to 4.5 cups of stock.  You don't need to make up the corn stock to six cups.  Next time I think I'll try this with chicken stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used pancetta as the base, saving the diced pieces to add in at the end. Wonderful! I skipped heavy cream, but did add in a couple tablespoons half and half to enrich the creaminess. At the end, I tossed in a chiffonade of fresh basil and lightly toasted pine nuts. I wish I'd thought to add lobster. Yum!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can add sour cream to give it an elotes flair. I know it's certainly not traditional, but I am all about mixing things up and using imagination.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is my favourite dish to make at the end of corn season. Here's how I up the ante: For the corn stock, I omit the onion/carrot/celery, and add a parmesan rind and lobster shell (optional: roasted at 450F for 20 minutes) instead. I decrease the total required stock to only 4 cups. It's more than enough. 6 cups of stock is way too much. For the risotto itself, I add raw, diced lobster meat with the last ladle of stock. Also, I don't bother whipping the cream and it's fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was fantastic.  I puréed the veggies for the stock (minus the corn husks) and boiled the stock for 4 hours before preparing the rest of the risotto.  The flavor at the suggested 30 minutes had not developed yet—not even close.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm going to give this an Elotes flair. I'm making it with corn stock. I am also going to caramelize the corn in butter in a saute pan. I'll do parm, but will add sour cream instead of heavy cream. I am also topping it with queso fresco. Can't wait to try it. It has to be delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Blend stock with corn kernels instead of cream

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Beautiful idea to capture summer in a risotto. I tasted it before adding whipped cream and actually preferred it without. Next time I might just start with more butter and skip the bother of whipping cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used frozen corn kernels because fresh not available.  Great taste, but it probably is better with fresh summer corn.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: From an old Bob Appetite recipe, puree about a third of the corn kernels in a blender with a little of the stock before adding it to the risotto. It makes a wonderful creamy risotto. No need to add cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How much do you think would it compromise this recipe if I used frozen corn?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Cooked by R - Didn’t add corn until the end because we like crisp veggies.

Also added some left over green onions, and used a red onion ; both good ideas that were delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used chicken stock, a full 2 cups of corn, added a roasted and diced poblano pepper in the last 10 minutes of cooking, and omitted the cream. It was absolutely delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Perfect dish for corn season! Definitely worth making the corn stock. To enhance the corn flavor, I used three ears of corn for the broth and risotto. I followed another reader’s suggestion - blend 1/3 kernels with a bit of corn stock, and subbed in for the heavy cream. Topped with seared scallops.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Way too salty, wouldn’t use any salt in it and cut back maybe by half the Parmesan.  But whipped cream a dramatic touch!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Baby's first risotto! I used vegetable stock from Trader Joe's. The rice needed more time + liquid to get to a good consistency - it was still quite crunchy at 40 min w/ 6 C stock, and very salty, so I added water until it reached a good consistency and was less intensely salted. I think the cream was a positive addition and wouldn't skip it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very good recipe which I modified because I accidentally thawed shrimp stock....I used shrimp stock (Ina Garten's) and roasted the corn on the grill and added at the end; also added 1/2 chopped jalapeño with the onions  - used 6 cups of stock for 1.5 cups of rice.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely love this recipe! I’ve made it several times, sometimes using corn stock, other times using chicken or vegetable stock. I use sweet onions or shallots if I don’t have leeks. I always use the cream. The only thing I change is the salt level. I find that the Parmesan adds quite a bit of salty flavor, so I cut the recommended salt level in half.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Skip the cream. I’ve made this a handful of times and haven’t used the cream. It’s perfect without it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Didn’t add bacon the second time around and think it is better without - the flavors of the rice and corn come through more. added the corn and some peas from the garden about 10 minutes before the rice was done so there was some added texture.  Didn’t use any cream, plenty lush without it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes. A whole lot of yum. Like sage reviewers before me, I creamed a bit of the corn with some broth and loved the texture and color it gave. Also added garlic to the leek at the beginning because, duh. One time added sautéed scallops to the top (more garlic and umami) and the next mixed in some juujed up lump crab. Surprised at how easy this is and it goes without saying, make the corn stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I wanted to make Ina Gartens  corn and lobster risotto but it has disappeared from the internet. So I made this with some minor changes and added lobster. Delicious!I didn’t have leeks so used big scallions from farmers market. Used sparkling wine and no heavy cream. Added lobster during last stage with extra butter. Stirred up, topped with shredded basil and chopped scallops with lemon wedge.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I agree with the comment not he cream possibly flattening the flavor. I used instead one recipe's worth of Whitney Wright's corn butter in place of it. It may not be quite as light, but it's a nice boost to the corn flavor and makes a super creamy risotto even creamier. https://food52.com/recipes/83740-one-ingredient-sweet-corn-butter-recipe

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was definitely delicious!  I made the corn stock and added some 1/2 and 1/2 to round it up after straining. I believe 5 cups of liquid adequate. I followed suggestions and fried a nice think piece of good bacon crispy to crumble on top. I used more like 1/2-2/3 cup grated sharp cheddar. Yes- incredibly creamy and delicious!  Due to bacon and cheddar, I used less salt. I sautéd an herb mixture when I sautéed the risotto. I didn’t have celery so I added a few shakes of fennel seeds.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I subbed a white onion for the leek and used store bought stock. It was delicious though next time I'd like to try it with homemade stock as it tasted a bit too salty.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This is my third time around with this winning recipe!  2nd time adding shrimp was delicious!  This time?  Lump crab claw- 1/4 #. 
I’m living in the Coastal SE so I can and do buy from a fish monger with their own boats. 
Seafood is a perfect match!  I’m thinking some Country ham (the South’s version of prosciutto) would add if I keep an eye on the rest of the salt content).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: No cream needed

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did this tonight and it came out delicious. I did not have the corn stock so used the vegetable stock. I added chanterelle mushrooms too as I bought them freshly from the FM today- great addition. My problem is that for one cup of rice, I did not use six cups of stock, maybe 3. And my rice came out so little it was enough for three people and the recipe said 4 servings. Had I known that I would have used two cups. I did not use arborio, but the real risotto rice so maybe that's the difference?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: 1/2 yellow onion works if leeks aren’t happening; vegetable stock works well instead of chicken or corn stock; a bit more wine tightens up the flavor; good quality canned corn works in a pinch; do 1.5x the Parmesan cheese; 2 tbsp milk (not skim) works instead of the heavy cream; this is a HUGE family winner for us.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this with Parmesan broth (see amazing recipe on the site) and simmered the corn stock veggies in the broth. Unbelievably good!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love the corn stock and easy- took me longer to cook down but worth the wait so light and elegant

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Didn’t add cream and only added 1/4 cup parm and still delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made it exactly as directed and it was a hit! Agree with others that the cream was unnecessary. Will try with aged cheddar next time per others’ recommendations.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Yummy! I doubled the herbs and parm.
Love the tarragon idea!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love making risotto - corn risotto, yes please!  A nice variation is some toasted cumin seeds, maybe a piece of grilled wild salmon and a spoonful of gremolata. Pairs perfectly with Chardonnay.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Every August my husband stops at the lobster guy in Portland, ME and picks up 6-8 lobster.  We cook two and par cook then plugen in ice water the rest.  I pick them and put them in vacuum sealed bags for risotto and chowder and lobster mac and cheese for the year.  When using lobster in risotto, toss in par cooked lobster near the end.  It is moister and has more flavor.  I think I have to make corn and lobster risotto for Labor Day weekend this year. Larry, Sam, and Emily Thanks.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was good and I like the cream. I think it is super bland so I would spice it up, but that's me.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is delicious! I did not add the cream at the end to cut down on the fat.  The sweet and crunchy flavor of the corn was the star of the dish. The chives add a nice touch of freshness that complements the overall creaminess and sweetness of the dish. This is a 5 star dish for sure!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this as directed, but followed advice and added corn cobs to simmering chicken broth instead of making the corn stock.  I love corn and my husband loves rice so this was a dish made in heaven.  Absolutely delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this tonight EXACTLY as written and it was SUPERB. (Yes, those two words deserve ALL CAPS.) Follow other commenters' "advice" about adding bacon and puréeing corn at your own peril. I strongly suggest you cook the dish as the chef intended first and then determine for yourself if it needs improving. It doesn't.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious. I used the chicken stock instead of making my own corn stock, and it was still phenomenal. Highly recommend!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very good but more cheesy than other risottos I have enjoyed.  The creaminess seems to come from a full cup of cheese more so than any of the corn.  We added some fresh tomatoes when serving and it help "lighten" the cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicous!  A tasty add:  shrimp and polish sausage.  Yum!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is super creamy and delicious. I wish I hadn’t added the extra butter at the end, it was unnecessary. I also added 3/4 cup of parm at the end Instead of a full cup and I omitted the cream. My 10 yr old liked it and I did too but you need to be in the mood for a rich meal.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Superb!!  No need for the additional butter or cream.  Plenty creamy with just the Parmesan and the starch form the rice. I alternated chicken stock with some more white wine as it simmered.  Added bacon and scallions at the end and can’t imagine it without. Mmmmmmm.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I 1 1/2’ed this recipe (teenage boys) and thought the corn to rice ratio was off. Next time I would use 2 ears of corn to 1 1/2 cups of rice. It needs another herb while cooking (I will use thyme) and it definitely needs the richness of the dairy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Not sure why, but something was missing for me. There's richness from all the butter and parmesan and sweetness from the corn and vegetables in the corn stock. That's it, though. I wanted something to pop about it. I wouldn't make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this exactly as directed, and it was a perfect blend of sweetness and funkiness.  Loved it!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was absolutely fantastic! Everyone’s favorite dish at thanksgiving. I did not do the cream thing at the end. But totally didn’t need it. I did make the corn stock and I think that made the whole dish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I hope to make this today, adding some unwhipped cream into the risotto at the end. Also, I plan to grate, not slice off, some of the kernels, to obtain more corn liquid.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Blended corn with some stock and added a splash of milk instead of heavy cream. Added extra garlic to the stock, and did half water half chicken stock. Added Tony’s to the stock and leek. A splash of extra wine halfway through

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Would creme fraiche work at the end instead of whipping the cream? I think Ina Garten does that in her crab risotto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love this recipe!  It is a staple for us when corn is in season.  As mentioned by someone else, I puree about a quarter of the corn with some stock and then add it to the risotto.  Still add the cream.  Delicious.  Left overs make a great breakfast!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is also very good with the addition of diced summer tomatoes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Omit the cream next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe is delicious, a serious keeper! Skip the cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added some shrimp. Did not add cream. Absolutely delish. Think I’ll had a squeeze of lemon to finish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this as written with the corn stock (highly recommended as I think chicken stock would overwhelm the corn flavor). It's a pain to make (takes twice as long than regular risotto), but it's absolutely divine!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It was worth standing over the hot stove and stirring for an hour in the summertime! Added some chopped mushroom with the leeks. I used four corn cobs in the corn stock recipe and felt it still could have used more to strengthen the stock favor.  Delicious.  Will make again with frozen corn in the wintertime.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Make stock ahead- risotto takes 45.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was delicious. I added some sliced garlic, omitted the cream because I didn’t have any on hand, used only half the parm, and went with chicken stock but simmered the corn cobs in it for about 15 mins before and left them in throughout the cooking process- they really infused a sweet corn flavor and creaminess to the chicken stock. 

I suggest topping with more fresh chopped herbs, or a drizzle of pesto or chili oil and serving with barbecue chicken thighs and a tomato salad! Super summery!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Super tasty - took a little while to make, since I made the corn stock, too. But worth the effort. I was skeptical that it would taste like corn, or that the recipe would use all the stock, but I followed through and made it almost entirely as specified, except that I added a pinch of MSG with the salt and pepper. Really nice results. Tasted like corn, with some nice complexity from the stock. Would and will make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! Added some fresh herbs, which were lovely. Needed a fair amount more liquid, though I wonder whether some of it had cooked off in the sauce pan on the back of the stove during the rest of the prep. I added probably 1.5 more cups of water after a full “6 cups” of chicken stock. I did whip the cream, though it melted immediately upon hitting the risotto…I do not think it’s possible any of the whipped in air could have survived the folding but it certainly didn’t hurt. Tastes wonderful!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Blended an ear of corn with the stock and left out the cream to keep it a lighter summer dish served it along side of pan seared sea scallops, it was a big hit with guest.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Agree with others that 4 cups of corn broth is the max needed. And sprinkling on Costco’s Truffle, Parmesan, & Black Garlic Seasoning before serving took this to the next level!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Second time I’ve made this… it’s a family tradition now. I doubled the recipe since I had a large group and we had plenty and lots of leftovers. I made the stock in the morning and let it simmer all day which was perfect. I did not need all the stock. I added extra corn which I think is highly warranted.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this with mods (julienned shallots, garlic and straight corn stock) and of course it’s amazing. I already loved making cheesy pasta with corn as an easy satisfying and inexpensive weeknight meal. Love the upgrade as risotto. It rules. Make it!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Served it with Shrimp and it was a hit!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Also added chopped parsley and chives.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I followed someone else's suggestion and added lobster, well, mock lobster since the store didn't have lobster. It was delicious! I didn't have time to make the corn stock, but I plan to do it next time. This is a wonderful use of sweet summer corn.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is EXCELLENT as a perfect summer risotto base and blank canvas. I riffed a little by adding chopped tomatoes at the start and braised radish greens at the end, then topped with chili oil. I didn't have heavy cream or time to whip, but Greek yogurt seemed to work just fine in its place.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used tarragon bloomed in the butter step ( dried) added fresh tarragon at the end.  Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Hands down my favorite summer risotto paired with scallops. So easy, creamy, fresh. My only modifications, I take about 1/2 cup of the corn and cream it in a small processor Vs using cream. Excellent!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can easily make half this recipe and still have enough for four.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Amazing. Blended handful of corn with broth to make a slurry. Skipped the cream. Added lots of basil from the garden.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added chopped fiori di zucca from the garden near the end, delicious!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very tasty! Start a little early. Can get the leeks going before straining the broth.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Summer in a bowl! 3 ears of corn from the farmer’s market and this was a great use for them. Made the stock the night before but started with a 32 oz box of low sodium chicken broth, plus 2 c water and all ingredients for the corn stock, plus one dried arborol chile for the slightest heat. Made the risotto largely as written but didn’t have heavy cream so tried a little corn/broth purée as recommended by other reviewers, and pecorino instead of Parmesan. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tastes just fine without the cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Fantastic. Almost doesn’t need the cream. But boy does it make it decadent. Goes great with shrimp scampi and a little gem salad with a tart vinaigrette.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent and a crowd pleaser!!  No need for cream - I made the corn stock as written with the leek tops as well and boiled it for over an hour.  Tripled the recipe for a crowd.  Added fresh tarragon, parsley and a lemon at the end (I find it needs the acidity.)  Love love love

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added charred poblano strips for a lil depth. Big hit all around. Repeat of the dish requested a week later!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used three ears of corn because I love fresh corn. If you have a weakness for fresh corn as I do, go for it! Risotto is plenty creamy and there 's more of the corn you love. Definitely will make this again and make a regular part of my dinner repertoire.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used all 6 Cups of corn stock and it came out perfectly. You could get by without the whipped cream at the end, but it does add a certain elegance.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Only used about 4 cups of corn stock (maybe it depends on the particular rice?) and added in some par-cooked green beans from the garden along with the corn.  No cream.  Served with some little balls of fresh mozzarella at the bottom of the bowl, but burrata would be better! And next time add bacon.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love the idea ... doubled the leek and chive amount ... agree with others re: cream - not necessary - also cut the amount of parmesan in half.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Your neck will hurt after making it but it’s worth it!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Tim Thanks for "boiling down" the comments!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I use vegetable stock?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I double the recipe? Would it need any modifications?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This appears to be a good recipe based on my experience cooking corn risotto from others. Though I generally only comment after trying the recipe at least once, I have to say that no self-respecting Italian would ever finish this with heavy cream. Adding butter and cheese at

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Why not vegetable stock (rather than chicken stock), since the recipe for corn stock is all vegetables?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Mary Cochrane Do whatever you want!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I just made this and I gotta say, making the corn stock as written results in a super flavorful stock that truly enhances this dish. Maybe a well-made vegetable stock would work, but the rich corn flavors would have to be muted.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used to love corn...actually, I still do, but then I grew some in my garden. Although I had 9 stalks, I only got 9 ears! I thought I'd done something wrong. I gave the corn extra water and added compost half-way through the season because I'd read corn needed the extra nutrients. But, why so little, when I thought I'd done everything correctly?! So, I looked it up...this is NORMAL! One cob per stalk and 1 stalk per plant. I can't eat corn anymore. That's just too inefficient for our planet!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: so good.  everyone loved it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Could this be frozen? I imagine the dream of having this on a cold winter night.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Carol Mathias. Remove the corn kernels from the cob and freeze on a sheet pan. I make the corn broth at the same time and then freeze it for some summer pleasure in the dark cold winter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I had most of the ingredients on hand, though green onions and shallots will have to stand in for the leeks — and crème fraîche for the whipped cream. With an insalata caprese to start, this risotto ought to be enough for dinner. The corn broth is already perfuming the house.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was phenomenal. I loved the pop of the kernels in the risotto and you HAVE to do the corn broth!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: After some debate and research I took a chance and made this with sushi rice, cuz I had pounds of it and no arborio. Worked very well, didn't use all 6 cups of the corn broth, kept testing for doneness. Delicious! Topped with some gently cooked eggs. Happy campers.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: TIME SAVER - you can substitute frozen corn for fresh corn in this recipe and it works great.  And it is available all year long.  Give it a try!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this per recipe and it turned out great. I wonder though-do I really need to whip the cream? It melts the minute it hits the hot rice mixture, so could I not just put in the unwhipped cream and call it a day? Also, I wonder if it would have a nicer texture with less parm or maybe no parm as I found it slightly sticky. I do like the idea of putting half the corn in the blender. I am a cream lover, so maybe the cream instead of the parm? I think this recipe has many excellent possibilities.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The best risotto I've ever made. When it came time to add the corn I decided to dump it all in the remaining stock and bring that up to a simmer. Then proceeded with adding a ladle or so at a time with corn in it. Just wanted to make sure the rice would be done.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did not use the cream at the end but otherwise followed the recipe.  It was good but very bland. Next time I will add garlic to actual risotto instead of just the stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Throw some butter-seared scallops on this, maybe some micro-greens, and a drizzle of basil oil, and you have a very nice company-worthy summer dinner. I've used bacon to start this (rendered, removed, sprinkled in at the end) and it's fantastic. The stock is amazing as well. I had no idea corn cobs could make such a fantastic stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this nearly exactly as written, I didn’t whip the cream, but otherwise exactly as written, WOW! BANG, ZOOM! A keeper of a recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This is SO good!  Some notes - 

- I tasted it after adding the cheese and butter, and before adding the whipped cream.  It was already amazing and I might just leave out the cream going forward.

- I misread the instructions on making the corn stock and didn't cover the pot as it simmered.  I ended up with only 4 cups of stock but rich flavor, so I added 1 3/4 cups of water and 1/4 cups coconut aminos to get the 6 cups needed.  It ended up tasting amazing so maybe this was a happy accident!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I thought I would love this but I didn’t. I made it per instructions. We liked it but it wasn’t worth the time for me. I made the corn stock, fresh grated Parmesan, etc. The whole thing by recipe, but it just didn’t knock me over.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made the corn stock, reduced it and after straining added chicken stock to make 6 cups.  (Did not use all of the stock.) Per a suggestion added bacon which I cooked first then drained on paper towels.  Added the leeks to the pan and when they were soft added a few chopped cloves of garlic. Followed the rest of the recipe adding the bacon and corn at the end, just sauteeing until the already tender corn was a bit cooked. Served alongside some leftover sausages smoked in a pellet grill. YUM!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is an extremely time-consuming, multi pan, multi step recipe that disappoints entirely. It was neither good risotto nor good creamed corn, two things that I love.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Flori Cooks   Shoot.  So disappointed for you.  I made it yesterday and it was so lovely, It does take a lot of time to cook the risotto, maybe almost an hour?   If you feel up to it, try again when you have lots of time, because my fam thought it spectacular.  Bon appetite!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A recipe that my entire family loved!! I kept it simple and left out the cream and Parmesan. It was delicious and a huge hit!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delicious, and I didn’t even have all the ingredients. I used Meyer lemon honey vinegar in place of wine. I used chicken broth and frozen sweet white corn. Didn’t have heavy cream, so I just added 2 tbsps half & half with the butter and Parmesan. I’ll definitely make the corn broth & cream next time!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Definitely with the corn stock and the creamed corn option. Fabulous.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made recipe as written but added sugar snaps near end - to keep crunch- and it was delicious!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: And a note - we did not use cream but mushed up some of the corn as one of the notes suggested.  Delish

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We made according to instructions and topped with sautéed wild mushrooms. Delicious! (Although whipping the cream seemed unnecessary since it instantly melted when added.)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I found this very disappointing. I make risotto frequently and love corn so this seemed like a great idea.  I also liked the idea of making a corn broth. But this just missed the mark on so many levels. In spite of the corn broth it really lacked flavor. Spend your risotto time on other recipes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What would be a good menu to incorporate this dish?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used chicken stock since I always have homemade in stock.  And we love corn so used 3 ears of corn.  We LOVED this recipe and will make it many times in the future.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I bought 3 small steamed lobsters for this dish. Shelled the lobsters and cut them in chunks. Added the shells to the corn broth which really added a nice touch. As I cooked the risotto, I warmed the lobster in 2 tablespoons of butter and 2 cloves of minced garlic. When adding the last bit of broth, I added the lobster and butter. Even though fish and cheese is a big no no, I did add the. Parmesan. This was one of the best dishes I've made recently.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love fresh corn. I love risotto. But this tasted flat to me.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It was okay. We both commented it could use twice as much corn.  I think I’m just not a big risotto fan.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I didn't puree the corn before I added it to the risotto, but I agree, no cream is necessary. Next time, I won't add the cream. If it's made right, meaning take the time to add the hot broth a bit at a time, the risotto is creamy enough! I encourage anyone making this to add the fresh chopped chives at the end. Not only do they give the dish a bright finish of color, but they add a nice fresh flavor. 
It is definitely worth the time to make the corn broth. It adds depth.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn't need as much liquid as called for. I found it also freezes and travels well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: While I was tempted to just go with chicken broth, I am glad I took the time to make the corn broth. What a delicious dish! I used the heavy cream to finish and then added the chives. We opted to add some lobster and wow, was that good. We live in the Midwest, so don’t have the best access to lobster. We bought two small lobster tails. We used the micro wave to loosen the meat from the shells, and then added it about 3 to 5 minutes before finishing with the heavy cream and chives. The lobster meat was tender and not over cooked. We had leftovers and it reheated well with just a splash of heavy cream. Yummy!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added basil at end and small ham cubes when I added corn. Blended 1/3 of corn and some chicken stock as suggested instead of cream. Used corn from 4 ears of corn and 4 cups of chicken broth.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Pretty good. I made the corn stock with chicken broth instead of water. There wasn't a significant difference in flavor from regular risotto that I could tell other than the corn added some textural interest. Served with seared scallops and fresh basil. I did used the cream but it was not necessary

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: When served as a side, this recipe took too long, involved at least 4 bowls/pots and was generally exhausting. The flavor was good, nice take on risotto, but way too much prep and cleanup. Just not worth it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Enjoyed this and would make it again.  I made some substituitons:  used chicken broth (4C were plenty), Calrose rice (sacrilegious, I'm sure, but, I did it for cost savings), and skipped the heavy cream (I used the creamed corn idea from another poster).

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: While labor intensive, this was a delicious restaurant quality dish. I made the stock; however, my stock ended up a deeper brown color.  The finished dish was much darker than the photo.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We did not use the cream. For the broth we took approximately 1/2 cup of corn with with water, cooked it in the microwave and then used an immersion blender to make it into a cream and then added it back to the broth to make the broth to have more corn flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: add roasted poblano

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, and it was delish. We had it for lunch with Melissa Clark’s broiled salmon, and there is plenty for another lunch serving tomorrow. I had enough ingredients to make a double batch of the corn stock, so I froze half to make the NYT’s Slow Cooker Corn Chowder in a few weeks.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made the corn broth and made recipe as stated including optional chives. I tasted the risotto prior to adding whipped cream and it tasted great. I think I preferred it w/o the whip cream although both tasted good. The cold whip cream cooled the risotto down a bit, so I think this was my main issue.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’m sorry to report that this dish is absolutely worth the effort and fussiness. (And yep that includes the corn stock). I used salted butter, but otherwise stayed true to the recipe. Will make again, perhaps doubling the recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great recipe! Made exactly as directed and wouldn’t change a thing. Served with grilled chicken but shrimp would also be a great choice.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Love this recipe. First time I made as is but 2nd time I added an extra leek and had no regrets. Served with gulf shrimp cooked in a creole seasoned butter sauce. Fantastic, wife and I each had seconds.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Deelish. 2nd time making it. This time I decided to make a few changes. I subbed red onion/garlic for the leeks. Subbed vegetable broth for corn stock. Added red pepper flakes. Added toasted pinenuts in recipe, and as garnish. Used chives in the recipe, and as garnish. Used parm cheese as a binder 3/4 way through. Then as final sub, I blended in crumbled goat cheese to add more of a creamy consistency. Served with a 6 ounce prime filet and a glass of cab. Bravissimo!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made the corn stock and it was definitely a nice change from using our usual veggie/chicken broth. also created our own cream from extra corn kernels as was suggested. My wife loved this recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I recommend using the corn stock. It is delicious, and of course, vegetarian.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious, but timing is much longer than suggested, especially if making the broth (which was delicious).  Only modification is I added a teaspoon of chicken base to the broth, and didn't whip the cream.  Would be nice with a garnish of basil and perhaps something crunchy...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was amazing! I followed the recipe exactly as written (including the corn broth) and it was so delicious!  I have tried making risotto’s in the past and for some reason the rice comes out slightly undercooked- but this was tender and creamy. This will definitely be a go to recipe in my house for years to come. I loved the corn flavor!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very cheese heavy, i wish the corn flavor stood out more

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The first time I made this, the corn seemed a little sparse, so the next time, I doubled the corn.  It worked out well.  It gives a bit more texture, and more intense corn taste.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Substituted whole milk for heavy cream and doubled the amount of Parmesan. Turned out great! Added an extra stick of celery and some garlic and onion powder, too.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used chicken stock and left out the leek. I am broke and have no space in my kitchen so I just put the heavy cream towards the end and let it absorb. So so tasty. And because it is winter I used frozen corn. Didn’t use all 6 cups of the stock, I used about 5 cups.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I made this during the 
Ate summer with fresh corn. OMG! This was so good, I couldn’t believe it. Once the fresh seasonal corn was gone, I tried making it with canned corn. Blech. Keep it fresh, y’all.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I think this is one of the best things I ever made!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Skipped the heavy cream as suggested in some comments. Served with a lite cherry tomato/lime/cilantro salad on top. So so good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very nice! I make a similar corn risotto but add diced poblanos and top with toasted pine nuts.  Colorado cook here. Mmm.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This is great base soup, easy to whip up with almost no prep. I suspect you can add almost anything (veggies, sausage, etc), but if you do, I'd recommend making sure to scale up the amount of lemon juice you use; the freshness is a critical flavor component.

I added kale, (which I didn't quite cut small enough so we did a lot of chewing...). I also used a potato masher to soften things up a bit at the end for a creamier soup, and added a bit of paprika and cayenne.

Great recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If you're using canned chickpeas, why would this need to cook for an hour?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was really good... after it had the chance to sit for a day or two. I took some hints from a similar recipe that I make often (Chickpea Stew with Orzo & Mustard Greens), and sauteed some fennel & a shake of red pepper flakes with the onions, & threw in some chopped cherry tomatoes halfway through the cooking time. Also added a few handfuls of baby spinach & some lemon zest at the end. Finally, I had used all my Parmesan rinds, so added a 2-in chunk of cheese to cook down.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sounds great "J," i'll give it a try. I added my own (not frozen) Kale and some precooked black rice. And a dash or three of liquid smoke. Wow.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My husband's cousin in Tuscany makes a chickpea based soup, but cuts up an onion, garlic, celery and carrot, along with stock (made with a stock cube).  She finishes by blending it with a hand blender.  a drizzle of  olive oil gives it a lovely finish.   Super easy, super yummy, with little flecks of colour.   I think you could use this  as a sort of "stone soup" recipe, with whatever happens to be in the vegetable drawer.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Lori I first tried this soup in Umbria in someone’s home on our honeymoon 9 years ago. We’ve been cooking it ever since!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I thought this soup was delicious and simple. Like always, I was too lazy to finely dice the onion so it ended up as more of a rough chop but I didn't think it was a detriment at all, especially after I let them sauté for so long they had almost caramelized. I added in a large pinch of saffron with the chickpeas which added a lovely flavor. Like Tim, I mashed it up a bit and greatly increased the amount of lemon juice. It was just perfect for a cold winter night. Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Instant Pot version: At step two, add 1 1/4 C dried chickpeas (rinsed but not soaked) and 1qt chicken stock. 40 min on high pressure with natural release. Add lemon and salt at the end. Top with Parmesan. Delicious and so easy! I’ll make more next time and add some greens. Although it’s also fine on its own.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added diced a potato at the beginning with the chick peas; added mushrooms for the last few minutes; as for spices, also added paprika and crushed red pepper. Delicious, simple, and warming on a cold night!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Could chicken broth be used in place of water?  Could cumin or some other seasoning be used to add flavor?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Of course!  :-)  You should experiment and post your results!  :-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Of course! Go to town!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is very bright and delicious. You could add flavors but it is great as is. We ate it as stew over jasmine rice.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add spinach maybe

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Super simple soup, you can eat it plain or add more veggies and meat to it. As a college student on a budget I would highly recommend to anyone who wants to make something healthy cheap and flavorful.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What recipe only has one clove of garlic?  Is that even a thing?  ;-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Surely they mean one head.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @David for me that means no cloves of garlic, which I regularly delete from recipes with excellent results. Garlic just doesn’t work for some of us.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @David Believe it or not, some of us don’t really like garlic very much.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I only had 1 can of chickpeas, so I substituted one of them with some chopped zucchini. I also added some rosemary, thyme, red pepper flake, and paprika for added flavor. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Lemons are magic in soups.  This is a wonderful soup made with chick peas cooked from scratch, warms over nicely, added greens just at end to steam for a few minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cooked this today and it was delicious - more delicious than expected. I planned to use chicken broth but was out so I used water as instructed, but was skeptical. It came out great. It doesn't need broth! I upped the garlic to 3 cloves, put in 1 tsp of red pepper flakes with the garlic, added a diced potato with the chickpeas, and a handful of parsley and cilantro sprigs 30 minutes in. Also added some Taylor ham (I'm in NJ). Delicious and flavorful. I plan to reheat with some kale!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I loved this soup!  Additions during the simmering phase:a splash of white wine, a rind of parmesan, and a bunch of cilantro tied in a bouquet garni.  Didn't even need stock!  Garnished with grated parm and chopped fresh cilantro.  Will definitely make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A simple recipe like this shouldn’t be so good, but it is. I’ve made it twice now. The first time I followed the recipe and it was great, the second time I cooked in broth instead of water and added a couple handfuls of roughly torn kale in the last few minutes of cooking time and it was even better. DON’T skimp on the lemon juice, it really brings the whole dish together. A sprinkle of fresh grated Parmesan at the end is perfection.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This could be treated as the great Tunisian soup - a meal in a bowl - lalabi. As served in Tunis, the soup base is similar to this. It is the canvas for additions such as: a soft boiled egg or chopped egg, capers, chopped garlic, tuna, more lemon, and harissa (wonderful hot pepper condiment from Tun. that you can buy in a tube). When I serve this I let folks help themselves to condiments they like. I do add lots of carrots and some tomatoes to the broth, though.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Only had one can of chickpeas so I added a few handfuls of kale and a diced orange bell pepper. I didn’t have much time so I covered and let it boil for about 20 minutes in stead of bubbling for an hour. Served with garlic toast. So easy and yummy!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was skeptical of how good this could be - right up until the point of adding the lemon juice. That really brought the whole dish together! A couple of modifications that I made - I added the salt and pepper at the beginning, and I added some spinach to wilt right at the end.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So bright and lemony! I used chicken stock instead of water and WOWZERS! You really could add rice or shredded chicken or really anything to this delicious recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have to say I was very skeptical about this soup, but the preparation and list of ingredients are so simple that I had to try it. I let it cook for more than 1 hour and stirred it many times to incorporate the starch, creating a creamy soup but still with small bits of chickpeas. The parmesan is a great finish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a satisfying and delicious soup. I followed the recipe exactly. I added a Parmesan rind while it cooked. I think cooking for an hour was key to the success of the dish. I used 1/2 of a pretty large lemon, so it may have counted as a whole lemon. I’d say the recipe yields 3 bowls of soup. Next time, I’ll double it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used the juice of a full lemon, torn up kale, and some paprika. Absolutely delicious. And it gets even better the next day.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is so good, and so simple! I added a few chopped carrots to the onions and a little basil at the end. I’ll make it again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Adding a handful of diced cherry tomatoes halfway through and wilted spinach at the end makes for even more flavour. Also added a teaspoon of cumin and a pinch of red pepper flakes with the garlic. Lots of parmesan to finish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This is a great recipe! It’s simple yet delicious! 
I add nothing to it- eat it as is. I love to dip in it toasted bread spread with quick homemade pesto (when feeling festive) or just rubbed with garlic and olive oil (when feeling lazy)
Don’t skip it.
.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add salt and pepper (a lot of pepper) when you start the hour long simmer. It adds a lot more flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Could this be served cold?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Hi Pat, unfortunately not. This soup is best served hot.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added a couple dashes of liquid hemlock smoke to the "Chick Pea soup.  SHAZAMMM!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was so pleasantly surprised how good this was considering the short ingredient list. Made a few tweaks: used half chicken stock/half water, and added 2-3 bay leaves. Didn't add parm - just salt, pepper, a generous portion of lemon juice, and a little drizzle of olive oil on top. So bright and warming!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Any reason why you wouldn't keep the aquafaba in?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add a lot of rosemary, mash up some of the chickpeas, and you’ll think you’re in Rome.  Mmmmmmmmm

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added 1 lb. of ground lamb to the sautéed onion and garlic and continued to cook until it was browned. Then after adding the chickpeas, I added 1 tsp. each of ground cumin and coriander, and 1/2 tsp. of turmeric. I added juice from a whole lemon. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I took this recipe in a new direction and thoroughly enjoyed the results:

1. Use an entire garlic bulb; ~6 cloves
2. Blend it until smooth and bisque-like
3. Garnish with tarragon

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I blended too, it was good

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added vegetable broth and made it like a more liquid soup.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Swap one can chopped tomatoes for 1can chickpeas
Blitz chunky
Scant handful pasta after blitz

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just made the instant pot version suggested in the comments, and it's terrific.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: i am reading this after cobbling together a dinner of items i had on hand, both because of budget concerns & to use up some stray items in my pantry.  I won't go into my entire recipe, but is pretty close to this one. but I will share that I did the entire thing in the microwave. I just do the onions on medium --you'll have to play aroudn with your machine, I start at 10, and then go from there.  It is a common method since this was published. Works fantastic and doesn't compromise taste.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I dislike the texture of chickpeas, so I am going to try this with cannellini beans, which are creamier to start with and probably will break down faster. I usually use them for a hummus-like dip too. Although they have slightly less protein than chickpeas, they are richer in B vitamins, so they are a nutritionally sound alternative. I won't discard all of the liquid in the can and will take care not to over-salt the soup. Remember Melissa's caution about flavorless unsalted canned beans!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is not a soup. It is brothy beans, and they will be infinitely better if you make them from dried. Top with a poached egg, some grated parm, and a glug of olive oil, maybe some greens, and then you’re closer to soup territory.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Why rinse away the aquafaba?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: In my experience, it can have a kind of canned flavor and/or be overly salty.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This soup is a miracle! Made the reader’s instant pot version with dry beans & chicken broth, left it and went to work, came home, added lemon juice, used the stick blender on it and presto, wonderful soup at the end of the day. So grateful!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I’ve been cooking a version of this soup for almost a decade after trying it in a home in Umbria. Here is what I’ve learned:
-Use leeks instead of white onion and sauté it with some rosemary (could be dried or fresh)
-The best flavor came from if I started with raw chickpeas and let them cook in a slow cooker while I worked all day. The broth is so so flavorful.
-If I were using canned chickpeas, I use high quality and dump in the chickpeas along with their liquid. It thickens the sauce nicely.
- I always use stock, not water. 
-always finish with a drizzle of good olive oil and Parmesan if you have it.
- excellent additions to this are fennel (you can sautee with the leeks and rosemary) and tossing in kale at the end.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add chopped carrots and celery after step 2 and use (chicken) broth instead of water for more flavor. I also recommend adding a chunk of fresh ginger and fresh parsley.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I love a recipe that delivers maximum flavour with little effort. I can see how this concoction could be subject to infinite variation (fresh rosemary, mushrooms, bits of kale that need a new home) but sometimes is nourishing to make something so simple that delivers beyond one's expectations. This is when cooking soup becomes alchemy.   I woudl love more recipes like this one. A new NYT category please? No work great results.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is it possible to add vegetables to make it a little more complex without altering the recipe too much? Carrots, green beans, broccoli, etc?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Spinach would be tasty, but I added kale for something with a bit more heft to it. Super good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What adjustments would be needed if I wanted to cook this using dried (but soaked of course) chickpeas instead of canned?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I always add a bit of sake. So comforting.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I tried it with 3 extra cloves of garlic, rosemary, shredded or ground chicken and swiss chard. Healthy and delish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So easy to make. I put it in a slow cooker for a few hours and left it alone. Unlike a lot of NYT recipes this one is both affordable and relies on ingredients that I usually already have.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Beautiful soup for a rainy day!! I added chopped green onions, Slivered sauteed broccoli stems..YUM

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yes, a simple soup.  But gee, after all that gentle bubbling, it still tasted like a can of chickpeas in water, with a little bit of lemon.  No magic alchemy.  I still have quite a bit left, so tomorrow, I`ll add in leftover roasted vegetables, some Italian herbs, and some stock. i think it will be quite good then, even if it isn`t so simple.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Easy and very tasty. I used chicken stock for some added flavor and it turned out wonderful.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So Simple. I had a double smoked bacon end so I tossed it in while simmering. About 10 minutes before we ate I added sun dried cherry tomatoes and a few handfuls frozen kale. My kids were sad there weren't more leftovers.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I agree this is a great base! I added ham, used one can of chickpeas and one can of great northern beans, chicken broth instead of water, and added carrot for a little color. Skipped the parm (ham is already so salty). So basically I made a different soup, but was inspired by this one and was very pleased with how comforting a meal it was :-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I cooked dried garbanzos in the oven at 275 degrees, with 2 cloves of garlic, a bay leaf, and a ¼ teaspoon each of dried rosemary and thyme. For the soup, I didn't have onion, but found ¾ cup of freeze-dried shallots and sautéed those before adding the garlic. Used 4 cups of garbanzos with the cooking liquid in place of water.

I was out of Parmesan, which would have been delicious, but the soup was fantastic without it. Magical flavors I'm still savoring an hour later.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’m wondering if I can reserve the liquid from the can and add it back in with the water

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added chopped fresh rosemary, but forgot to add lemon juice (next time). It was delicious, so easy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Black chickpeas are a delicious alternative to white chickpeas. Although they do require overnight pre soaking and then pressure cooking. They do sprout well and the sprouts can be added to salads. AI generated: "Black chickpeas have higher amounts of protein, fiber, and certain minerals like magnesium and zinc compared to white chickpeas, while also containing more potent antioxidants like anthocyanins and flavonoids due to their dark pigment."

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This reminds me of a soup my Basque grandmother use to make,  The only difference is that she added Chorizo (Basque not Mexican) with the onion while it cooked, and did not add  lemon.  I made this version as written, and it is simple, comforting and the lemon adds a touch of brightness.  Next time I would add a sprinkle of Piment d'Espelette.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Delicious and so easy!
Made the following modifications:
Used the aquafaba from the chickpeas and chicken bone broth instead of water, no added salt, juice of the entire lemon, four garlic cloves and added parm rinds. Upon reheating added some kale.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add a half teaspoon of ground cumin to the onions and garlic before adding the chick peas. I use the juice of a whole lemon, or add a bit of finely grated lemon zest

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is really good, and I especially appreciated the info on doing this in the instant pot.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Use low sodium chicken stock instead of water.  This, plus a generous amount of parmesan, then blended in a vita mix was amazing!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was regretting not following the recipe as written, since I haven't made this before.  I had some carrot, celery and kale that needed to be used, so I added them.  One whole lemon, veggie better than bouillon & much more garlic than written.  I thought I'd kind of wrecked the whole thing last night, and that this was going to be a batch of soup that was "just OK".  Having it for lunch today, it has no business being this good. Finishing a bowl as I'm typing, seriously considering another.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was one of the best soups I ever tasted. I used three cloves garlic and two tsp’s ginger. As suggested by others I doubled the lemon juice. Garnished with cashews and Parmesan. It was really awesome.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is a great tasting and very impressive looking cake. Definitely took me a lot longer than 90 minutes, though. Three notes: the cakes rose less than I anticipated, so the layers were thin; though when the cake was assembled it was the perfect size. It took quite a while for the syrup to hit 240 degrees - at least 10 minutes after the initial 3 of boiling. And I needed to beat the meringues + syrup for about 10 minutes to get to the right consistency.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I don't have a candy thermometer :( what can I do to judge the temperature  instead?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You really do need a candy thermometer because the frosting is temperature sensitive.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have the same question?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: You can use the cold water method. You'll want the very beginnings of the soft ball stage. It's going to be harder to get what you need but if you want to give it at try without a thermometer, this is your best bet. https://www.recipetips.com/kitchen-tips/t--1459/how-to-test-stages-of-sugar.asp

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am not the best baker and was daunted by this intensive recipe. However, after baking it and serving it a party I won't make any other chocolate cake again. This one is PERFECT and to die for....the icing is excellent too. Great for all occasions and beautiful at that.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great cake! I added 1 t. espresso powder to the dry ingredients to enhance the flavor

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How long do you need to assemble and cook it? thank you...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Sharon 4 days ago
We made this and it is a process, but good. The cakes were not as impressive or super moist. Ended up only cutting the "round domes" off for the crumble. The frosting yielded more than needed. Overall, for time and effort in, we can't say that it was worth it even though we liked it. Probably wouldn't make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Don't be scared off from this recipe by the Italian meringue -- the final result is worth it!! The icing is light and wonderful, and the chocolate cake is rich and delicious. The full recipe took me just under 90 minutes to complete, and got rave reviews from all my friends!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Boiled icing like this can be tricky. It is affected by humidity and altitude. Growing up we called this divinity frosting and didn't use a thermometer but gauged it by the "strings". It is my very favorite frosting but I use a thermometer for the infrequent times I make it now.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The cake itself was great but no one in my family, including myself, liked the frosting.  If I make this again I will go with my standard butter cream frosting I learned to make in a Wilton decorating class

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Traditional boiled icing, a version of Italian meringue when done a little differently with butter beaten in could become a pure white buttercream

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The cake is delicious, but the icing tasted as if there was too much vanilla. One tablespoon seemed to overpower the lightness of the lovely marshmallow frosting.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I think that the amount of vanilla to be used in the frosting, should be only 1 teaspoon; must have been some kind of a typo, as, 1 tablespoon, equals 3 teaspoons

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The frosting sounds like a more complicated version of seven-minute frosting. Has anyone substituted? How did it turn out?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can prepackaged egg whites be used for the icing? Seems so due to the cream of tartar addition but would love to know for sure. Thank you - recipe sounds delightful!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Has anyone made this without the chocolate chips?  I am looking for a classic devil's food cake and this seems pretty close except for the chips.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This looks like the cake on the cover of Dorie Greenspan's classic book "Baking." Which is an excellent recipe and book.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cake is delicious, though a little dry. Frosting is ok, but I’ll try another marshmallow frosting recipe next time. Not enough flavor, though it’s a great texture.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Rather than dumping the soggy dough on a floured board, I leave the dough in the mixing bowl, dip both damped hands down the far side of the bowl under the dough raising it up and folding it over towards you.  Turn the bowl 90 degrees and repeat 3 more times until you have completed 4 folds, 360 degrees around the bowl.  By the 3rd sequence, dough will be fairly easy to handle. Cleanup is much easier as the dough never leaves the original bowl until is ready to shape and much easier to handle.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: With all this scraping, folding, cutting, folding again, etc., this recipe is an excellent candidate for an accompanying video. Just reading, it seems unbearably complicated.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My thoughts exactly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A video would be very helpful, for any bread recipe in general so you know what the dough should look like and how moist it should be. For Ciabatta, The Great British Baking Show Masterclass has a great video on how to make this bread.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just use plenty of flour on the work surface and use two bench scrapers. You are right - a video would certainly help. It’s very tricky. For me, the bread came out wonderfully.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
A little clarification, please:

"Using a bench scraper, flip the top edge down to just below the center, then flip the bottom side up above the center. Do the same with each side, then turn dough over and dust off the flour."

I'm not entirely certain what you're doing with the bench scraper, could you provide more information regarding this?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The dough is a very wet dough...so it's easier to use the scraper to lift the edges as you "fold" the dough (a gentle form of kneading) to help develop the gluten.  The exercise resembles folding a piece of paper into 3rds before it goes into an envelope - first fold the dough in the vertical orientation; then in the horizontal orientation.  It takes about 15 seconds and it doesn't have to be pretty, just somewhat gentle to keep the gluten strands intact.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Is it just me or was the result very salty?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This dough was way too wet and sticky for me to work with. It was almost soup-like. I added a little more flour, but was still unable to form individual buns. It wasn't a total lose. I put the entire blob in the cast-iron cooker, and it all came out OK... But will not repeat....

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe has a technical omission that should be accounted for in step 7 - the warmed jam must be cooled to room temperature before it can be spread on the rolled out dough, else the butter and cream cheese in the dough will soften to the point of making the rolling-up step impossible.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've made about 6 batches of these and did not have the problem you're describing at all.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: thank you for the tip

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Too many ingredients in the filling. I agree w/ Jo.L, that Nathan's and Heatter's are simpler.

Traditional rugelach have EITHER a preserve filling; or a cinnamon/nut/raisin mixture; but never both; and certainly never both + chocolate.

Come to think of it, chocolate was never an ingredient in our Russian grandmother's babka, rugelach, or any dessert. I guess because there was no chocolate in her shtetl. :-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I agree wholeheartedly!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: actually my great aunts recipe calls for apricot jam and cinnamon, walnut raisin..from Russia...so maybe there is no such thing as never both...:)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I'd really appreciate it (as would a million others) if the Times would stop assuming we all have a food processor.  How about providing directions for alternative methods?  A regular mixer?  Stand, or hand held?  or, as my bubbe made her rugelach, by hand...
Thanks, Times.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If someone doesn't have a food processor, then they could use the time-tested techniques that one would always use for incorporating butter/sour cream into flour-cutting in with a pastry blender or two knives.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The dough here is a "classic" like one passed down in my family from a Brooklyn (née Austria) grandmother. Other fillings are possible and hers differed from this one. None had chocolate. Her most popular was a dab of orange marmalade - not necessary to soften it before spreading - plus chopped walnuts for crunch; or a mix of sugar, raisins, chopped walnuts, and cinnamon. No sugar on top. Experiment! And be prepared - rolling up the sections can be fiddly; don't over fill.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Half pound butter, half pound cream cheese, 1-1/2 c flour. Roll out rectangle. on floured board. Spread with grape jelly (use offset spreader), chopped walnuts, raisins and sugar-cinnamon mixture. Slice in half. Roll each half jelly roll style and slice using pizza cutter. My family has been doing it this way for 70+ years and it has never failed to be a hit

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I found this just a little bit too fussy--but then, I'm an old person.  I have another rugelach recipe, courtesy of Joan Nathan, and one from Maida Heatter, both of which are more simple and, I think, just as delicious!  Perhaps I'm not accustomed to the chocolate, which I don't remember from either the rugelach my grandmother made (along with her killer blintzes) or the Jewish Bakeshop in Cincinnati, where my mother bought rugelach for the holidays.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Correct, dear Jo.L.  You don't remember chocolate because it's a convention brought to Jewish cooking by baby boomers.  The Eastern European kitchen didn't know chocolate.  And that, more than any other reason, is why I don't make anything from our ancestors with chocolate.  Chocolate babka?  Nope.  Chocolate ruggies?  Nope.  :-)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Don't roll too tightly, it'll keep the dough from getting flakey in the middle.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My Grandma used sour cream and an egg yolk to make the dough.  I think sour cream would have been more typical in Russia than cream cheese.  Anyway the recipe makes really delicious ruggies.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Delicious.  Made one batch with peach jam and one batch with raspberry. Crust was ultra flaky and filling yummy. 

One problem was getting the cream cheese and butter to meld in the food processor. Sounded as though it was going to burn out the motor. 

It may help to let the butter get softer rather than just leaving it out for 10 minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious, light and flaky.  I may never buy rugelach again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious, but if following the directions this will only makes 32 cookies, not 36 (cutting two circles into 16 pieces).   And I split the fillings--half the dough got preserves, cinnamon and sugar, and currants, the other half got preserves, cinnamon and sugar, and chocolate.  Both delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I make the dough without a food processor? Will a pastry blender work?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sure it will. Blend until coarse crumb texture like a shortbread.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: While these cream-cheese-based rugalach look and probably taste delicious, I make mine with a chilled, yeast-leavened dough that yields a somewhat sweet, but flaky cookie.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am interested in your recipe if you're willing to share.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written however I used gluten free flour. Everyone said it was the best rugalach they’d ever had - bar none. Gluten free dough is a little stiffer to work so patience and beaten egg and water for glue are needed. I agree with the comments about chocolate. While my wife likes chocolate, I don’t recall them as a youth. I’m making a batch for a big party and I believe I’ll make only a few of the chocolate ones for my bride.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I tried baking these on two different racks. That didn’t work for me. With the second batch, I will bake each batch individually. Great recipe. Thank you.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
...what can I say...my friends scoffed them down with their pre-dinner wine.They reminded me of NYC and the Second Avenue bakery.
thank-you.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: nevermind, just saw the answer

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very good recipe, but labor-intensive and a bit too chocolatey for my taste. Next time I will make the dough and leave out the chocolate. It overwhelms. No subtlety of flavor and can't taste the jam, cinnamon. But chocolate lovers will be in heaven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I had to add some liquid to the dough, I used about 1 TBL lemon juice and 1 TBL of half and half (just what I had on hand). This made it much easier to form into a ball without making it too sticky. 
You can substitute any flavor of jam and I think these would come out well. I believe that in western Europe a chocolate filling is common and the fruity fillings are more common to the east. Both are great options for this..it's fun to experiment with flavor!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This makes 32 cookies, not 36.  Not a big deal unless you are trading multiple dozens with others in a cookie swap.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can you use Neufchatel low fat cream cheese instead of regular cream cheese? I always have one and not the other.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have and they turn out just fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great recipe. This was my first time making rugelach, and the recipe was easy to follow, and they came out delicious. Everyone loved them.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did you have an issue with the dough being hard to keep together to form a ball to cut in half? I've made 4 batches of the dough so far, following the recipe exactly, and it's happened every time. With enough effort, I can get it into enough of a ball to form a disc, but it's always a struggle and the dough is very crumbly. Bakes really nicely but I wish the first step was easier. Any thoughts?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: is the decorative sugar put on before cooking or after?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: i subbed pistachio for the nuts, and left out the raisins/currants. and made sure to let the jam cool (as someone mentioned in the previous comments) completely (don’t wanna melt the dough!). perfect level of sweetness (but this will rest on the sweetness of the jam you use, too). these are absolutely delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is one of our time-tested repeat holiday cookie recipes. We have made them every year since the Times first published the recipe. A delicious, delicate, crunchy winner!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Reminds me of visiting Helmi-täti (aunt) around Joulu (Christmas) but hers were cut into big round flower with many small petals.
Smells and tastes like a warm heart.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just made again and they turned out great again. I followed the recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great cookies! We’ll be making them again this year. They are very light and airy like wafers.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Good for Almost Everything Pie Dough Recipe
commentBody: Yum! I’ve never tried a pie crust from scratch and followed this to the letter ... turned out great! I had to transfer the dough to the freezer a few times throughout the rolling process (a silicon mat proved invaluable, as did a hefty bench scraper and lots of flour). I managed to make two 4.5” personal savory veggie pies out of this recipe, including a lattice top for each. I par-baked them for 12 mins at 350F with a tin foil lining weighted with dried beans, then followed my recipe as normal.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Good for Almost Everything Pie Dough Recipe
commentBody: This was the first truly successful pie crust I've ever made. No soggy bottoms. I did freeze it and bake it from frozen. It needed about an extra 5-10 minutes in my oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Good for Almost Everything Pie Dough Recipe
commentBody: Hi there! I'm an experienced pie baker. I followed this recipe exactly, along with the chilling. I was disappointed with its crispness and toughness and will go back to my own default recipe for pie pastry (adapted from an old one of  J Child) that uses both a.p. flour and pastry flour and 3 kinds of fat. Plus some lemon juice. Stay safe!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Good for Almost Everything Pie Dough Recipe
commentBody: For me, big pieces of butter are not fine because they've melted and left holes in my crust.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I make a pie very much like this, but I prebake the shell
and coat the bottom with a thin layer of chocolate by chopping semisweet chocolate chips and spreading them on the bottom of the shell while hot. I let the pie shell cool then put the filling in. The chocolate coating keeps the shell from getting soggy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have done this with a thin layer of white chocolate.  Really gilding the lily.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious pie, but, not enough cream topping. It took me a lot more than an hour to make. It was difficult to put the brown sugar through the sieve so I pressed it with a fork to take out the clumps.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Go get a flour sifter! It works wonders!!!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Or, even better: put the brown sugar in the food processor and pulse for a few seconds!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
