Serve the same day but custard can be made and kept in fridge covered with plastic wrap. ( be sure it touches the top of the custard)
Double the whipped topping

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
maybe use all those egg whites for meringue instead of whipped cream.
put chocolate bits (semi-sweet) in bottom of pie - but not so much!
An all day project!  But boy, is it good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We loved this pie!  Not to sweet and the texture of the custard was amazing. We used a store bought crust due to time constraints; we’ll make the crust recommended next time.  We baked the crust for 10 minutes and added bittersweet chips to half. So good!!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I took a number of short cuts, I didn't sift the sugar and used a bought graham cracker base.
This recipe is based on Ireland's wonderful banoffee, it isn't as rich as banoffee, but is more expensive. I would make one and a half times more filling if I make this again and lightly flake a little dark chocolate or cocoa over the cream.
However, if I'm going to make a rich, creamy dessert with bananas, I'll probably resort to Darina Allen's banoffee and not this.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The pastry cream curdled for me.  Low heat, perhaps instead of the recommended medium heat?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: I baked these last year.  They did come soft, almost mushy.  A parchment lining is foolproof for getting anything out of a baking pan.  I used  Scharffenberger unsweetened and Ghiradelli 60% but the flavor was muted.  I wouldn't make them again.  I like the Classic Brownies in Greenspan's "Baking with Dorie" better.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely perfect brownies! You have to watch the baking time carefully, so that they stay moist. I've made them a few times already, also delicious with walnuts or pecans.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: I've tried these several times, and they have excellent flavor, but they are impossibly fragile and don't cook in 28 minutes.  The recipe in Baking with Julia provides for 1.25 cups of flour, and even this seems like not quite enough.  I wish I could find this recipe with weighted measures so that I could follow it more precisely.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: Thank you for this recipe - I've also tried them with chopped nuts and topped with powdered sugar and they came out perfect each time!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: I could not get these out of the pan. Fragile is putting it mildly. I don't know if I did something wrong, but even with a fair amount of extra cooking time they stayed beyond gooey, more like a thick liquid.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: We had the same problem.  The brownies wouldn't set after even 10 extra minutes of baking

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: Taking note of the comments, I sprayed my pan with baking spray (flour/butter).  They seem to be coming out quite easily.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: This also turned out very gooey for me, though not undesirably so. Flavor is very rich and decadent, particularly given the gooeyness. Small pieces are enough to satisfy.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: This recipe needs to be edited. It doesn't make sense. There are two cups of sugar, but we are told to add one cup to the chocolate and butter, and twice told to do different things with the remaining sugar and an unspecified number of eggs.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody: It says to use 1 c. of the sugar to mix with the chocolate, and 1 c. to mix with the eggs (4 of them specified in the ingredients list). You add 1/2 of the sugar-egg mixture to the chocolate, and then you whip the other half of the sugar-egg mixture some more until it has doubled in volume, and then you fold that into the chocolate-sugar mixture as well.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Rick Katz’s Brownies for Julia Child Recipe
commentBody:
These brownies turned out perfectly, flat and soft. I used a 13 inch baking tray and greased it lightly so I didn't have any problems getting them out.
Also I only used high quality dark chocolate because in Germany it is almost impossible to buy unsweetened chocolate on short notice. To compensate, I reduced the sugar a little.
Everyone was highly complimentary of the result! I have tried many brownie recipes, and this one definitely ranks high in texture and taste.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Best cheesecake recipe that I have found- creamy and decadent. I have made it with the foil, and without. Easiest to just put a roasting pan of hot water in the low rack of the oven at the same time, and put the springform pan on a cookie sheet on middle rack. No worries for leaking, and no cracking happened. Wait for just the right jiggle in the center, and cool slowly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is Dorie Greenspan's recipe. Why haven't you given her credit? This is word for word from her cookbook sitting on my counter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Clean cuts with a taut line of dental floss

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I was just about to add this comment ... I have used unflavored dental floss to cut my cheese cakes for years.  Works like a charm.  This articular cheese cake is classic and very good.  I do like to add a bit of citrus rind, either lemon or orange, but it's very good on its own.

commentBody: A WARM, NOTwet knife works well

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed this recipe to a 't' and it turned out SOO good-- an hour and a half was plenty of time in the oven for the filling to bake. I used Tate's chocolate chip cookies for the crust to make it gluten free and it was magnificent. WOW! Guests said it was better than The Cheesecake Factory. Will definitely be making this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: In place of graham I like to use Zweibackh crumbs.  Gives it a very delicate flavor, not as sweet, but same measurements for all else in a graham crust.  This recipe is adaptable to lots of other additions, I generally add lemon or orange zest, have added rum flavoring in place of vanilla, if carefully shaved into tiny bits, some chocolate ... all good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Loved everything about this cheesecake!  Light and full of flavor.  To intensify the vanilla flavor, I also cut open a vanilla bean and scraped it into the cheesecake mixture prior to baking.  Yummy!

author: Emily Weinstein

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: For people having trouble with cracking and leaking check your springform to make sure it's not dented/fitting in the rim. Also I put cake in the empty pan before putting it in the oven, then added the boiling water to the pan, so I didn't have to move the bath, which might cause some water to splash into the foil. I foiled almost to the rim of the pan, and poured the water only 1/2 way up. No leaks or cracks.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
So good. Worth every minute. I like to put a piece of parchment in the bottom of the pan to make it easier to transfer to a cake plate. Its a favorite for the holidays.
(Will be trying the dental floss trick next time.)

commentBody:
Use only wide heavy duty aluminum foil.

Took longer than 1.5 hours

author: Emily Weinstein

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this exactly per recipe except changed crust to go gluten free.  For the crust I used a bag of Tate's Ginger Zinger cookies minus the crystalized ginger which I had to pick out and the listed amount of other ingredients.  For the filling I used 1 cup s. cream and a 1/2 cup heavy cream.  There were barely enough crumbs to  cover the bottom and sides (only 1 cup), but I think the flavor profile and ratio of cake to crust was perfect so I'll use the same amount next time.  Keeper!

commentBody: Has anyone ever heard used plain Greek yogurt for all or part of the sour cream

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn't bake nearly as long. A browned cheesecake is easily overcooked and isn't nearly as smooth and creamy. It only took an hour to set up to "just jiggly" all across the cake, just barely starting to brown at the edges. Let it cook slowly on the counter until nearly room temp before refrigerating. Added 1/4 cup sprinkles, 1 tsp almond baking emulsion & 2 tsp "butter vanilla" baking emulsion for a "funfetti birthday cake" cheesecake. Topped with whipped cream icing  Great base recipe!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: In fact, when adding a delicious icing of sour cream (with gelatin or agar-agar) there's really no need to bother with the water bath during the baking. Also, it's so much prettier, especially if you put edible flowers on top for the Jewish holiday Shavuot for instance. Here are a few jokes about cheesecake https://schibboleth.com/blooming-jewish-cheesecake-with-tablets-of-the-l...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
My family LOVES this cake! Thank you, New York Times!

Ensure you aerate the filling as directed, as this will make a huge difference in your final product. For me, the trickiest part of this recipe is the graham cracker crust, in particular achieving an even thickness when pressing it into the pan.

I like that you can adjust the filling flavouring, or keep it as is. When I keep the original flavouring, I serve the cake with homemade, preserved fruits that I spoon on top when serving.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The mixing is important, be patient and wait for it to come together. You will see it happen. Use an oven thermometer to double check your baking temp.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I use a roasting bag, like you'd use for a turkey, and I add a layer of foil around that, but the foil isn't even needed. Just roll and tuck the excess bag just below the rim of the pan and you will have a leak proof situation and yummy crunchy crust.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've made this tart four times in the past month using a variety of fruits in the place of the raspberry jam. The crust is shortbread-like.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
tart dough: combine 1 1/4c flour, 1t sugar, 1/2t salt in FP. add 6T cold butter cut into bits, process until coarsely mixed. combine 1 lg egg and 1t ice water, add to FP in 3 additions. gather mixture into a ball on the counter, form a disk, chill min 3hrs. 

roll out to 12" diameter and 1/4" thick, line tart pan and trim excess. prick with a fork, chill min 1hr. 

cover/press with buttered foil, bake @400 on sheet pan for 20mins. remove foil n bake for 3-5mins more, let cool before filling.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Bruce--good question!  I think "food prcessor"? I'm going to use mine because that's how I usually make pie crust.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’m guessing FP = food processor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Tomato-Mustard Tart
Add 1 very large (or) 2 average sized tomatoes.  Other choices are 2-3 plum tomatoes (or)  15-20 cherry/grape tomatoes.  If using cherry/grape tomatoes, cut in half.  If using round or plum tomatoes, cut  into 1/3 to 1/2 slices.  Arrange the halves (cut side down) or the slices in the filled tart shell.  Do not worry if they sink into the filling.  Just make sure the filling does not rise above the sides of the crust.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious!  So good, I made it again the next day, this time with cherry tomatoes instead of carrots.  Perfect for a picnic.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Sadly ... We didn’t like this at all. The vegetables didn’t have any flavor. It might be better if the carrots & leeks were sautéed instead of steamed. Won’t be making it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Just made this from Dorie’s book and it is really wonderful—much more than the sum of its parts. I found that one normal leek and two small carrots would have more than enough veggie for a 9 1/2” tart. 

That said, this isn’t an “adaptation” of any kind. It’s  Dorie’s words and recipe almost verbatim, right down to the exhortation about the freshness of the mustard and the suggestion of how to arrange the vegetables. Same ingredients, same quantities, same prep. That doesn’t seem cool to me...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow, really tasty.  I made it as more as a quiche than a tart:  9.5" pie pan, upped the eggs to 4, cream to 1/2 cup, onions not leeks (cut relatively large slices and steam).  Fun looking if you use rainbow carrots, but easy on the purple ones as they bleed color when steamed.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delish. Followed the tart dough recipient from the linked article. Following the exact recipe as directed and used Grey Poupon Dijon and whole grain mustard from Whole Foods.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious as written, even better/more main dish-y with crab meat stirred into the filling.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I took the suggestion to saute rather than steam the vegetables for more flavor. I used a pre-made gluten-free pie crust. Didn't have heavy cream so used some half-and-half and butter to substitute. Came out well! It's a unique way to enjoy mustard.

commentBody: Just made this with the last 2 of our own large heirloom apples from last fall--poached them in a cup of water with 1/3 cup sugar, a wisp of stick cinnamon, twist of lemon peel and a cardamom pod.  Put it all in the pot together, simmered till apples softened, then took them out and cooked down the syrup.  Exquisite partner for a 49 year old sauternes--life should be so good.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I see a lot of complaints about baking time -- Covering with foil, too much browning, not done at 55 min.
May I suggest investing in an oven thermometer? All ovens run at a variable rate -- gas or electric, some are hotter than the temp set; many are colder. I bake professionally in people's homes and always bring thermometer. It's amazing to see the 100 degree swings in ovens. PLEASE. They're not expensive, and they'll save your recipes more often than you realize.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I wanted this to be more of a coffee cake, so I decreased butter in cake by one tablespoon and added two tablespoons of sour cream. Also added cinnamon to the topping and additional cinnamon to cake. Perfect at 52 minutes. Looks beautiful and tastes wonderful!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made it exactly as the recipe says. Even with everything mise en place the night before, this took half an hour to get into the oven, but it came out beautiful and delicious, and disappeared quickly as people took yet another piece! Very nice for a special breakfast or tea.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious and moist but next time I would double the cinnamon, a tad more nutmeg, and either double the lemon zest or add some lemon extract... yummmm.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I'm a Belgian reader, and find the butter tbs, very inconvenient.
I found 1 tbspoon butter = 14.2 g ! The cake is cooking. No overflow with a 8x8 inches metal pan Le Creuset.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Since I experienced disaster recently with a NYT sour cream coffeecake, I made 2 adjustments when making this recipe: (1) I used a tube pan (not 8x8 so batter overflows) and (2) no butter in the streusel (which sank & was gooey in the other recipe) but I did sprinkle a few pats on the top before baking. Re-adjusted this turned out beautiful & delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This is a "best in class" cake! My family likes a little cinnamon, so I added 1/4 teaspoon to the topping.
Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious cake, all the family loved it! I didn’t have any buttermilk so used yogurt instead, worked beautifully. Will definitely make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: At end of max cooking time (65 mins), topping gold but center wet (liquid bubbling up). Covered it loosely w/foil to prevent burning top & checked every 5 mins for total of 90 mins -- still wet, so turned oven off & left it in (still covered w/foil) 15 mins -- looked less wet but still damp.  After cooling on rack 30 mins, center had sunk in deeply -- texture fine & tasted good, but what happened?  Oven temp is OK, used 8" square Pyrex -- maybe 9" square instead?  So popular, I'd like to fix it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This cake was easy to make, not too sweet, and delicious the next 2 mornings with my coffee. The crumb is especially nice. Set timer for 50 minutes to start testing for doneness; you want the cake light and moist.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I agree with what Ellen said.  This is a great treat for both family and guests to enjoy.  Perfect for dessert or breakfast.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used a metal meatloaf pan, cooked for 65 mins, turned out beautifully. Agree with others that this isn't a "sweet" bread, but I find it the perfect sweetness level especially if you like sugar/creamer in your coffee. I added-- and recommend--the zest of a whole small orange (roughly the size of baseball) which came out to be approx 1 tsp. because I couldn't figure out what a 1/4 of an orange really meant. I would make this again if I had buttermilk I needed to use up.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Kefir is a perfect substitute for buttermilk. I always have some on hand as I use that instead of milk for morning breakfasts.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This cake is perfection—especially the crumb topping, which I plan to appropriate for apple crisp this weekend. Made a few small changes:
- Made this GLUTEN-FREE by swapping in 1:1 King Arthur GF flour. Worked perfectly.
- Rather than zest, which my husband hates (I know, I know), I used the juice of one lemon. Next time might use even more.
- We didn’t have buttermilk, so I used the 1/2 c. whole milk   3/4 tbsp white vinegar tip another commenter recommended.
Took a long time, but worth it

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used frozen blueberries and I thought it didn't taste much like blueberries.  It seemed more plain coffee cake.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly. The cake was moist and delicious, with the blueberries a nice counterpoint to the sweetness of the crumlble topping. I would definitely make this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this because I had beautiful fresh blueberries, left over crumb topping from another recipe and some buttermilk in the fridge.  I was amazed at how thick the batter was- next time I would add an additional 1/4 cup of buttermilk or milk for ease of folding in the blueberries.  The end result, baked for under 55 minutes was good.  Sour cream would be a nice rich addition!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used half whole wheat flour and sour cream thinned with a little milk as that was all that I had.  Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A bit labor intensive, yes, but an absolute winner. I will be making this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Didn’t see the notes about pan size and used a 9 inch cake pan instead of the suggested pan. Almost overflowed. Had to bake for almost 80 minutes.   Will try bundt or large springform next time.  Flavor is really nice - not too sweet.  Will try again when local nj blueberries are in season.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Good coffee cake but nothing special. Lacks of soul. I have bake it for 55 minutes according to instructions. Streusel is a bit to crusty for my taste. I do not thing it is a repeat on my list.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mark Bittman* tells how to “sour” regular milk by adding 2 tablespoons of white vinegar to 1 3/4 cup of milk that is at room temperature. (I use a waterbath; a microwave is okay, too.) Let mixture sit for about 10 min. until it clabbers (thickens with lumps). For this recipe, I added just -3/4 tblsp. to a half-cup milk. My “buttermilk” gave a wonderfully moist zing to this most delicious of crumb cakes. [*How to Cook Everything]

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Spectacular! Overflowed an 8 inch square pan. Should be baked with another bigger pan on the rack beneath it to catch spill, or maybe try a 9 x 9 pan?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Surprised to find a disappointing experience at this site! The cake sagged and was soggy in the middle, even after an hour plus in the oven, but also seemed dry and not that flavorful. The crumble topping was nice but not unusual. Meh.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this coffee cake exactly as presented...it was outstanding!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Easy to assemble and really delicious.  I'll definitely make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Do I have to buy a whole container of buttermilk in order to use 1/2 a cup? Is there no workaround?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Buy powdered buttermilk and mix it as needed. It keeps for months. You can purchase it in the baking section of a large grocery store.  OR you can use 15 T milk and 1 T white vinegar or lemon juice (mix and let stand ~ 5 minutes).  I do the former but the latter works.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thank you Lee!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I use yogurt in place of buttermilk in almost all cases. I almost always have plain yogurt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Good coffee cake, but nothing special. I have baked it for 55 minutes.
Streusel is a bit to crusty for my taste. Maybe I will change some things next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was great! I only had whole milk, not buttermilk, and it turned out fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Mark Bittman* tells how to “sour” regular milk by adding 2 tablespoons of white vinegar to 1 3/4 cup of milk that is at room temperature. (I use a waterbath; a microwave is okay, too.) Let mixture sit for about 10 min. until it clabbers (thickens with lumps). For this recipe, I added just -3/4 tblsp. to a half-cup milk. My “buttermilk” gave a wonderfully moist zing to this most delicious of crumb cakes. [*How to Cook Everything]

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used soy milk instead of buttermilk and used a 9x9 pan. It was great!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This is the standard "plain" cake at my house.  Over the years I have - decreased the oil to 1/3 cup; subbed sour cream or creme fraiche for the yogurt; used greek yogurt (full fat, low fat, non-fat); subbed orange or grapefruit zest for lemon, and served with matching fruit compote; added candied ginger or shredded unsweetened coconut; subtracted the zest and increased the vanilla to 2 tsp. - all good!  The texture changes a little with each swap, but it is always a darn good cake!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Can any of you experience bakers suggest how to swap in butter for the oil? I don't like the texture of oil cakes, but like the idea of this slightly sour one. (I got flagged and scolded for posting on a recipe that I had not made yet, but I assume open questions are allowed...or should be).

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Amanda Hesser says three-quarters of a cup of oil is about the same as one cup of butter. Other recipes say melt the butter and measure out an amount equal to the oil in the recipe. That's about 1 to 1.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I would melt the butter to match the amount of oil...or use coconut oil...you might get a better texture with that.  I sometimes think oil is better in cakes as it's lighter...butter tends to make the cake too heavy and the crust too "sticky".

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: American butter is about 81% fat, the rest being water and other solids. That is, one cup of butter is only .8 cups oil. If you're not using weight, then the easiest estimate for this is 3/4cup (.75cup) oil to 1 cup butter, but note you have a little extra water in your recipe if so!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Step 3 mentions a round pan.  What size?  8" 9"  Strange it's never mentioned except in baking timing.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: A 9" cake pan holds approximately the volume as the 8x4 pan.  It will cook faster, tho.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 8” round works well, but you can also use an 8 1/2” x 4” loaf pan.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 8” round works well.  Or use an 8 1/2” x 4” loaf pan for 50-60 minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
butter an 8 1/2-x-4 1/2-inch loaf pan
bake 35 to 40 minutes for the round cake or 50 to 55 minutes,

Is anyone else confused by the directions? Step 1 says to butter a loaf pan, but in Step 3 to bake 35 50 40 minutes for round or
50 to 55 minutes . . . (for loaf pan?)

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
I finally got around to making this recipe today and it's delicious.
I did make some variations to fit my preferred flavor profile.

I used 1/2 cup sugar
And instead of lemon zest I used 1 teaspoon of Rose Water and 1/2 teaspoon of crushed cardamom seeds with a hint of Saffron
And I skipped the glaze

My kids have eaten half the cake in the first 5 mins. I think I'll be making another batch for the guests coming over tomorrow.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Your substitutions sound wonderful and I've just made rosewater!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this in an 8x8 square pan. It was perfectly done in 30 min. Instead of marmalade glaze, I made a lemon glaze with lemon juice and powdered sugar, spreading it over the cake as soon as it came out of the oven. Absolutely perfect!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
I made this with orange instead of lemon and followed the suggestion of 1/2 sugar. It barely rose, which I imagine is partly from the lack of sugar. Can anyone provide insight?

However, the flavor was lovely and improved over time. Rather than buy marmalade, I made a thin glaze with leftover orange zest, 1 T fresh squeezed orange juice, and about 3/4 c powdered sugar and poured it over the top, and it soaked in to produce a lovely moist but balanced texture. Perfect with tea.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: The recipe has 1 cup sugar, that's quite a bit. If it didn't rise for you, your baking powder may have been a bit old. Check the oven temp as well. Also, try beating the eggs first with an electric mixer, then add sugar and beat some more, before adding the yogurt. Then proceed with the recipe. This is an oldie but goodie, nicely adapted for our times. My mom used to make a similar one for us pretty much every week. I still have the recipe using cocoa mugs as measures.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: For those wanting to stay away from industrial oils, coconut oil or light olive oil or butter all work well.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: A very good recipe. I used only 1/2 cup of sugar.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 10 stars, I had a half a lemon so used it and I had lemon olive oil. I used a 1/4 C of lemon oil and a 1/4 of vegetable oil along with the zest of a half a small lemon. Reduced sugar to 3/4 C. will try 1/2 C next time. Used the juice from the half a lemon, some OJ and 3/4 powdered sugar for glaze. 1 1/2 all purpose flour, make sure you mix in baking powder and salt at beginning, so you don't have to stir it in later, that's a recipe writing mistake why not say add it to flour, Superb.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Excellent flavor and texture and quick to prepare. I used a smaller size bundt pan (approximately 48oz or 850ml) rather than a loaf pan, then after cooling dusted it with powdered sugar. It was a thing of beauty and a perfect base for fresh strawberries and whipped cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: For a while I've been really curious about the origins of this cake. I've found it in an Italian cookbook that said every Italian household cooks it. Certainly every household in Spain makes it, too (though we use a standard, individual yogurt container as the measuring reference for most other ingredients here), and here it's called French... Any insights?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
I just made the cake in a loaf pan and added 1 tsp. rose water to the flavourings suggested ( lemon zest and vanilla).
I simplified the method by adding the oil to the wet mixture and then the dry ingredients. Perfect results and a very tasty cake.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: it looks very delicious it is a good idea for mothers day like a good breakfast in bed when waking up because it is a great detail for your special day and accompanied with a good drink.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Has anyone tried making this recipe with less sugar?
Would 1/2 cup still work?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: try olive oil?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Used almond meal, 3/4 c. sugar, 1/2 tsp. orange oil, sour cream, Trader Joe's Seville marmelade (fine cut) unstrained. Baked 45-50 minutes. Haven't tried it yet, but t looks and smells great.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Nice and moist but I would increase the zest next time to 2 lemons.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Could you please clarify what you mean by "omit the almonds and use another 1/2 cup all-purpose flour?" Do you mean, either use 1/2 cup ground almonds, or use 1 whole cup of regular flour? And, is almond meal the same as ground almonds?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Commercial almond meal is exactly the same thing as almond flour.        Both are finely ground almonds. The recipe calls for EITHER 1 cup all purpose white flour, plus 1/2 almond meal/flour OR just use 1 & 1/2 cups all purpose white flour.  The almond meal/flour adds nice flavor and softer texture to the cake.  I have made it both ways and generally use the combined white flour/almond meal mix.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: French Yogurt Cake With Marmalade Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Easy to make and turned out great as specified! I will try with a less sugar (1/3 c?), more lemon zest. Maybe alter to olive oil for an olive oil cake.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent right from the oven--moist and delicious.  Used 1 1/3 cup corn, which was perfect.  Next morning they were good, but I think they'd be great if I had heated them up. Used canola oil, not corn oil, and it was fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Subbed the corn kernels for frozen blueberries and they were delicious! Next time I would probably sprinkle some sugar over the tops before baking and they'd be perfect!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My family loved these! I didn't have buttermilk so I used a cup of milk with a tablespoon of lemon juice in it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is the same as Doris Greenspan's recipe.  I have made them many times. I usually use the buttermilk powder, so easy to keep that on hand.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add jalapeños and drop the sugar! Fantastic

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Perfect! I added 1 large jalapeño to half the batter. These by far are best cornbread muffins ever!!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn't love these. Used Quaker Oats Corn Meal and wonder if that might have been an issue. They were a little bland and think more salt might have helped. I'd like to have tasted a stronger corn flavor.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: We did not enjoy these on their own.  They were bland - which i found shocking with so much whole kernels of corn.  But I made them to sop up the juice from the chicken with preserved lemons.  They were perfect for that, but not on thier own.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: 1/2 jar of pnut butter?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Right?! What size jar?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made a vegetarian version of this delicious dish by omitting the meat, doubling the veggies and using vegetable broth. I also added chopped collard greens, fresh ginger and garlic to add to the mix.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Can chicken stock be used if I omit the bullion? 
How many ounces of peanut butter?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Make sure it’s unsalted and unsweetened peanut butter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I would have taken a photo. But it was gone before I had a chance. I have an original recipe passed down. Very easy. Beat 1 cup of butter or margarine, 2 cups sugar,  blend well. Add 4 eggs. One at a time until mixed well. Add 2 tbsp. Vanilla and beat until fluffy.  Add 3 cups flour ( i used Gold Medal self raising) and a pinch of salt.Add a 1 scant c.milk. mix until smooth.sprinkle pecans broken up on top. Bake in a greased pan for 55 min. At 325  cool on a rack. Enjoy.  81/2 by 21/2

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Came out too sweet the first time. Second attempt, I used 1 cup sugar and 3.5 cups flour. Didn’t bother separating eggs. Came out perfectly. I suppose it isn’t 1-2-3-4, but it tastes better to me.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I believe ingredients should be listed in the way they are incorporated into the recipe. That way you can make most recipes, the second time, with only the ingredient list. For example:

Sugar
Butter
Vanilla
Egg yolks
Flour
Baking Powder
Milk
Egg Whites

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this using bob's red mill gluten free flour mix and it turned out great!!!  I do wish, though, that the step #6 was moved to the 1st or 2nd step! i missed it until the end and ended up with a lot more dishes.... served two single layer cakes with powdered sugar sprinkled on the top - had chopped up strawberries and whipped cream on the side upon serving. Will maekke this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This is a very old recipe.  It was published in THE AMERICAN FRUGAL HOUSEWIFE by Lydia Maria Child in 1833.  Here is the recipe:
"Cup cake is about as good as pound cake, and is cheaper.  One cup butter, two cups sugar, three cups of flour, and four eggs, well beat together, and baked in pans or cups.  Bake twenty minutes and no more.:
I used to bake this cake in the log cabin at Genesee Country Museum in Mumford NY

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this as a sheet cake and frosted it with a double batch of Alice Water's whipped cream, then decorated it with berries.  It was absolutely delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Pray tell me, what is “unsweetened” butter?? I am not concerned about showing my ignorance and realize this recipe was published several years ago, but after searching through the comments I found only one referenced question which was unanswered. Searched the internet which provided an unhelpful explanation: https://www.leaf.tv/articles/what-is-the-difference-between-butter-sweet-cream-butter/ So does the recipe mean cultured French style butter or unsalted butter???

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm sure it was a typo, and is supposed to be unsalted

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: If sweet butter is unsalted butter, is unsweetened butter salted?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious butter cake.  I cubed it and served it with the pudding from the Boston Creme Pie recipe drizzled with chocolate sauce. It was devoured.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this cake numerous time using buttermilk. It is a GREAT cake!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I found this cake to be a little dry and tough. Looking for a more tender cake

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was the first cake I made from scratch and on my own.  I was about 12 years old at the time.  As I remember, it had a very smooth, pound-cake-like texture.  And like the photo shows, we served it sans icing and with fresh blackberries we'd picked at my Aunt's farm.

Auld Lang Syne.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make this cake all the time. I don't usually bother separating the eggs which makes things easier especially when I'm in a hurry and it's still delicious. I also like to add a little extra vanilla. It's a nice basic cake that can be served in many different ways: in its plain state it goes great with a cup of coffee for breakfast, it's delicious when served with strawberries and whipped cream for dessert, and it can be dressed up for a birthday with icing and coconut flakes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: made 1/2 recipe and used 3/4 cup suger

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Would decrease baking powder, and leave out the corn starch. It's more like a pound cake

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Would decrease the baking powder and leave out the corn starch next time. Was more like pound cake. Very good with lemon curd and strawberries!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What on earth is “unsweetened” butter?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: 9” round pan or 9” square pan?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The photo is a round cake.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I should have read more carefully:) Step 6 doesn’t come at the end...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I use this yellow cake in a lot of cake recipes, BUT I bake it in three layers for 20 - 25 minutes at 350 degrees. Folding in the egg whites always insures a beautiful crumb!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: The first time I made this, it was terribly bland. The second time I added 4 teaspoons of vanilla (instead of 1) and it was delicious! And I didn’t bother separating the eggs.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Why the name?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: i am guessing its the quantities of ingredients (ie - 4 eggs, 3 cups flour, etc...)

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Check the ingredient list and you see 1,2,3,4.  Great old time cake which I have made many times and got the recipe from "The Joy of Cooking" more than 50 yrs ago.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: What is unsweetened butter?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be cooked in a bundt pan?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add 1 tbsp lemon zest and 2 tsp juice

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe says to make two 9" cake rounds for a two layer cake, but there are no directions for what to put between the layers.  It seems odd to have one cake on top of another without a binding frosting of some sort.  Can anyone clarify this for me before I bake these?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Instead of shrimp broth, 8 oz clam juice and a can of vegetable broth. Add shrimp at the very end.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I followed the recipe and doubled it. It was very well received. I wouldn't change a thing....well, maybe add a little Parmesan at the end? But it really doesn't need anything else.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excited to make this dish but will likely use an easier method for cooking risotto.  Rather than stirring endlessly on the stove, make the base on the stove with the olive oil, shallots, and garlic and then bring 3/4 of the stock plus one cup of water to a boil.  Then, add the rice and cook in a dutch oven at 375 for 45-50 minutes.  Meanwhile, cook the shrimp and tomatoes, and then add everything (including last cup of stock) to the dutch oven and stir vigorously for a couple of minutes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This recipe works well with other solid fish and scopes at the last minute.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Coated shrimp with a little olive oil, garlic, and red pepper and sauteed briefly.  Saved shrimp and juices to the side and then proceeded with step 2.  No basil oil but a handful of basil and a little park at the end did the trick.  Very nice little dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I make risotto often, this is one of the best recipes yet. I use leeks instead of shallots. I used to precook shrimp and add at end but much prefer this method, adds flavour.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made half, used chicken stock, and remembered that risotto isn't that time consuming if you mis en place like a mad woman.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I use Better Than Bouillon Lobster  for the stock.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used extra dry vermouth instead of white wine - worked well!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
"You want the liquid at a steady simmer, moving but not bubbling away like a lunatic."

Sublime writing. Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
used Imagine Seafood stock 1 box (4cups), added 1 tsp of shrimp paste. used a small onion instead of shallot. did not put shrimp. used dried basil at the end, no basil oil. put small pinch of saffron after 2 cups of broth (next time should put in with wine) Next time should use actual white wine (instead of salted cooking white wine) to decrease saltiness
Quite tasty. great with sautéed shrimp with saffron/cumin.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Hello! Is it possible to cook the shrimp aside and then add it to the risotto only to finish?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is the way I have made my shrimp and lobster risottos in the past. I think seasoning then pre-cooking seafood and tossing it in the hot rice to finish cooking prevents the shrimp/lobster from being overcooked.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Lacked flavor in my humble opinion.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Needs hot sauce

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Lots of adjusting! Added red pepper flakes with shallots and garlic, and a small can of chopped oysters with the tomatoes. I had frozen, pre-cooked shrimp, so I defrosted per package instructions and tossed in at the end to warm. No shrimp broth, so used mixture of fish stock and water. I like my risotto mushy so I cooked longer than instructed, and also added additional wine as I went, a dollop of butter, and a handful of grated parm. Finished w/ fresh basil and served w/ salad. Yum!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! Added about two teaspoons of paprika. Took longer than stated in the description but it was really worth it. Works fine with all sort of seafood and fish stock as well. Next time I'll might add some chilli or cayenne pepper. Thank you!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added 1/4 tsp of cayenne pepper instead of white pepper and 1/2 cup of Parmesan cheese with the shrimp and tomatoes.  Made the risotto creamier and a touch of heat added flavor to the dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. But, as many have noted, adding the shrimp with the last cup of liquid makes for overcooked shrimp. Instead, add the tomatoes as directed, but the shrimp just as the risotto is finishing. If you need to, just put a lid on it and let it sit off the stove for two minutes if the shrimp isn’t quite done. The only change I made was to add a little thyme.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Great results.  Don’t salt the rice when adding the wine.  The broth has enough salt to carry the dish. Did with sun dried tomatoes.  Added parsley for color prior to the basil oil.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
The flavor is great and altogether it was not hard. Only knock is that it takes a while, but it's not super involved once you start mixing the stock with the rice so it's good for watching a show or something. The shrimp and tomatoes give it a very nice flavor.

I used chicken stock instead of shrimp stock and it was just fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added smoked paprika, nice addition

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Really delicious risotto! I made a quick stock with shrimp shells and bottled lobster juice with some water. It worked perfectly. I also made 1/2 of the recipe for two. All good. Try Sancerre or Sauvignon blanc. The prefect interlude between holiday feasts.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Skipped the basil oil and hit it with a generous dollop of butter and some parsley at the end. It was AMAZING. Used stock made from a gallon freezer bag full of saved up shrimp shells - the delight of making something this delicious from what many might have tossed out cannot be overstated.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Too much liquid that takes too long to be absorbed, overcooking the shrimp and cherry tomatoes. Cut back the shrimp stock by 1/4 and don't add stock at the end.  Instead, when the shrimp and tomatoes are added, turn off the heat and let sit for 5'; serve.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: In response to those recommending the addition of Parmigiano: I realize this is not an American concern, but Italians never mix seafood and cheese. Logically or not, the combination is considered distasteful; it's a cultural thing. Instead, they would top this dish with a grating of bottarga, the dried fish roe from Sardinia which can be found in American specialty shops and possibly at Whole Foods.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Go light on the shrimp

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yummy! I left the tomatoes out because I didn't have any and added some saffron. It was awesome!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: As others, I made my own shrimp stock and dice the very last tomatoes from our garden. And, following the practice of my auntie from Parma, I violated what many have claimed as an inviolable law not to mix seafood and cheese, I added Pecorino Romano and Parmigiano Reggiano. Call the food police if you must. It was wonderful. I'd do it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added a little saffron and Parmesan at the end, but would have been excellent as is.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: add shrimp after all broth added then add the tomatoes when shrimp1/2 done

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used 41-50 wild Mexican shrimp which cooked perfectly with the last bit of stock, which I make whenever I cook shrimp and keep in the freezer. Instead of basil oil, I topped each serving with slivers of fresh basil. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was an excellent way to use left over cooked shrimp.  I made several changes to use up extra heirloom tomatoes.  I added two chopped tomatoes after cooking the rice in step 2.  Cooked them down until the liquid was absorbed, then added wine and cooked until absorbed.  At this point only about half a cup of broth(chicken) was needed.  Then added cooked shrimp, frozen peas, and some Parmesan.  Will repeat.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Well, this is probably the first recipe I have tried that did not wow me.  For all the effort of constantly stirring the risotto, it was just OK.  I did even take the shells off the shrimp and cover them with a bit of water and simmered 15 minutes to get a rich shrimp flavor...but in the end I and husband decided if we want risotto, we will go to a restaurant.  I do think the basic recipe was good, but it just lacked that wow factor I expect from risotto.  Sorry.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn’t have the basil oil, but a splash of white truffle-infused olive oil was delicious and allowed us to skip adding the Parmesan that’s not called for in the recipe ( it which I otherwise would have added). I really enjoyed the instructions on this recipe and thought that they’ll help me with risotto in general.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cut tomatoes into smaller than quarters

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This it the first time I've tried to tackle risotto and thanks to this recipe, it came out perfectly. I'll admit, I was a little skeptical at first based on how vague the instructions were, but man did it taste good. Kudos to the author.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Kids loved it!  Will add to rotation.  Used clam juice and added water and boiled the shrimp shells.  Threw in some peas for color and had a amazing Wednesday night dinner.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I only had chicken broth.  This recipe didn't work for me.  I got a really strong tomato taste that overwhelmed everything else.  I don't think my tomatoes were completely ripe.  I think if I just had shrimp, it would have been fine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cooked the risotto in the oven with 4 c chicken broth at 350.  Took it out after 40 minutes.  Stored in 1/2 c white wine, Parmesan, and another c of broth.  Sautéd the shrimp & tomatoes in olive oil.  Added to Dutch oven when done. Springier herbs before serving.  Family LOVED it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Yummy and good instructions.  More like two servings for us as a main course.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn't have shrimp broth, so I used vegetable, but I started by sautéing the shrimp w olive oil salt and pepper prior to step 2.  I added shrimp, tomatoes and roasted asparagus (5 min for 2 in pieces at 400 degrees) at the end.  We used basil oil as well as a little parmesan and it was delicious.  This was also great reheated w a little bit of broth.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: the cherry tomatoes make all the difference! balance with acidic and tart taste. delicious

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is killer! I had 3 cups of homemade shrimp stock, added another cup of Better Than Bouillon lobster flavor. The basil oil is important. I had a blender failure and had to mash the basil into the oil with a spoon. Added a sprinkle of Parmesan at the table. Will make this again and again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Oh MY - sublime!  Except for using half chicken stock, half shrimp broth (didn't have enough of the latter), I followed the recipe exactly. Past risottos have leaned toward under/overcooked; this admittedly time-consuming recipe is worth every minute adding broth as it renders the perfect risotto. Used fresh grape tomatoes, thyme, and basil from my garden; those elements only added to the glorious combo of flavors, Creamy, succulent, rich, and so delicious. Dreamy comfort food in hectic times.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is my go-to risotto recipe! I added 1/4 cup of chopped sun dried tomato strips, a dozen raw shrimp into bite sized pieces and a few shakes of red pepper flakes to the risotto about 3 minutes before coming off the stove. My hubby regrets he can only give two thumbs up!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Add shrimp when it is almost done, to make sure shrimp doesn’t overcook.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent recipe.  The dish really pops with a hit of acid at the end.  A squeeze of lemon does the trick.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this with a quick shrimp stock made with shells and clam juice. I’m thinking the author assumed the stock was highly seasoned- mine was not as I wanted to avoid over salting. I quickly sautéed the shrimp before adding to risotto, and deglazed the pan with my stock.
I found this rather bland in flavor. Salt , a bit of pesto and Parmesan helped but maybe a really good stock is important here as there aren’t many ingredients.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So good, and so glad I didn’t wimp out and use boxed veg broth.   I don’t get calling anything not cooked via gradual additions of broth and stirring “risotto” — the creaminess of risottos is only gained via gradual cooking and stirring.  Otherwise it’s pilaf or something.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I’ve never made risotto before and this turned out amazing! Did it on a whim and had to substitute a lot of ingredients since I was missing a bunch of them, though otherwise followed the steps. Used 1/3 c mezcal   2/3 c water instead of the wine, 1/2 lb shrimp   1/2 lb cod, 1/2 onion instead of shallot, chicken broth instead of stock (recommend otherwise adding little of no salt) and added paella spices my friend brought me from Spain. Absolutely delicious

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used Better Than Bullion “LobsterBase” to make the stock.  Delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Was fabulous 
I did slightly tweak….added fresh corn off the cob, was really delicious 
Directions were perfect.
Made shrimp stock with shrimp shells, wine, chicken broth, water and parsley stems
Yum
4 good servings

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was really amazing.  I didn’t have any shrimp shells so I just  used chicken stock and some clam juice.  Didn’t see what to do with basil oil or how to make it so just stirred in chopped fresh basil at the end.  Definitely will make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made with lobster mushrooms for me.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I also rescued the shells and made fresh shrimp broth. I didn’t have basil oil so subbed in store bought pesto sauce to add some color and pizzazz. A delicious midweek meal served with a green salad.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Excellent recipe. I boiled the shrimp shells in the vegetable broth and used more garlic. Touch of cheese was good addition. Used carnaroli rice.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Maybe it’s been noted before, but make your own shrimp stock with the shells. Very simple, just agua and salt. No need to buy pricey shrimp or seafood stock. Made this recipe numerous times, keeps getting better, especially with some reggiano!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: tasty!  we both liked it

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this and followed the instructions. At the end, before serving, I made some adjustments. From the garden I took a good handful of fresh basil, rosemary, and oregano. Cut the herbs into smaller pieces and added them to the stock as it was added to the arborio. The tomatoes added great flavor to the dish, but I didn't quarter them. I just cut them in half and that worked. Another comment mentioned adding some olive oil and red pepper flakes to the shrimp which I did, a good addition.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
maybe it's just me, being a visual person- but i really need images with recipes to inspire me...
thank god there was one in the original article ;)
especially soups can be so different from each other. so this is mor of a clear , brothy soup. i like that !

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Having a photograph makes a world of difference to me also.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Me too

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cooking for a vegetarian guest, I skipped the prosciutto. Subbed cooked chickpeas for fava; 3c mixed greens for spinach, chard & asparagus; aquafaba instead of chicken stock. Used a package of frozen artichoke hearts cooked according to microwave instructions. Added 2 diced red potatoes. So tasty.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did a a kinda sorta version of this.  2 big leeks, 1 bunch asparagus, 1 small bunch spinach, about 2 cups total of shelled raw fava beans.  Also no prosciutto; they had good smoked chunk bacon at the store so I used that instead.  Came out great!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is great.  If your market doesn't have fava beans, lima beans will work perfectly.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious. Per the comments of others, I cut the butter down to 3T. Instead of 1.5 C of whipping cream, I used a combination of cream, half and half, and skim milk. I chopped up an onion and sauteed that in the butter before adding the garlic. I substituted chopped up kale for the prosciuto. I used gruyere instead of swiss cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Double the cauliflower and you have perfection.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How can this recipe be labeled "vegetarian" when it contains prosciutto?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I see no vegetarian label.  In fact it says this is a good side for a pork loin.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: prosciutto is candy

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: As Christine suggested, I doubled the amount of cauliflower. Roasted half while following the recipe with the rest, then combined all and mixed well before putting everything into the dish and topping with Gruyere and parsley. Also used two extra cloves of garlic, but kept the amounts of everything else (except the cauliflower) the same. Wonderful!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This, though decadent, is the BEST cauliflower dish I have ever had. The recipe realistically makes 6 servings, about 500 cal per serving.
Altered recipe using 3 oz prosciutto, 1 seeded Anaheim pepper, and parmesan-reggiano grated cheese. No salt was needed with prosciutto. I prepped through the boiling, let it sit covered on stove for an hour, and put in oven before dinner. Next time consider only 2 oz prosciutto, partial/full substitution half and half for heavy cream, and hotter pepper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used bacon (didn't have prosciutto) and cut the fat from butter as I did with the milk and used gruyere.  Very nice.  Very rich.  Doesn't need the prosciutto or bacon.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be made ahead and refrigerated a day or two or frozen without the topping? Bake when ready to serve?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: did you ever receive a response to whether this dish can be made a day in advance? Gratinee of Cauliflower

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: @Vivian I’d like to know also!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did this twice and the second time changed the whipping cream and swiss cheese to 3/4 cup. The original was way too rich for me!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious but rich and a little oily.  I used light cream; next time I'll try half and half, and use only 4-5 T butter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this again, for Thanksgiving, using half-and-half and cutting butter back to 5 T.  Perfect!  This will be a (luxurious) staple at our house.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Outstanding. I did not use any butter. Just enough olive oil to keep garlic and cauliflower from burning. I had left over whole milk on hand so I used that - full amount recipe called for. Perfectly creamy enough without using cream. Did not use prosciutto, as we had vegetarians around. As someone said above, kale would have subbed perfectly. Also as someone else said only needed one cup of cheese, and if I made it again I’d use a little less, still. Used Jarlsberg cheese and it was a great fit.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tried this tonight.  Too much milk/cream, and the bake time was way too long.  We like our cauliflower a little crunchy, and this made it overdone.  I may try this again by making a cheese sauce and pouring that over the slightly cooked cauliflower, then just browning the cheese topping under the broiler.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I am a vegetarian so I did this without the prosciutto and it was amazing. There is just two of us and we ate the whole thing.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: add different cheeses.  we did Jarlsberg (for the swiss) and then added mozzarella as well. verrrrry yummy

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: To keep the garlic from over browning I reverse the sauté order of the prosciutto and garlic and it needs 1/3 cup of shallots.   I also add 2 cups of mushrooms to cauliflower sauté.  And I prefer Alpenzeller as the choice for an alpine cheese.   When served with a salad this makes an excellent entree.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: My cauliflower was past prime, my prosciutto had been in the freezer for two years, and I only had 2% milk. Turned out to be a pretty good dish. I want to try it again with good ingredients. I bet it's off the charts.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Thank you. Love this! For a good vegetarian version, substitute 100 g (3.5 oz) fresh shiitake mushrooms for the prosciutto. Sauté the shiitake first. Then add the garlic for only a minute before adding the cauliflower. To keep the natural architecture of the cauliflower, rather than slicing, I just separate the florets into small ones including their attachment to the stem. After sautéing the cauliflower, I add 100 ml (½ cup) dry white wine to the hot skillet and stir until evaporated.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this recipe for years (pretty much exactly as written) starting with when it was published in "The Silver Palate Good Times Cookbook." While this may not be the healthiest cauliflower dish around, it's certainly one of the most delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cut the butter in half an just used 1 cup of cream. Also omitted the prosciutto.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wait, are you suppose to cook the cauliflower florets in the skillet?  A head that's large, and likely 3 pounds?  I'd go with baking the cauliflower first for 15-20 minutes before I'd try and fit it in a skillet or even worse boil it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made with turkey bacon (that's what we had) and an aged white cheddar (no swiss), and it was amazing.

It would be easy to wilt some spinach in there for extra vegetables.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So, so delicious, planning to serve this at my next dinner party! My variations: 1/2 cup of cream and 1 cup skim milk, I added thyme and chilli flakes, no prosciutto (a few shallots instead), and topped with Gruyère and panko instead of Swiss!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added extra prosciutto to make it a main dish and used half and half instead of the cream.  It was delicious but very rich.  I will make it again  with less prosciutto or even none.  I like my cauliflower a little crisper so I would cut down on the oven time about 10 minutes.  Good for a main dish but without the prosciutto it would be a wonderful side as well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this when i was in Italy and didn't use prosciutto or salt. I used 2 small anchovies and added them when you would add the prosciutto. I also used oat milk in place of cream. Delicious especially when I used a stronger more flavorful cheese. My Italian friends loved it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Also used olive oil no butter needed!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious flavor but way too rich for my taste.  I will try again and cut down on the fat content.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I, too, found this recipe way too rich.  I assume it can be "tamed" back by adding a variety of low fat cheeses...though to me that defeats the purpose of a dish like this.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can this be made ahead and refrigerated?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Prosciutto made it too salty for my taste and amount of butter was excessive; I shall cut down on both if I make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious but rich and a little oily. I used light cream; next time I'll try half and half, and use only 4-5 T butter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I find it easier to steam or microwave the cauliflower and then pour the sauce over it before baking it as per recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: very good served with a salad. Great light dinner option.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Really good. I used half the cream as I only had half. It it worked well. I find it much better than making a white sauce with milk

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used Emmental cheese and fat-free half & half. But I did use the full 6 Tbs of butter. I had only 3 oz of prosciutto. I added some panko on top, before adding the cheese, helped absorbed some of the half & half and added some body to the dish. Delish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this vegan by subbing vegetarian salami for the prosciutto, soy milk for the cream and almond pepperjack cheese for the Swiss cheese.  Eliminated the pinch of cayenne as the almond cheese was pepperjack flavor.  Delicious.  Went back for seconds!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Added about 2 teaspoons of Dijon mustard to mix just before baking. Worked well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was superb. Sauteed the cauliflower in olive oil instead of butter, used mozzarella instead of Swiss cheese, and used half cream half milk for the sauce, but otherwise followed the recipe. Will definitely make this again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I also used just 4 T butter and substituted half and half for heavy cream.  It was divine.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is fantastic. Cut the butter to 4 tbsp, and used 1/2 cup of heavy cream with 1 cup whole milk. Turned out beautifully. I've made it twice, once assembled to the point of baking and refrigerated for a day and it turned out just the same.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Tasty, usually boring cauliflower remained a bit crunchy. No cayenne, used sriracha sauce, left a nice tingle on the tongue.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was great! Had to make it without the prosciutto for a veggie-friendly potluck and it was still delicious -- followed the recipe almost exactly, but broke up the cauliflower into slightly larger chunks and used a mix of Emmentel and Jarlsberg. I look forward to trying the meat version.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Use milk instead so cream or a combination of skim and cream.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did this tonight....I very briefly steamed the cauliflower before adding it to the prosciutto garlic mixture. Then I followed the recipe exactly. It was delicious, but have to say that I prefer my prosciutto to be crispy, so next time I will fry the prosciutto separately and crumble it on the top after taking the dish out of the oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I did a veggie version by replacing sun dried tomatoes for the prosciutto. Amazinggggg...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Agree with other reviewer that you must double the cauliflower or this doesn't work.  I doubled it, then added just two tablespoons more of butter & kept all other ingredient amounts as written.  Used a 9"x9" square casserole and just a sprinkling of parsley on top with about 1 cup Swiss cheese, also buttered the casserole pan.  Delicious and rich!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I used bacon instead of prosciutto, sauteed the bacon before the garlic till almost done, then added half the amount of butter, then the garlic. Also subbed mix of pecorino and cheddar for Swiss. Rave reviews!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious. Needs some "Pick me up" as noted by other bloggers. I have added a small onion and some jalapeno (1/2 of it).  A strong tasting cheese like Gruyere will be nice.  Some Parmesan on to is a  must.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow, rich and creamy.  Great taste. It was great with BBQ pork shoulder as a lower carb side for macaroni and cheese. Next time I’ll par-boil the cauliflower to cut down on the need for a lot of butter to sauté until soft, use an ultra-pasteurized low fat milk and low fat cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Left out the flour. Added a handful of Parmesan and handful of pepper Jack cheese to heavy cream before pouring over the cauliflower. After 30 mins in oven, switched to the broiler for 4 mins.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: A classic gratin, very good.  The first "cauliflower cheese" I've ever made that wasn't watery. I used olive oil instead of butter and half & half instead of cream to keep the doctor happy ;-) Still delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cut out the proscuitto. As a vegetarian gratin, I found Cauliflower Gratin with Leeks and White Cheddar a million times better!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I didn’t have any cream so I used whole milk. It was so delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Way too rich. Should have followed others advice : halve  butter, ,cream and cheese. Was a miasma of goo as written

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I'm celiac so I used GF flour as a thickener. I also topped with GF panko and cheese. I didn't add meat and used whole milk.

Delicious! Very rich !! (Even without heavy cream). A decedent side but easy to make. And quick!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
This was absolutely fantastic! I made a few changes.. I used what I had..
About a cup of heavy cream mixed with a little 2% milk, s+p, about a half cup of chopped shallots, about 1 1/2c grated Parmesan and Gruyère, topped with panko mixed with garlic, butter and chopped parsley — thx to leftover ingredients for Oysters Rockefeller!
Sensational!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Just made this last night and guests were bowled over by the decadent creaminess. A good recipe but be ready to eat.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I ended up cutting the butter down to about four tablespoons. I added half an onion with the garlic. I also used about fifty percent whipping cream and the other fifty percent half and half. This was really good. The prosciutto really added a nice saltiness to the dish. Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cut out the proscuitto and used Alpro 5% cooking cream, and cooked the garlic and cauliflower in a little bit of olive oil. Otherwise followed as written. A very successful recipe, everyone loved it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm wondering if you could substitute two tablespoons of rice flour for regular flour. I'm always on the lookout for good recipes for my gluten-free friends, who are multiplying and keeping me on my toes cooking for them.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delish!  Two (major) changes:  half & half for the heavy cream, and Trader Joe's Quattro Formaggio for the Swiss.  I ate every bite of my share before I touched the flank steak and rosemary mushrooms on my plate.  Added a scattering of fresh bread crumbs on the top, which gave added crunch, and used a fairly heavy hand when sprinkling the cayenne.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It is in the oven now and looks wonderful. Changed out the prosciutto for bok choy which I had in the garden. I am a vegetarian so that is good. To the butter and garlic I added some garden shallots. This reminded me of Thanksgiving, so I added fresh sage from the garden. I have the most beautiful head of cheddar cauliflower that I bought from a local orchard/farm.  Kept all the butter and fulll fat cream. Can't wait to eat this.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How did the bok choy work?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this for New Year's Day dinner.  Used a big beautiful head of organic cauliflower.  I prepared precisely pursuant to NYT recipe.  I did not care for the aroma that was more cabbage than cauliflower, but the family didn't complain.  Decided I prefer the flavor, texture and ease of whipped cauliflower with plenty of butter, sour cream and salt and pepper.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is superb!  I didn't cut down on anything.  Served it w/tomahawk chop and a salad.  My husband and brother-in-law went back for thirds.  It's a keeper.  BTW the golden cauliflower and parsley were my own.  Going to try it w/the purple cauliflower next week.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Cut back on butter, added sautéed onion, and thinned the cream as others suggested. This is now a basic go-to for family meals as a side or main.

Have made this multiple times using what I had on hand (i.e. bacon, ham, anchovy). Added a shredded carrot for color and sweetness and used a mix of on-hand cheese (swiss, gruyere and mystery). Son suggested that Monterey Jack would be a easy switch up, which I will try next time.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I'm vegetarian, so no prosciutto. Substituted some kale from my  container garden and threw in a couple of small chili peppers from the same in lieu of cayenne pepper. Believe in using whatever is in my fridge/larder and only had a small head of cauliflower, so I substituted some broccoli for the additional cauliflower. Used half butter and half olive oil. Delicious!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very nice recipe! However, wrong step here....instead of adding the flour directly to the pan mixture, it's better to mix the flour thoroughly into the cream before adding it, making it a smooth mixture without lumps. A small whisk or gravy shaker will work well for this. Also, a little onion powder added to the cream mixture too, yum yum!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I cut a rather large head as thinly as possible. After adding cauliflower and sautéing for a minute or two, I covered the pan for a minute or two....used bacon I had cooked earlier (didn't have prosciutto) and used some cheddar and a few dots of goat cheese....good.  Probably will reduce butter to 4 T next time and add a little parm and nutmeg and bake for 15 minutes next time so the cauliflower shines through.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made this with, dare I write it, soy creamer instead of cream, but it was still delicious, though thinner.  Good alternative to my (French) mom's traditional cauliflower gratin, which is steam or boil florets and stem pieces, make bechamel separately with salted butter/whole milk steeped with bay leaf/fresh nutmeg, put cauliflower pieces in dish, cover with bechamel sauce, in the oven, no cheese needed - delicieux! Proscuitto a great addition, served with pork loin - everybody loved it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! Had a 3 lb cauliflower, could have been larger for the amount of sauce - sprinkled Parmesan over Swiss which browned up nicely under broiler - stands up well with roast beef - definitely make again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Add in sauteed onion/shallots...
decrease whipped cream by 1/2 (maybe some stock?)
decrease cheese to 1 cup

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Would this freeze well?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
OMG...if you even think you might like the sound of this recipe, you should make it immediately!  So good just as the recipe is written, I used half and half (had it on hand) instead of cream, but the rest was per recipe (might have had a heavier hand with the cayenne, but that is how we roll around here).

Simply put, you will have a new "A" list dish as soon as you make it.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Cooked with a pork loin that only needed 25 minutes at 425. Riced and microwaved for 5 min. 3/4 c cream substituted garlic powder and gruyere. Delish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very tasty, but much too oily/fatty as written. Will be cutting cream, butter and cheese in future iterations. It seems this is more an excuse to eat cheese and cream than it is to enjoy a vegetable in a different way. Keep this in mind when considering this dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used half of the cream and flour due to having only one small head of cauliflower on hand.  Didn't have any prosciutto around so made it without, and it was still wonderfully creamy, generous, and satisfying. I think having it with something pork-y with a crunchy skin and pairing it with pinot gris from either Alsace or New Zealand would be delicious.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Made exactly as the recipe.  I love cream, and I love prosciutto.  But this is too rich!  Use half and half, and crispy bacon, and maybe mix the cheese in before baking.  I might also use Gruyere instead of Swiss.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I served this as a side for a holiday supper. Though delicious, it was much too rich to eat more than a forkful! Leftovers were tasty when mixed/diluted with  a fair amount of pasta.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Use part cream and milk.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So delicious! I'm eating leftovers for lunch right now. I, like others cut the butter in half. I used a blend of Swiss and Gruyere. Yum...

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I made this last night to go with pork chops.

We had no whipping cream (it had gone off) so used light cream (10%) instead, and only 3/4 of a cup - that seemed more than enough. I did a bit of extra Gruyere and stirred that in to the cauliflower to make up for some of the missing cream.

Was delish. The dish I baked it in had high sides, and that seemed to impede the browning of the cheese and cauliflower, but the taste was still amazing. Very simple and quick as well.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very good! I added some red cabbage and used onion because I was out of garlic.  I think this is a good and flexible recipe, so I'll be making it again!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Used Italian sausage instead of prosciutto. Delicious

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Pretty good. Lessened the sauce but kept the cauliflower amount the same, per comments. Even with cayenne, s&p and a little nutmeg, I felt it could've used more oomph. Not toooo creamy but I shredded fresh garlic jack cheese on top and put buttered panko on top of that. Not bad. Not a home run either but I'd make it again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I added 1/2 an onion I had on hand and a bit of Swiss chard as well.
I sautéed the onion with the cauliflower. I'm a vegetarian so no meat.  Added the garlic when the cauliflower was almost done.
Used 1cup of cream, 3tbs. Of butter, and 1 1/2 cups of Swiss cheese, ~ 8 oz. was cautious with the salt, as wasn't sure how salty the cheese would be.  Add more salt next time and maybe use a different cheese as flavor of dish without meet is rather bland.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was a fairly easy side that did, in fact, go well with a pork loin, just as the description suggests. I added some kale for extra veg, and used whole milk and random bits of cheese because that’s what our quarantine pantry had. Also, bread crumbs on top, because why not? If I made again, I would add some mustard powder or horseradish to cut through the richness.

author: Emily Weinstein
