commentBody: Used bacon (no prosciutto on hand).  Should have drained the grease, but very tasty.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is pretty incredible.  I increased the cauliflower by about a half and baked in a 9 x 13 to feed more people and it’s very forgiving and turned out wonderful.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I added diced ham and frozen, chopped artichokes and peas. It was great and, with crusty bread, a whole meal.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Hot pepper - of any nature - is a great addition to this almost chowder of a casserole. Try jalapeño/fresh spicy pepper sautéed with the garlic; canned adobo or its sauce; hot pepper flakes; or just lots of cayenne. 
Also, it’s easy to substitute almost any milky liquid for the heavy cream but add gradually after the flour has been mixed in or you lose the creamy goodness that makes this recipe delicious. 
I doubled the prosciutto - purchased as a slab and diced. 2 cups gruyere. Swiss is meh.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Feta cheese worked well as a substitute for goat cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wow!  Perfection!  I followed the suggestion of cutting the butter in half as well as 1/2 whole milk, 1/2 heavy cream.  Next time I'll cut down on the salt as the prosciutto already adds plenty of salt to the dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Per suggestions,  reduced the butter by half. Eliminated the Swiss cheese, as the first time I made this dish the cheese was overwhelming (I think the cauliflower should be the star here). Instead I topped it with a cup of parmesan. I like my cauliflower crunchy, so I sauteed for two minutes, baked for 10 minutes, and broiled until browned.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I steamed the cauliflower first.  Reduced the butter to just a spoonful then gently sautéed some over ripe cherry tomatoes I had in the fridge.  Only then did I add the garlic.  Then quickly added some leftover pork loin, reduced the heavy cream by half and added a small amount of leftover chicken stock.  Then into the oven with some Parmesan and Emmentaler grated cheese - delicious and not so heavy.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: what size cooking vessel are people typically using here? the shallow au gratin dish referenced doesn't account for volume

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  I halved the recipe and still added a good pinch of cayenne.  Perfect accomplimment to simple chicken or pork dish.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I have made this for years for Thanksgiving.  Always a favorite.  I like all the suggestions on cutting back on the cream, different cheeses, etc I agree that it can be a little oily so this may be the year to perfect the dish!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Good. Little watery. Add a little more flour.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Delicious! Used 1 Corning ware pan for stovetop and oven. Used 2 T. Butter, reconstituted dry milk in lieu of cream; beef salami and turkey mortadella for meat.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Whole milk is fine; with one small cauliflower, I used one cup milk, 3 Tbsp butter, 2 Tbsp flour, and about a cup of cheese, divided (3/4 mixed in, 1/4 sprinkled on top). Subbed garlic powder for the garlic--it blends better--and added about a Tbsp of dijon mustard. Cauliflower taste still came through.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Too soupy and I don’t know why. Used a gigantic head of cauliflower because that’s what came in the farm box this week. Cauliflower and cheese should have been measured in ounces, not “big”.  I didn’t have heavy cream so I used half and half. Sauce seemed thin so I used 4 Tbsp flour. Used 8 ounces of cheddar mixed in with cauliflower, added deli Swiss on top. Don’t like prosciutto so I left it out. Didn’t have parsley.  Very watery.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this again…just a couple of weeks after I made it the first time.  It is still delicious.  This time I had some heavy cream but I only used 1/2 c. Cream and 1 cup whole milk. Too greasy. I will make it again with milk no cream. I have been adding toasted sourdough breadcrumbs on top of the cheese.  It adds an extra dimension and texture.  I highly recommend the breadcrumbs.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was tasty, but my overarching question is what does “large” mean when it comes to cauliflower? I reduced the butter to 4T and used olive oil instead. Used 1/2c of soy heavy cream — crazy thick! — mixed with 1c of soy milk. Chose Jarlsberg for the cheese. And, for the cauliflower, used 1/2 purple and 1/2 white. Following others suggestions, added several handfuls of a kale/chard mix. Cooking instructions worked, but we ended up with 3 reasonably-sized servings. Hence, the question about a measurable quantity of cauliflower.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this and found it extremely rich even though I substituted chicken broth for some of the cream. Still, it was tasty, so I'll try again with other modifications. Doubling the caulifower as another writer said may be the answer.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Pretty bland and overly rich as written, but I will try something similar in the future. I made it pretty much as written but added kale and cut down on the cream and butter, Also added some panko to the top.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This was delicious! Added some dijon mustard to the heavy cream mix for a bit of flavor. Topped with panko crumbs in the final baking stage.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Did the lower fat substitutions others suggested. Used a truffled cheese with parm and mozz since that’s what i had. Added some mushrooms. This is a great flavor and easy prep. Would also be good baked under a layer of toasted buttered croutons, or with a few ounces of pasta thrown in. Will definitely make again.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made this last night when the chill finally hit NYC and it felt like winter had arrived. I followed the recipe and it was delicious. I'm not sure I would change much. It would pair well with Pork Tenderloin or Country Ham.  The sauce from the dish was so lovely. It would have gone great with the meats!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I altered the method, but not the basic ingredients, and it worked out beautifully for our NY’s Eve dinner. Instead of adding flour then cream then topping with cheese, I melted some of the butter in a small, separate pot from the cauliflower, whisked in the flour, then cream, then added the cheese until melted and thickened. (seasoned with s and p, Dijon mustard and red pepper flakes). Poured the cheese mixture over the pre cooked cauliflower, then topped with Panko, Parmesan and olive oil mixture. Cooked as directed, but did a quick broil on low at the end to crisp up the topping. Really good!!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Easy and comforting on a cold night. Cut recipe in half, used milk instead of cream then folded in partially steamed frozen cauliflower, no prosciutto, added sprinkling of dried bread crumbs for crunch. Nice reminded of Silver Palate recipes.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is also from the infamous Silver Palate cookbook #1, it's an all time favorite - great for a big crowd.  I cooked a five pounds pork loin using this recipe for 12 on Christmas.  Watch the madeira sauce closely in the roasting pan and replenish it to keep from burning.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: So delicious... used port because I had no Madeira...excellent substitution... made the full complement of sauce although I had a much smaller pork loin... just so good!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Or, as alternative to the cookbook instruction, see this from article that linked w/the NYT Cooking recipe.  Between step 1 and 2, per the article, see this:  "...stuffing the pork loin with apricots and prunes by cutting a long slit in the meat lengthwise, nestling the dried fruit in between the two halves of the loin, and binding the whole thing up with cooking twine."  You have to create a pocket with knife (or original recipe) or cut a slit, to get the fruit in per step 2.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: This is one of the most delicious recipes of all time. I have cooked it many times and it never fails to delight my guests. Super easy, it is a bonus for any cook, from novice to expert. Also delicious the next day, but don't count on any leftovers.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: ...And this is one reason I rarely cook pork. Dry, tough and ok flavor but not great. Meh

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I love to cook with pork and it is always a favorite with guests. This one was no exception! Used a chunk of pork entrecote. Delicious and incredibly moist. Served aside of brussel sprouts with bacon and maple syrup and foil-baked potatoes with ranch dressing spices, bacon and cheddar. Yummmyy! 
How could anyone get this recipe to come out dry??? Will certainly add this to my many favorite pork roast/tenderloin recipes!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Fabulous!!! I've used for a brunch buffet and dinners. Great served cold

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I made some arbitrary cooking method changes along the way: I Sous vide the pork with the full recipe for 2.5 hours at 137 degrees.  Deliciously moist and tender.  I used the Madeira/molasses sauce in the sous vide bag and then poured it over the pork when served.  Next time, I’ll reduce the sauce to thicken it to accentuate the flavors.  Highly recommend.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Stuff w/ prune apricot minced onion mayo bread crumbs in roulade: ~ 1 C.
. Marsala honey clementine juice.<2lbs, bake at 425 ~40 minutes on rack. Sweetness not overpowering, super moist. And timing good for roastie Yukons. With big wedges Honey Crisp and carrots 🥕 on the sheet pan. Success for this solo cook is delicious dinner and only meat leftover...reheat on scalloped potatoes or in stuffing?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I've misplaced my Silver Palate (gifted to us in 1984) and I wonder--No salt??

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: How long is the chicken in the oven? Or is that just to keep it warm while you reduce the soup?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: And try with matzoh balls.  No reason not to use the recipe on the Streits or other brand box.  A small amount of salt, very finely ground pepper, and our favorite: freshly grated nutmeg.  When adding liquid to the mixture, use selzer instead of soup or water.  They can rest in the boiling water, turned off until the chicken is done.  Surprise yourself!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Isn’t this exactly Mark Bittman’s recipe from his book How to Cook Everything?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: I used 1/2 butter and 1/2 olive oil  Also felt recipe needed more liquid for my clams so used a full cup of wine.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: Yes, and some garlic!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: You should check to make sure clams/mussels are alive. Discard open shells. Also clean well and purge the sand by soaking in cold water with cornmeal. As the bivalve siphons water through itself it will spit out the sand and the cornmeal will replace the sand so the lucious meat is not gritty.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody:
Solid basic recipe.  Couple of notes:
-Most modern market mussels are rope-grown and already purged; you don't need the steps Lynn suggests in notes. Just wash in a couple of bowls of cold water.
- Like Gayle I would add chopped garlic with the onion.
-Lemon wedges are unnecessary, but fresh basil at end is nice.
-For company or decadence, use the white wine (or vermouth) and swirl in a couple of teaspoons of butter or cream before pouring over mussels.
-Serve 1 lb/person.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: Maybe my definition of 'hot' is different from Emily's, but it shouldn't take anywhere near 10 minutes for the mussels to open.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: Clams take a few minutes longer to cook than mussels.  Make sure the clams and mussels are cleaned. Put clams in with liquid; cover; cook 2-3 minutes. THEN add the mussels. Cook about another 3-4 minutes, stirring often to get liquid into all the mollusks. Otherwise, mussels are overcooked by the time clams open. Garnish with lemon wedges, chopped parsley, and fresh fennel fronds.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: I made my own version, but it came out great. First, I chopped 3 pieces of bacon and cooked them in the pot. Once they cooked, I added shallots and a healthy does of minced garlic. After a few minutes, I added half a cup of Modelo Negra, the clams, and some fresh lemon juice. Cooked for 10 minutes and served. Perfect. My Maine-born dad wouldn't have approved of the ingredients, but the great thing about this recipe is that it is what you make it. So, choose what works for you.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: Simple and good.  With only 1/2 cup of wine my clams produced more broth than we could use. Great with crusty bread.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: I always put the clams in salted water with cornmeal to clean them.  I used butter instead of oil and garlic as well as the shallots. 2 cups of Chardonnay plus a couple of sprigs of parsley in the broth.  Delicious!! I freeze any leftover broth to use when I’m making Gumbo.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: Very good and easy recipe. I opted to use my favorite ale, and it was very tasty. Like some others I also put a little bit of butter, and some lemon juice and a dip but then ended up putting it in the sauce with a little bit of salt. I had no parsley so I actually use basil which was very nice.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: I sautéed garlic, then added a can (15oz) of hard cider and cooked them in that, then added cilantro.  Delicious -- especially with yeasted herbed dinner rolls made partially with coarse corn meal for a crunchy texture contrast to the clams.  Fabulous. The leftover broth and clams will go into a chowder.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Simple Steamed Clams or Mussels Recipe
commentBody: I use white wine, about 3/4 cup for 2 lb of mussels, and I sweat a mirepoix, (chopped carrot, celery, onion), rather than just onion, then garlic for a moment, in the pot before I add the wine and mussels.  Makes the broth more flavorful. I often add oregano and pepper flakes as well as parsley. I serve with good crusty rolls-- garlic or rosemary flavored, or just plain ciabatta rolls.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Weirdly enough, all that rotation is unnecessary. Turn the heat up a it and just roast it (after you've really schmeared it with plenty of butter.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I like to use USC's Michael Romano's prep: salt the chicken all over and leave it the fridge overnight UNCOVERED so the skin gets dry and therefore crispy in the oven.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
I've used this Cook's Illustrated method of cooking a bird many times. The chicken does come out nicely cooked but the marks on the sides are not very attractive. I've gone back to roasting in a rack and leave the thing alone until a thermometer registers the proper temperature.

Of course, brine the chicken for at least an hour:
2 quarts water
½ cup table salt or 1 cup Diamond Kosher salt.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Makes the oven WAAAAAAY too messy, but delivery croustade.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Wouldn’t using an oven on CONVECTION ROAST obviate the turning if the chicken is on a rack?

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I use a beer can rack without the beer can: no rotating necessary.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Start off for 10mins breast up, then turn upside down to allow the birds fat to drain through the breast keeping it moist!
Or use a large Dutch oven or better still - terra-cotta brick.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: Since I used this crust with an apple pie I can't comment on the filling here, but I will say that the vodka crust is now my go-to crust for all baked pies; the vodka evaporates more quickly than the water leaving a flakier crust behind. My other advice would be to use good quality European butter (which has a higher fat content), meaning there's less water in the butter which, like the vodka, keeps the crust incredibly flakey.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: The folks at Cook's Illustrated say that the vodka pie crust recipe is better for pies like apple or blueberry, in which the crust is not partially cooked ahead of time. They recommend a pate brisee for pumpkin and sweet potatoe pies, in which the crust is partially pre-baked.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This crust is perfect; I will never make a pumpkin pie without it.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This is, hands down, the best crust I’ve ever made. There was no pie crust recipe with the lemon meringue pie from Alice Waters (and by the way, run don’t walk over to that recipe too!).  Crust flaked beautifully and was flavorful. It’s also pretty fast with the food processor technique and only 45 minutes to chill in the fridge. Next time I would double the recipe and make an extra to freeze. If there was a 6th star I’d give it!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Why, oh why do recipes for blind baking say to use foil? Never use foil, chances are good the dough will stick and you’ll lose your crust. Use parchment!

Learned this the hard way.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: I put theses 2 recipes together years ago, whenever ATK first came out with this pumpkin pie recipe. It was a huge success from the start. I have been using the vodka pie crust also since the day ATK published it. It is absolutely no fail. Sussing out good recipes has always been one of my talents.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody:
I was so focused on getting the crust just right, pre-baking and all and then mixing together all the ingredients for the filling, that I somehow managed to overlook all that cooking the filling on the stove part!
At first I was disappointed because the pie had still not set after 1hr. of baking. Then I let it cool down and it set into the best tasting pumpkin pie I ever had. What was all that cooking for? It seems unnesserary and would add a lot of prep time.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: I have cooked this many times since 2007 and it always kills. Thing is, you gotta commit and do the steps, including the sieve part; don't skimp, it is worth it in the end. Two tips: the filling will be about 50% over what you need for a mortal pie tin, put the rest in ramekins and cook alongside OR prep a very large pie tin to start. Also, the cooking time listed is very timid, mine is usually in there ~20m over what is listed, but i keep checking till i see that perfect jiggle. Magnificent!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: The filling looks generous in this recipe. What size pie pan works best?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: The filling generously filled a 9" pie pan for me.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: The crust was a disaster - the sides of the crust slid down about halfway down the pan. Recommend pricking the crust all over before baking to reduce movement. I spent 30 minutes pushing the filling through a sieve (which was completely clogged with the grated fresh ginger). Recipe should define how much leftover 'goop' you should discard after straining. Food processor would be easier. Lots of excess filling. Pie filling bubbled in baking and surface of pie is pocked with craters.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: Your pie crust dough was too warm when you placed it in oven resulting in the goop you describe. You need to refrigerate it twice at 15 minutes each time before baking it and make sure to use pie weights or pennies.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: The finished crust is wonderfully crisp and tender, but I found it much harder to work with than my usual pâte sucrée recipe. I live in a very dry part of the country and needed to double the amount of liquid to make the mixture come together. The filling is rich in flavor and worth the effort.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This pie was absolutely delicious. I was skeptical about whether putting the filling through a strainer was worth the effort, but it made the filling velvety smooth. I followed the crust recipe to a T and it came out perfectly. My only quibble is I wasn't sure whether the pie, once filled, should still be baked on the lowest rack or moved to the middle rack. I moved it around while baking and that's probably why it took longer than the recipe states to set.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This seems like more liquids (milk, yams) than usual for a pumpkin pie.  I don't want my pie to overflow on the crust, burning the pastry edge.  What size pie plate should be used?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody:
I tried this recipe but had neither vodka nor canned yams, so used whiskey and some fresh sweet potatoes that I candied before adding into the mix. I also used all butter instead of part shortening. 
When I opened my can of pumpkin I realized it was a pie filling, not just fruit. This made me really sad , but there was no going back! So I omitted the sugar.
The result was still delicious, but if I make it again I’ll use fresh pumpkin and I might actually replace the yam with more pumpkin too!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This pumpkin pie was a lot more work than what I saw my grandma do, but worth every minute. My family asked that I never lose this recipe! I used canned sweet potatos and because I 2.5x the recipe I used 2c heavy wipping cream and a half cup of evaporated milk. I wanted to make sure I had enough filling for 2 pies and 2.5x was the only mistake I made - I filled all the ramekins in my house and then some! I also used ground ginger instead of fresh because I ran out. This is a super recipe.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone had a problem with this crust falling apart in the oven?  Mine just did.  It looked perfect going in, but within 5 minutes, a huge chunk of the pinched trim fell off into the bottom of the oven.  Sad, so sad.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: What size pie pan is this for?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This is one of my favorite pies. I was not able to get any candied yams so I replaced it with a second can of pumpkin, and it still turned out great. I did have some issues with the amount of time it took to bake, as it took about an hour altogether.  I ended up increasing the temperature to 350 and baked for an addition half hour. Would definitely make this again!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: Super yum!! Excellent crust but pretty time consuming. Will say while adding the vodka and cold water, I was skeptical, as the dough if one follows the amounts of fluid to be added, would yield a very wet initial crust. I used a little over half of the recommended fluid. I let it sweat for a few minutes before wrapping w plastic. But the several stages of chilling after rolling the dough with lots of additional flour, resulted in a beautiful golden crust. Avoid any more than negligible handling.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: Used for crust

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This pie and crust are amazing.  The pie filling does not crack like most pumpkin pies, and the straining process makes it the smoothest, creamiest pumpkin pie you can imagine.  The downside is that this pie/crust takes a long time to make, including multiple bakings of the crust, and the straining is not easy. But I believe it’s worth it!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: So close to perfect, but wish I'd tweaked flavors more. I used 1/4 C of brown sugar, cut white sugar to 1/4 C, and increased maple syrup to 1/2 C. Used an immersion blender after cooking filling, which was so much easier than straining, and created a very smooth texture. Added 1/8 tsp ground cloves, and subbed Bourbon for Vanilla. Recipe's 1 tsp of salt made it saltier than we like, and I wish I'd increased the spices so they popped more. Baked 85 mins to slight jiggle - perfect texture!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody:
Change seasonings to:
1 tsp cinnamon
1/2 tsp ginger
1/4 tsp nutmeg or allspice
1/8 tsp cloves. 

Adjust to taste

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: Simply the best pumpkin pie! Have been making it for years and will make no other. I use the crust for other pies, too!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: This is my 'go to' pumpkin pie for Thanksgiving although I use Alice Waters pie crust recipe from The Art of Simple Food cookbook which has never failed me. Pie gets raves every Thanksgiving. Even my dog drools when I bring it to table!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: The pie was silky smooth. Everyone like it. So make it once. But then, like everyone says, make this recipe for pie dough again and again. This is the only one I will use for now on!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Pie With a Vodka Crust Recipe
commentBody: I have found that I need to use more fluid, the dough does not hold together?  I have ended up adding another tablespoon of the vodka water mixture.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: This soup turned out great! I added fresh rosemary and thyme as well as a dried bay leaf while cooking the beans. Used navy beans and did not soak. Cooked about 2-2.5 hrs and they were tender as can be. Held out about 1.5 cups of beans and pureed the rest with an immersion blender. Hot sauce added individually when served.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: How long should the beans be soaked?  (I’m going to use cannellini/white beans). Thanks!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: If you are using white beans, they MUST be soaked beforehand.  I did not, since the recipe said it wasn't necessary, and after cooking a few hours, the beans were still uncooked, plus their "skins" we're intact so it was impossible to put them through a food mill to mash them.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: Quite good, easy and versatile. I used meaty ham bone left over from a baked ham. Rancho Gordo Yellow Indian Woman beans (quick-soaked), collards and green cabbage, a bay leaf and a little smoked paprika. Pandemic lunch for several days!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Browned my onions and garlic before adding soup stock and beans.
Later added a couple of carrots and chopped up ginger bits.  Only used 2cups beans, well soaked and 3 bunches of collards.  Didn't have ham hock so used meaty cuts of Black Forest bacon ham.  Tasted great!

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: I have a question. Do you mash all the beans?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: I removed about a third of the beans before pureeing the rest with an immersion blender. Glad I did, as the whole beans made it heartier.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: We love this soup at our house.  Wonderful and easy and always better the next day.  I add red pepper flakes while cooking and a bottle of Tabasco sits on the table for those who like it even spicier.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: White Bean and Collard Soup Recipe
commentBody: Used ~ 4cups kale, blend beans a little less or remove some beans before blending and add back after. Added diced ham at the end that was from leftovers

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Curried Squash Soup With Frizzled Leeks Recipe
commentBody: I don’t see the instructions for the frizzled leeks … perhaps the post is outdated?

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Curried Squash Soup With Frizzled Leeks Recipe
commentBody: This soup is light enough for summer yet rich and delicious with great depth of flavor. I notched the heat up a bit by increasing the curry powder to 2 rounded teaspoons.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Curried Squash Soup With Frizzled Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
A long-time favorite. 
I modify slightly to make it vegetarian, and skip the frizzled leeks if pressed for time: still a winner.

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Curried Squash Soup With Frizzled Leeks Recipe
commentBody: Bland. This needs something—sherry, hot sauce. Not necessary to make drizzled leeks. You could use durkee fried onions

author: Emily Weinstein
recipeTitle: Curried Squash Soup With Frizzled Leeks Recipe
commentBody:
I used three tablespoons of curry - one spicy curry and two regular curry and a quarter tsp of red pepper flakes.  

I wish I had cooked the leeks and squash separate at first and then used a potato smasher on the squash.  Instead I fished them out of the soup, blended and then added back to leeks and broth. 

It was great!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Very delicious!  CAUTION:  i wished I had used a larger pan (it was bigger than what was recommended, but even still). The oil erupted over the sides and I had a raging river of oil everywhere. You’ll need a big pan.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: I frizzled up some leeks to use as a garnish for a potato and leek soup, and it worked really well. The semi-burnt, crunchy leeks added a ton of extra taste and texture to the soup. I made more leeks than I needed to garnish that soup. So by the time the soup was gone there were still a bunch of frizzled leeks left over. Turns out they are good in almost everything. Add some to boiling water and it makes an oniony broth. Dash some on top of any salad or pasta or add it to a grilled cheese.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: It’s possible to make version of this recipe in an air fryer, just toss the leek bits in oil and air fry, tossing often, until frizzled. Tasty and less mess.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Really great and easy.  Loved these.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Temp was way too high. Leeks burned immediately after put in oil.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: There is a bit of confusion in the instructions vs. the photo: instructions say to cut the leeks crosswise into 2” pieces, then cut those lengthwise into thin strips. The photo shows leeks that have been thinly cut crosswise. Either should be delicious!
